            "output": "Title: Password Storage - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series\nURL: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThis cheat sheet advises you on the proper methods for storing passwords for authentication. When passwords are stored, they must be protected from an attacker even if the application or database is compromised. Fortunately, a majority of modern languages and frameworks provide built-in functionality to help store passwords safely.\n[...]\nHowever, once an attacker has acquired stored password hashes, they are always able to brute force hashes offline. Defenders can slow down offline attacks by selecting hash algorithms that are as resource intensive as possible.\n[...]\n- Use Argon2id with a minimum configuration of 19 MiB of memory, an iteration count of 2, and 1 degree of parallelism.\n- If Argon2id is not available, use scrypt with a minimum CPU/memory cost parameter of (2^17), a minimum block size of 8 (1024 bytes), and a parallelization parameter of 1.\n- For legacy systems using bcrypt, use a work factor of 10 or more and with a password limit of 72 bytes.\n- If FIPS-140 compliance is required, use PBKDF2 with a work factor of 600,000 or more and set with an internal hash function of HMAC-SHA-256.\n- Consider using a pepper to provide additional defense in depth (though alone, it provides no additional secure characteristics).\n[...]\nIn this strategy, a password is hashed as usual using a password hashing algorithm. The resulting password hash is then hashed again using an HMAC (e.g., HMAC-SHA256, HMAC-SHA512, depending on the desired output length) before storing the resulting hash in the database. In this case the pepper is acting as the HMAC key and should be generated as per requirements of the HMAC algorithm.\n[...]\nAn alternative approach is to pre-hash the user-supplied password with a fast algorithm\n[...]\nbcrypt(H($password)), $salt, $cost)`).. This can be dangerous because of null bytes in the hash output value and because of password shucking.\n[...]\nPassword shucking uses the fact, that it is easy to check if`bcrypt(base64(H($password))), $salt, $cost) == bcrypt(base64($leaked_hash), $salt, $cost)`. If the inner hash function`H` is used with the same password somewhere else and known to an attacker cracking the password can be reduced to breaking the hash function`H`. Just using pure SHA-512, ( i.e.`bcrypt(base64(sha512($password))), $salt, $cost)`) is a dangerous practice and is as secure as just using pure SHA-512. Password shucking only works if a leaked hash is known to the attacker, either through a breach database or rainbow tables. To mitigate password shucking a pepper can be used.\n[...]\nTo summarize if bcrypt has to be used and the password should to be pre-hashed you should do`bcrypt(base64(hmac-sha384(data:$password, key:$pepper)), $salt, $cost)` and store the pepper not in the database.\n[...]\nThe PBKDF2 algorithm requires that you select an internal hashing algorithm such as an HMAC or a variety of other hashing algorithms. HMAC-SHA-256 is widely supported and is recommended by NIST.\n[...]\n- PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1: 1,400,000 iterations\n- PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256: 600,000 iterations\n- PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512: 220,000 iterations\n[...]\nWhen PBKDF2 is used with an HMAC, and the password is longer than the hash function's block size (64 bytes for SHA-256), the password will be automatically pre-hashed. For example, the password \"This is a password longer than 512 bits which is the block size of SHA-256\" is converted to the hash value (in hex):`fa91498c139805af73f7ba275cca071e78d78675027000c99a9925e2ec92eedd`.\n[...]\nGood implementations of PBKDF2 perform pre-hashing before the expensive iterated hashing phase. However, some implementations perform the conversion on each iteration, which can make hashing long passwords significantly more expensive than hashing short passwords. When users supply very long passwords, a potential denial of service vulnerability could occur, such as the one published in Django during 2013. Manual pre-hashing can reduce this risk but requires adding a salt to the pre-hash step.\n\n---\n\nTitle: MASWE-0024: Improper Use of Message Authentication Code (MAC) - OWASP Mobile Application Security\nURL: https://mas.owasp.org/MASWE/MASVS-CRYPTO/MASWE-0024/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nMASWE-0024: Improper Use of Message Authentication Code (MAC) - OWASP Mobile Application Security\n[...]\n# MASWE-0024: Improper Use of Message Authentication Code (MAC)\n[...]\nImproper use of MACs in security sensitive contexts affecting data integrity.\n[...]\n- 💬 Connect with Us\n- Using HMAC with keys with insufficient entropy\n- Using HMAC with missing timestamp (or nonce)\n- Using MAC‑then‑encrypt or encrypt‑then‑MAC incorrectly, leaking information via timing or error messages\n- Allowing predictors (users or attackers) to control data inputs, creating scenarios where forged or replayed tags bypass integrity checks.\n- Hash functions lacking collision resistance (e.g., MD5 or SHA‑1 used in HMAC)\n- Use of non‑cryptographic checksums (e.g., CRC‑32 instead of HMAC)\n- MAC constructions that fail outside narrow assumptions (e.g., raw CBC‑MAC on variable‑length messages)\n- Tags that are too short significantly lower the effort required for forgery\n\n---\n\nTitle: Cryptographic Storage - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series\nURL: https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Cryptographic_Storage_Cheat_Sheet\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nPasswords should not be stored using reversible encryption - secure password hashing algorithms should be used instead. The Password Storage Cheat Sheet contains further guidance on storing passwords.\n[...]\nWhere available, authenticated modes should always be used. These provide guarantees of the integrity and authenticity of the data, as well as confidentiality. The most commonly used authenticated modes are GCM and CCM, which should be used as a first preference.\n[...]\nIf GCM or CCM are not available, then CTR mode or CBC mode should be used. As these do not provide any guarantees about the authenticity of the data, separate authentication should be implemented, such as using the Encrypt-then-MAC technique. Care needs to be taken when using this method with variable length messages\n[...]\nFor RSA, it is essential to enable Random Padding. Random Padding is also known as OAEP or Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding. This class of defense protects against Known Plain Text Attacks by adding randomness at the beginning of the payload.\n[...]\nSpecifically, version 1 UUIDs are comprised of a high precision timestamp and the MAC address of the system that generated them, so are not random (although they may be hard to guess, given the timestamp is to the nearest 100ns). Type 4 UUIDs are randomly generated, although whether this is done using a CSPRNG will depend on the implementation. Unless this is known to be secure in\n[...]\nspecific language or framework, the randomness of UUIDs should not be relied upon.\n[...]\nKeys should be randomly generated using a cryptographically secure function, such as those discussed in the Secure Random Number Generation section. Keys should not be based on common words or phrases, or on \"random\" characters generated by mashing the keyboard.\n[...]\nSecurely storing cryptographic keys is one of the hardest problems to solve, as the application always needs to have some level of access to the keys in order to decrypt the data. While it may not be possible to fully protect the keys from an attacker who has fully compromised the application, a number of steps can be taken to make it harder for them to obtain the keys.\n[...]\nHardware Security Module\n[...]\nHSM).\n[...]\nHashiCorp Vault\n[...]\n.NET framework\n[...]\nWhere possible, encryption keys should themselves be stored in an encrypted form. At least two separate keys are required for this:\n[...]\nKEK)\n[...]\nFor this to be effective, the KEK must be stored separately from the DEK. The encrypted DEK can be stored with the data, but will only be usable if an attacker is able to also obtain the KEK, which is stored on another system.\n[...]\nA key derivation function (KDF) could be used to generate a KEK from user-\n[...]\ninput (such a passphrase), which would then be used to encrypt a randomly generated DEK. This allows the KEK to be easily changed (when the user changes their passphrase), without needing to re-encrypt the data (as the DEK remains the same).\n\n---\n\nTitle: 04-hashing-and-hash-based-functions\nURL: https://cornucopia.owasp.org/taxonomy/asvs-5.0/11-cryptography/04-hashing-and-hash-based-functions\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n04-hashing-and-hash-based-functions\n\n \n\n \n\n \n\n \n\n \n\n# Hashing and Hash-based Functions \n\n \n\n## V11.4.1 \n\n \n\n Verify that only approved hash functions are used for general cryptographic use cases, including digital signatures, HMAC, KDF, and random bit generation. Disallowed hash functions, such as MD5, must not be used for any cryptographic purpose. \n\n \n\n Required for Level 1, 2 and 3 \n\n \n\n### Related CAPEC™ Requirements \n\n \n\n CAPEC™ (3.9):  112,  114,  145,  157,  162,  20,  204,  216,  218,  220,  272,  31,  37,  39,  461,  473,  55,  594,  620,  68,  94,  97 \n\n \n\n## V11.4.2 \n\n \n\n Verify that passwords are stored using an approved, computationally intensive, key derivation function (also known as a \"password hashing function\"), with parameter settings configured based on current guidance. The settings should balance security and performance to make brute-force attacks sufficiently challenging for the required level of security. \n\n \n\n Required for Level 2 and 3 \n\n \n\n### Related CAPEC™ Requirements \n\n \n\n CAPEC™ (3.9):  112,  20,  31,  37,  473,  55,  97 \n\n \n\n## V11.4.3 \n\n \n\n Verify that hash functions used in digital signatures, as part of data authentication or data integrity are collision resistant and have appropriate bit-lengths. If collision resistance is required, the output length must be at least 256 bits. If only resistance to second pre-image attacks is required, the output length must be at least 128 bits. \n\n \n\n Required for Level 2 and 3 \n\n \n\n### Related CAPEC™ Requirements \n\n \n\n CAPEC™ (3.9):  112,  114,  145,  157,  184,  20,  216,  218,  220,  272,  31,  37,  39,  438,  442,  473,  475,  523,  55,  594,  620,  68,  690,  75,  94,  97 \n\n \n\n## V11.4.4 \n\n \n\n Verify that the application uses approved key derivation functions with key stretching parameters when deriving secret keys from passwords. The parameters in use must balance security and performance to prevent brute-force attacks from compromising the resulting cryptographic key. \n\n \n\n Required for Level 2 and 3 \n\n \n\n### Related CAPEC™ Requirements \n\n \n\n CAPEC™ (3.9):  112,  20,  31,  37,  473,  55,  97 \n\n \n\n## Disclaimer \n\n \n\n Credit via  OWASP ASVS.For more information visit:  The OWASP ASVS Project or  Github respository.. A programmer can attempt to remedy the password management problem by obscuring the password with an encoding function, such as base 64 encoding, but this effort does not adequately protect the password.\n[...]\nSimilarly, using fast cryptographic hash functions or lightly modified constructions (for example, chaining SHA-256 and SHA-512) does not adequately protect stored passwords against rainbow table or precomputation attacks. Such approaches remain computationally cheap and can be recomputed by attackers. OWASP recommends using dedicated, memory-hard password hashing functions such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2.\n[...]\nStoring a plaintext password in a configuration file allows anyone who can read the file access to the password-protected resource. Developers sometimes believe that they cannot defend the application from someone who has access to the configuration, but this attitude makes an attacker’s job easier. Good password management guidelines require that a password never be stored in plaintext.",

            "output": "Title: RFC 2104 - HMAC: Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication\nURL: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc2104\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAbstract\n\n   This document describes HMAC, a mechanism for message authentication\n   using cryptographic hash functions. HMAC can be used with any\n   iterative cryptographic hash function, e.g., MD5, SHA-1, in\n   combination with a secret shared key.  The cryptographic strength of\n   HMAC depends on the properties of the underlying hash function.\n[...]\n1. Introduction\n\n   Providing a way to check the integrity of information transmitted\n   over or stored in an unreliable medium is a prime necessity in the\n   world of open computing and communications. Mechanisms that provide\n   such integrity check based on a secret key are usually called\n   \"message authentication codes\" (MAC). Typically, message\n   authentication codes are used between two parties that share a secret\n   key in order to validate information transmitted between these\n   parties. In this document we present such a MAC mechanism based on\n   cryptographic hash functions. This mechanism, called HMAC, is based\n   on work by the authors [BCK1] where the construction is presented and\n   cryptographically analyzed. We refer to that work for the details on\n   the rationale and security analysis of HMAC, and its comparison to\n   other keyed-hash methods.\n[...]\n1,\n[...]\n2. Definition of HMAC\n\n   The definition of HMAC requires a cryptographic hash function, which\n   we denote by H, and a secret key K. We assume H to be a cryptographic\n   hash function where data is hashed by iterating a basic compression\n   function on blocks of data.   We denote by B the byte-length of such\n   blocks (B=64 for all the above mentioned examples of hash functions),\n   and by L the byte-length of hash outputs (L=16 for MD5, L=20 for\n   SHA-1).  The authentication key K can be of any length up to B, the\n   block length of the hash function.  Applications that use keys longer\n   than B bytes will first hash the key using H and then use the\n   resultant L byte string as the actual key to HMAC. In any case the\n   minimal recommended length for K is L bytes (as the hash output\n   length). See section 3 for more information on keys.\n[...]\n4. Implementation Note\n\n   HMAC is defined in such a way that the underlying hash function H can\n   be used with no modification to its code. In particular, it uses the\n   function H with the pre-defined initial value IV (a fixed value\n   specified by each iterative hash function to initialize its\n   compression function).  However, if desired, a performance\n   improvement can be achieved at the cost of (possibly) modifying the\n   code of H to support variable IVs.\n\n   The idea is that\n[...]\nThese intermediate results are stored and then used to\n   initialize the IV of H each time that a message needs to be\n   authenticated.  This method saves, for each authenticated\n[...]\nSuch a savings may be\n   significant when authenticating short streams of data\n[...]\nin the above\n[...]\n6. Security\n\n   The security of the message authentication mechanism presented here\n   depends on cryptographic properties of the hash function H: the\n   resistance to collision finding (limited to the case where the\n   initial value is secret and random, and where the output of the\n   function is not explicitly available to the attacker), and the\n   message authentication property of the compression function of H when\n   applied to single blocks (in HMAC these blocks are partially unknown\n   to an attacker as they contain the result of the inner H computation\n   and, in particular, cannot be fully chosen by the attacker).\n\n   These properties, and actually stronger ones, are commonly assumed\n   for hash functions of the kind used with HMAC. In particular, a hash\n   function for which the above properties do not hold would become\n   unsuitable for most (probably, all) cryptographic applications,\n   including alternative message authentication schemes based on such\n   functions.  (For a complete analysis and rationale of the HMAC\n   function the reader is referred to\n[...]\nBCK1].)\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 2085 - HMAC-MD5 IP Authentication with Replay Prevention\nURL: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2085\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAbstract\n\n   This document describes a keyed-MD5 transform to be used in\n   conjunction with the IP Authentication Header [RFC-1826]. The\n   particular transform is based on [HMAC-MD5].  An option is also\n   specified to guard against replay attacks.\n[...]\n1. Introduction\n\n   The Authentication Header (AH) [RFC-1826] provides integrity and\n   authentication for IP datagrams. The transform specified in this\n   document uses a keyed-MD5 mechanism [HMAC-MD5].  The mechanism uses\n   the (key-less) MD5 hash function [RFC-1321] which produces a message\n   digest. When combined with an AH Key, authentication data is\n   produced. This value is placed in the Authentication Data field of\n   the AH [RFC-1826]. This value is also the basis for the data\n   integrity service offered by the AH protocol.\n[...]\n```\nRFC 2085                        HMAC-MD5                  February 1997\n\n   To provide protection against replay attacks, a Replay Prevention\n   field is included as a transform option.  This field is used to help\n   prevent attacks in which a message is stored and re-used later,\n   replacing or repeating the original.  The Security Parameters Index\n   (SPI) [RFC-1825] is used to determine whether this option is included\n   in the AH.\n\n   Familiarity with the following documents is assumed: \"Security\n   Architecture for the Internet Protocol\" [RFC-1825], \"IP\n   Authentication Header\" [RFC-1826], and \"HMAC-MD5: Keyed-MD5 for\n   Message Authentication\" [HMAC-MD5].\n\n   All implementations that claim conformance or compliance with the IP\n   Authentication Header specification [RFC-1826] MUST implement this\n   HMAC-MD5 transform.\n[...]\nRFC 2085                        HMAC-MD\n[...]\n2.1 Replay Prevention\n\n   The Replay Prevention field is a 64-bit value used to guarantee that\n   each packet exchanged between two parties is different.  Each IPsec\n   Security Association specifies whether Replay Prevention is used for\n   that Security Association.  If Replay Prevention is NOT in use, then\n   the Authentication Data field will directly follow the SPI field.\n\n   The 64-bit field is an up counter starting at a value of 1.\n\n   The secret shared key must not be used for a period of time that\n   allows the counter to wrap, that is, to transmit more than 2^64\n   packets using a single key.\n\n   Upon receipt, the replay value is assured to be increasing.\n[...]\nimplementation may accept out of order packets\n[...]\nto accept out of order\n[...]\n\"out of\n[...]\n2.2 Authentication Data Calculation\n\n   The authentication data is the output of the authentication algorithm\n   (MD5).  This value is calculated over the entire IP datagram. Fields\n   within the datagram that are variant during transit and the\n   authentication data field itself, must contain all zeros prior to the\n   computation [RFC-1826].  The Replay Prevention field if present, is\n   included in the calculation.\n\n   The definition and reference implementation of MD5 appears in [RFC-\n   1321].  Let 'text' denote the data to which HMAC-MD5 is to be applied\n   and K be the message authentication secret key shared by the parties.\n   If K is longer than 64-bytes it MUST first be hashed using MD5.  In\n   this case, K is the resulting hash.\n[...]\nis described in more detail, along with example\n[...]\ncode and performance\n[...]\n, in [HMAC-MD\n[...]\n]. Implementers\n[...]\nshould consult [HMAC-MD5] for more information on this technique\n      for keying a cryptographic hash function.\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 2104 - HMAC: Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication\nURL: http://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2104\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAbstract\n\n   This document describes HMAC, a mechanism for message authentication\n   using cryptographic hash functions. HMAC can be used with any\n   iterative cryptographic hash function, e.g., MD5, SHA-1, in\n   combination with a secret shared key.  The cryptographic strength of\n   HMAC depends on the properties of the underlying hash function.\n[...]\n1. Introduction\n\n   Providing a way to check the integrity of information transmitted\n   over or stored in an unreliable medium is a prime necessity in the\n   world of open computing and communications. Mechanisms that provide\n   such integrity check based on a secret key are usually called\n   \"message authentication codes\" (MAC). Typically, message\n   authentication codes are used between two parties that share a secret\n   key in order to validate information transmitted between these\n   parties. In this document we present such a MAC mechanism based on\n   cryptographic hash functions. This mechanism, called HMAC, is based\n   on work by the authors [BCK1] where the construction is presented and\n   cryptographically analyzed. We refer to that work for the details on\n   the rationale and security analysis of HMAC, and its comparison to\n   other keyed-hash methods.\n[...]\nRFC 2104                          HMAC                     February 1997\n\n   HMAC can be used in combination with any iterated cryptographic hash\n   function. MD5 and SHA-1 are examples of such hash functions. HMAC\n   also uses a secret key for calculation and verification of the\n   message authentication values. The main goals behind this\n   construction are\n\n   * To use, without modifications, available hash functions.\n     In particular, hash functions that perform well in software,\n     and for which code is freely and widely available.\n\n   * To preserve the original performance of the hash function without\n     incurring a significant degradation.\n\n   * To use and handle keys in a simple way.\n\n   * To have a well understood cryptographic analysis of the strength of\n     the authentication mechanism based on reasonable assumptions on the\n     underlying hash function.\n\n   * To allow for easy replaceability of the underlying hash function in\n     case that faster or more secure hash functions are found or\n     required.\n\n   This document specifies HMAC using a generic cryptographic hash\n   function (denoted by H). Specific instantiations of HMAC need to\n   define a particular hash function. Current candidates for such hash\n   functions include SHA-1 [SHA], MD5 [MD5], RIPEMD-128/160 [RIPEMD].\n   These different realizations of HMAC will be denoted by HMAC-SHA1,\n   HMAC-MD5, HMAC-RIPEMD, etc.\n\n   Note\n[...]\nthe use of\n[...]\n3. Keys\n\n   The key for HMAC can be of any length (keys longer than B bytes are\n   first hashed using H).  However, less than L bytes is strongly\n   discouraged as it would decrease the security strength of the\n   function.  Keys longer than L bytes are acceptable but the extra\n   length would not significantly increase the function strength. (A\n   longer key may be advisable if the randomness of the key is\n   considered weak.)\n\n   Keys need to be chosen at random (or using a cryptographically strong\n   pseudo-random generator seeded with a random seed), and periodically\n   refreshed.  (Current attacks do not indicate a specific recommended\n   frequency for key changes as these attacks are practically\n   infeasible.  However, periodic key refreshment is a fundamental\n   security practice that helps against potential weaknesses of the\n   function and keys, and limits the damage of an exposed key.)\n[...]\nadversarial effect on information authenticated in the past.  This\n   is in sharp contrast with encryption, where\n[...]\nfuture if, and when\n[...]\nRFC 2104\n[...]\nA correct implementation of the above construction, the choice of\n[...]\nrandom (or\n[...]\nare all essential ingredients for the security of\n   the integrity verification mechanism provided by HMAC.\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 2202 - Test Cases for HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1\nURL: http://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2202\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAbstract\n\n   This document provides two sets of test cases for HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-\n   SHA-1, respectively. HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1 are two constructs of\n   the HMAC [HMAC] message authentication function using the MD5 [MD5]\n   hash function and the SHA-1 [SHA] hash function. Both constructs are\n   used by IPSEC [OG,CG] and other protocols to authenticate messages.\n   The test cases and results provided in this document are meant to be\n   used as a conformance test for HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1\n   implementations.\n[...]\n1. Introduction\n\n   The general method for constructing a HMAC message authentication\n   function using a particular hash function is described in section 2\n   of [HMAC]. We will not repeat the description here. Section 5 of\n   [HMAC] also discusses truncating the output of HMAC; the rule is that\n   we should keep the more significant bits (the bits in the left,\n   assuming a network byte order (big-endian)).\n\n   In sections 2 and 3 we provide test cases for HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-\n   1, respectively. Each case includes the key, the data, and the\n   result.  The values of keys and data are either hexadecimal numbers\n   (prefixed by \"0x\") or ASCII character strings in double quotes. If a\n   value is an ASCII character string, then the HMAC computation for the\n   corresponding test case DOES NOT include the trailing null character\n   ('\\0') in the string.\n[...]\nare cross-verified by three\n   independent implementations, one from NIST and two from IBM Research.\n   One IBM\n[...]\nuses optimized code that is very different\n   from\n[...]\n. The code for HMAC-MD5 is similar, just replace the strings\n   \"SHA\" and \"sha\" with \"MD5\" and \"md5\". The proposed mechanism\ncombines the (key-less) MD5 hash function [RFC-1321] with a shared\nsecret key.  The description of HMAC-MD5 in this document is\nindependent of its use in any particular protocol. An analogous\nmechanism can be used in combination with other iterative hash\nfunctions, e.g., SHA.\n[...]\nThe description of this transform in this document\nis independent of its use in any particular protocol. It is intended\nto serve as a common mechanism for the many protocols (especially,\nIETF protocols) that require integrity verification based on\na shared secret key (e.g., IP Authentication Header [RFC-1826]).\n[...]\nThe definition and reference implementation of MD5 appears in\n[RFC-1321].  Let `text' denote the data to which HMAC-MD5 is\nto be applied and K be the message authentication secret key shared by\nthe parties. The key K can be of any length up to the block length of\nthe hash function, namely, 64 bytes for MD5 (however, 16 bytes is the\nminimal recommended length for keys -- see section 3).\n[...]\nThe key for HMAC-MD5 can be of any length (keys longer than 64 bytes \nare first hashed using MD5).  However, less than 16 bytes is strongly \ndiscouraged as it would decrease the security strength of the function. \nKeys longer than 16 bytes are acceptable but the extra length would not \nsignificantly increase the function strength. (A longer key may be \nadvisable if the randomness of the key is considered weak.)\n[...]\nagainst potential weaknesses\n[...]\nImplementation of HMAC-MD5 requires the implementation of MD5\n(see [RFC-1321]) and the calculation described in Section 2.\n[...]\n. However, if desired, a performance\n[...]\nimprovement can be achieved by\n[...]\nsimple adaptation of\n[...]\npresented next.\n[...]\nto implement HMAC\n[...]\nand has no\n[...]\neffect on inter-operability.)\n[...]\nThe idea is that the intermediate results of MD5 on the\n64-byte blocks (K XOR ipad) and (K XOR opad) can be\nprecomputed only once at the time of generation of the key K, or before\nits first use. These intermediate results (called \"contexts\", and\nstored under MD5's structure MD5_CTX [RFC-1321]) are then used to\ninitialize the MD5 function each time that a message needs to be\nauthenticated.  (This involves setting the MD5_CTX variable to the\nstored value and applying MD5Update to the data.) This method saves,\nfor each authenticated message, \nthe application of the MD5's compression function (MD5Update) on two\n64-byte blocks; a savings which may be significant when authenticating\nshort streams of data.  We stress that the stored contexts need to be\ntreated and protected the same as secret keys.\n[...]\nThe security of the message authentication mechanism presented here\ndepends on cryptographic properties of MD5: the resistance to\ncollision finding (limited to the case where the initial value is\nsecret and random, and where the output of the function is not\nexplicitly available to the attacker), and the message authentication\nproperty of the compression function of MD5 when applied to single\nblocks (these blocks are partially unknown to an attacker as they\ncontain the result of the internal MD5 computation and, in particular,\ncannot be fully chosen by the attacker).\n[...]\nA correct implementation of the above construction, the choice of\nrandom (or cryptographically pseudorandom) keys, a secure key\nexchange mechanism, frequent key refreshments, and good secrecy\nprotection of keys are all essential ingredients for the security of\nthe integrity verification mechanism provided by HMAC-MD5.\n[...]\n_len,\n[...]\nkey XORd\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 4231 - Identifiers and Test Vectors for HMAC-SHA-224, HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-384, and HMAC-SHA-512\nURL: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc4231/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAbstract\n\n   This document provides test vectors for the HMAC-SHA-224,\n   HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-384, and HMAC-SHA-512 message authentication\n   schemes. It also provides ASN.1 object identifiers and URIs to\n   identify use of these schemes in protocols using ASN.1 constructs\n   (such as those built on Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions\n   (S/MIME) [4]) or protocols based on XML constructs (such as those\n   leveraging XML Digital Signatures [5]).\n\n   HMAC-SHA-224 is the realization of the HMAC message authentication\n   code [1] using the SHA-224 hash function, HMAC-SHA-256 is the\n   realization of the HMAC message authentication code using the SHA-256\n   hash function, HMAC-SHA-384 is the realization of the HMAC message\n   authentication code using the SHA-384 hash function, and HMAC-SHA-512\n   is the realization of the HMAC message authentication code using the\n   SHA-512 hash function.  SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512 are\n   all described in [2].\n[...]\n3.1. HMAC with MD5\n   provides data origin authentication and integrity protection.\n\n   Further information on the other components necessary for ESP and AH\n   implementations is provided by [Thayer97a].\n[...]\n1.  Introduction\n\n   This memo specifies the use of MD5 [RFC-1321] combined with HMAC\n   [RFC-2104] as a keyed authentication mechanism within the context of\n   the Encapsulating Security Payload and the Authentication Header.\n   The goal of HMAC-MD5-96 is to ensure that the packet is authentic and\n   cannot be modified in transit.\n\n   HMAC is a secret key authentication algorithm. Data integrity and\n   data origin authentication as provided by HMAC are dependent upon the\n   scope of the distribution of the secret key. If only the source and\n   destination know the HMAC key, this provides both data origin\n   authentication and data integrity for packets sent between the two\n   parties; if the HMAC is correct, this proves that it must have been\n   added by the source.\n[...]\n2. Algorithm and Mode\n\n   [RFC-1321] describes the underlying MD5 algorithm, while [RFC-2104]\n   describes the HMAC algorithm. The HMAC algorithm provides a framework\n   for inserting various hashing algorithms such as MD5.\n\n   HMAC-MD5-96 operates on 64-byte blocks of data.  Padding requirements\n   are specified in [RFC-1321] and are part of the MD5 algorithm.  If\n   MD5 is built according to [RFC-1321], there is no need to add any\n   additional padding as far as HMAC-MD5-96 is concerned.  With regard\n   to \"implicit packet padding\" as defined in [AH], no implicit packet\n   padding is required.\n\n   HMAC-MD5-96 produces a 128-bit authenticator value.  This 128-bit\n   value can be truncated as described in RFC 2104.  For use with either\n   ESP or AH, a truncated value using the first 96 bits MUST be\n   supported.  Upon sending, the truncated value is stored within the\n   authenticator field.  Upon receipt, the entire 128-bit value is\n   computed and the first 96 bits are compared to the value stored in\n   the authenticator field.  No other authenticator value lengths are\n   supported by HMAC-MD5-96.\n\n   The length of 96 bits was selected because it is the default\n   authenticator length as specified in [AH] and meets the security\n   requirements described in [RFC-2104].\n[...]\nlength of 128\n[...]\nations in [\n[...]\ncryptographic attacks against\n[...]\nstrongest attack know against HMAC\n[...]\nbyte block hash\n[...]\ninvolving the successful\n[...]\nwould generally be\n[...]\nbe unusable.\n[...]\nimportant to consider that\n[...]\ncriteria from the\n[...]\n.\n\n   [RFC-2104] also discusses the potential additional security\n[...]\nstrongly encouraged to perform\n[...]\ntruncation.\n\n   As [RFC-2104] provides a framework for incorporating various hash\n   algorithms with HMAC, it is possible to replace MD5 with other\n   algorithms such as SHA-1. [RFC-2104] contains a detailed discussion\n   on the strengths and weaknesses of HMAC algorithms.\n\n   As is true with any cryptographic algorithm, part of its strength\n   lies in the correctness of the algorithm implementation, the security\n   of the key management mechanism and its implementation, the strength\n   of the associated secret key, and upon the correctness of the\n   implementation in all of the participating systems.  [RFC-2202]\n[...]\ncontains test vectors and example code to assist in verifying the\n[...]\ncorrectness of HMAC-MD5-96 code.\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 4868 - Using HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-384, and HMAC-SHA-512 with IPsec\nURL: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc4868\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAbstract\n\n   This specification describes the use of Hashed Message Authentication\n   Mode (HMAC) in conjunction with the SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512\n   algorithms in IPsec.  These algorithms may be used as the basis for\n   data origin authentication and integrity verification mechanisms for\n   the Authentication Header (AH), Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP),\n   Internet Key Exchange Protocol (IKE), and IKEv2 protocols, and also\n   as Pseudo-Random Functions (PRFs) for IKE and IKEv2.  Truncated\n   output lengths are specified for the authentication-related variants,\n   with the corresponding algorithms designated as HMAC-SHA-256-128,\n   HMAC-SHA-384-192, and HMAC-SHA-512-256.  The PRF variants are not\n   truncated, and are called PRF-HMAC-SHA-256, PRF-HMAC-SHA-384, and\n   PRF-HMAC-SHA-512.\n[...]\n1.  Introduction\n\n   This document specifies the use of SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512\n   [SHA2-1] combined with HMAC [HMAC] as data origin authentication and\n   integrity verification mechanisms for the IPsec AH [AH], ESP [ESP],\n   IKE [IKE], and IKEv2 [IKEv2] protocol.  Output truncation is\n   specified for these variants, with the corresponding algorithms\n   designated as HMAC-SHA-256-128, HMAC-SHA-384-192, and HMAC-SHA-512-\n   256.  These truncation lengths are chosen in accordance with the\n   birthday bound for each algorithm.\n\n   This specification also describes untruncated variants of these\n   algorithms as Pseudo-Random Functions (PRFs) for use with IKE and\n   IKEv2, and those algorithms are called PRF-HMAC-SHA-256, PRF-HMAC-\n   SHA-384, and PRF-HMAC-SHA-512.  For ease of reference, these PRF\n   algorithms and the authentication variants described above are\n   collectively referred to below as \"the HMAC-SHA-256+ algorithms\".\n\n   The goal of the PRF variants are to provide secure pseudo-random\n   functions suitable for generation of keying material and other\n   protocol-specific numeric quantities, while the goal of the\n   authentication variants is to ensure that packets are authentic and\n   cannot be modified in transit.  The relative security of HMAC-SHA-\n   256+ when used in either case is dependent on the distribution scope\n   and unpredictability of the associated secret key.  If the key is\n   unpredictable and known only by the sender and recipient, these\n   algorithms ensure that only parties holding an identical key can\n   derive the associated values.\n[...]\n2.1.1.  Data Origin Authentication and Integrity Verification Usage\n\n   HMAC-SHA-256+ are secret key algorithms.  While no fixed key length\n   is specified in [HMAC], this specification requires that when used as\n   an integrity/authentication algorithm, a fixed key length equal to\n   the output length of the hash functions MUST be supported, and key\n   lengths other than the output length of the associated hash function\n   MUST NOT be supported.\n\n   These key length restrictions are based in part on the\n   recommendations in [HMAC] (key lengths less than the output length\n   decrease security strength, and keys longer than the output length do\n   not significantly increase security strength), and in part because\n   allowing variable length keys for IPsec authenticator functions would\n   create interoperability issues.\n[...]\n2.1.3.  Random\n[...]\nStrength\n\n   [HMAC\n[...]\ndiscusses requirements for key material, including a\n[...]\n2.3.  Truncation\n\n   The HMAC-SHA-256+ algorithms each produce an nnn-bit value, where nnn\n   corresponds to the output bit length of the algorithm, e.g., HMAC-\n   SHA-nnn.  For use as an authenticator, this nnn-bit value can be\n   truncated as described in [HMAC].  When used as a data origin\n   authentication and integrity verification algorithm in ESP, AH, IKE,\n   or IKEv2, a truncated value using the first nnn/2 bits -- exactly\n   half the algorithm output size -- MUST be supported.  No other\n   authenticator value lengths are supported by this specification.",

            "output": "Title: N/A\nURL: https://csrc.nist.gov/files/pubs/fips/198-1/final/docs/fips-198-1_final.pdf\nPublished: 2009-02-20T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: NIST Computer Security Division (CSD)\nHighlights:\n890\n[...]\nersburg, MD\n[...]\nita Furlani, Director\n[...]\nAbstract \nThis Standard describes a keyed-hash message authentication code (HMAC), a \nmechanism for message authentication using cryptographic hash functions. HMAC can \nbe used with any iterative Approved cryptographic hash function, in combination with a \nshared secret key. \nKeywords: computer security, cryptography, HMAC, MAC, message authentication, \nFederal Information Processing Standards (FIPS).\n[...]\n11. Implementation Schedule. Guidance regarding the testing and validation to FIPS \n198-1 and its relationship to FIPS 140-2 can be found in IG 1.10 of the Implementation \nGuidance for FIPS PUB 140-2 and the Cryptographic Module Validation Program at \nhttp://csrc.nist.gov/groups/STM/cmvp/index.html. \n12. Qualifications\n[...]\nThe security afforded by the HMAC function is dependent on \nmaintaining the secrecy of\n[...]\nkey and the use of an appropriate Approved hash function\n[...]\nTherefore, users must guard against disclosure of these\n[...]\nimplementation is secure\n[...]\nused. The\n[...]\nProviding a way to check the integrity of information transmitted over or stored in an \nunreliable medium is a prime necessity in the world of open computing and \ncommunications. Mechanisms that provide such integrity checks based on a secret key \nare usually called message authentication codes (MACs). Typically, message \nauthentication codes are used between two parties that share a secret key in order to \nauthenticate information transmitted between these parties. This Standard defines a MAC \nthat uses a cryptographic hash function in conjunction with a secret key. This mechanism \nis called HMAC [HMAC]. HMAC shall use an Approved cryptographic hash function \n[FIPS 180-3]. HMAC uses the secret key for the calculation and verification of the \nMACs. \n2. GLOSSARY OF TERMS AND ACRONYMS \n2.1 Glossary of Terms \nThe\n[...]\nHMAC uses a key, K, of appropriate security strength, as discussed in NIST Special \nPublication (SP) 800-107 [SP 800-107], Recommendation for Applications Using \nApproved Hash Algorithms. When an application uses a K longer than B-bytes, then it \nshall first hash the K using H and then use the resultant L-byte string as the key K0; detail \ncan be found in Table 1 in Section 4 below. \n4. HMAC SPECIFICATION \nTo compute a MAC over the data ‘text’ using the HMAC function, the following \noperation is performed: \nMAC(text) = HMAC(K, text) = H((K0 ⊕ opad )|| H((K0 ⊕ ipad) || text))\n[...]\n5. TRUNC\n[...]\nA well-known practice with MACs is to truncate their outputs (i.e., the length of the \nMACs used is less than the length of the output of the HMAC function L). Applications \nof this standard may truncate the outputs of the HMAC. When truncation is used, the λ\nleftmost bits of the output of the HMAC function shall be used as the MAC. For \ninformation about the choice of λ and the security implications of using truncated outputs \nof the HMAC function, see SP 800-107. \n6. IMPLEMENTATION NOTE \nThe HMAC algorithm is specified for an arbitrary Approved iterative cryptographic hash \nfunction, H. In the HMAC algorithm, values of the ipad and the opad depend on the \nblock size, B, of the Approved hash function. An HMAC implementation can easily \nreplace one Approved iterative hash function, H, with another Approved iterative hash \n5 \n \nfunction, H’ by generating new ipad and opad using the block size of H’ instead of H as \ndefined in Section 2.3.\n[...]\nFor \neach authenticated message\n[...]\n, this method saves\n[...]\napplication of the hash \nfunction of H on two B-byte blocks (i.e., on (K ⊕ ipad) and (K ⊕ opad)). This saving \nmay be significant when authenticating short streams of data. These stored intermediate \nvalues shall be treated and protected in the same manner as secret keys\n\n---\n\nTitle: SP 800-107, Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms | CSRC\nURL: https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/107/final\nPublished: 2009-02-25T04:24:43.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSP 800-107, Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms | CSRC\n[...]\n# Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms\n[...]\nCryptographic hash functions that compute a fixed- length message digest from arbitrary length messages are widely used for many purposes in information security. This document provides security guidelines for achieving the required or desired security strengths when using cryptographic applications that employ the approved cryptographic hash functions specified in Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 180-3. These include functions such as digital signature applications, Keyed-hash Message Authentication Codes (HMACs) and Hash-based Key Derivation Functions (HKDFs).\n[...]\nCryptographic hash functions that compute a fixed- length message digest from arbitrary length messages are widely used for many purposes in information security. These include functions such as digital signature applications, Keyed-hash Message Authentication Codes (HMACs) and Hash-based Key Derivation Functions (HKDFs). Authentication Code (HMAC): Specification of HMAC and Recommendations for Message Authentication | CSRC\nURL: http://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/224/ipd\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSP 800-224, Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC): Specification of HMAC and Recommendations for Message Authentication | CSRC\n[...]\n### NIST SP 800-224 (Initial Public Draft)\n[...]\n# Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC): Specification of HMAC and Recommendations for Message Authentication\n[...]\nThis publication includes the HMAC specification from Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 198-1, The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC) (2008) and incorporates some requirements from SP 800-107r1(Revision 1), Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms (2012). This development was proposed by the Crypto Publication Review Board based on the reviews of FIPS 198-1 and SP 800-107r1 in 2022. The final version of SP 800-224 is expected to be published concurrently with the withdrawal of FIPS 198-1.\n[...]\nA message authentication code (MAC) scheme is a symmetric-key cryptographic mechanism that can be used with a secret key to produce and verify an authentication tag, which enables detecting unauthorized modifications to data (also known as a message). This NIST Special Publication (whose current version is an initial public draft) specifies the keyed-hash message authentication code (HMAC) construction, which is a MAC scheme that uses a cryptographic hash function as a building block. The publication also specifies a set of requirements for using HMAC for message authentication, including a list of NIST-approved cryptographic hash functions, requirements on the secret key, and parameters for optional truncation.\n[...]\nA message authentication code (MAC) scheme is a symmetric-key cryptographic mechanism that can be used with a secret key to produce and verify an authentication tag, which enables detecting unauthorized modifications to data (also known as a message). This NIST Special Publication (whose current version is an initial public draft) specifies the keyed-hash message authentication code (HMAC) construction, which is a MAC scheme that uses a cryptographic hash function as a building block. The publication also specifies a set of requirements for using HMAC for message authentication, including a list of NIST-approved cryptographic hash functions, requirements on the secret key, and parameters for optional truncation.\n[...]\ncryptography; hash function; HMAC; MAC; message authentication code; PRF; pseudorandom function; standard; truncation\n[...]\nPublication: https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.SP.800-224.ipd Download URL\n[...]\nSupplemental Material: Comments received on SP 800-224 ipd (pdf)\n\n---\n\nTitle: FIPS 198-1, The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC) | CSRC\nURL: https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/fips/198/1/final\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nFIPS 198-1, The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC) | CSRC \n\nYou are viewing this page in an unauthorized frame window.\n\nThis is a potential security issue, you are being redirected to https://csrc.nist.gov.\n[...]\n### FIPS 198-1\n\n# The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC)\n[...]\nThis Standard describes a keyed-hash message authentication code (HMAC), a mechanism for message authentication using cryptographic hash functions. HMAC can be used with any iterative Approved cryptographic hash function, in combination with a shared secret key.\n[...]\nThis Standard describes a keyed-hash message authentication code (HMAC), a mechanism for message authentication using cryptographic hash functions. HMAC can be used with any iterative Approved cryptographic hash function, in combination with a shared secret key.\n[...]\nMAC; message authentication; Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS); computer security; HMAC; cryptography\n[...]\nPublication: https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.FIPS.198-1 Download URL\n\n---\n\nTitle: N/A\nURL: https://csrc.nist.gov/csrc/media/projects/cryptographic-algorithm-validation-program/documents/mac/hmacvs.pdf\nPublished: 2016-05-09T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: Hall, Timothy A.\nHighlights:\nThe Keyed-Hash Message Authentication \nCode Validation System (HMACVS)\n[...]\nThis document, The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code Validation System (HMACVS)\nspecifies the procedures involved in validating implementations of the Keyed-Hash Message \nAuthentication Code (HMAC) as specified and approved in FIPS 198-1, The Keyed-Hash \nMessage Authentication Code (HMAC) [1]. The HMACVS is designed to perform automated \ntesting on Implementations Under Test (IUTs). This document provides the basic design and \nconfiguration of the HMACVS.\n[...]\nThis document defines the purpose, the design philosophy, and the high-level description of the \nvalidation process for HMAC. The requirements and administrative procedures to be followed \nby those seeking formal validation of an implementation of an HMAC are presented. The \nrequirements described include a specification of the data communicated between the IUT and \nthe HMACVS, the details of the tests that the IUT must pass for formal validation, and general \ninstruction for interfacing with the HMACVS. Additionally, an appendix is also provided \ncontaining samples of input and output files for the HMACVS.\n[...]\nThis document specifies the tests required to validate IUTs for conformance to HMAC specified \nin [1]. When applied to an IUT, the HMACVS provides testing to determine the correctness of \nthe implementation of HMAC. The HMACVS is composed of a single test that determines the \nconformance to the cryptographic specification.\n[...]\nThe successful completion of the tests contained within the HMACVS is required to be validated \nas conforming to the HMAC standard. Testing for the cryptographic module in which the \nHMAC is implemented is defined in FIPS PUB 140-2, Security Requirements for Cryptographic \nModules [2].\n[...]\nThe HMACVS is designed to test conformance to the HMAC specification rather than provide a \nmeasure of a product’s security. The validation tests are designed to assist in the detection of \naccidental implementation errors, and are not designed to detect intentional attempts to \nmisrepresent conformance. Thus, validation should not be interpreted as an evaluation or \nendorsement of overall product security.\n[...]\nThe HMACVS tests the implementation of HMAC for its conformance to the HMAC standard. \nThe testing for HMAC consists of one test called the Random Message Test. The Random \nMessage Test provides 15 sets of messages and keys for each hash algorithm and hash size/key \nsize/MAC size combination supported by the IUT. \n6.1 Configuration Information\n[...]\nTo initiate the validation process of the HMACVS, a vendor submits an application to an \naccredited laboratory requesting the validation of its implementation of HMAC. The vendor’s \nimplementation is referred to as the Implementation Under Test (IUT). The request for \nvalidation includes background information describing the IUT along with information needed \nby the\n[...]\nVS to perform the specific tests.\n[...]\n• Which underlying hash algorithms are supported \no SHA-2 algorithms: SHA-1, SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA\u0002512/224, or SHA-512/256\n[...]\no SHA-3 algorithms: SHA3-224, SHA3-256, SHA3-384, SHA3-512\n[...]\n• For each of the underlying hash algorithms specified above, supported key sizes, K, \nrelated to the block size, B, of the underlying hash algorithm. That is, does the IUT \nsupport key sizes of K B.\n[...]\n• For each of the underlying hash algorithms supported, the length, t, in bytes, of the \nMAC the IUT is able to produce. Choices for the MAC lengths are:\n[...]\nand/or SHA\n[...]\nThe Random Message Test provides a series of random message and keys to the IUT. The IUT \ngenerates an HMAC for the messages using the keys provided. The HMACVS verifies the \ncorrectness of the HMACs produced by the IUT.\n[...]\nThe HMACVS:\n[...]\nA. Compares the contents of the RESPONSE file with the contents of the FAX file.\n[...]\nB. If all values match, records PASS for this test; otherwise, records FAIL. \n6\n\n---\n\nTitle: Message Authentication Codes | CSRC\nURL: http://csrc.nist.gov/Projects/message-authentication-codes\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThe message authentication code (MAC) is generated from an associated message as a method for assuring the integrity of the message and the authenticity of the source of the message. A secret key to the generation algorithm must be established between the originator of the message and its intended receiver(s).\n[...]\nCurrently, there are three approved* general-purpose MAC algorithms: HMAC, KMAC, and CMAC.\n[...]\nKeyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC)\n[...]\n- The initial public draft of NIST SP 800-224, Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC): Specification of HMAC and Recommendations for Message Authentication, was released for public comment on June 28, 2024, and the comment period closed September 6, 2024. This publication was proposed by the NIST Crypto Publication Review Board based on the reviews of FIPS 198-1 and SP 800-107r1 in 2022. The final version of SP 800-224 is expected to be published concurrently with the withdrawal of FIPS 198-1.\n[...]\n- FIPS 198-1, The Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC)(July 2008), specifies a mechanism for message authentication using an approved hash function. The approved hash functions are specified in FIPS 180-4, Secure Hash Standard, and FIPS 202, SHA-3 Standard: Permutation-Based Hash and Extendable-Output Functions. Specific guidelines in connection with HMAC's security properties are provided in NIST SP 107 Revision 1, Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms.\n[...]\n6/23/\n[...]\n5 - See a Federal Register Notice announcing NIST's proposal to officially withdraw FIPS 198-1 and move the HMAC specification to SP 800-224. Comments are due by 7/\n[...]\n- KMAC is specified in NIST SP 800-185, SHA-3 Derived Functions: cSHAKE, KMAC, TupleHash and ParallelHash(December 2016). KMAC is a keyed hash function based on K ECCAK, which is specified in FIPS 202, SHA-3 Standard: Permutation-Based Hash and Extendable-Output Functions . There are two variants of KECCAK, KMAC128 and KMAC256.\n[...]\n#### Testing HMAC,\n[...]\nAC Implementations\n[...]\nTesting requirements and validation lists are available from the Cryptographic Algorithm Validation Program (CAVP).\n[...]\nFIPS-\n\n---\n\nTitle: SP 800-107 Rev. 1, Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms | CSRC\nURL: https://csrc.nist.gov/Pubs/sp/800/107/r1/Final\nPublished: 2012-08-24T03:57:38.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSP 800-107 Rev. 1, Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms | CSRC\n[...]\n### NIST SP 800-107 Rev. 1\n[...]\n# Recommendation for Applications Using Approved Hash Algorithms\n[...]\n- The supplementary material currently in SP 800-107 Rev. 1 has been moved to the hash functions webpage.\n- Next, the requirements listed in SP 800-107 Rev.1 that are not currently addressed in other standards will be moved to a new Implementation Guidance (IG) developed by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). You can use an HMAC to verify both the integrity and authenticity of a message.\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hmac.HMAC(key, algorithm)\n[...]\nHMAC objects take a`key` and a HashAlgorithm instance. The`key` should be randomly generated bytes and is recommended to be equal in length to the`digest_size` of the hash function chosen. You must keep the`key` secret.\n[...]\nThis is an implementation\n[...]\n```\n>>> from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import hashes, hmac\n>>> key = b'test key. Beware! A real key should use os.urandom or TRNG to generate'\n>>> h = hmac.HMAC(key, hashes.SHA256())\n>>> h.update(b\"message to hash\")\n>>> signature = h.finalize()\n>>> signature\nb'k\\xd9\\xb29\\xefS\\xf8\\xcf\\xec\\xed\\xbf\\x95\\xe6\\x97X\\x18\\x9e%\\x11DU1\\x9fq}\\x9a\\x9c\\xe0)y`='\n[...]\nIf`algorithm` isn’t a HashAlgorithm instance then`TypeError` will be raised.\n[...]\nTo check that a given signature is correct use the`verify()` method. Cryptography 45.0.0 documentation\nURL: https://cryptography.io/en/45.0.0/hazmat/primitives/cryptographic-hashes/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n# Message digests (Hashing) \n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.Hash(algorithm) \n[...]\nA cryptographic hash function takes an arbitrary block of data and calculates a fixed-size bit string (a digest), such that different data results (with a high probability) in different digests.\n[...]\nThis is an implementation of HashContext meant to be used with HashAlgorithm implementations to provide an incremental interface to calculating various message digests.\n[...]\nKeep in mind that attacks against cryptographic hashes only get stronger with time, and that often algorithms that were once thought to be strong, become broken. Because of this it’s important to include a plan for upgrading the hash algorithm you use over time. For more information, see Lifetimes of cryptographic hash functions.\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.XOFHash(algorithm) \n[...]\nAn extendable output function (XOF) is a cryptographic hash function that can produce an arbitrary amount of output for a given input. The output can be obtained by repeatedly calling squeeze() with the desired length.\n[...]\n## SHA-2 family \n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.SHA\n[...]\nSHA-224 is a cryptographic hash function from the SHA-2 family and is standardized by NIST. It produces a 224-bit message digest.\n[...]\nSHA-256 is a cryptographic hash function from the SHA-2 family and is standardized by NIST. It produces a 256-bit message digest.\n[...]\nBLAKE2 is a cryptographic hash function specified in RFC 7693. BLAKE2’s design makes it immune to length-extension attacks, an advantage over the SHA-family of hashes.\n[...]\nWhile the RFC specifies keying, personalization, and salting features, these are not supported at this time due to limitations in OpenSSL.\n[...]\nSHA-3 is the most recent NIST secure hash algorithm standard. Despite the larger number SHA-3 is not considered to be better than SHA-2. Instead, it uses a significantly different internal structure so that if an attack appears against SHA-2 it is unlikely to apply to SHA-3. SHA-3 is significantly slower than SHA-2 so at this time most users should choose SHA-2.\n[...]\nSHA-1 is a cryptographic hash function standardized by NIST. It produces an 160-bit message digest. Cryptanalysis of SHA-1 has demonstrated that it is vulnerable to practical collision attacks, and collisions have been demonstrated.\n[...]\n## Extendable Output Functions \n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.SHAKE128(digest_size) [source] \n[...]\nSHAKE128 is an extendable output function (XOF) based on the same core permutations as SHA3. It allows the caller to obtain an arbitrarily long digest length. Longer lengths, however, do not increase security or collision resistance and lengths shorter than 128 bit (16 bytes) will decrease it.\n[...]\nThis class can be used with Hash or XOFHash. When used in Hash finalize() will return `digest_size` bytes. When used in XOFHash this defines the total number of bytes allowed to be squeezed.\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.SHAKE256(digest_size) [source] \n[...]\nSHAKE256 is an extendable output function (XOF) based on the same core permutations as SHA3. It allows the caller to obtain an arbitrarily long digest length. Longer lengths, however, do not increase security or collision resistance and lengths shorter than 256 bit (32 bytes) will decrease it.\n[...]\nThis class can be used with Hash or XOFHash. When used in Hash finalize() will return `digest_size` bytes. When used in XOFHash this defines the total number of bytes allowed to be squeezed.\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.HashAlgorithm [source] \n[...]\nsha256\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.ExtendableOutputFunction [source] \n[...]\nAn interface applied to hashes that act as extendable output functions (XOFs). The currently supported XOFs are SHAKE128 and SHAKE256.\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hashes.HashContext [source] \n\n---\n\nTitle: Primitives — Cryptography 47.0.0 documentation\nURL: https://cryptography.io/en/stable/hazmat/primitives/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n- Message authentication codes Cipher-based message authentication code (CMAC) Hash-based message authentication codes (HMAC) Poly1305\n[...]\n- Message digests (Hashing) Hash XOFHash SHA-2 family BLAKE2 SHA-3 family SHA-1 MD5 SM3 Extendable Output Functions Interfaces\n\n---\n\nTitle: Hash-based message authentication codes (HMAC) — Cryptography 46.0.6 documentation\nURL: https://cryptography.io/en/46.0.6/hazmat/primitives/mac/hmac/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n# Hash-based message authentication codes (HMAC) \n[...]\nHash-based message authentication codes (or HMACs) are a tool for calculating message authentication codes using a cryptographic hash function coupled with a secret key. codes (or HMACs) are a tool for calculating message authentication codes using a cryptographic hash function coupled with a secret key. You can use an HMAC to verify both the integrity and authenticity of a message.\n[...]\nclass cryptography.hazmat.primitives.hmac.HMAC(key, algorithm) \n[...]\nHMAC objects take a `key` and a HashAlgorithm instance. The `key` should be randomly generated bytes and is recommended to be equal in length to the `digest_size` of the hash function chosen. You must keep the `key` secret.\n[...]\nThis is an implementation of RFC 2104.\n[...]\n> > > from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import hashes, hmac\n> > > key = b'test key. Beware! authentication codes as the first cryptographic concepts serving authenticity\nare systems where the state of information is captured in something known as a MAC tag,\nand with these MAC tags calculated based on the information and a symmetric key K.\nThe integrity of the information can then\nbe verified later on by recalculating the MAC tag under the same symmetric key K.\n[...]\nA popular implementation of message authentication\ncodes are Hash-based message authentication codes – in short HMACs.\nHMACs were designed having the hash functions\nMD5, SHA-1 and SHA-2 in mind, which are 3 hash functions internally built according\nto the same principles.\n[...]\nSHA-3 is internally built very different than\nMD5, SHA-1 and SHA-2, and as such, SHA-3 specifically doesn’t This assurance is provided using a Hashing Algorithm... but not simply by itself, that leaves you open to an attacker modifying your message and re-calculating the digest. HMAC Security\nURL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TcmLVilowb8\nPublished: 2016-06-06T17:53:09.000Z\nAuthor: Udacity\nHighlights:\nLet's discuss the security of HMAC.\n[...]\nFirst of all, the security of HMAC\ndepends on the cryptographic strength of the underlying hash function.\nThat is, the underlying hash\nfunction used in HMAC must satisfy the basic properties of one wayness and\ncollision resistant.\n[...]\nFurther, compared with\nthe cryptographic hash function, it is much harder to launch\na successful collision attack on HMAC.\nThe main reason is that a secret\nkey is hashed together with the message content.\nAs a result,\nwithout knowing the secret key, an attacker cannot\ncompute the correct HMAC.\n[...]\nFor example, supposed the attacker is\nable to obtain the HMAC of message M1, and he wants to find another message\nthat have a collision with M1.\nThat is, a different message\nM2 that's not the same as M1, but had the same HMAC value as M1.\nBut without a secret key, the attacker cannot compute\nthe correct HMAC value for M2.\nThat is, the attacker does\nnot even know whether M1 or M2 will have collision in HMAC.\n[...]\nIn summary,\nbecause of the use of the secret key, HMAC is much more secure than\na cryptography hash function.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Cryptography with Python 31: Using HMAC in Python\nURL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GEnc09lBmjs\nPublished: 2022-07-19T05:35:08.000Z\nAuthor: Cyrill Gössi\nHighlights:\n🔷🔷 About 🔷🔷\n\nThis Fundamental Cryptography in Theory and Python lesson presents, how a hash-based message authentication code (HMAC) can be used in Python.\n[...]\n- 00:00 Recap of message authentication codes (MAC)\n- 01:04 Using HMAC-SHA-256 in Python\n- 03:11 Wrong message detection with HMAC-SHA-256 in Python\n- 04:41 Wrong key detection with HMAC-SHA-256 in Python\n- 05:33 Wrong mac tag detection with HMAC-SHA-256 in Python\n- 06:21 Wrong hash function detection with HMAC-SHA-256 in Python\n[...]\nA hash-based message-authentication code, abbreviated as HMAC, is a message authentication code constructed on top of cryptographic hash functions and as a system allows verifying the integrity of a message based on MAC tags associated with the message and relative to a symmetric key.\n[...]\nWith this, it’s a system that allows a recipient to verify the source of information if the symmetric key used is known to only be available to for example the recipient and exactly one communicating peer.\n[...]\nIf either the message was modified, or the wrong key was used, or a wrong MAC tag was received, or a wrong hash function underlying the HMAC is used, then the HMAC verification process will fail, and the message received should be deemed as not authentic.\n[...]\nThe following demonstration will show, how HMACs can be used in Python and how the behaviour of HMACs can be explored in the case of the different error scenarios.\n[...]\nFor this demo, I opted to use HMAC-SHA-256, which has SHA-2 with 256-bit hash values as the underlying hash function.\n[...]\nThen, a symmetric key is required. With HMAC-SHA-256, a key of at most 512 bits is recommended, but here I decided to go for a randomly sampled 256-bit key, which is assumed to be secure.\n[...]\nThen I constructed the HMAC-SHA-256, for which I had to get hold of the SHA-256 hash function which can be found in the cryptography library inside the hashes module, and with SHA-256 and the symmetric key at hand, I could then construct the HMAC-SHA-256 object by using the HMAC class from the cryptography library HMAC module.\n[...]\nThen it was time to calculate a MAC tag for the message and to verify that this MAC tag for the original correct message and under the correct symmetric key indeed verifies as a correct MAC tag.\n[...]\nit was time\n[...]\nerror scenarios.\n[...]\nThe first error scenario was attempting to verify the MAC tag with a now-modified message.\n[...]\nThe second error scenario was attempting to verify the MAC tag with a different key than the key used to calculate the MAC tag.\n[...]\nThe 3rd error scenario was attempting to verify the message with a wrong MAC tag, and the 4th error scenario was attempting to verify the message and MAC tag with a wrong HMAC using a different underlying hash function.\n[...]\nGoing into the 1st scenario, setting up a wrong message and an attempt to verify its results in an InvalidSignature exception, thus showing that this error scenario is indeed properly detected by an HMAC.\n[...]\nWrapping this into a try-except clause that asserts that the exception was indeed thrown then had the program running again and verifying that the exception was indeed thrown.\n[...]\nThe investigation of the remaining 3 error scenarios was the same, indicating that each of the error scenarios is indeed detected by HMACs, with the responsibility of properly dealing with each error scenario then of course being delegated to upstream clients of the system.\n[...]\nThis concluded the demonstration of how to use HMACs in Python, which is a cryptographic primitive allowing for the verification of the authenticity of messages and data received.",
