The construction takes password P and salt S and computes F(P, S, c, i) = U_1 XOR ... XOR U_c where U_1 = HMAC(P, S || INT(i)) and U_j = HMAC(P, U_{j-1}). The iteration count c forces an attacker to repeat the HMAC chain for every candidate; the salt prevents rainbow-table precomputation. The implementation uses`crypto.subtle.importKey('raw', passwordBytes, 'PBKDF2', false, ['deriveBits'])` then`crypto.subtle.deriveBits({ name: 'PBKDF2', salt, iterations, hash }, keyMaterial, keyLength * 8)`. The browser\\'s native crypto (BoringSSL, NSS) runs HMAC-SHA-256 at around 200MB/sec, so 600,000 iterations takes around 100 milliseconds. That delay is the point: multiplied by billions of candidate passwords, offline brute force becomes infeasible.\n[...]\nPBKDF2 is the elder statesman: RFC 2898 (2000) then RFC 8018 (PKCS #5 v2.1, 2017), approved by NIST SP 800-132. Its weakness relative to modern alternatives is parallelism: HMAC alone is trivially GPU/ASIC parallelizable. bcrypt (1999) introduced sequential memory patterns that punish GPU porting. scrypt (RFC 7914) added memory-hardness - 128MB RAM per guess makes cracking rigs expensive. Argon2 (PHC winner 2015, RFC 9106) is state of the art with three variants: Argon2d, Argon2i, and Argon2id (OWASP 2024 recommendation). OWASP\\'s 2024 cheat sheet ranks them Argon2id > scrypt > bcrypt > PBKDF2. PBKDF2 remains acceptable because it is in every stdlib and FIPS-approved; use Argon2id when you can.\n[...]\n## PBKDF2 in the Browser vs CLI vs Native Libraries\n[...]\nThe Web Crypto`deriveBits` path here produces identical output to`openssl kdf -kdfopt digest:SHA256 -kdfopt pass:\"mypassword\" -kdfopt hexsalt:... -kdfopt iter:600000 -keylen 32 PBKDF2` or`hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac(\"sha256\", password, salt, 600000, 32)` in Python. The browser implementation has one practical advantage: it runs on the user\\'s device, so the password never touches the server - exactly the architecture 1Password and Bitwarden use for vault unlock. For server-side password verification, use`bcrypt`,`argon2`, or`phc-crypto` libraries rather than hand-rolling PBKDF2. Never roll your own in pure JavaScript: naive implementations leak timing through non-constant-time`===` comparison.\n[...]\nstill considered okay\n[...]\nPBKDF2 is good enough for most threat models and is in every language's standard library plus the Web Crypto API. Argon2 is better because its memory-hard design (128MB RAM per guess at default settings) makes GPU and ASIC attacks roughly 1000x more expensive. If your stack supports Argon2id (Node: argon2 package, Python: argon2-cffi, Go: golang.org/x/crypto/argon2, PHP: built-in) use it. PBKDF2 remains the right choice when you need FIPS 140-3 compliance (Argon2 is not yet FIPS-approved), when you are deriving keys in a browser (Argon2 is not in Web Crypto), or when you must interoperate with legacy protocols like WPA2.\n[...]\nNo. crypto.subtle.importKey wraps the password bytes into a CryptoKey object that stays inside the browser's crypto module. deriveBits runs locally in the native crypto implementation. No fetch, no XHR, no service-worker intercept. Disconnect the network after the page loads and the tool keeps working. If you are hashing a password for a production system, remember that the plaintext still has to reach your server via HTTPS - the hash-at-rest design only protects against database leaks, not network compromise.\n[...]\n### AES-256 Encrypt / Decrypt Online - Free, In-Browser\n[...]\nEncrypt and decrypt text with AES-128, AES-192, or AES-256 in GCM, CBC, or CTR mode. PBKDF2 key derivation, entirely in your browser. Open tool\n\n---\n\nTitle: Argon2 in browser\nURL: https://argon2-browser.quexten.com/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nArgon2 in browser\n[...]\n# Argon2 in browser\n[...]\nArgon2 is a password-hashing function that summarizes the state of the art in the design of memory-hard functions and can be used to hash passwords for credential storage, key derivation, or other applications.\n[...]\nHere Argon2 library is compiled for browser runtime. Like encryption, authentication can be either symmetric or asymmetric. Authentication is necessary for effective encryption.\n[...]\nA nonce is a n umber used once. Nonces are used in many cryptographic protocols. Generally, a nonce does not have to be secret or unpredictable, but it must be unique. A nonce is often a random or pseudo-random number (see Random number generation). Since a nonce does not have to be unpredictable, it can also take a form of a counter.\n[...]\nencryption and decryption use\n[...]\nThere are separate encryption and decryption keys. Typically encryption is performed using a public key, and it can then be decrypted using a private key. Fernet is an implementation of symmetric (also known as “secret key”) authenticated cryptography. Fernet also has support for implementing key rotation via MultiFernet.\n[...]\nGenerates a fresh fernet key. Keep this some place safe! If you lose it you’ll no longer be able to decrypt messages; if anyone else gains access to it, they’ll be able to decrypt all of your messages, and they’ll also be able forge arbitrary messages that will be authenticated and decrypted.\n[...]\nEncrypts data passed. The result of this encryption is known as a “Fernet token” and has strong privacy and authenticity guarantees.\n[...]\n| Parameters: | data (bytes) – The message you would like to encrypt. |\n| --- | --- |\n| Returns bytes: | A secure message that cannot be read or altered without the key. It is URL-safe base64-encoded. This is referred to as a “Fernet token”. |\n| Raises: | TypeError – This exception is raised if data is notbytes. |\n[...]\nDecrypts a Fernet token. If successfully decrypted you will receive the original plaintext as the result, otherwise an exception will be raised. It is safe to use this data immediately as Fernet verifies that the data has not been tampered with prior to returning it.\n[...]\nThis class implements key rotation for Fernet. It takes a `list` of Fernet instances and implements the same API with the exception of one additional method: MultiFernet.rotate():\n[...]\nMultiFernet performs all encryption options using the first key in the`list` provided. MultiFernet attempts to decrypt tokens with each key in turn. A cryptography.fernet.InvalidToken exception is raised if the correct key is not found in the `list` provided.\n[...]\nKey rotation makes it easy to replace old keys. You can add your new key at the front of the list to start encrypting new messages, and remove old keys as they are no longer needed.\n[...]\nToken rotation as offered by MultiFernet.rotate() is a best practice and manner of cryptographic hygiene designed to limit damage in the event of an undetected event and to increase the difficulty of attacks. For example, if an employee who had access to your company’s fernet keys leaves, you’ll want to generate new fernet key, rotate all of the tokens currently deployed using that new key, and then retire the old fernet key(s) to which the employee had access.\n[...]\nRotates a token by re-encrypting it under the MultiFernet instance’s primary key. This preserves the timestamp that was originally saved with the token. If a token has successfully been rotated then the rotated token will be returned. If rotation fails this will raise an exception.\n[...]\n## Using passwords with Fernet ¶\n[...]\nIt is possible to use passwords with Fernet. To do this, you need to run the password through a key derivation function such as PBKDF2HMAC, bcrypt or Scrypt.\n[...]\nFernet is built on top of a number of standard cryptographic primitives. Specifically it uses:\n[...]\n- AES in CBC mode with a 128-bit key for encryption; using PKCS7 padding.\n- HMAC using SHA256 for authentication.\n- Initialization vectors are generated using `os.urandom()`.\n[...]\n## Limitations ¶\n\nFernet is ideal for encrypting data that easily fits in memory. As a design feature it does not expose unauthenticated bytes. This means that the complete message contents must be available in memory, making Fernet generally unsuitable for very large files at this time.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Authenticated encryption — Cryptography 44.0.0 documentation\nURL: https://cryptography.io/en/44.0.0/hazmat/primitives/aead/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAuthenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD) are encryption schemes which provide both confidentiality and integrity for their ciphertext.             "output": "Title: ImperialViolet - AES-GCM-SIV\nURL: https://www.imperialviolet.org/2017/05/14/aesgcmsiv.html\nPublished: 2017-05-14T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: Adam Langley\nHighlights:\nAEADs combine encryption and authentication in a way that provides the properties that people generally expect when they “encrypt” something. This is great because, historically, handing people a block cipher and a hash function has resulted in a lot of bad and broken constructions. Standardising AEADs avoids this.\n[...]\nCommon AEADs have a sharp edge though: you must never encrypt two different messages with the same key and nonce. Doing so generally violates the confidentiality of the two messages and might break much more.\n[...]\nThere are some situations where obtaining a unique nonce is easy, for example in transport security where a simple counter can be used. (Due to poor design in TLS 1.2, some TLS implementations still managed to duplicate nonces. The answer there is to do what ChaCha20-Poly1305 does in TLS 1.2, and what everything does in TLS 1.3: make the nonce implicit. That means that any mistakes result in your code not interoperating, which should be noticed.)\n[...]\nBut there are situations where ensuring nonce uniqueness is not trivial, generally where multiple machines are independently encrypting with the same key. A system for distributing nonces is complex and hard to have confidence in. Generating nonces randomly and depending on statistical uniqueness is reasonable, but only if the space of nonces is large enough. XSalsa20-Poly1305 (paper, code) has a 192-bit nonce and good performance across a range of CPUs. In many situations, using that with random nonces is a sound choice.\n[...]\nHowever, lots of chips now have hardware support for the AES-GCM AEAD, meaning that its performance and power use is hard to beat. Also, a 192-bit nonce results in a ~40% increase in overhead vs the 96-bit nonce of AES-GCM, which is undesirable in some contexts. (Overhead consists of the nonce and tag, thus the increase from 96- to 192-bit nonces is not 100%.)\n[...]\nBut using random nonces in a 96-bit space isn't that comfortable. NIST recommends a limit of 232 messages when using random nonces with AES-GCM which, while quite large, is often not large enough not to have to worry about. Because of this, Shay Gueron, Yehuda Lindell and I have been working on AES-GCM-SIV (paper, spec): AES-GCM with some forgiveness. It uses the same primitives as AES-GCM, and thus enjoys the same hardware support, but it doesn't fail catastrophically if you repeat a nonce. Thus you can use random, 96-bit nonces with a far larger number of messages, or withstand a glitch in your nonce distribution scheme. (For precise numbers, see section 5.3 of the paper.)\n[...]\nThere is a performance cost: AES-GCM-SIV encryption runs at about 70% the speed of AES-GCM, although\n[...]\nruns at the\n[...]\nspeed. (Measured using current BoringSSL on an Intel Skylake chip with 8KiB messages.) But, in any situation where you don't have a watertight argument for nonce uniqueness,\n[...]\nmight be pretty cheap\n[...]\nto the alternative.\n[...]\nFor example, both TLS and QUIC need to encrypt small messages at the server that can be decrypted by other servers\n[...]\nsituation where many servers are independently\n[...]\nGoogle's QUIC implementation is in the\n[...]\nswitching to using AES-GCM-\n[...]\nAEADs that can withstand nonce duplication are called “nonce-misuse resistant” and that name appears to have caused some people to believe that they are infinitely resistant. I.e. that an unlimited number of messages can be encrypted with a fixed nonce with no loss in security. That is not the case, and the term wasn't defined that way originally by Rogaway and Shrimpton(nor does their SIV mode have that property). So it's important to emphasise that AES-GCM-SIV (and nonce-misuse resistant modes in general) are not a magic invulnerability shield. Figure four and section five of the the paper give precise bounds but, if in doubt, consider AES-GCM-SIV to be a safety net for accidental nonce duplication and otherwise treat it like a traditional AEAD.\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 8452: AES-GCM-SIV: Nonce Misuse-Resistant Authenticated Encryption\nURL: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8452\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nResearch Group.\n[...]\n1.  Introduction\n\n   The concept of Authenticated Encryption with Additional Data (AEAD)\n   [RFC5116] couples confidentiality and integrity in a single\n   operation, avoiding the risks of the previously common practice of\n   using ad hoc constructions of block-cipher and hash primitives.  The\n   most popular AEAD, AES-GCM [GCM], is seeing widespread use due to its\n   attractive performance.\n\n   However, some AEADs (including AES-GCM) suffer catastrophic failures\n   of confidentiality and/or integrity when two distinct messages are\n   encrypted with the same key and nonce.  While the requirements for\n   AEADs specify that the pair of (key, nonce) shall only ever be used\n   once, and thus prohibit this, this is a worry in practice.\n\n   Nonce misuse-resistant AEADs do not suffer from this problem.  For\n   this class of AEADs, encrypting two messages with the same nonce only\n   discloses whether the messages were equal or not.  This is the\n   minimum amount of information that a deterministic algorithm can leak\n   in this situation.\n\n   This memo specifies two nonce misuse-resistant AEADs:\n   AEAD_AES_128_GCM_SIV and AEAD_AES_256_GCM_SIV.  These AEADs are\n   designed to be able to take advantage of existing hardware support\n[...]\nSIV                    April 201\n[...]\nof AES-GCM (for\n[...]\n-GCM-\n[...]\navailable memory of some machines, tempting implementations to\n   release unverified plaintext.\n\n   A detailed cryptographic analysis of AES-GCM-SIV appears in\n   [AES-GCM-SIV], and the remainder of this section is a summary of that\n   paper.\n\n   The AEADs defined in this document calculate fresh AES keys for each\n   nonce.  This allows a larger number of plaintexts to be encrypted\n   under a given key.  Without this step, AES-GCM-SIV encryption would\n   be limited by the birthday bound like other standard modes (e.g.,\n   AES-GCM, AES-CCM [RFC3610], and AES-SIV [RFC5297]).  This means that\n   when 2^64 blocks have been encrypted overall, a distinguishing\n   adversary who is trying to break the confidentiality of the scheme\n   has an advantage of 1/2.  Thus, in order to limit the adversary's\n   advantage to 2^-32, at most 2^48 blocks can be encrypted overall.  In\n   contrast, by deriving fresh keys from each nonce, it is possible to\n   encrypt a far larger number of messages and blocks with AES-GCM-SIV.\n\n   We stress that nonce misuse-resistant schemes guarantee that if a\n   nonce repeats, then the only security loss is that identical\n   plaintexts will produce identical ciphertexts.  Since this can also\n   be a concern (as the fact that the same plaintext has been encrypted\n   twice is revealed), we do not recommend using a fixed nonce as a\n   policy.  In addition, as we show below, better-than-birthday bounds\n   are achieved by AES-GCM-SIV when the nonce repetition rate is low.\n   Finally, as shown in [BHT18], there is a great security benefit in\n   the multiuser/multikey setting when each particular nonce is reused\n   by a small number of users only.  We stress that the nonce misuse-\n   resistance property is not intended to be coupled with intentional\n   nonce reuse; rather, such schemes provide the best possible security\n   in the event of nonce reuse.  Due to all of the above, it is\n   RECOMMENDED that AES-GCM-SIV nonces be randomly generated.\n\n   Some example usage bounds for AES-GCM-SIV are given below.  The\n   adversary's advantage is the \"AdvEnc\" from [key-derive] and is\n   colloquially the ability of an attacker to distinguish ciphertexts\n   from random bit strings.  The bounds below limit this advantage to\n   2^-32.  For up to 256 uses of the same nonce and key (i.e., where one\n   can assume that nonce misuse is no more than this bound), the\n   following message limits should be respected (this assumes a short\n   additional authenticated data (AAD), i.e., less than 64 bytes):\n\n      2^29 messages, where each plaintext is at most 1 GiB\n\n      2^35 messages, where each plaintext is at most 128 MiB\n\n---\n\nTitle: Rfc5116\nURL: https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc5116.txt\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThis document defines algorithms for Authenticated Encryption with\n[...]\n), and defines a uniform interface and a\n[...]\n. The interface and registry can be used\n[...]\nas an application-independent set of\n[...]\nalgorithm suites. This\n[...]\napproach provides advantages in efficiency and security\n[...]\n. Nonce\n[...]\nIn this document, we define an AEAD algorithm as an abstraction, by\n specifying an interface to an AEAD and defining an IANA registry for\n AEAD algorithms. We populate this registry with four AEAD algorithms\n based on the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) in Galois/Counter\n Mode [GCM] with 128- and 256-bit keys, and AES in Counter and CBC MAC\n Mode [CCM] with 128- and 256-bit keys.\n[...]\nnonce provided to distinct\n[...]\n, unless each and every nonce is zero-\n[...]\nthe nonce formation method\n[...]\nin Section 3\n[...]\n2, and MAY use any\n[...]\nthat meets the\n[...]\nAEAD algorithm securely.\n\n If an application is unable to meet the uniqueness requirement on\n nonce generation, then it MUST use a zero-length nonce. Randomized\n[...]\nor stateful algorithms, which are defined below, are suitable for use\n with such applications. Otherwise, an application SHOULD use nonces\n[...]\nwith a length of twelve octets. Since algorithms are encouraged to\n[...]\nsupport that length, applications should use that length to aid\n interoperability.\n[...]\nIt is essential for security that the nonces be constructed in a\n manner that respects the requirement that each nonce value be\n distinct for each invocation of the authenticated encryption\n operation, for any fixed value of the key. In this section, we call\n attention to some consequences of this requirement in different\n scenarios.\n[...]\n.\n\n Many problems with nonce reuse can be avoided by changing a key in a\n situation in which nonce coordination is difficult.\n\n Each AEAD algorithm SHOULD describe what security degradation would\n result from an inadvertent reuse of a nonce value.\n[...]\ndescribe what security\n[...]\ndegradation would result from an inadvertent reuse of a nonce value\n[...]\n5.1.1. Nonce Reuse\n\n The inadvertent reuse of the same nonce by two invocations of the GCM\n encryption operation, with the same key, but with distinct plaintext\n values, undermines the confidentiality of the plaintexts protected in\n those two invocations, and undermines all of the authenticity and\n integrity protection provided by that key. For this reason, GCM\n should only be used whenever nonce uniqueness can be provided with\n assurance. The design feature that GCM uses to achieve minimal\n latency causes the vulnerabilities on the subsequent uses of the key.\n[...]\n20\n[...]\nIt may be desirable to define an AEAD algorithm that uses the generic\n composition with the encrypt-then-MAC method [BN00], combining a\n common encryption algorithm, such as CBC [MODES], with a common\n message authentication code, such as HMAC-SHA1 [RFC2104] or AES CMAC\n [CMAC]. An AEAD algorithm of this sort would reflect the best\n current practice, and might be more easily supported by crypto\n modules that lack support for other AEAD algorithms.\n[...]\nAEAD algorithms that rely on distinct nonces may be inappropriate for\n some applications or for some scenarios. Application designers\n[...]\nshould understand the requirements outlined in Section 3.1.\n[...]\nA software implementation of the AEAD encryption operation in a\n Virtual Machine (VM) environment could inadvertently reuse a nonce\n due to a \"rollback\" of the VM to an earlier state [GR05].\n Applications are encouraged to document potential issues to help the\n user of the application and the VM avoid unintentional mistakes of\n this sort. The possibility exists that an attacker can cause a VM\n rollback; threats and mitigations in that scenario are an area of\n active research. For perspective, we note that an attacker who can\n trigger such a rollback may have already succeeded in subverting the\n security of the system, e.g., by causing an accounting error.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Understanding Extended-Nonce Constructions - Dhole Moments\nURL: https://soatok.blog/2021/03/12/understanding-extended-nonce-constructions/\nPublished: 2021-03-12T09:47:39.000Z\nAuthor: Post author\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\tBy  Soatok\nHighlights:\nThe AEAD modes used to secure most of the Internet has one glaring weakness: If you reuse a nonce, your messages can be decrypted with simple algebra. (In the case of AES-GCM, but not ChaCha20-Poly1305, this kind of misuse allows attackers to also henceforth forge packets at your whim. I don’t like AES-GCM.)\n[...]\nThe first solution is to use a nonce misuse-resistant mode, such as AES-GCM-SIV, which prevents cryptanalytic attacks from a limited number of nonce reuses for different messages. These constructions are relatively new and less-studied than their contemporary counterparts, but as time goes on we’re slowly accumulating confidence in their security. However, their performance characteristics are a little disappointing.\n[...]\nThe other solution is to use a variant of an existing AEAD mode that accepts an extended nonce–one large enough that users can generate it randomly and never risk collisions in a practical setting.\n[...]\nAn extended-nonce variant of ChaCha20-Poly1305, called XChaCha20-Poly1305, accepts a 256-bit key and 192-bit nonce (with an internal 32-bit block counter). Consequently, if you generate a 192-bit nonce randomly, your 2^-32 collision probability occurs after 2^80 messages rather than 2^32.\n[...]\nIt might seem tempting to just overwrite the constants with the additional nonce bytes (or XOR them with the existing constants).\n[...]\nThis is a bad idea. If those constants were somehow initialized to all`0` bits, and your key was all`0` bits, and your nonce/counter was all`0` bits, ChaCha would output all`0` bits.\n[...]\nChaCha2\n[...]\nWith all of that in mind, while it’s tempting to try to extend the nonce by updating the internal ChaCha state with the extra nonce bytes, it’s not a great idea to clobber the ChaCha constants.\n[...]\nThe extended-nonce constructions specified by Daniel J Bernstein in the XSalsa20 paper and this IETF RFC draft both do (effectively) the same thing:\n[...]\n- Take your “extended nonce” of 192 bits, and split it into a 128-bit segment and a 64-bit segment.\n[...]\n- Run the first 128 bits of the nonce (as the full nonce/counter input) through the underlying PRF with the key without the final addition. (This PRF variant is called HSalsa20 and HChaCha respectively.)\n[...]\n- Use the output of the HSalsa20/HChaCha step as the key to encrypt data with the remaining 64 bits of the nonce.\n[...]\nThis is a pretty trivial construction, and it costs slightly less than encrypting one additional block of plaintext (which is very fast for both algorithms). Whether or not the X-variant is secure depends entirely on two things: If the normal stream cipher is secure, and if the H-variant of the underlying PRF is secure.\n[...]\nBecause only half the internal state is returned, and it’s the half that corresponds to the public values (constants, nonce), you can get the same half-block security guarantee without needing that final addition. (The final addition is useful for ensuring ChaCha can encrypt large amounts of plaintext efficiently, but here we’re just hashing a subkey out of the extension to the nonce.)\n[...]\nWhen you assemble\n[...]\n1. An attacker cannot recover half of the internal state from ChaCha outputs (due to the final addition which prevents the computation from being inverted).\n[...]\n2. An attacker cannot recover half of the internal state from HChaCha outputs (due to only returning a public computation of these inputs, and half of the internal state not being revealed).\n[...]\n3. The underlying transformations used in ChaCha are an efficient way to ensure high bit diffusion in reduced rounds, and the number of rounds is very conservative for the security target of the cipher.\n[...]\n4. The internal state of the PRF is 512 bits, which targets a 256-bit security level against birthday collisions.\n[...]\n5. The best known attacks only reduce the cost of ChaCha7 to about 2^230, which is computationally infeasible. ChaCha20 remains unaffected by this cryptanalysis.\n\n---\n\nTitle: N/A\nURL: https://eprint.iacr.org/2016/475.pdf\nPublished: 2016-05-17T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nWe investigate nonce reuse issues with the GCM block cipher mode as\n[...]\nCBC was deployed in TLS (implicit IVs, lack of strict padding and MAC\u0002then-Pad-then-Encrypt). Before TLS 1.2, the RC4 stream cipher was the\n[...]\nOne of the most concerning attacks is the so-called “forbidden attack” by\n[...]\nJoux which exploits nonce reuse [18] and allows an adversary to reconstruct\n[...]\nthe authentication key. This then leads to ef\u001bcient forgery attacks enabling\n[...]\nmaster key. In this paper we \u001brst show that several TLS implementations\n[...]\nare vulnerable to nonce reuse attacks and use repeated nonces after few\n[...]\nAES-GCM [5] is a block-cipher mode of operation which provides authen\u0002ticated encryption with associated data (AEAD). It uses counter mode to\n[...]\n• Nonce (8\n[...]\nIn his comments to NIST Joux [18] described an attack against GCM if\n[...]\nnonces are reused. This attack allows an attacker to learn the authentica\u0002tion\n[...]\nand forge messages. Because the uniqueness of nonces is typically\n[...]\nIn TLS, GCM requires a 96-bit nonce where 32 bits are derived along with\n[...]\nsession key material and remain static for the duration of the session and\n[...]\nthe other 64 bits are transmitted explicitly in each record. As highlighted\n[...]\nin Section 3, nonce uniqueness is essential for GCM’s security. However,\n[...]\nthe TLS speci\u001bcation does not provide clear guidelines to developers how\n[...]\nto choose the 64-bit explicit nonce [22].\n[...]\nThe easiest secure way is a counter. Given that the nonce value is 64-\n[...]\nbit long, a repeating nonce will only happen after 2\n[...]\n64 TLS records. It is\n[...]\nnot realistic that many encryptions happen over a single TLS connection.\n[...]\nThere are two counter\n[...]\nin widespread use:\n[...]\nFaulty implementations may send duplicate nonces, e. g. by always send\u0002ing the same value as a nonce or repeating a nonce for two encryption\n[...]\noperations. A single repeated nonce is usually enough to fully recover the\n[...]\nconnection’s authentication key. In such faulty implementations, authen\u0002ticity is lost and an attacker is able to manipulate TLS-protected content.\n[...]\nA less clear risk is present if an implementor chooses to use random values\n[...]\nas a nonce. If only a few TLS records are encrypted with the same key,\n[...]\nthen a random nonce does not pose a risk. However, if a large number\n[...]\nof records is encrypted with the same key, the risk may become relevant.\n[...]\nWe conclude that an attack on an implementation using random nonces\n[...]\nis unlikely, but it cannot be de\u001bnitely excluded. For safety reasons random\n[...]\nnonces should be avoided and a counter should be used.\n[...]\nOur results show that there is a signi\u001bcant risk of GCM getting implemented\n[...]\ntion gives developers little guidance on how to implement GCM securely.\n[...]\nThe TLS drafts for Chacha20-Poly1305 [20] (which has been submitted\n[...]\nAES-OCB [\n[...]\n] both specify\n[...]\ngenerate nonces from record sequence numbers and shared secrets in a de\u0002\n[...]\nconstruction prevents that implementations choose\n[...]\nThe other alternative is to rely on cryptographic algorithms that inher\u0002ently resist nonce-misuse, i.e. such ciphers uphold their security guar\u0002antees, even if a nonce-key pair is reused for different messages. The\n[...]\nare inherently “of-\n[...]\n\u001cine”, meaning that two passes over the data are necessary in order to\n[...]\nperform authenticated encryption. These algorithms usually operate in\n[...]\na MAC-Then-Encrypt-like manner where \u001brst message and associated\n[...]\ndata are processed to produce the authentication tag and then the latter\n[...]\nis used as the nonce for the encryption algorithm. Such nonces are also\n[...]\noften known as synthetic IVs (SIV), a term \u001brst coined by Rogaway and\n[...]\nShrimpton [30] in 2006. Examples of nonce misuse-resistant algorithms\n[...]\ninclude AES-SIV [13], AES-GCM-SIV [12], AEZ [14], HS1-SIV [19], and MRO\n[...]\nfrom the MEM-AEAD [9] cipher family.\n\n---\n\nTitle: N/A\nURL: https://ftp.ripe.net/rfc/rfc9771.pdf\nPublished: 2025-05-07T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: Andrey Bozhko\nHighlights:\n7.  Nonce Misuse\n[...]\nAn Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) algorithm provides confdentiality\nfor the plaintext to be encrypted and integrity for the plaintext and some associated data\n(sometimes called \"Header\"). AEAD algorithms play a crucial role in various applications and\nhave emerged as a signifcant focus in cryptographic research.\n[...]\nWhile confdentiality and data integrity (the conventional properties of AEAD algorithms) sufce\nfor many applications, some environments demand other uncommon cryptographic properties.\nThese often require additional analysis and research. As the number of such properties and\ncorresponding research papers grows, inevitable misunderstandings and confusion arise. This is\na common situation when related but formally diferent properties are named identically or\nwhen some security properties only have folklore understanding and are not formally defned.\n[...]\nConsequently, the risk of misusing AEAD algorithms increases, potentially resulting in security\nissues.\n[...]\na secret key K of a fxed bit length, a nonce N, associated data A, and a plaintext P\n[...]\nThroughout this document, by default, we consider nonce-based AEAD algorithms, which have\nan interface as defned above, and we give no other restrictions on their structure. However,\nsome properties considered in the document apply only to particular classes of such algorithms,\nlike AEAD algorithms based on block ciphers (such algorithms use a block cipher as a building\nblock). If that is the case, we explicitly point that out in the corresponding section.\n[...]\nConfdentiality of plaintext in the presence of leakages in the encryption operation is\n[...]\nunachievable if an adversary can repeat the nonce used to encrypt the plaintext in other\n[...]\nencryption queries. Confdentiality can be achieved only for plaintexts encrypted with fresh\n[...]\nnonces (analogously to nonce-misuse resilience; see Section 4.3.7). For further discussions, see\n and .\n[...]\nDefnition:\n[...]\nAn AEAD algorithm provides confdentiality for the nonce value used to encrypt plaintext.\nThe algorithm includes information about the nonce in the ciphertext and doesn't require the\nnonce as input for the decryption operation. \nSecurity notions:\n[...]\nAs discussed in ,\n[...]\nway any metadata might\n[...]\nAs pointed out in , even using a\n[...]\npreserving way to manage\n[...]\nmight be a challenging problem for an application.\n[...]\n3.7. Nonce Misuse\n[...]\nDefnition:\n[...]\nAn AEAD algorithm provides security (resilience or resistance) even if an adversary can\nrepeat nonces in its encryption queries. Nonce misuse resilience and resistance are defned\nas follows:\nSecurity is provided for messages encrypted with non-repeated\n(fresh) nonces (correctly encrypted messages).\nSecurity notions:\n[...]\nChosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA) resilience (confdentiality), authenticity resilience\n[...]\n(integrity), Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA) resilience (authenticated encryption) \nExamples:\n[...]\nalgorithms preferred for\n[...]\n-standardized algorithms exist.\n[...]\nrequires careful consideration, and it is advised to contact the algorithm designers for reference\n[...]\nimplementations and implementation guidelines.\n[...]\nWhen specifying security requirements for an AEAD algorithm in an application, it be\nindicated, for every required security property, whether only integrity or confdentiality is\n[...]\nnecessary. Additionally, for each security property, it be specifed whether an analysis\n[...]\nin an alternative security notion is required. We also note that some additional properties come\n[...]\nwith trade-ofs in terms of classical security and efciency, and they may only be supported in\n[...]\nnon-standardized or modifed AEAD algorithms. Once a nonce is reused, AES-GCM completely falls apart.\n[...]\nIf you’ve ever worked with AES-GCM, you may have heard that reusing a nonce can lead to catastrophic security failures. In this post, we will look at how exactly all security guarantees of AES-GCM can be broken when a nonce is reused even once.\n[...]\nGCM is a method for authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD). “Authenticated encryption” means that the mode of operation allows us to validate that a given ciphertext was indeed generated using the secret key, and that it has not been modified. This is called “authenticating” the ciphertext and is essential for secure communication. Without it, a bad actor could modify the ciphertext and the recipient would not be able to detect it, leading to garbled plaintext in the best case, and a huge security issue in the worst case.\n[...]\nIn this post, we’ll look at the two parts of GCM (encryption and authentication) seperately. We’ll start by seeing how encryption works with AES-GCM, where the nonce comes into play, and how the encryption breaks when a nonce is reused. After that, we’ll dive into the authentication part and see how it can also be broken when a nonce is reused.\n[...]\nA nonce is a random number transfered along with each ciphertext that may never be reused under the same key. We use the nonce as an additional input to the block cipher when generating the keystream so that for each ciphertext, the keystream is different. This means that even if an attacker knows the plaintext and ciphertext for one message, they cannot use that information to decrypt any other ciphertext. When a nonce is reused, however, the keystream is the same, and an attacker can use the same technique as above to decrypt any ciphertext that was encrypted using the same nonce.\n[...]\nThe second and equally important part of GCM is the authentication. This means that we are able to validate that a given ciphertext was indeed generated using the secret key, and that it has not been modified. At the risk of repeating myself, remember that the encryption part of GCM only ensures that the ciphertext cannot be decrypted without the key, but it does not ensure that is has not been tampared with. The encryption operation is a simple XOR operation, so if an attacker changes a single bit in the ciphertext from a`0` to a`1`, the corresponding bit in the plaintext will also change. This means that even though the ciphertext cannot be decrypted without the key, an attacker can still modify the ciphertext and the recipient would not be able to detect it. With a single known plaintext-ciphertext pair, an attacker is even able to recover the entire keystream and thus encrypt any plaintext they want. Hence, we need to authenticate the ciphertext.\n[...]\nWe have now seen how AES-GCM works and how the authentication tag is computed. We have also seen that the nonce is used in the computation of the authentication tag. But what happens if the nonce is reused?\n[...]\n`T1 ⊕ T2 = ((U1``0`` ⨂ H``3``) ⊕ (U1``1`` ⨂ H``2``) ⊕ (U1``2`` ⨂ H) ⊕ E``k``(y``0``)) ⊕ ((U2``0`` ⨂ H``\n[...]\nIf we can find a solution for this polynomial equation, we can recover the`H` value. Why is this interesting? Remember that`H` is the`GHASH` key, which is derived from the AES key. If we can recover`H`, we can use it to compute the`GHASH` function for any data we want. This means that we can authenticate any data we want, even if we don’t know the AES key. Combined with the keystream recovery demonstrated early, this leads to a full break of the AES-GCM encryption scheme.\n[...]\ncan use the Cantor-Zassen\n[...]\nAlthough more complicated than the keystream recovery, when a nonce is reused, we can use polynomial factorization to figure out the`H` key, which ultimately let’s us authenticate any data we want. So, in total, the attack goes like this:\n\n---\n\nTitle: AES-GCM Misuse\nURL: https://hacky.solutions/blog/2022/07/AES-GCM-misuse\nPublished: 2022-07-01T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThis article showcases several, realistic, vulnerabilities associated with AES-GCM. The vulnerabilities are:\n[...]\n- key exchange\n- nonce reuse: plaintext recovery\n- nonce reuse: hash subkey recovery\n- unlimited message size\n- unlimited messages\n[...]\nAn Additional caveat is that vanilla (EC)DHE is insecure because it does not authenticate the parties. Instead, you should use an Authenticated Key Exchange (AKE) to protect against an active attacker.\n[...]\n## Nonce Reuse: Plaintext Recovery\n[...]\nAES-GCM is (in)famously brittle with respect to its nonces. It is imperative that the nonce is never reused with the same key otherwise plaintext recovery and ciphertext forgery is possible.\n[...]\n.ciph\n[...]\nkey = AES\n[...]\n=128)\n[...]\nGCM(\n[...]\nsend(cipher.encrypt(nonce, data=note1, associated_data=None))\nsend(cipher.encrypt(nonce, data=note2, associated_data=None))\n[...]\nA passive attacker will capture the two\n[...]\nThe diagram does not include the GHASH components because it is irrelevant here. AES-GCM will create one-time-pad for each plaintext block by appending a 32-bit incrementing counter to the input IV, which is encrypted with AES using the secret key. Then the one-time-pad is xor-d with its associated plaintext block to encrypt it.\n[...]\nThe critical flaw with nonce reuse is that the one-time-pads become identical:\n[...]\nattack demonstrated, we used a plaintext padded with 0s to better illustrate the attack. However, in practice it is often possible to manipulate the plaintext to include known values which can leak partial messages.\n[...]\nAn important note is this only produces the xor-d plaintexts if the nonces are identical for the blocks, including the counter.\n[...]\n## Nonce Reuse: Hash Subkey Recovery\n[...]\nThe previous topic showcased how nonce reuse will leak xor-d plaintexts. However, nonce reuse can allow an attacker to forge “authenticated” ciphertexts.\n[...]\nThe GCM part of AES-GCM is Galois Counter Mode which is able to compute a hash over the ciphertext and any associated data. I will not explain how Galois fields work here. The hash is given by GHASH where:\n[...]\nmodes.ECB()).encrypt\n[...]\ndef gen_auth_tag(nonce: bytes, aad: bytes, ciphertext: bytes) -> bytes:\n    # individually pad aad and ciphertext to 16 bytes\n    ...\n\n    to_bytes = lambda l, x: x.to_bytes(l, byteorder=\"big\")\n    to_int = lambda x: int.from_bytes(x, byteorder=\"big\")\n\n    subkey = to_int(encrypt(b\"\\0\" * 16))\n    j0 = nonce + b\"\\0\\0\\0\\x01\"\n\n    aad_len        = to_bytes(8, len(aad))\n    ciphertext_len = to_bytes(8, len(ciphertext))\n    combined = aad + ciphertext + aad_len + ciphertext_len\n\n    s = ghash(subkey, combined)\n\n    return to_bytes(16, s ^ to_int(encrypt(j0)))\n[...]\nThis will compute a polynomial parameterized by X (the subkey) for some message.\n[...]\nbut if X (i.e., the subkey) is repeated then:\n[...]\n```\n\ng(X) = X^n*Cn + X^n-1*Cn-1 + ... + X*C1 + J\n[...]\nh(X) = X^n*Dn + X^n-1*\n[...]\n1 + ... + X*\n[...]\n1 + J\n[...]\n// addition on GF(2^1\n[...]\ng(X) + g\n[...]\n) + h\n[...]\n// add the polynomials and the authentication tags\n[...]\n+ Cn) + ... +\n[...]\nIn this form, an attacker can solve the equation which produces possible values of the subkey. Once an attacker has derived the subkey, the attacker can forge ciphertexts.\n[...]\n## Unlimited Message Size\n[...]\nIf we have 232 plaintext blocks then the 32-bit counter will overflow which repeats the nonce for subsequent blocks.\n[...]\n## Unlimited Messages\n[...]\nAES has a block size of 128 bits so all nonces are 128 bits instead of 256-bit for AES-256. Therefore, after encrypting 264 messages you have a 50% probability of repeating a nonce. Furthermore, if we have a random nonce then after 248 messages we expect a 50% probability of duplication. For real usage, it is necessary to have a cut-off probability (generally 2-32).\n[...]\nDo not write this yourself; use an existing library to handle it all for you.\n[...]\n- use XChaCha20-Poly1305 (or at least AES-GCM-SIV)\n- read NIST SP 800-38D",

            "output": "Title: Factoring the Noise protocol matrix | Latacora\nURL: https://latacora.micro.blog/2018/07/18/factoring-the-noise.html\nPublished: 2018-07-18T15:59:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n. WireGuard inherits its\n[...]\nThe other protocols are defined by two letters. From the spec:\n[...]\nThe first character refers to the initiator’s static key:\n[...]\n- I = Static key for initiator Immediately transmitted to responder, despite reduced or absent identity hiding\n- X = Static key for initiator Xmitted (\\“transmitted\\“) to responder\n- K = Static key for initiator Known to responder\n- N = No static key for initiator\n[...]\nThe second character refers to the responder’s static key:\n[...]\n- X = Static key for responder Xmitted (“transmitted“) to initiator\n- K = Static key for responder Known to initiator\n- N = No static key for responder\n[...]\nNN provides confidentiality against a passive attacker but neither party has any idea who you’re talking to because no static (long-term) keys are involved. For most applications none of the *N suites make a ton of sense: they imply the initiator does not care who they’re connecting to.\n[...]\nFor most applications the client (initiator) ought to have a fixed static key so we have a convenient cryptographic identity for clients over time. So really, if you wanted something with an N in it, you’d know.\n[...]\nThe responder usually doesn’t know what the key is for any initiator that happens to show up. This mostly makes sense if you have one central initiator that reaches out to a lot of responders: something like an MDM or sensor data collection perhaps. In practice, you often end up doing egress from those devices anyway for reasons that have nothing to do with Noise. So, K* is out.\n[...]\ntrade privacy (\n[...]\nIX doesn’t provide privacy for the initiator at all, but that’s the side you usually care about. It still has the roundtrip downside, making it a niche variant.\n[...]\nXX and XK require an extra round trip before they send over the initiator’s static key. Flip side: they have the strongest possible privacy protection for the initiator, whose identity is only sent to the responder after they’ve been authenticated and forward secrecy has been established.\n[...]\nIK provides a reasonable tradeoff: no extra round trip and the initiator’s key is encrypted to the responder’s static key. That means that the initiator’s key is only disclosed if the responder’s key is compromised. You probably don’t care about that. It does require the initiator to know the static key of the responder ahead of time but that’s probably true anyway: you want to check that key against a trusted value. You can also try private keys for the responder offline but that doesn’t matter unless you gratuitously messed up key generation. In conclusion, you probably want IK.\n[...]\nThis breakdown only works if you’re writing a client-server application that plausibly might’ve used mTLS instead. WireGuard, for example, is built on Noise_IK. The other variants aren’t pointless: they’re just good at different things. If you care more about protecting your initiator’s privacy than you do about handshake latency, you want Noise_XK. If you’re doing a peer-to-peer IoT system where device privacy matters, you might end up with Noise_XX. (It’s no accident that IK, XK, and XX are in the last set of protocols standing.)\n[...]\nConclusion It’s incredible how much has happened in the last few years to make protocols safer, between secure protocol templates like Noise, new proof systems like Tamarin, and ubiquitous libraries of safer primitives like libsodium. So far, the right answer for a safe transport has almost always been TLS (Transport Layer Security), perhaps mutually authenticated. That’s not going to change right away, but if you control both sides of the network and you need properties hard to get out of TLS, Noise is definitely The Right Answer. Just don’t stare at the eldritch rune matrix too long. You probably want Noise_IK. Or, you know, ask your security person :)\n\n---\n\nTitle: ROCA vs. ROBOT: An Eternal Golden Braid | Latacora\nURL: https://latacora.micro.blog/2018/08/08/roca-vs-robot.html\nPublished: 2018-08-08T22:52:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nencrypted email!\n[...]\nis, by itself, a head\n[...]\ncryptographic vulnerability. The\n[...]\nROCA broke all the Yubikeys. Also, Estonia. There will be ROCA-vulnerable RSA keys hidden in mission-critical infrastructure systems for the next 20 years. The real-world impact of ROCA is immense.\n[...]\nThe problem with ROCA is that it’s a problem with an exploit that takes core-years to execute. It’s a real vulnerability, but it’s closer to theory than any previous Pwnie nomination.\n[...]\nROBOT broke Facebook, Paypal, Cisco, a bunch of people running F5 middleboxes, Citrix, BouncyCastle, Erlang, WolfSSL, and Unisys ClearPath MCP. ClearPath! Someone finally broke it!\n[...]\nThe problem with ROBOT is that it’s cryptographically less interesting than ROCA. It exploits one of the better-known vulnerabilities in cryptography engineering: Bleichenbacher’s 1998 RSA oracle.\n[...]\nROBOT is practical. Practical is good. The Pwnies are about “pwnage”; they’re about things that offensive security people can actually accomplish in the field, against real world systems. ROBOT broke the Unisys ClearPath MCP.\n[...]\nROCA is “practical” in a cryptographic sense. As a cryptosystem, the Infineon RSA generator it targets is a smoking crater. But put yourself in the shoes of a red team in 2018. Assume you’ve actually identified a vulnerable key to target. How long will it take you to factor that key? For a 2048 bit key, it’s around “100 CPU-years”.\n[...]\nROBOT, different story. ROBOT is based on an older vulnerability, but the ROBOT research finally completes the weaponization of that vulnerability—not just in exploiting a single target in a single set of circumstances, but also in detecting it in the first place. In fact, in doing that, they found new ways to tickle the Bleichenbacher vulnerability, uncovering it in systems thought to be secure. The ROBOT methodology probably will get used by smart crypto testers in the future; it contributes to the craft in a broader way than ROCA.\n\n---\n\nTitle: https://www.latacora.com/blog/2018/09/30/analyzing-github-ssh-key-encryption/\nURL: https://latacora.micro.blog/2018/09/30/analyzing-a-simple.html\nPublished: 2018-09-30T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nhttps://www.latacora.com/blog/2018/09/30/analyzing-github-ssh-key-encryption/\n\n---\n\nTitle: https://www.latacora.com/blog/2021/08/11/remediating-aws-imdsv1/\nURL: https://latacora.micro.blog/2021/08/11/remediating-aws-imdsv.html\nPublished: 2021-08-11T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nhttps://www.latacora.com/blog/2021/08/11/remediating-aws-imdsv1/",

            "output": "Title: What Does It Mean To Be A Signal Competitor? - Dhole Moments\nURL: https://soatok.blog/2024/07/31/what-does-it-mean-to-be-a-signal-competitor/\nPublished: 2024-07-31T16:25:41.000Z\nAuthor: Post author\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\tBy  Soatok\nHighlights:\n### How You Encrypt Matters\n[...]\nSignal normalized the use of AES-256-CBC with HMAC-SHA256.\n[...]\nFacebook’s “Secret Conversations” feature deviated from this and preferred AES-GCM for attachments, but this bit them when the Invisible Salamanders attack was discovered.\n[...]\nThe way Signal uses AES+HMAC is fine for their use case, but building a secure committing AEAD mode (rather than merely AE) out of these primitives is nontrivial.\n[...]\nIf you’re aiming to compete with Signal on security, you should, at minimum, expect to engage with a cryptography auditing firm at least once a year to review and re-review your protocol designs and implementations.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Reviewing Signal’s Cryptography, Part 4 - Dhole Moments\nURL: https://soatok.blog/signal-crypto-review-2025-part-4/\nPublished: 2025-02-18T05:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAs you can see by`signal_crypto::aes_256_cbc_encrypt`, the message is encrypted with AES-256 in CBC mode. The key and IV are both sourced from`message_keys`, which is returned from`chain_key` a few lines above.\n[...]\n1. Verifying the authentication tag must be constant-time.\n2. IVs must be handled correctly. It’s not just enough to not repeat IVs for a given AES key; they also must be unpredictable. It’s not adequate to increase a counter for new IVs.\n3. If the IV is transmitted, the authentication tag must also protect the IV. After all, if you can freely manipulate the IV, you can control the first 16 bytes of plaintext on the decrypt path.\n4. The ciphertext must be authenticated, lest you introduce a padding oracle attack.\n[...]\nYes. When it calls`SignalMessage::new()` on lines 86-95, this inevitably computes a MAC over the ciphertext.\n[...]\nSince`message`(which includes the ciphertext) is part of`serialized`, the last place to check is the definition for compute_mac():\n[...]\n```\n\nfn compute_mac(\n\tsender_identity_key: &IdentityKey,\n\treceiver_identity_key: &IdentityKey,\n\tmac_key: &[u8],\n\tmessage: &[u8],\n) -> Result<[u8; Self::MAC_LENGTH]> {\n\tif mac_key.len() != 32 {\n\t\treturn Err(SignalProtocolError::InvalidMacKeyLength(mac_key.len()));\n\t}\n\tlet mut mac = Hmac::<Sha256>::new_from_slice(mac_key)\n\t\t.expect(\"HMAC-SHA256 should accept any size key\");\n\n\tmac.update(sender_identity_key.public_key().serialize().as_ref());\n\tmac.update(receiver_identity_key.public_key().serialize().as_ref());\n\tmac.update(message);\n\tlet mut result = [0u8; Self::MAC_LENGTH];\n\tresult.copy_from_slice(&mac.finalize().into_bytes()[..Self::MAC_LENGTH]);\n\tOk(result)\n}\n\n```\n[...]\nAs you can see, this not only authenticates the ciphertext, but the sender and receivers’ identity keys as well.\n[...]\nCritically, this is an Encrypt Then MAC construction. There are dumb ways to combine block cipher modes and message authentication codes, but Signal was originally designed by the person who coined the Cryptographic Doom Principle, so I’d expect nothing less.\n[...]\nNo, it does not. As you can see in the previous section, the`ctext` variable that gets passed to`SignalMessage::new()` is what ends up being authenticated by`compute_mac()`.\n[...]\nIf you look closely at the code that implements AES-CBC encryption in libsignal, you’ll note that the IV isn’t attached to the ciphertext.\n[...]\n32, 32],\n[...]\nThe IV, encryption key, and AES are all derived from the ratcheting protocol, using HKDF-HMAC-SHA256. This means the IV should be indistinguishable from random.\n[...]\nThis code uses AES-256-CBC + HMAC-SHA2 (Encrypt then MAC), with constant-time tag verification.\n[...]\nAlthough the IV isn’t covered by the MAC, it’s implicitly calculated from the same KDF that produces the keys used by AES-256-CBC and HMAC-SHA256.\n[...]\nFurther, the MAC binds the\n[...]\nto the sender and recipients’ identity keys. This prevents us from doing any sort of confused deputy shenanigans.\n[...]\nSignal used to use HMAC-SHA256, but then an integer overflow was discovered to allow MACs to be bypassed(slides 34-39). For new attachments, they now use an Incremental MAC calculated over the input stream.\n[...]\nAfter verifying the MAC, the\n[...]\nThere’s a lot of glue code around this, but you ultimately arrive at incremental_mac.rs in the Rust code of libsignal.\n[...]\nThis calculates MACs over sequences of ciphertext, and then validates them in order. This means that each chunk of ciphertext includes a MAC at the end, which doesn’t just validate the current chunk; it also validates all preceding chunks too.\n[...]\nThe final HMAC tag at the end should be equivalent to an HMAC over the entire ciphertext, taken in one shot.\n[...]\nAlthough Signal doesn’t include the IV in their HMAC calculation, this value is generated from the same KDF as the keys used for both encryption and authentication and the IV isn’t communicated with the ciphertext, so there is no avenue for exploitation.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Invisible Salamanders Are Not What You Think - Dhole Moments\nURL: https://soatok.blog/2024/09/10/invisible-salamanders-are-not-what-you-think/\nPublished: 2024-09-10T11:39:15.000Z\nAuthor: Post author\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\tBy  Soatok\nHighlights:\nEver since the Invisible Salamanders paper was published, there has been a quiet renaissance within my friends and colleagues in applied cryptography for studying systems that use Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) constructions, understanding what implicit assumptions these systems make about the guarantees of the AEAD mode they chose to build upon, and the consequences of those assumptions being false.\n[...]\nFast AEAD constructions based on polynomial MACs, such as AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305, were designed to provide confidentiality and integrity for the plaintext data, and optionally integrity for some additional associated data, in systems where both parties already negotiated one shared symmetric key.\n[...]\nIn this sense, AEAD constructions were an incredible success. So successful, in fact, that most cryptographers urge application developers to use one of the fast AEAD modes as the default suggestion without looking deeper at the problem being solved. This is a good thing, because most developers will choose something stupid like ECB mode in the absence of guidance from cryptographers, and AEAD modes are much, much safer than any hand-rolled block cipher modes.\n[...]\nCryptographers have concluded that AEAD constructions based on polynomial MACs–while great for performance and rejection of malformed packets without creating DoS risks–tend to make the same assumption. This is even true of misuse-resistant modes like AES-GCM-SIV and extended-nonce constructions like XSalsa20-Poly1305.\n[...]\nWhen discussing this implicit assumption of only one valid key in the systems that use these AEAD modes, we say that the modes are not key-committing. This terminology is based on what happens when this assumption is false.\n[...]\nConsequently, you can take a single, specially crafted ciphertext (with an authentication tag) and decrypt it under multiple different keys. The authentication tags will be valid for all keys, and the plaintext will be different.\n[...]\nSystems can mitigate this issue by introducing an explicit key commitment scheme(based on a cryptographic hash rather than a polynomial MAC) or by using a committing cipher mode (such as AES + HMAC, if done carefully).\n[...]\n1. Use HMAC, or (failing that) something built atop cryptographic hash functions, rather than a Polynomial MAC.\n2. Use an AEAD cipher designed with multi-recipient integrity as a security goal.\n3. Compute a non-invertible, one-way commitment of the encryption key.\n[...]\nencrypt(\n      $plaintext\n[...]\nData\n    );\n[...]\nciphertext . $tag;\n  }\n[...]\n/**\n   * An improved function looks something like this\n   */\n  public function newEncrypt(string $plaintext, string $assocData = ''):\n[...]\n{\n    // Avoid birthday bound issues with 256-bits of randomness\n    $longerNonce\n[...]\n// Derive a subkey and synthetic nonce\n    $tmp = hash_hkdf('sha512', $this->key, 44, self::NEW_ENCRYPT_KEY . $longerNonce);\n[...]\nsubstr($tmp, 3\n[...]\n// New: Key commitment\n    $commitment = hash_hkdf('sha512', $this->key, 32, self::NEW_COMMITMENT . $longerNonce);\n\n    // Most of this is unchanged    \n    $tag = '';\n    $ciphertext = openssl_encrypt(\n      $plaintext,\n      'aes-256-gcm',\n      $encKey,\n      OPENSSL_RAW_DATA,\n      $nonce,\n      $tag,\n      $assocData\n    );\n    return $longerNonce . $commitment . $ciphertext . $tag;\n  }\n\n}\n[...]\nIt’s important that the commitment be stored with the ciphertext, rather than bundling it with the key.\n[...]\nIf you’re building a network protocol that uses AEAD to encrypt data over an insecure network (e.g., WireGuard), keep up the good work.\n[...]\nAnd, if the answer is “yes”, then you should always explicitly add a key-commitment mechanism to your system design.\n[...]\nEventually, I expect a new generation of AEAD modes will be standardized that explicitly provide key-commitment.\n[...]\nWhen these new designs\n[...]\ntrusted by experts, feel free to update my advice to “prefer using those\n\n---\n\nTitle: Going Bark: A Furry’s Guide to End-to-End Encryption - You can often exploit the Key ID (`\"kid\"`) header to bypass the`\"alg\"` check and reintroduce the RSA/HMAC type confusion attack.\n[...]\nWhether an attacker is disabling security entirely (`alg = none`), exploiting algorithm confusion (`alg: RSA -> HMAC`), or leveraging a lack of type safety between symmetric and asymmetric key material, the fundamental problem is that they have the freedom to change anything at all.\n[...]\nThis is the PASETO approach\n[...]\nPASETO\n[...]\nPASETO v3 uses NIST cryptography (ECDSA P-384, AES-CTR + HMAC-SHA384 (encrypt then MAC), HKDF).\n[...]\nPASETO v4 uses modern cryptography (Ed25519, XChaCha20 + BLAKE2b (encrypt then MAC), HKDF).\n[...]\nVersioned protocols technically meet the vaguest sense of cryptographic agility, but each version is hard-coded for maximum security. An attacker cannot disable security (there is no “alg=none” equivalent) or choose wild ciphersuites (RC4 + SHA1, MAC-then-Encrypt), unless a protocol version is defined that permits dumb options.\n[...]\nImagine you have 10+ years worth of files stored in the cloud that were encrypted client-side using AES in CBC mode. One day, your penetration test vendor suggests migrating to AES in GCM mode.\n[...]\nNope. You can decrypt an AES-GCM ciphertext by telling the system to decrypt it as if it were AES-CBC.\n[...]\nBy virtue of allowing more than one algorithm at the same time, you will be vulnerable to downgrade attacks forever. This is also true if you never supported CBC Mode before, but your application permits its usage if an attacker selects it.\n[...]\nUsing the CBC-GCM example above, this is how you have to migrate your encryption. In this order:\n[...]\n1. Update every application in scope to support reading the new cryptography protocol and/or message format.\n2. Only after step 1 is complete, update every application to start writing the new cryptography protocol and/or message format.\n3. Only after step 2 is complete, re-encrypt all the old records to use the new cryptography protocol and/or message format.\n4. Only after step 3 is complete, turn off support for the old mode.\n[...]\n1. Enable support for the new encryption protocol version (preferably on a different TCP/UDP port) across your fleet.\n[...]\n2. Once everyone is using the new version, disable the old protocol version (and consider blocking the old port, if applicable).\n\n---\n\nTitle: Understanding Extended-Nonce Constructions - Dhole Moments\nURL: https://soatok.blog/2021/03/12/understanding-extended-nonce-constructions/\nPublished: 2021-03-12T09:47:39.000Z\nAuthor: Post author\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t \t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t \n\t\t\t\t\t\t\tBy  Soatok\nHighlights:\nMost of the data encrypted on the Internet these days uses an AEAD mode, which consists of a stream cipher (which may be built out of a hash function or a block cipher) and a polynomial authenticator (in this order: Encrypt, then Authenticate).\n[...]\nThe first solution is to use a nonce misuse\n[...]\naccumulating confidence in\n[...]\nHowever, their\n[...]\ncharacteristics are a\n[...]\nIn AES-GCM, you reject invalid messages before decryption. AES-GCM-SIV is Authenticate-then-Encrypt, instead of Encrypt-then-Authenticate. This two-pass rejection is an additional performance penalty that isn’t often considered by users of nonce misuse-resistant modes.\n[...]\nThe other solution is to use a variant of an existing AEAD mode that accepts an extended nonce–one large enough that users can generate it randomly and never risk collisions in a practical setting.\n[...]\nAn extended-nonce variant of ChaCha20-Poly1305, called XChaCha20-Poly1305, accepts a 256-bit key and 192-bit nonce (with an internal 32-bit block counter). Consequently, if you generate a 192-bit nonce randomly, your 2^-32 collision probability occurs after 2^80 messages rather than 2^32.\n[...]\nChaCha, like Salsa20 before it, is a stream cipher whose core is an ARX-based pseudorandom function (although it’s often called a hash function) whose output is mixed with the plaintext, using XOR. This paper explains its deviation from the original Salsa20 design.\n[...]\nThe extended-nonce constructions specified by Daniel J Bernstein in the XSalsa20 paper and this IETF RFC draft both do (effectively) the same thing:\n[...]\n- Take your “extended nonce” of 192 bits, and split it into a 128-bit segment and a 64-bit segment.\n- Run the first 128 bits of the nonce (as the full nonce/counter input) through the underlying PRF with the key without the final addition. (This PRF variant is called HSalsa20 and HChaCha respectively.)\n- Use the output of the HSalsa20/HChaCha step as the key to encrypt data with the remaining 64 bits of the nonce.\n[...]\nThis is a pretty trivial construction, and it costs slightly less than encrypting one additional block of plaintext (which is very fast for both algorithms). Whether or not the X-variant is secure depends entirely on two things: If the normal stream cipher is secure, and if the H-variant of the underlying PRF is secure.\n[...]\nChaCha outputs blocks of 512 bits which are combined with 512 bits of plaintext (using XOR) to encrypt. The final output is the initial added to the result of N (typically 20) rounds of the ChaCha round function.\n[...]\nBecause only half the internal state is returned, and it’s the half that corresponds to the public values (constants, nonce), you can get the same half-block security guarantee without needing that final addition. (The final addition is useful for ensuring ChaCha can encrypt large amounts of plaintext efficiently, but here we’re just hashing a subkey out of the extension to the nonce.)\n[...]\nthe pieces,\n[...]\nXChaCha\n[...]\n1. An attacker cannot recover half of the internal state from ChaCha outputs (due to the final addition which prevents the computation from being inverted).\n[...]\n2. An attacker cannot recover half of the internal state from HChaCha outputs (due to only returning a public computation of these inputs, and half of the internal state not being revealed).\n[...]\n3. The underlying transformations used in ChaCha are an efficient way to ensure high bit diffusion in reduced rounds, and the number of rounds is very conservative for the security target of the cipher.\n[...]\n4. The internal state of the PRF is 512 bits, which targets a 256-bit security level against birthday collisions.\n[...]\n5. The best known attacks only reduce the cost of ChaCha7 to about 2^230, which is computationally infeasible. ChaCha20 remains unaffected by this cryptanalysis.",

messages.\n[...]\n### TL;DR: which one should I use?\n[...]\nIf the target CPU has hardware AES acceleration (modern Intel or ARM CPU), `AEGIS-256` is the safest choice.\n[...]\nIf not, use `XChaCha20-Poly1305-IETF`.\n[...]\nXChaCha20-Poly1\n[...]\nXChaCha20-Poly1305 applies the construction described in Daniel Bernstein’s Extending the Salsa20 nonce paper to the ChaCha20 cipher in order to extend the nonce size to 192-bit.\n[...]\nThis extended nonce size allows random nonces to be safely used, and also facilitates the construction of misuse-resistant schemes.\n[...]\nThese functions accept an optional, arbitrarily long “additional data” parameter. These data are not present in the ciphertext, but are mixed in the computation of the authentication tag.\n[...]\n- Use the AEGIS ciphers, that, under common scenarios, are assumed to be safe against these attacks.\n[...]\n- Prepend `H(key, nonce || ciphertext_tag)` to the ciphertext\n- Verify this prior to decryption. This can be done with `crypto_auth()` and `crypto_auth_verify()`.\n[...]\nThis assumes that attackers don’t have control over associated data. If they do, associated data `ad` must be included in the input of the hash function as well: `H(key, nonce || ciphertext_tag || ad)`. In that case, as an optimization, `ad` can be left empty in the encryption and decryption functions.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Encrypting a set of related messages | Libsodium documentation\nURL: https://libsodium.gitbook.io/doc/secret-key_cryptography/encrypted-messages\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThe `crypto_secretbox`, `crypto_box` and `crypto_seal` APIs are designed to encrypt independent messages.\n[...]\nSimply encrypting individual messages with a random nonce doesn’t respect these\n[...]\nFor sequences of messages, libsodium 1.0.14 and beyond implement the `crypto_secretstream` API that satisfies the above constraints.\n[...]\nis recommended to encrypt files or for secure communications over a reliable protocol with ordering guarantees, such as TCP.\n[...]\nOn older versions of the library, and with transport protocols featuring weaker ordering and reliability guarantees, these constraints can be satisfied using AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Additional Data) constructions.\n[...]\nThe same key will be used to encrypt a set of messages. This is perfectly acceptable as long as that key is combined with a unique nonce for each message.\n[...]\nThe easiest way to achieve this is to choose a random initial nonce, and to increment it after each message:\n[...]\nfirst nonce used\n[...]\nencrypt a set of messages\n[...]\nIn order for the set of messages to be decrypted, the initialization vector must be transmitted. Unlike the key, it doesn’t have to be secret, so it can be prepended to the ciphertext, before the first message.\n[...]\nAEAD constructions encrypt messages and append an authentication tag. That authentication tag is computed using the following data:\n[...]\n- The secret key\n- The nonce\n- The message, before or after encryption\n- Optionally, some additional data\n[...]\none that was\n[...]\nNote that the nonce is included in the computation of the tag. A valid tag for a given ciphertext and nonce will not verify with the same ciphertext, but a different nonce.\n[...]\nAs described above, a simple way to ensure that a sequence of received messages matches what the originator sent is to set the initial nonce to a given value, and increment it after every message.\n[...]\nThe originator only sends the initialization vector. Individual messages do not contain a copy of the nonce used to encrypt them. They don’t have to, since the recipient can perform the same operation as the sender, namely increment the nonce after every received encrypted message, in order to decrypt the stream.\n[...]\nIf the stream being decrypted doesn’t match the original stream, because messages have been altered, removed, added, duplicated or reordered, the authentication tag will not match the one computed by the recipient using the expected nonce. This issue will be immediately detected by the decryption function.\n[...]\nIn that situation, a copy of the nonce, or value representing the difference with the initial nonce, can be added to every encrypted message. Since a message is encrypted and authenticated using a unique nonce in addition to the key, the decryption process will immediately detect an encrypted message whose attached nonce has been tampered with.\n[...]\n## Short nonces\n[...]\nCiphers such as `AES` do not feature nonces large enough to be randomly chosen without taking the risk of repeating a nonce.\n[...]\nMore accurately, a single nonce shouldn’t be used to encrypt different messages with the same key. Using the same nonce to encrypt different messages with different keys is perfectly safe.\n[...]\nTherefore, ciphers with short nonces can be safely used, but require keys to be frequently rotated, in addition to generating a new key for every stream. This requires support from application-level protocols, and can be tricky to implement.\n[...]\nAn alternative is to use a nonce extension mechanism. A large (160 bits or more) nonce `N` is used by the protocol. Its initial value can be randomly chosen.\n[...]\n- Using a pseudorandom function, a subkey and a shorter nonce are derived from the key and the large nonce\n- The cipher is used with this subkey and short nonce to encrypt or decrypt a message\n[...]\nUnless a cipher such as `AES` is a requirement, using a cipher with a longer\n[...]\nis easier and\n\n---\n\nTitle: Quickstart and FAQ | Libsodium documentation\nURL: https://libsodium.gitbook.io/doc/quickstart\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSome operations include variants with slightly different algorithms. For example, authenticated encryption can be done with XChaCha20-Poly1305 or AES-GCM. For consistency and to make high-level wrappers using dynamic dispatch easier to implement, all the variants of a function share the same prototype.\n[...]\n## How do I encrypt data?\n[...]\n### One-shot encryption where everything fits in memory\n[...]\n1. Create a secret key using `crypto_secretbox_keygen()`.\n2. Create a nonce using `randombytes_buf(nonce, sizeof nonce)`.\n3. Use `crypto_secretbox_easy()` to encrypt the message and send/store the resulting ciphertext along with the nonce. Unlike the key, the nonce doesn't have to be secret.\n4. Use `crypto_secretbox_open_easy()` to decrypt the ciphertext using the same key and nonce.\n[...]\nWhen `A` encrypts a message for `B` using a shared secret key using `crypto_box()`, `crypto_secretbox()`, `crypto_seal()`, `crypto_secretstream()`, or `crypto_aead()`, an authentication tag is also computed and should be sent to `B` along with the encrypted payload.\n[...]\nDuring the decryption process, the secret key is used to check that the authentication tag is\n[...]\nfor the given encrypted message. Reusing a nonce would destroy the confidentiality of messages sharing the same nonce and allow an attacker to craft additional valid ciphertexts.\n[...]\nOn a platform where counters cannot be maintained and no trusted source of randomness exists, deterministic encryption can be used as a last resort.\n[...]\nXChaCha20-SIV\n[...]\nsuch a construction,\n[...]\nbe used for key wrapping.\n\n---\n\nTitle: AES256-GCM | Libsodium documentation\nURL: https://libsodium.gitbook.io/doc/secret-key_cryptography/aead/aes-256-gcm\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nIn addition, nonces are short and repeated nonces would totally destroy the security of this scheme. Nonces should thus come from atomic counters, which can be difficult to set up in a distributed environment.\n[...]\nUnless you absolutely need AES-GCM, use AEGIS-256 (`crypto_aead_aegis256_*()`) instead. It doesn’t have any of these limitations.\n[...]\nOr, if you don’t need to authenticate additional data, just stick to `crypto_secretbox()`.\n[...]\n- Encrypts a message with a key and a nonce to keep it confidential\n- Computes an authentication tag. This tag is used to make sure that the message, as well as optional, non-confidential (non-encrypted) data, haven’t been tampered with.\n[...]\nIn combined mode, the authentication tag is directly appended to the encrypted message. This is usually what you want.\n[...]\n```c\nint crypto_aead_aes256gcm_encrypt(unsigned char *c,\n                                  unsigned long long *clen_p,\n                                  const unsigned char *m,\n                                  unsigned long long mlen,\n                                  const unsigned char *ad,\n                                  unsigned long long adlen,\n                                  const unsigned char *nsec,\n                                  const unsigned char *npub,\n                                  const unsigned char *k);\n\n```\n[...]\nThe function `crypto_aead_aes256gcm_encrypt()` encrypts a message `m` whose length is `mlen` bytes using a secret key `k` (`crypto_aead_aes256gcm_KEYBYTES` bytes) and a public nonce `npub` (`crypto_aead_aes256gcm_NPUBBYTES` bytes).\n[...]\nThe encrypted message, as well as a tag authenticating both the confidential message `m` and `adlen` bytes of non-confidential data `ad`, are put into `c`.\n[...]\n`nsec` is not used by this particular construction and should always be `NULL`.\n[...]\nThe function always returns `0`.\n[...]\nThe public nonce `npub` should never ever be reused with the same key. The `crypto_kx_*()` API can be used to do so.\n[...]\nWhen using AES-GCM, it is also recommended to switch to a new key before reaching ~ 350 GB encrypted with the same key.
