No backends. No simulated math.\n[...]\n`crypto-lab-padding-oracle` is a browser-based interactive demonstration of the **CBC padding oracle attack** (Vaudenay 2002) — a chosen-ciphertext attack that decrypts any AES-CBC ciphertext without knowing the key, using only a one-bit padding validity oracle. All cryptographic operations use the browser's native WebCrypto API: real AES-128-CBC encryption with PKCS#7 padding, real AES-256-GCM for the AEAD defense demo, and real padding validation on every oracle query. The security model is symmetric-key cryptography — the attack exploits the combination of CBC mode and observable padding validation errors, not a weakness in AES itself.\n[...]\n- **Teaching the Vaudenay 2002 attack** — the demo walks through single-byte recovery, full-block recovery, and multi-block decryption with live oracle query counts, making the O(256 × 16 × n) complexity tangible.\n-\n[...]\n- **Comparing CBC with AEAD** — Panel 6 demonstrates AES-256-GCM tamper rejection side-by-side with CBC, showing why AEAD eliminates the attack class entirely.\n- **Security training and CTF preparation** — the interactive byte-grid visualizer and speed controls make the attack mechanics concrete for hands-on learners.\n[...]\n- **Do not use this as a production encryption library** — the oracle and key live in the same browser context; there is no network oracle, no real confidentiality boundary, and no key management.\n[...]\n## Live Demo\n[...]\nThe demo has six tabbed panels. Panels 2–4 let you encrypt arbitrary plaintext with a random AES-128-CBC key, then run the full padding oracle attack at adjustable speeds (slow/medium/fast) while watching byte-by-byte recovery on an interactive grid. Panel 6 lets you encrypt with AES-256-GCM, tamper with the ciphertext, and see authentication fail with no plaintext revealed.\n[...]\n- **Using CBC without Encrypt-then-MAC** — MAC-then-encrypt or encrypt-only CBC is fundamentally vulnerable; the only complete fix is authenticated encryption (AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305).\n[...]\n- **ASP.NET ViewState** — Microsoft's web framework used AES-CBC to protect ViewState and session cookies, leading to full plaintext recovery via MS10-070 before the patch.\n[...]\n| Demo | Description |\n| --- | --- |\n| crypto-lab-aes-modes | CBC, CTR, GCM modes — padding oracle panel included |\n| crypto-lab-timing-oracle | Timing oracle attacks — companion to Lucky Thirteen |\n| crypto-lab-shadow-vault | ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD in depth |\n| crypto-compare | Symmetric cipher comparison tool (Symmetric category) |\n| crypto-lab | Full crypto-lab collection landing page |\n\n---\n\nTitle: WebCryptoAPI Labs\nURL: https://excelliumsa.github.io/WebCryptographyAPI-Study/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nWebCryptoAPI Labs\n[...]\nExport key using exportKey (AES key) Export key using wrapKey (AES key) Export key marked as non-extractable (AES key) Store key export using IndexedDB store (AES key) Store CryptoKey object using IndexedDB store (AES key) Open CyberChef AES recipe\n[...]\nChoose a test case... Pseudo-Random Number Generation (PRNG) Hashing (SHA-512) Encryption with symmetric key (AES-GCM) Encryption with asymmetric key (RSA-OAEP) Signature operation with secret (HMAC) Signature operation with asymmetric key pair (ECDSA) Key derivation based on a password (PBKDF2)\n\n---\n\nTitle: Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) | Tink\nURL: https://developers.google.com/tink/aead\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n# Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)\n\nThe Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) primitive is the most\ncommon primitive for data encryption and is suitable for most needs.\n\nAEAD has the following properties:\n\n- Secrecy: Nothing about the plaintext is known, except its length.\n- Authenticity: It is impossible to change the encrypted plaintext underlying the ciphertext without being detected.\n- Symmetric: Encrypting the plaintext and decrypting the ciphertext is done with the same key.\n- Randomization : Encryption is randomized. Two messages with the same plaintext yield different ciphertexts. Attackers cannot know which ciphertext corresponds to a given plaintext. If you want to avoid this, use Deterministic AEAD instead.\n\n### Associated data\n\n> [!CAUTION]\n> Caution: Associated data is authenticated but NOT encrypted.\n\nAEAD can be used to tie ciphertext to specific associated\ndata. Suppose you have a database with the fields `user-id`\nand `encrypted-medical-history`. In this scenario, `user-id` can be used as\nassociated data when encrypting `encrypted-medical-history`. This prevents an\nattacker from moving medical history from one user to another.\n\nAssociated data is optional. If specified, decryption only succeeds if the same\nassociated data is passed to both encrypt and decrypt calls.\n\n### Choose a key type\n\nWhile we recommend AES128_GCM for most uses, there are various key types for\ndifferent needs. AES128 offers 128-bit security, and AES256 offers 256-bit\nsecurity.\n\nThe two notable security constraints when choosing a mode are:\n\n1. QPS: How many messages are encrypted with the same key?\n2. Message size: How large are the messages?\n\nSupported key types:\n\n- AES-CTR-HMAC (AES128_CTR_HMAC_SHA256, AES256_CTR_HMAC_SHA256) with a 16-byte Initialization Vector (IV) is the most conservative mode with good bounds.\n- AES-EAX (AES128_EAX, AES256_EAX) is slightly less conservative and slightly faster than AES128_CTR_HMAC_SHA256.\n- AES-GCM (AES128_GCM, AES256_GCM) is usually the fastest mode with the strictest limits on the number of messages and message size. When these limits on plaintext and associated data lengths (below) are exceeded, AES-GCM fails catastrophically by leaking plaintext and the authentication portion of the AES-GCM internal key. AES-GCM is not robust [ABN] nor key-committing [GLR]. It is possible to generate a ciphertext that is decryptable with two different keys. This can lead to practical attacks [DGRW]. Carefully review your threat model if the key is chosen by the adversary.\n- AES-GCM-SIV (AES128_GCM_SIV, AES256_GCM_SIV) is nearly as fast as AES-GCM. It has the same limits as AES-GCM on the number of messages and message size, but when these limits are exceeded, it fails in a less catastrophic way: it may only leak the fact that two messages are equal. This makes it safer to use than AES-GCM, but it is less widely used in practice. To use this in Java, you have to install Conscrypt.\n- XChaCha20-Poly1305 (XCHACHA20_POLY1305) has a much greater limit on the number of messages and message size than AES-GCM, but when it does fail (very unlikely), it also leaks key material. It isn't hardware accelerated, so it can be slower than AES modes in situations where hardware acceleration is available.\n\n> [!NOTE]\n> Note: All listed algorithms support a minimum length of 2^32^ bytes for the plaintext and associated data.\n\nLearn more about the wire format of the AEAD ciphertext.\n\n### Security guarantees\n\nAEAD implementations offer:\n\n- CCA2 security.\n- At least 80-bit authentication strength.\n- The ability to encrypt at least 2^32^ messages with a total of 2^50^ bytes. No attack with up to 2^32^ chosen plaintexts or chosen ciphertexts has success probability larger than 2^-32^.\n\n> [!CAUTION]\n> Caution: AEAD offers no secrecy guarantees for associated data.\n\n### Example use cases\n\nSee I want to encrypt data and I want to bind ciphertext to\nits context.\n\n---\n\nTitle: A Practical Guide to the Web Cryptography API - DEV Community\nURL: https://dev.to/voracious/a-practical-guide-to-the-web-cryptography-api-4o8n\nPublished: 2020-09-09T23:46:06.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nA Practical Guide to the Web Cryptography API - DEV Community\n[...]\nClient-side encryption is a feature I had wanted to implement in octo for a while now. When it finally came time to tackle it, I was surprised at the sparse real-world examples on the topic. The documentation on MDN is robust, but it requires a lot of jumping around to individual method APIs. I hope this article is helpful for anyone out there looking for guidance.\n[...]\nNote: The Web Cryptography API is asynchronous, so I use the async/await syntax in\n[...]\narticle for concision.\n[...]\nThe Web Cryptography API was initially exposed through a nonstandard interface called Crypto, but it was later standardized through a new interface called SubtleCrypto. This article will focus on the public SubtleCrypto interface exposed at`window.crypto.subtle`.\n[...]\nFor the purposes of this article, we are going to use a symmetric algorithm. The public-key (asymmetric) strategy has a hard limit on how much data it can encrypt based on key size:`(keyBits / 8) - padding`. Symmetric encryption uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt data, and it does not have the same constraint. There are a few supported algorithms, but the recommended symmetric algorithm is`AES-GCM` for its authenticated mode.\n[...]\ncan encrypt data,\n[...]\nto encode it into a byte stream\n[...]\ncan achieve this\n[...]\nThis little utility\n[...]\nan IV is\n[...]\nintroduces true randomness\n[...]\n. When using the\n[...]\nto encrypt multiple sets of data, it is possible to derive relationships between the\n[...]\n. IVs ensure that\n[...]\nNow that we have all of our utilities in place, we can implement our`encrypt` function! As mentioned above, we will need it to return both the cipher and the IV so that we can decrypt the cipher later.\n[...]\nhttps://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SubtleCrypto/encrypt\nconst encrypt = async (data, key) => {\n  const encoded = encode(data)\n  const iv = generateIv()\n  const cipher = await window.crypto.subtle.encrypt({\n    name: 'AES-GCM',\n    iv: iv,\n  }, key, encoded)\n\n  return {\n    cipher,\n    iv,\n  }\n}\n[...]\nMost practical applications of encryption involve transmission or storage of said encrypted data. When data is encrypted using SubtleCrypto, the resulting cipher and IV are represented as raw binary data buffers. This is not an ideal format for transmission or storage, so we will tackle packing and unpacking next.\n[...]\nof the process\n[...]\nAfter decrypting, we will need to decode our resulting byte stream back into its original form. We can achieve this with the`TextDecoder` class. This utility will be used by our`decrypt` function later.\n[...]\nNow we just need to implement the`decrypt` function. As mentioned before, we will need to supply not just the key but also the IV that was used in the encryption step.\n[...]\ntle.decrypt\n[...]\n}, key,\n[...]\nit into Practice\n[...]\nLet's write an app! Now that all of our utilities are built, we just need to use them. We will encrypt, pack, and transmit our data to a secure endpoint. Then, we will retrieve, unpack, and decrypt the original message.\n[...]\n```\nconst app = async () => {\n  // encrypt message\n  const first = 'Hello, World!'\n  const key = await generateKey()\n  const { cipher, iv } = await encrypt(first, key)\n\n  // pack and transmit\n  await fetch('/secure-api', {\n    method: 'POST',\n    body: JSON.stringify({\n      cipher: pack(cipher),\n      iv: pack(iv),\n    }),\n  })\n\n  // retrieve\n  const response = await fetch('/secure-api').then(res => res.json())\n\n  // unpack and decrypt message\n  const final = await decrypt(unpack(response.cipher), key, unpack(response.iv))\n  console.log(final) // logs 'Hello, World!'\n}\n[...]\nThat's all there is to it! We have successfully implemented client-side encryption.\n[...]\nAs a final note, I just want to share octo, a writing app for developers, one more time. Compare ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR, and GCM modes.\n[...]\n100% Private - Runs Entirely in Your Browser\n[...]\nNo data is sent to any server. All processing happens locally on your device.\n[...]\nLoading interactive tool...\n[...]\nAll modes take a block cipher, a key, and (usually) an initialization vector (IV) or nonce as inputs. They differ in how they chain blocks together:\n[...]\n| Mode | Full Name | Type | Parallel Encrypt | Parallel Decrypt | Authentication |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| ECB | Electronic Codebook | Block | Yes | Yes | No |\n| CBC | Cipher Block Chaining | Block | No | Yes | No |\n| CTR | Counter | Stream | Yes | Yes | No |\n| GCM | Galois/Counter Mode | Stream | Yes | Yes | Yes (AEAD) |\n| CCM | Counter with CBC-MAC | Stream | No | No | Yes (AEAD) |\n| CFB | Cipher Feedback | Stream | No | Yes | No |\n| OFB | Output Feedback | Stream | No | No | No |\n[...]\n- ECB encrypts each block independently — identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, visibly leaking patterns (the famous \"ECB penguin\" demonstration)\n- CBC chains each block to the previous ciphertext block via XOR, so identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertext — but encryption cannot be parallelized\n- CTR converts AES into a stream cipher by encrypting sequential counter values and XORing with plaintext — fully parallelizable in both directions\n- GCM extends CTR with a Galois field multiplication step that authenticates both the ciphertext and any additional unencrypted data (like packet headers)\n[...]\n3. Never reuse nonces in GCM — GCM nonce reuse is catastrophic: it reveals the authentication key and enables plaintext recovery. Use a counter or random 96-bit nonce with collision probability tracking.\n[...]\n5. Understand IV requirements — CBC requires unpredictable IVs (use CSPRNG). CTR/GCM require unique nonces (counters are fine). Mixing these requirements causes vulnerabilities.\n[...]\nCBC (Cipher Block Chaining) XORs each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block before encryption. This means identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertext blocks, hiding patterns. An Initialization Vector (IV) is used for the first block. Changing the IV produces completely different ciphertext for the same plaintext.\n[...]\nECB encrypts blocks independently (insecure). CBC chains blocks together. CFB converts a block cipher into a stream cipher. OFB generates a keystream independent of plaintext. CTR uses a counter for parallelizable encryption. GCM adds authentication to CTR mode for authenticated encryption. Each has different performance and security tradeoffs.\n[...]\nFor most applications, use GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) as it provides both confidentiality and authentication with good performance. Use CBC when GCM is not available. Never use ECB for anything beyond single-block encryption. CTR is good for parallelized encryption. This is covered in CISSP Domain 3.\n[...]\nAn IV is a random or unique value used with the encryption key to ensure that the same plaintext encrypts to different ciphertext each time. IVs must be unpredictable (CBC) or unique (CTR/GCM) but do not need to be secret. Reusing IVs with the same key can compromise security, especially in CTR and GCM modes.\n[...]\nThis tool is provided for informational and educational purposes only. RFC 8452. Part of crypto-lab.\n[...]\ncrypto-lab-nonce-guard is a browser-based comparison of AES-GCM (NIST SP 800-38D) and AES-GCM-SIV (RFC 8452) focused on nonce misuse resistance. Both are authenticated encryption schemes producing a ciphertext and authentication tag. AES-GCM requires strict nonce uniqueness — reusing a nonce allows an attacker to recover the XOR of two plaintexts and algebraically recover the GHASH authentication key, enabling tag forgery. AES-GCM-SIV uses a synthetic IV derived from the plaintext, so nonce reuse degrades only to leaking whether two plaintexts were identical — the keystream is never reused and the authentication key is never exposed. The security model is symmetric AEAD with a 256-bit key.\n[...]\nEnter two messages and toggle nonce reuse on. Click \"Encrypt Both\" to encrypt under both AES-GCM and AES-GCM-SIV with the same key and nonce. Click \"Run Attack\" to execute the XOR recovery attack — watch AES-GCM expose the XOR of both plaintexts while AES-GCM-SIV reveals nothing beyond whether the messages were identical.\n[...]\n- AES Modes — ECB, CBC, CTR, GCM modes\n- ChaCha20 Stream — ARX stream cipher with nonce reuse demo\n- MAC Race — HMAC, Poly1305, GHASH comparison\n- crypto-lab home\n\n---\n\nTitle: systemslibrarian/crypto-lab-padding-oracle\nURL: https://github.com/systemslibrarian/crypto-lab-padding-oracle\nPublished: 2026-04-07T19:39:06.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nBrowser-based CBC padding oracle attack demo — full Vaudenay 2002 chosen-ciphertext attack with real AES-CBC, byte-by-byte plaintext recovery, and coverage of ASP.NET, Lucky Thirteen, and POODLE exploits. No backends. No simulated math.\n[...]\n`crypto-lab-padding-oracle` is a browser-based interactive demonstration of the **CBC padding oracle attack** (Vaudenay 2002) — a chosen-ciphertext attack that decrypts any AES-CBC ciphertext without knowing the key, using only a one-bit padding validity oracle. All cryptographic operations use the browser's native WebCrypto API: real AES-128-CBC encryption with PKCS#7 padding, real AES-256-GCM for the AEAD defense demo, and real padding validation on every oracle query. The security model is symmetric-key cryptography — the attack exploits the combination of CBC mode and observable padding validation errors, not a weakness in AES itself.\n[...]\n- **Teaching the Vaudenay 2002 attack** — the demo walks through single-byte recovery, full-block recovery, and multi-block decryption with live oracle query counts, making the O(256 × 16 × n) complexity tangible.\n[...]\n- **Security training and CTF preparation** — the interactive byte-grid visualizer and speed controls make the attack mechanics concrete for hands-on learners.\n[...]\n- **Do not use this as a production encryption library** — the oracle and key live in the same browser context; there is no network oracle, no real confidentiality boundary, and no key management.\n[...]\nThe demo has six tabbed panels. Panels 2–4 let you encrypt arbitrary plaintext with a random AES-128-CBC key, then run the full padding oracle attack at adjustable speeds (slow/medium/fast) while watching byte-by-byte recovery on an interactive grid. Panel 6 lets you encrypt with AES-256-GCM, tamper with the ciphertext, and see authentication fail with no plaintext revealed.\n[...]\n- **Distinguishable error responses** — returning HTTP 500 for padding errors versus HTTP 200 for other failures gives an attacker a direct one-bit oracle; this is exactly what broke ASP.NET (MS10-070 / CVE-2010-3332).\n[...]\n- **Timing side-channels in padding validation** — even constant-time padding checks can leak information through MAC computation length differences, as demonstrated by Lucky Thirteen against TLS CBC cipher suites.\n[...]\n- **Ignored padding bytes in SSL 3.0** — SSL 3.0 only validates the last padding byte, allowing POODLE to recover one plaintext byte per ~256 requests without a traditional padding oracle.\n[...]\n- **Predictable IVs in TLS 1.0** — reusing the last ciphertext block as the next record's IV (BEAST) enables a related chosen-plaintext attack against CBC, even without a padding oracle.\n[...]\n- **Using CBC without Encrypt-then-MAC** — MAC-then-encrypt or encrypt-only CBC is fundamentally vulnerable; the only complete fix is authenticated encryption (AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305).\n[...]\n- **TLS 1.0–1.2** — CBC cipher suites were standard in TLS for over a decade; TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) removed them entirely because of the padding oracle attack class.\n[...]\n- **ASP.NET ViewState** — Microsoft's web framework used AES-CBC to protect ViewState and session cookies, leading to full plaintext recovery via MS10-070 before the patch.\n[...]\n- **IPsec ESP** — the Encapsulating Security Payload protocol supports AES-CBC mode for VPN tunnels; implementations must use Encrypt-then-MAC to avoid padding oracles.\n[...]\n- **OpenSSL / GnuTLS / NSS** — all three major TLS libraries required patches for Lucky Thirteen timing side-channels in their CBC padding validation paths.\n[...]\n- **PKCS#7 / CMS (S/MIME)** — the Cryptographic Message Syntax uses CBC with PKCS#7 padding for email encryption; Efail (2018) demonstrated related plaintext exfiltration against S/MIME implementations.\n\n---\n\nTitle: CryptoHack – Symmetric Cryptography - ECB CBC WTF\nURL: https://cryptohack.org/courses/symmetric/ecbcbcwtf/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nCryptoHack – Symmetric Cryptography - ECB CBC WTF \n\n<-- Prev\n[...]\n## Symmetric Cryptography\n[...]\nECB CBC WTF\n\n55 pts · 6780 Solves· 45 Solutions\n[...]\nHere you can encrypt in CBC but only decrypt in ECB. That shouldn't be a weakness because they're different modes... right?Play at https://aes.cryptohack.org/ecbcbcwtf\n\n---\n\nTitle: GCM Nonce Reuse\nURL: https://pentesterlab.com/exercises/gcm-nonce\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nGCM Nonce Reuse Exercise!\n# GCM Nonce Reuse\n *Bookmarked!*\nThis challenge covers the impact of nonce reuse on GCM\n[...]\nIn this exercise, we explore the critical vulnerability that arises from reusing the same nonce (IV) in Galois/Counter Mode (GCM) encryption. The lab provides a practical demonstration where users must register an account, post messages, and utilize XOR operations to retrieve the encryption key. By understanding the provided encryption code, which mistakenly uses a fixed IV, and leveraging XOR operations, participants can decode the encrypted messages and uncover the key.\n[...]\nThe video transcript walks through the steps needed to exploit this weakness, from registering an account to posting messages and finally using a Ruby script to perform the necessary XOR operations. This exercise emphasizes the importance of proper nonce management in encryption algorithms and highlights the severe security implications of neglecting this aspect.",

Configure the key, nonce, AAD, and tag length to export ciphertext with integrity or to verify inbound payloads.\n\n## AES-GCM encryption\n\nRun authenticated encryption entirely in your browser. Configure keys, nonces, AAD, and tag lengths to export ciphertext + auth tags, or supply those values to verify and recover plaintext.\n\nPlaintext input\n\nClearPasteCalculate\n\nCiphertext + tag output\n\nThe result is ciphertext concatenated with the tag.\n\nOperation\n\nTag length\n\nKey size\n\nSecret key\n\nRandom\n\nShort keys are zero-padded; longer inputs are truncated.\n\nNonce / IV\n\nRandom\n\nNonces must match on both sides and should never be reused with the same key.\n\nAdditional authenticated data (optional)\n\nClear AAD\n\n## AES-GCM tips\n\nAES-GCM layers a GHASH authenticator on top of CTR mode, so ciphertext and auth tags must always travel together. Any bit flip or mismatch during decryption causes authentication to fail.\n\nNever reuse a nonce with the same key. Twelve random bytes are the de facto standard, and longer tags mean stronger integrity guarantees. Additional authenticated data (AAD) is left in the clear but still protected by the tag.\n\nNo padding is required—GCM accepts any plaintext length. Make sure both parties agree on key size, nonce, AAD, tag length, and encodings before exchanging data.\n\n© 2026 CipherTools. All computations run locally in your browser.",

Since AES encrypts exactly 128 bits at a time, a mode of operation specifies how to handle messages of arbitrary length — how blocks relate to each other, whether encryption can be parallelized, and whether the mode provides authentication in addition to confidentiality.\n\nChoosing the right mode is as important as choosing the right cipher. A strong cipher like AES can be rendered insecure by a poorly chosen mode, and the wrong mode can introduce vulnerabilities even when the underlying algorithm is sound.\n\n## How Block Cipher Modes Work\n\nAll modes take a block cipher, a key, and (usually) an initialization vector (IV) or nonce as inputs. They differ in how they chain blocks together:\n\n| Mode | Full Name             | Type   | Parallel Encrypt | Parallel Decrypt | Authentication |\n| ---- | --------------------- | ------ | ---------------- | ---------------- | -------------- |\n| ECB  | Electronic Codebook   | Block  | Yes              | Yes              | No             |\n| CBC  | Cipher Block Chaining | Block  | No               | Yes              | No             |\n| CTR  | Counter               | Stream | Yes              | Yes              | No             |\n| GCM  | Galois/Counter Mode   | Stream | Yes              | Yes              | Yes (AEAD)     |\n| CCM  | Counter with CBC-MAC  | Stream | No               | No               | Yes (AEAD)     |\n| CFB  | Cipher Feedback       | Stream | No               | Yes              | No             |\n| OFB  | Output Feedback       | Stream | No               | No               | No             |\n\n**AEAD** (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) modes like GCM and CCM provide both confidentiality and integrity in a single operation, eliminating the need for a separate HMAC.\n\n## Visual Differences Between Modes\n\nThis tool visualizes how each mode processes plaintext blocks. The key differences to observe:\n\n* **ECB** encrypts each block independently — identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, visibly leaking patterns (the famous \"ECB penguin\" demonstration)\n* **CBC** chains each block to the previous ciphertext block via XOR, so identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertext — but encryption cannot be parallelized\n* **CTR** converts AES into a stream cipher by encrypting sequential counter values and XORing with plaintext — fully parallelizable in both directions\n* **GCM** extends CTR with a Galois field multiplication step that authenticates both the ciphertext and any additional unencrypted data (like packet headers)\n\n## Common Use Cases\n\n* **Learning cryptography**: Visualize why ECB mode leaks information and why CBC/CTR/GCM modes do not\n* **Security architecture decisions**: Choose the right mode based on performance requirements, parallelization needs, and whether authentication is required\n* **Code review**: Verify that application code uses appropriate modes — flag ECB usage as a critical vulnerability\n* **Compliance documentation**: Explain to auditors why your implementation uses GCM over CBC and reference NIST SP 800-38D\n\n## Best Practices\n\n1. **Default to AES-GCM** — For most applications, GCM provides the best combination of performance, parallelism, and built-in authentication.\n2. **Never use ECB for structured data** — ECB is only safe for encrypting single blocks (like individual keys). For any multi-block data, it leaks patterns.\n3. **Never reuse nonces in GCM** — GCM nonce reuse is catastrophic: it reveals the authentication key and enables plaintext recovery. Use a counter or random 96-bit nonce with collision probability tracking.\n4. **Use CTR+HMAC if GCM is unavailable** — Encrypt-then-MAC using CTR mode and HMAC-SHA256 provides equivalent security to GCM when AEAD is not available in your library.\n5. **Understand IV requirements** — CBC requires unpredictable IVs (use CSPRNG). CTR/GCM require unique nonces (counters are fine). Mixing these requirements causes vulnerabilities.\n\n## Frequently Asked Questions\n\nCommon questions about the Cryptographic Mode Visualizer\n\nWhy is ECB mode insecure?\n\nECB (Electronic Codebook) encrypts each block independently with the same key. Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, revealing patterns in the original data. The classic \"ECB penguin\" demonstration shows that an encrypted image in ECB mode still reveals the shape of the original image.\n\n[Read More](https://inventivehq.com/blog/cryptography-hashing-complete-guide)\n\nHow does CBC mode solve the ECB problem?\n\nCBC (Cipher Block Chaining) XORs each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block before encryption. This means identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertext blocks, hiding patterns. An Initialization Vector (IV) is used for the first block. Changing the IV produces completely different ciphertext for the same plaintext.\n\n[Read More](https://inventivehq.com/blog/cryptography-hashing-complete-guide)\n\nWhat is the difference between block cipher modes?\n\nECB encrypts blocks independently (insecure). CBC chains blocks together. CFB converts a block cipher into a stream cipher. OFB generates a keystream independent of plaintext. CTR uses a counter for parallelizable encryption. GCM adds authentication to CTR mode for authenticated encryption. Each has different performance and security tradeoffs.\n\nWhich block cipher mode should I use?\n\nFor most applications, use GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) as it provides both confidentiality and authentication with good performance. Use CBC when GCM is not available. Never use ECB for anything beyond single-block encryption. CTR is good for parallelized encryption. This is covered in CISSP Domain 3.\n\n[Read More](https://inventivehq.com/blog/tls-configuration-hardening-guide)\n\nWhat is an Initialization Vector (IV)?\n\nAn IV is a random or unique value used with the encryption key to ensure that the same plaintext encrypts to different ciphertext each time. IVs must be unpredictable (CBC) or unique (CTR/GCM) but do not need to be secret. Reusing IVs with the same key can compromise security, especially in CTR and GCM modes.\n\n[Read More](https://inventivehq.com/blog/cryptography-hashing-complete-guide)\n\n## Explore More Tools\n\nContinue with these related tools\n\n[SecurityAES Encryption ToolEncrypt and decrypt text using AES encryptionTry it now](https://inventivehq.com/tools/aes-encryption-tool)\n\n[SecurityHash Generator - MD5, SHA-256, SHA-512Generate MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, and SHA-512 hashes for text and files. Check malware hashes with VirusTotal integration.Try it now](https://inventivehq.com/tools/hash-generator)\n\n[SecurityEntropy AnalyzerFree Shannon entropy calculator for malware analysis. All cryptographic operations use the browser's native WebCrypto API: real AES-128-CBC encryption with PKCS#7 padding, real AES-256-GCM for the AEAD defense demo, and real padding validation on every oracle query. The security model is symmetric-key cryptography — the attack exploits the combination of CBC mode and observable padding validation errors, not a weakness in AES itself.\n[...]\n## Live Demo\n[...]\n**https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab-padding-oracle/**\n[...]\nThe demo has six tabbed panels. Panels 2–4 let you encrypt arbitrary plaintext with a random AES-128-CBC key, then run the full padding oracle attack at adjustable speeds (slow/medium/fast) while watching byte-by-byte recovery on an interactive grid. Panel 6 lets you encrypt with AES-256-GCM, tamper with the ciphertext, and see authentication fail with no plaintext revealed.\n[...]\n## Related Demos\n[...]\n| Demo | Description |\n| --- | --- |\n| crypto-lab-aes-modes | CBC, CTR, GCM modes — padding oracle panel included |\n| crypto-lab-timing-oracle | Timing oracle attacks — companion to Lucky Thirteen |\n| crypto-lab-shadow-vault | ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD in depth |\n| crypto-compare | Symmetric cipher comparison tool (Symmetric category) |\n| crypto-lab | Full crypto-lab collection landing page |\n\n---\n\nTitle: Crypto Lab — Paul Clark / systemslibrarian\nURL: https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nCrypto Lab — Paul Clark / systemslibrarian\n[...]\nBlock Cipher ModesAES ModesECB, CBC, CTR, GCM, and CCM with live padding oracle attack. Both are authenticated encryption schemes producing a ciphertext and authentication tag. AES-GCM requires strict nonce uniqueness — reusing a nonce allows an attacker to recover the XOR of two plaintexts and algebraically recover the GHASH authentication key, enabling tag forgery. AES-GCM-SIV uses a synthetic IV derived from the plaintext, so nonce reuse degrades only to leaking whether two plaintexts were identical — the keystream is never reused and the authentication key is never exposed. The security model is symmetric AEAD with a 256-bit key.\n[...]\n## 3. Click \"Run Attack\" to execute the XOR recovery attack — watch AES-GCM expose the XOR of both plaintexts while AES-GCM-SIV reveals nothing beyond whether the messages were identical.\n[...]\n**Cross-links\n[...]\n20 Stream\n[...]\n- MAC Race —\n[...]\n- crypto-lab home\n\n---\n\nTitle: Crypto Lab — Paul Clark / systemslibrarian\nURL: https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nCrypto Lab — Paul Clark / systemslibrarian\n[...]\nNo demos found.\n[...]\nNoise Protocol FrameworkNoise PipeNN, XX, IK, and IKpsk2 handshake patterns with real X25519 arithmetic, live transport encryption, and a WireGuard deep dive.             "output": "    Padding Oracle — crypto-lab    [Skip to main content](#main-content)\n\nCryptanalysis 🌙\n\n# Padding Oracle CBC Chosen-Ciphertext Attack — Full Walkthrough\n\nTopics covered: AES-CBC PKCS#7 Chosen-Ciphertext Vaudenay 2002\n\n**Why this matters:** The padding oracle attack has broken ASP.NET, TLS, and dozens of other systems. One bit of information — valid or invalid padding — is enough to decrypt any CBC ciphertext without the key.\n\n1 CBC & Padding 2 Single Byte 3 Full Block 4 Full Decryption 5 Hall of Fame 6 Defenses\n\n## CBC Mode and PKCS#7 Padding\n\nAES-CBC decrypts each block as `P[i] = AES⁻¹(C[i]) ⊕ C[i−1]`. The last block must end with valid PKCS#7 padding. An implementation that tells you *whether* padding is valid — even just via an error code — is a padding oracle.\n\n### CBC Decryption Flow\n\nThe padding check at the end is the oracle. An HTTP 200 vs 500, a TLS alert code, or even a measurable timing difference — all are sufficient to mount the full attack.\n\n### PKCS#7 Padding Rules\n\nIf *n* bytes of padding are needed, all *n* bytes must equal *n*. A full block of padding (n = 16) is always appended if plaintext fits exactly.\n\n### Valid Padding\n\n### Invalid Padding\n\n### The Oracle\n\nHTTP HTTP 200 = Valid vs HTTP 500 = Invalid\n\nTLS Handshake Alert = Valid vs Bad Record MAC = Invalid\n\nTiming Faster response = Valid vs Slower response = Invalid\n\nThe oracle need not be explicit. Any distinguishable behavior (response time, error message content, error code) leaks one bit per query. That one bit, iterated, decrypts everything.\n\nTry Live Oracle Query\n\n## Single Byte Recovery — The Core Attack\n\nThe padding oracle attack recovers one plaintext byte at a time. Starting with the last byte: probe all 256 values of C\\[n−1\\]\\[15\\] until the oracle returns \"valid 0x01 padding\". Then: `I[15] = probe ⊕ 0x01` and `P[15] = I[15] ⊕ C[n−1][15]`.\n\n1\n\nModify last byte of C\\[n−1\\] — try all 256 values (0x00–0xFF)\n\n2\n\nSubmit modified (C\\[n−1\\]′, C\\[n\\]) to the oracle\n\n3\n\nFind the value that produces valid 0x01 padding: `AES⁻¹(C[n]) ⊕ C[n−1]′ = 0x01`\n\n4\n\nXOR with 0x01 to recover intermediate: `I[15] = probe ⊕ 0x01`\n\n5\n\nXOR I\\[15\\] with original C\\[n−1\\]\\[15\\] to recover: `P[15] = I[15] ⊕ C[n−1][15]`\n\n### Live Attack\n\nPlaintext to encrypt:  Any text — will be AES-CBC encrypted with a random key.\n\nAnimation speed: Slow (educational) Medium Fast (no delay)\n\nEncrypt Run Attack Stop\n\nClick \"Encrypt\" to begin.\n\nWhat's happening\n\nIV:\n\nCiphertext:\n\n### Byte Recovery Grid\n\nUnknown Probing Intermediate found Recovered\n\nOracle queries: **0**\n\n## Full Block Recovery\n\nExtend single-byte recovery across all 16 bytes. For byte at position *j* (counting from right), set up padding `0x(16−j)` by fixing up already-recovered bytes: `C[n−1]′[k] = I[k] ⊕ padByte` for all known *k > j*. Total: ~**O(256 × 16) = 4096 oracle calls** per block (worst case).\n\n### Live Block Recovery\n\nPlaintext: `\"Attack at dawn!!\"` (exactly 16 bytes — one block). The full block will be recovered byte-by-byte.\n\nAnimation speed: Slow (educational) Medium Fast\n\nEncrypt Target Block Run Full Block Stop\n\nClick \"Encrypt Target Block\" to start.\n\nWhat's happening\n\n### Plaintext Bytes (P\\[n\\])\n\n### Intermediate (I = AES⁻¹(C\\[n\\]))\n\nOracle queries: **0**\n\n### XOR: Intermediate ⊕ C\\[n−1\\] = Plaintext\n\n## Full Ciphertext Decryption\n\nApply block recovery to every ciphertext block. The IV is required for block 0. Attack complexity: **O(256 × 16 × n)** where *n* is the number of ciphertext blocks. Watch the total oracle query count grow — and recognize that this attack succeeds against any CBC implementation with a padding oracle.\n\n### Full Decryption Attack\n\nPlaintext to encrypt and decrypt: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. Secrets hidden in CBC ciphertext. Multiple blocks recommended — see total query count grow.\n\nSpeed: Slow (educational) Medium Fast (show full attack speed)\n\nGenerate Ciphertext Run Full Attack Stop\n\nEnter plaintext and click \"Generate Ciphertext\".\n\nWhat's happening\n\nOracle queries: **0**\n\n**Note on Block 0:** Recovering block 0 requires the IV. In most real attacks, the IV is transmitted alongside the ciphertext (TLS record header, HTTP response) — making full recovery possible. If the IV is secret, only blocks 1–n are recoverable from the ciphertext alone.\n\n## Real-World Exploits Hall of Fame\n\nThe padding oracle attack is not theoretical. It has broken production systems serving billions of users. These exploits share a common thread: CBC mode + a padding oracle = full plaintext recovery.\n\n### Why TLS 1.3 Eliminated CBC\n\nTLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018) removed all CBC cipher suites. Only AEAD cipher suites are permitted: AES-GCM, AES-CCM, and ChaCha20-Poly1305. The reason is exactly this attack: CBC with any padding oracle — whether explicit error responses or timing side-channels — is fundamentally broken. AEAD ciphers authenticate before decrypting, so no padding oracle can exist.\n\n## Defenses and Why AEAD Wins\n\nThere are several ways to stop the padding oracle attack. Only AEAD eliminates the attack class entirely — the others reduce risk but require careful implementation.\n\n### Defense 1: Encrypt-then-MAC\n\nCompute a MAC over the *ciphertext* (not the plaintext). Verify the MAC before attempting decryption. If the MAC is invalid, reject immediately — no decryption, no padding, no oracle.\n\n**Requires:** Constant-time MAC comparison. Timing differences in comparison can still leak information.\n\nEncrypt (CBC) → MAC(ciphertext) → Verify MAC first → Then decrypt\n\n### Defense 2: Constant-Time Padding Validation\n\nProcess all padding bytes in constant time, regardless of where the first invalid byte is found. Return a single valid/invalid result only after examining the entire block.\n\n**Limitation:** Eliminates timing oracle but not error-response oracles. If the system still returns different error codes, the oracle remains. Lucky Thirteen shows that even microsecond differences matter.\n\n### Defense 3: Use AEAD — The Definitive Fix Recommended\n\nAEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) integrates encryption and authentication into a single primitive. AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 check the authentication tag *before* any decryption. No padding is used (stream mode). No oracle is possible because no information is revealed on failure.\n\nCheck auth tag (first!) → Reject if invalid → Decrypt (only if valid)\n\n### AES-GCM Live Demo: Tampering Rejected\n\nEncrypt a message with AES-256-GCM (WebCrypto). Then tamper with the ciphertext and watch authentication fail immediately — with no plaintext revealed.\n\nEncrypt with AES-GCM Tamper Ciphertext\n\n### Why This Matters in 2026+\n\n**CBC is still in the wild.** Legacy systems — payment processors, medical record systems, enterprise VPNs, and embedded devices — continue to use AES-CBC. Understanding the padding oracle attack is essential for:\n\n-   Penetration testers auditing legacy protocol implementations\n-   Developers evaluating whether to migrate away from CBC\n-   Security architects designing cryptographically sound systems\n-   Anyone in a role where \"we've always done it this way\" is the threat model\n\nThe fix is always the same: **use AEAD**.\n\n### Decision Guide: CBC+MAC vs AEAD\n\nScenario\n\nCBC + Encrypt-then-MAC\n\nAEAD (AES-GCM / ChaCha20-Poly1305)\n\nNew system design\n\nNot recommended\n\n✓ Use AEAD\n\nLegacy protocol migration\n\nInterim only — constant-time MAC required\n\n✓ Target state\n\nConstrained environment (no GCM hw)\n\nAcceptable with care\n\n✓ ChaCha20-Poly1305\n\nTLS / network protocol\n\nNever (Lucky Thirteen / POODLE)\n\n✓ TLS 1.3 mandates AEAD\n\n[GitHub](https://github.com/systemslibrarian/crypto-lab-padding-oracle)\n\n\"So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all for the glory of God.\" — 1 Corinthians 10:31",

            "output": "    Nonce Guard — AES-GCM vs AES-GCM-SIV \n\n# crypto-lab-nonce-guard\n\nAES-GCM vs AES-GCM-SIV — nonce misuse resistance compared\n\n🌙\n\n[The Nonce Problem](#section-a) [Live Attack Demo](#section-b) [SIV Visualizer](#section-c) [When to Use Which](#section-d)\n\n## A — The Nonce Problem\n\n### A1 — What is a nonce?\n\nA **nonce** (number used once) in AEAD schemes is a per-encryption value that must be *unique* for every encryption under the same key. It does not need to be secret. AES-GCM uses a **96-bit nonce** (12 bytes). The security proof assumes the nonce is never reused.\n\nNIST SP 800-38D calls it an **Initialization Vector (IV)**; RFC 5116 calls it a **nonce** — same concept.\n\n96-BIT NONCE (12 BYTES)\n\n### A2 — Why nonce reuse breaks AES-GCM\n\n#### Level 1 — Keystream reuse (confidentiality failure)\n\nAES-GCM encrypts by XORing plaintext with a keystream derived from (key, nonce, counter). If the same (key, nonce) pair is used twice:\n\nC₁ = P₁ ⊕ KS  \nC₂ = P₂ ⊕ KS  \n**C₁ ⊕ C₂ = P₁ ⊕ P₂**\n\nThe attacker recovers the XOR of the two plaintexts — which often recovers both plaintexts in practice.\n\n#### Level 2 — Authentication key recovery (integrity failure)\n\nAES-GCM's authentication tag uses a polynomial hash over GF(2¹²⁸) with a key `H = AES_K(0¹²⁸)`. When the same nonce is reused, the attacker can solve for H algebraically from two (ciphertext, tag) pairs. Once H is known, the attacker can **forge valid authentication tags** for arbitrary ciphertexts — total integrity failure.\n\ntag = GHASH\\_H(AAD, C) ⊕ E\\_K(nonce ‖ 0³¹1)\n\nGHASH\\_H uses H directly, making H recovery from two equations over GF(2¹²⁸) a solvable linear algebra problem.\n\n### A3 — The SIV construction (RFC 8452)\n\nAES-GCM-SIV uses a **synthetic IV** approach:\n\n1.  Derive a message-specific authentication key and encryption key from the (key, nonce)\n2.  Compute a tag over the plaintext using **POLYVAL** (a GF(2¹²⁸) variant defined in RFC 8452)\n3.  Use that tag as the **IV for AES-CTR encryption**\n\nThe IV is derived from the plaintext itself. If the same (key, nonce) pair encrypts two *different* plaintexts, the two IVs differ. Nonce reuse degrades to leaking only whether two plaintexts were identical — not the keystream, not the authentication key.\n\n256-bit Master Key + 96-bit Nonce\n\n↓\n\nKey Derivation (AES-CTR)\n\n↓\n\n128-bit Auth Key (H)\n\n256-bit Encryption Key\n\n↓\n\nPOLYVAL(H, AAD, plaintext) ⊕ nonce → Tag\n\n↓\n\nTag (with MSB cleared) = Synthetic IV → AES-CTR Encryption\n\n## B — Live Nonce Reuse Attack Demo\n\nMessage 1 Transfer $1000 to Alice First plaintext message for encryption\n\nMessage 2 Transfer $9000 to Mallory Second plaintext message for encryption\n\n Use same nonce (attack scenario)\n\nEncrypt Both Run Attack\n\n### 🔓 AES-GCM Vulnerable\n\nClick \"Encrypt Both\" to begin\n\n### 🔒 AES-GCM-SIV Resistant\n\nClick \"Encrypt Both\" to begin\n\n## C — Synthetic IV Construction Visualizer\n\n### C1 — Key Derivation\n\nAES-GCM-SIV derives per-message keys from the master key and nonce:\n\n256-bit Master Key + 96-bit Nonce\n\n↓\n\nAES-CTR(counter=0) → 128-bit Auth Key\n\n↓\n\nAES-CTR(counter=1,2) → 256-bit Encryption Key\n\n### C2 — POLYVAL Authentication\n\nPOLYVAL is a GF(2¹²⁸) polynomial hash defined in RFC 8452, using a different field representation than GHASH. The key distinction: POLYVAL's authentication key H is **derived per-message** from the (key, nonce) pair, not fixed per-key as in AES-GCM's GHASH.\n\ntag = AES\\_K(POLYVAL(H, AAD, plaintext) ⊕ nonce) & clear MSB\n\n### C3 — Tag-as-IV (Interactive)\n\nThe 128-bit POLYVAL tag becomes the synthetic IV after clearing one bit (preventing CTR counter overflow into tag space). Changing any byte of plaintext completely changes the tag and therefore the IV.\n\nEnter a message to see the SIV tag change:  Type any message to see how the AES-GCM-SIV tag changes with each character\n\n## D — When to Use Which\n\n### D1 — Comparison Table\n\nComparison of AES-GCM and AES-GCM-SIV properties\n\nProperty\n\nAES-GCM\n\nAES-GCM-SIV\n\nStandard\n\nNIST SP 800-38D\n\nRFC 8452\n\nNonce size\n\n96 bits\n\n96 bits\n\nNonce reuse consequence\n\nKey recovery + full plaintext XOR\n\nIdentical plaintext detection only\n\nPerformance\n\n~1 pass\n\n~2 passes (extra POLYVAL)\n\nFIPS approved\n\nYes\n\nNo (as of 2024)\n\nHardware acceleration\n\nAES-NI + PCLMULQDQ\n\nAES-NI + PCLMULQDQ\n\nOnline encryption\n\nYes\n\nNo — must buffer full plaintext\n\nDeployed in\n\nTLS 1.3, QUIC, IPsec\n\nGoogle internal, QUIC experiments\n\nRecommendation\n\n✅ Nonce uniqueness guaranteed\n\n✅ Nonce uniqueness uncertain\n\n### D2 — Decision Guidance\n\n#### Scenario 1 — Single server, sequential encryption\n\nAES-GCM with a counter nonce is safe. Nonce uniqueness is easy to guarantee. No need for SIV overhead.\n\n#### Scenario 2 — Distributed system, multiple encryptors\n\nCoordinating nonces across servers is error-prone. AES-GCM-SIV degrades gracefully on accidental reuse. Use SIV or use random 96-bit nonces with AES-GCM (collision risk is low at < 2³² messages per key).\n\n#### Scenario 3 — Key wrapping and key storage\n\nAES-GCM-SIV is designed for this — short messages, repeated access to the same key, nonce coordination difficult. Google's Tink library uses it for key wrapping.\n\n### D3 — Honest Limitations of AES-GCM-SIV\n\n-   **Not FIPS-approved** — cannot use in FIPS 140-2/3 contexts\n-   **No online encryption** — must have full plaintext before starting (not suitable for streaming)\n-   **Slightly slower** — the extra POLYVAL pass costs roughly 10–20% throughput\n-   **Not yet widely deployed** — operational experience is thin compared to AES-GCM\n\nPart of the [crypto-lab](https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab/) portfolio\n\n[AES Modes](https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab-aes-modes/) [ChaCha20 Stream](https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab-chacha20-stream/) [MAC Race](https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-lab-mac-race/)\n\n*\"So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all for the glory of God.\" — 1 Corinthians 10:31*",

            "output": "Title: ECIES Hybrid Encryption Scheme | Practical Cryptography for Developers\nURL: https://cryptobook.nakov.com/asymmetric-key-ciphers/ecies-public-key-encryption\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n# ECIES Hybrid Encryption Scheme\n[...]\nA hybrid encryption scheme similar to the previously demonstrated code is standardized under the name Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme (ECIES) in many crypto standards like SECG SEC-1, ISO/IEC 18033-2, IEEE 1363a and ANSI X9.63. ECIES is a public-key authenticated encryption scheme, which works similarly to the above code examples, but uses a KDF (key-derivation function) for deriving separate MAC key and symmetric encryption key from the ECDH shared secret. It has many variants.\n[...]\nThe ECIES standard combines ECC-based asymmetric cryptography with symmetric ciphers to provide data encryption by EC private key and decryption by the corresponding EC public key. The ECIES encryption scheme uses ECC cryptography (public key cryptosystem) + key-derivation function (KDF) + symmetric encryption algorithm + MAC algorithm, combined together like it is shown on the figure below:\n[...]\nThe input of the ECIES encryption consists of recipient's public key + plain text message. The output consists of sender's ephemeral public key (ciphertext public key) + encrypted message (ciphertext + symmetric algorithm parameters) + authentication tag (MAC code):\n[...]\nThe ECIES decryption takes the output from the encryption + the recipient's private key and produces the original plaintext message or detects a problem (e.g. integrity / authentication error):\n[...]\nThe ECIES encryption scheme is a framework, not a concrete algorithm. It can be implemented by plugging different algorithms, e.g. the secp256k1 or P-521 elliptic curve for the public-key calculations + PBKDF2 or Scrypt for KDF function + AES-CTR or AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 for symmetric cipher and authentication tag + HMAC-SHA512 for MAC algorithm (in case of unauthenticated encryption).\n[...]\nIn the next section we shall demonstrate through a code example how to use ECIES in practice.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Asymmetric Key Ciphers | Practical Cryptography for Developers\nURL: https://cryptobook.nakov.com/asymmetric-key-ciphers\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThe asymmetric key cryptosystems provide key-pair generation (private + public key), encryption algorithms (asymmetric key ciphers and encryption schemes like RSA-OAEP and ECIES), digital signature algorithms (like DSA, ECDSA and EdDSA) and key exchange algorithms (like DHKE and ECDH).\n[...]\nIn the RSA system, the input message should be transformed to big integer (e.g. using OAEP padding), while in ECC the message cannot be directly encrypted and more complex encryption scheme is used, based on the elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (ECDH). It will be explained in details later in this chapter. Additionally, asymmetric ciphers are significantly slower than symmetric ciphers (e.g. the RSA encryption is 1000 times slower than AES).\n[...]\nTo overcome the above limitations and to allow encrypting messages of any size, modern cryptography uses asymmetric encryption schemes (also known as public key encryption schemes / asymmetric encryption constructions / hybrid encryption schemes), like key encapsulation mechanisms (KEM) and integrated encrypted schemes, which combine asymmetric encryption with symmetric key ciphers.\n[...]\nExamples of such asymmetric encryption schemes are: RSA-OAEP, RSA-KEM and ECIES-KEM.\n[...]\nIntegrated encryption schemes (IES) are modern public key encryption schemes, which combine symmetric ciphers, asymmetric ciphers and key-derivation algorithms to provide secure public-key based encryption (PKE). In EIS scheme asymmetric algorithms (like RSA or ECC) are used to encrypt or encapsulate a symmetric key, used later by symmetric ciphers (like AES or ChaCha20) to encrypt the input message. Some EIS schemes provide also message authentication. Examples of EIS schemes are DLIES (Discrete Logarithm Integrated Encryption Scheme) and ECIES (Elliptic Curve Integrated Encryption Scheme).\n[...]\nA key encapsulation mechanisms (KEM) are asymmetric cryptographic techniques used\n[...]\nencapsulate a secret key\n[...]\n\"ephemeral symmetric key\"),\n[...]\nusing a symmetric cryptographic\n[...]\nit with the recipient\n[...]\ns public key\n[...]\ncryptography this process is\n[...]\nkey encapsulation\".\n[...]\nThe output from a KEM-based hybrid encryption scheme consists of KEM block, holding the encapsulated encrypted symmetric key (or certain parameters used to derive it), and DEM block (data encapsulation mechanism), holding the encapsulated symmetrically-encrypted data (cipher parameters + ciphertext + optionally an authentication tag).\n[...]\nKey encapsulation mechanisms (KEMs) are used in the hybrid encryption schemes and in the integrated encryption schemes, where a random element is generated in the underlying public-key cryptosystem and a symmetric key is derived from this random element by hashing. This approach simplifies the process of combining asymmetric and symmetric encryption. Examples of modern key encapsulation mechanisms are: RSA-KEM, ECIES-KEM and PSEC-KEM.\n[...]\n- Key encapsulation (KEM) refers to public-key encryption of another key (symmetric or asymmetric). It is used for creating provably secure hybrid encryption schemes, e.g. to encrypt an AES secret key by given ECC public key.\n[...]\nis used to encrypt,\n[...]\n-protect and transport cryptographic keys.\n[...]\nas cryptographic keys, without the\n[...]\nof nonces. For details see RFC 3394\n[...]\nIn cryptography key exchange algorithms (key agreement protocols / key negotiation schemes) allow cryptographic keys to be exchanged between two parties, allowing the use of a cryptographic algorithm, in most cases symmetric encryption cipher. For example, when a laptop connects to the home WiFi router, both parties agree on a session key, used to symmetrically encrypt the network traffic between them.\n[...]\nAuthenticated key agreement schemes authenticate the identities of parties involved in the key exchange and thus prevent man-in-the-middle attacks by use of digitally signed keys (e.g. PKI certificate), password-authenticated key agreement or other method.\n\n---\n\nTitle: 6 Asymmetric encryption and hybrid encryption · Real-World Cryptography\nURL: https://livebook.manning.com/book/real-world-cryptography/chapter-6\nPublished: 2025-09-02T12:35:21.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n- Asymmetric encryption to encrypt secrets to a public key\n- Hybrid encryption to encrypt data to a public key\n- The standards for asymmetric and hybrid encryption\n[...]\nIn chapter 4, you learned about authenticated encryption, a cryptographic primitive used to encrypt data but limited by its symmetry (both sides of a connection had to share the same key). In this chapter, I’ll lift this restriction by introducing asymmetric encryption, a primitive to encrypt to someone else’s key without knowing the key. Without surprise, asymmetric encryption makes use of key pairs and encryption will use public keys instead of symmetric keys.\n[...]\nHalfway through this chapter, you will see that asymmetric encryption is limited by the size of the data it can encrypt and by the rate at which it can encrypt it. To remove this obstacle, I’ll show you how to mix asymmetric encryption with authenticated encryption to form what we call hybrid encryption. Let’s get started!\n[...]\nFor this chapter, you’ll need to have read chapter 4 on authenticated encryption and chapter 5 on key exchanges.\n[...]\nThe first step in knowing how to encrypt a message is understanding asymmetric encryption (also called public key encryption). In this section, you will learn about this cryptographic primitive and its properties. Let’s take a look at the following real-world scenario: encrypted emails.\n[...]\n## 6.2 Asymmetric encryption in practice and hybrid encryption\n[...]\n### 6.2.1 Key exchanges and key encapsulation\n[...]\n### 6.2.2 Hybrid encryption\n[...]\n## 6.3 Asymmetric encryption with RSA: The bad and the less bad\n[...]\n### 6.3.1 Textbook RSA\n[...]\n### 6.3.2 Why not to use RSA PKCS#1 v1.5\n[...]\n### 6.3.3 Asymmetric encryption with RSA-OAEP\n[...]\n## 6.4 Hybrid encryption with ECIES\n\n---\n\nTitle: Hybrid Encryption - cryptography-kotlin\nURL: https://whyoleg.github.io/cryptography-kotlin/primitives/recipes/hybrid-encryption/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: Oleg Yukhnevich\nHighlights:\n# Hybrid Encryption¶\n[...]\nThis recipe follows the pattern used in CMS EnvelopedData (RFC 5652) and HPKE (RFC 9180). Large data is encrypted with AES-GCM using a random key, then that key is encrypted with RSA-OAEP. The recipient decrypts the AES key with their RSA private key, then decrypts the data.\n[...]\nEncrypt data for a recipient:\n[...]\n```\nval rsaOaep = provider.get(RSA.OAEP)\nval recipientKeyPair = rsaOaep.keyPairGenerator(3072.bits).generateKey()\n[...]\n// --- Generate a random AES-GCM key and encrypt the data ---\nval aesGcm = provider.get(AES.GCM)\nval aesKey = aesGcm.keyGenerator().generateKey()\nval ciphertext = aesKey.cipher().encrypt(plaintext = \"Large payload...\".encodeToByteArray())\n[...]\n// --- Encode the AES key to raw bytes, then encrypt it with RSA-OAEP ---\nval aesKeyBytes = aesKey.encodeToByteArray(AES.Key.Format.RAW)\nval encryptedKey = recipientKeyPair.publicKey.encryptor().encrypt(aesKeyBytes)\n[...]\n// Send: encryptedKey + ciphertext\n[...]\nThe recipient decrypts the AES key with their RSA private key, then decrypts the data:\n[...]\n```\n// --- Decrypt the AES key with the RSA private key ---\nval decryptedKeyBytes = recipientKeyPair.privateKey.decryptor().decrypt(encryptedKey)\n\n// --- Import the AES key and decrypt the data ---\nval restoredKey = provider.get(AES.GCM)\n    .keyDecoder()\n    .decodeFromByteArray(AES.Key.Format.RAW, decryptedKeyBytes)\nval plaintext = restoredKey.cipher().decrypt(ciphertext = ciphertext)\nprintln(plaintext.decodeToString()) // \"Large payload...\"\n[...]\nRSA-OAEP can only encrypt data shorter than its key size (roughly`keyBytes - 2 * hashBytes - 2`), so it is used to encrypt the small AES key rather than the data itself.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Java – Hybrid Cryptography example\nURL: https://mkyong.com/java/java-hybrid-cryptography-example\nPublished: 2016-11-30T05:51:06.000Z\nAuthor: Marilena\nHighlights:\nHybrid Cryptography is the silver lining between safe, but slow cryptography over big data (Asymmetric Cryptography) and unsafe but fast cryptography (Symmetric Cryptography). Hybrid Cryptography combines the speed of One-Key encryption and decryption along with the security that the Public-Private Key pair provides and thus considered a highly secure type of encryption.\n[...]\nThe most common methodology for Hybrid Cryptography is to encrypt the data using a Symmetric Key which will be then encrypted with the Private Key of the sender or the Public Key of the receiver. To decrypt, the receiver, will have to first decrypt the Symmetric key using the corresponding Asymmetric Key and then use that Symmetric Key to decrypt the data they received. Barnes \n Cisco\nHighlights:\nThis document describes a scheme for hybrid public key encryption (HPKE). This scheme provides a variant of public key encryption of arbitrary-sized plaintexts for a recipient public key. It also includes three authenticated variants, including one that authenticates possession of a pre-shared key and two optional ones that authenticate possession of a key encapsulation mechanism (KEM) private key. HPKE works for any combination of an asymmetric KEM, key derivation function (KDF), and authenticated encryption with additional data (AEAD) encryption function. Some authenticated variants may not be supported by all KEMs. We provide instantiations of the scheme using widely used and efficient primitives, such as Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) key agreement, HMAC-based key derivation function (HKDF), and SHA2.¶\n[...]\nEncryption schemes that combine asymmetric and symmetric algorithms have been specified and practiced since the early days of public key cryptography, e.g., [RFC1421]. Combining the two yields the key management advantages of asymmetric cryptography and the performance benefits of symmetric cryptography. The traditional combination has been \"encrypt the symmetric key with the public key.\" \"Hybrid\" public key encryption (HPKE) schemes, specified here, take a different approach: \"generate the symmetric key and its encapsulation with the public key.\" Specifically, encrypted messages convey an encryption key encapsulated with a public key scheme, along with one or more arbitrary-sized ciphertexts encrypted using that key. This type of public key encryption has many applications in practice, including Messaging Layer Security [MLS-PROTOCOL] and TLS Encrypted ClientHello [TLS-ECH].¶\n[...]\nprovides a subset of\n[...]\nprovided by the collection of schemes above but specified with sufficient clarity that they can be interoperably implemented. This scheme provides a variant of public key encryption of\n   arbitrary-sized plaintexts for a recipient public key.  It also\n   includes a variant that authenticates possession of a pre-shared key.\n   HPKE works for any combination of an asymmetric Key Encapsulation\n   Mechanism (KEM), key derivation function (KDF), and authenticated\n   encryption with additional data (AEAD) encryption function.  We\n   provide instantiations of the scheme using widely used and efficient\n   primitives, such as Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) key\n   agreement, HMAC-based key derivation function (HKDF), and SHA2.\n[...]\n5.  Hybrid Public Key Encryption\n\n   In this section, we define a few HPKE variants.  All variants take a\n   recipient public key and a sequence of plaintexts pt and produce an\n   encapsulated secret enc and a sequence of ciphertexts ct.  These\n   outputs are constructed so that only the holder of skR can\n   decapsulate the key from enc and decrypt the ciphertexts.  All the\n   algorithms also take an info parameter that can be used to influence\n   the generation of keys (e.g., to fold in identity information) and an\n   aad parameter that provides additional authenticated data to the AEAD\n   algorithm in use.\n\n   In addition to the base case of encrypting to a public key, we\n   include a variant that authenticates possession of a pre-shared key.\n   The authenticated variant contributes additional keying material to\n   the encryption operation. The PSK also\n   improves confidentiality guarantees in certain adversary models, as\n   described in more detail in Section 9.1.  We assume that both parties\n   have been provisioned with both the PSK value psk and another byte\n   string psk_id that is used to identify which PSK should be used.\n\n   The primary difference from the base case is that the psk and psk_id\n   values are used as ikm inputs to the KDF (instead of using the empty\n   string).\n\n   The PSK MUST have at least 32 bytes of entropy and SHOULD be of\n   length Nh bytes or longer. It can also check the validity\nof additional cleartext data in apps where you need to send headers or other metadata as cleartext.\n\nHPKE optionally incorporates sender authentication, allowing the recipient to validate the authenticity of\nmessages using the sender’s public key.\n\nHPKE is described in the Internet Research Task Force (IRTF) document\nRFC 9180.\n\n## Topics\n\n### Sending and receiving messages\n\n`HPKE.Sender`\n\nA type that represents the sending side of an HPKE message exchange.\n\n`HPKE.Recipient`\n\nA type that represents the receiving side of an HPKE message exchange.\n\n### Choosing cryptographic algorithms\n\n`HPKE.Ciphersuite`\n\nCipher suites to use in hybrid public key encryption (HPKE).\n\n`HPKE.AEAD`\n\nThe authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD) algorithms to use in HPKE.\n\n`HPKE.KDF`\n\nThe key derivation functions to use in HPKE.\n\n`HPKE.KEM`\n\nThe key encapsulation mechanisms to use in HPKE.\n\n`HPKE.DHKEM`\n\nA container for Diffie-Hellman key encapsulation mechanisms (KEMs).\n\n### Handling errors\n\n`HPKE.Errors`\n\nHybrid public key encryption (HPKE) errors that CryptoKit uses.\n\n---\n\nCopyright © 2026 Apple Inc. All rights reserved. | Terms of Use | Privacy Policy\n\n---\n\nTitle: Hybrid Public Key Encryption (HPKE): Architecture, Rationale, and Practical Operation – Silastron\nURL: https://silastron.com/2026/01/07/hybrid-public-key-encryption-hpke-architecture-rationale-and-practical-operation/\nPublished: 2026-01-07T14:37:14.000Z\nAuthor: Mehrnoush\nHighlights:\nHybrid Public Key Encryption (HPKE), standardized in RFC 9180, defines a robust and interoperable framework for encrypting data to public keys using a principled hybrid construction. HPKE formalizes how public-key key establishment, symmetric key derivation, and authenticated encryption are securely composed. This document provides a structured, educational overview of HPKE’s design goals, cryptographic components, operational modes, and execution flow, enabling practitioners and researchers to develop a precise mental model of how HPKE works and why it was designed this way.\n[...]\nKE addresses these issues\n[...]\ndefining a standardized, modular hybrid encryption framework with explicit security boundaries and well-defined interfaces.\n[...]\n## 3.\n[...]\nOverview (Intuitive\n[...]\n- A Key Encapsulation Mechanism (KEM) establishes a shared secret using public-key cryptography.\n- A Key Derivation Function (KDF) expands this secret into context-specific symmetric keys.\n- An Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) algorithm encrypts and authenticates application data.\n[...]\n## 4. Cryptographic Components\n[...]\nRFC 9180 specifies Diffie–Hellman–based KEMs, including:\n[...]\n### 4.2 Key Derivation Function (KDF)\n[...]\nThe shared secret produced by the KEM is not used directly. Instead, it is processed through a KDF to derive multiple cryptographic values.\n[...]\n- incorporates the HPKE mode,\n- binds application context via the`info` parameter,\n- optionally mixes in a pre-shared key (PSK).\n[...]\n- a base\n[...]\n### 4.3 Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)\n[...]\n- confidentiality of plaintext,\n[...]\n- authentication of associated data (AAD).\n[...]\n## 5. Modes of Operation\n[...]\nHPKE defines four modes of operation, each representing a distinct trust and authentication model. The modes differ in whether they authenticate the sender, incorporate a pre-shared key (PSK), or rely solely on public-key encryption.\n[...]\n### 5.1 Base Mode (Mode 0)\n[...]\nBase mode provides unauthenticated public-key encryption.The sender encrypts data to the recipient’s public key without proving its own identity and without using any pre-shared secret.\n[...]\n### 5.2 PSK Mode (Mode 1)\n[...]\nPSK mode extends Base mode by incorporating a pre-shared key into the key schedule.\n[...]\n- Is mixed into key\n[...]\n### 5.3 Auth Mode (Mode 2)\n[...]\nAuth mode provides sender authentication using asymmetric cryptography.\n[...]\n- The sender proves possession of a private key\n- The recipient can cryptographically verify\n[...]\nsender’s identity\n[...]\n- No pre-\n[...]\nkey is required\n[...]\n### 5.4 AuthPSK Mode (Mode 3)\n[...]\nAuthPSK mode combines asymmetric sender authentication with a pre-shared key.\n[...]\n### Table 1: HPKE Modes at a Glance\n[...]\n| Mode | Authentication | PSK | Primary Use Case |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Base (0) | None | No | One-way encryption to a public identifier (e.g., submission APIs, telemetry ingestion). |\n| PSK (1) | Symmetric (PSK) | Yes | Adding a shared secret factor to base encryption for improved confidentiality. |\n| Auth (2) | Asymmetric (Sender) | No | Authenticated messaging where the receiver must verify sender identity. |\n| AuthPSK (3) | Asymmetric + PSK | Yes | Highest-assurance channels with layered trust (e\n[...]\npre-provisioned devices). |\n[...]\n## 6. Worked Example: Base Mode Execution (Mode 0)\n[...]\nThis section presents a complete HPKE exchange in Base Mode, illustrating how the cryptographic components interact in practice.To emphasize HPKE’s design philosophy, the flow is deliberately divided into two phases:\n[...]\n- Setup Phase: Public-key and key-derivation operations (KEM + KDF)\n- Encryption Phase: Symmetric data protection (AEAD)\n[...]\n### 6.1 Setup Phase (KEM + KDF)\n[...]\n### 6.2 Encryption Phase (AEAD)\n[...]\n### Key Observations\n[...]\n## 7. Encryption Context and State Management\n[...]\n## 8. Export-Only Functionality\n[...]\n## 9. Security Properties and Implementation Considerations",

ECC is widely used to perform asymmetric cryptography operations, such as to establish shared secrets or for digital signatures. However, insufficient validation of public keys and parameters is still a frequent cause of confusion, leading to serious vulnerabilities, such as leakage of secret keys, signature malleability or interoperability issues.\n[...]\nIn elliptic curve cryptography, public keys are frequently sent between parties, for example to establish shared secrets using Elliptic curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDH). The goal of public key validation is to ensure that keys are legitimate (by providing assurance that there is an existing, associated private key) and to circumvent attacks leading to the leakage of some information about the private key of a legitimate user.\n[...]\nWhat can go wrong? Since multiplying the point at infinity by any scalar results in the point at infinity, an adversary may force the result of a key agreement to be zero if the legitimate recipient fails to check that the point received is not the point at infinity. This goes against the principle of contributory behavior, where some protocols require that both parties contribute to the outcome of an operation such as a key exchange. Failure to enforce this check may have additional negative consequences in other protocols.\n[...]\nWhat can go wrong? A malicious party sending a point in the orange subgroup, for example as part of an ECDH key agreement protocol, would result in the honest party performing operations limited to that small subgroup. Thus, if the recipient of a public key failed to check that the point was in the correct subgroup, the attacker could perform a so-called small subgroup attack (also known as subgroup confinement attacks) and learn information about the legitimate party’s private key12.\n[...]\nCurve25519, proposed by Daniel J. Bernstein and specified in RFC 7748, is a popular curve which is notably used in TLS 1.3 for key agreement.\n[...]\nAlthough Curve25519 has a cofactor of 8, some functions using this curve were designed to prevent cofactor-related issues. For example, the X25519 function used to perform key agreement using Curve25519 mandates specific checks and performs key agreement using only-coordinates, such that invalid curve attacks are avoided. Additionally, the governing RFC states in Section 5 that\n[...]\nWith the popularity of Curve25519 and the desire for cryptographers to design more exotic protocols with it, the cofactor value of 8 resurfaced as a potential source of problems. Ristretto was designed as a solution to the cofactor pitfalls. Ristretto is an abstraction layer, on top of Curve25519, which essentially restricts curve points to a prime-order subgroup.\n[...]\na strong contender\n[...]\nthe secure-by\n[...]\nis the Double\n[...]\nrecently proposed by Thomas Pornin. These\n[...]\nspirit to Ristretto\n[...]\nWith the ubiquitous use of elliptic curve cryptography, failure to validate elliptic curve points can be a critical issue which is sadly still commonly uncovered during cryptography reviews. While standards and academic publications provide ample directions to correctly validate curve points, implementations still frequently fail to follow these steps. For example, a vulnerability nicknamed Curveball was reported in January 2020, which allowed attacker to perform spoofing attacks in Microsoft Windows by crafting public points. Recently, we also uncovered a critical vulnerability in a number of open-source ECDSA libraries, in which the verification function failed to check that the signature was non-zero, allowing attackers to forge signatures on arbitrary messages, see the technical advisory Arbitrary Signature Forgery in Stark Bank ECDSA Libraries.\n[...]\nby NCC Group.\n\n---\n\nTitle: ECC Encryption / Decryption | Practical Cryptography for Developers\nURL: https://cryptobook.nakov.com/asymmetric-key-ciphers/ecc-encryption-decryption\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nIn this section we shall explain how to implement elliptic-curve based public-key encryption / decryption (asymmetric encryption scheme based on ECC). The elliptic curve cryptography (ECC) does not directly provide encryption method. Let's implement a fully-functional asymmetric ECC encryption and decryption hybrid scheme. It will be based on the `brainpoolP256r1` curve and the AES-256-GCM authenticated symmetric cipher.\n[...]\nThe above example starts from generating an ECC public + private key pair for the message recipient: `pubKey` + `privKey`, using the `tinyec` library. These keys will be used to encrypt the message `msg` through the hybrid encryption scheme (asymmetric ECC + symmetric AES) and to decrypt is later back to its original form.\n[...]\nmsg` by using the\n[...]\npubKey` and we obtain as a result the following set of output: { `ciphertext`, `nonce`, `authTag`, `ciphertextPubKey` }. The `ciphertext` is obtained by the symmetric AES-GCM encryption, along with the `nonce` (random AES initialization vector) and `authTag` (the MAC code of the encrypted text, obtained by the GCM block mode). Additionally, we obtain a randomly generated ephemeral public key `ciphertextPubKey`, which will be encapsulated in the encrypted message and will be used to recover the AES symmetric key during the decryption (using the ECDH key agreement scheme, as it was show before).\n[...]\nInternally, the `encrypt_ECC(msg, pubKey)` function first generates an ephemeral ECC key-pair for the ciphertext and calculates the symmetric encryption shared ECC key `sharedECCKey = ciphertextPrivKey * pubKey`. This key is an EC point, so it is then transformed to 256-bit AES secret key (integer) though hashing the point's `x` and `y` coordinates. Finally, the AES-256-GCM cipher (from `pycryptodome`) encrypts the message by the 256-bit shared secret key `secretKey` and produces as output `ciphertext` + `nonce` + `authTag`.\n[...]\nThe `decrypt_ECC(encryptedMsg{ciphertext, nonce, authTag, ciphertextPubKey}, privKey)` function internally first calculates the symmetric encryption shared ECC key `sharedECCKey = privKey * ciphertextPubKey`. It is an EC point, so it should be first transformed to 256-bit AES secret key though hashing the point's `x` and `y` coordinates. Then the AES-256-GCM cipher is used to decrypt the `ciphertext` + `nonce` + `authTag` by the 256-bit shared secret key `secretKey`. The produced output is the original plaintext message (or an exception in case of incorrect decryption key or unmatching `authTag`).\n\n---\n\nTitle: Key Exchange in SSL/TLS: Understanding RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curves\nURL: https://community.citrix.com/tech-zone/build/tech-papers/key-exchange-in-ssl-tls/\nPublished: 2025-01-31T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n# Key Exchange in SSL/TLS: Understanding RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curves\n[...]\n* January 8Contributed By: Steven Wright\n* Followers1\n* **RSS\n* Send us your feedback\n#### Table of contents\n[...]\nModern websites depend on seamless yet robust encryption to ensure data security. Understanding how key exchanges evolved - from RSA to Diffie-Hellman to ECDHE - unlocks the complete picture of how cryptography balances performance and security.\n[...]\nBut how do your browser and the server agree on a symmetric key without exposing it to potential eavesdroppers? This article unpacks the evolution of key exchange protocols - RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and ECDHE - to explain their trade-offs between security and performance.\n[...]\n2013,\n[...]\n2048-bit certificates have remained widely used, cryptographic standards and practices have continued to evolve in response to\n[...]\nand emerging threats. However, an alternative became necessary, with 4096-bit\n[...]\nkeys being significantly more computationally intensive than their 2048-bit counterparts. ECC emerged as that alternative. Today, ECC provides the same level of security as\n[...]\nbut with significantly smaller key sizes. For example, a 256-bit ECC key is considered equivalent in strength to a 3072-bit\n[...]\nkey, though this depends on the state of cryptanalysis techniques and computing power available.\n[...]\nit lacks forward secrecy. Suppose the server's private RSA key is ever compromised, whether tomorrow or years from now. In that case, an attacker who recorded any past traffic can retroactively decrypt the key exchange, retrieve the pre-master secret, and decrypt the traffic. For this reason, RSA key exchange is discouraged in modern TLS setups, which favor ephemeral methods like ECDHE (for example, TLS 1.3 uses ECDHE as its default key exchange method). The\n[...]\nkey exchange lacks forward secrecy, a critical limitation in modern cryptography. Since the same private key is reused across all sessions, a single compromise of the key renders all previously recorded traffic vulnerable to decryption. This creates a significant risk for sensitive data in environments requiring long-term security. Historical sessions remain at risk if the primary\n[...]\nkey is stolen later\n[...]\nDiffie-Hellman Ephemeral (DHE) uses a different approach to address the lack of forward secrecy in RSA key exchange. DHE generates new, temporary key pairs for each session. This 'ephemeral' (meaning short-lived or per session) design ensures that even if a private key from one session is compromised, past and future sessions remain secure.\n[...]\n-private key-\n[...]\nfocused on securely\n[...]\nElliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral (ECDHE) involves much smaller key sizes and is orders of magnitude faster.\n[...]\nElliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral (ECDHE) is simply taking the Diffie-Hellman concept and performing it over an elliptic curve rather than using huge primes. Each party picks a random integer (the private key), multiplies a known \"base point\" on the curve by this integer to get a public point, and exchanges these public points. By combining the public point they receive with their private key, they compute a shared secret - and never actually send it over the wire. This approach is much faster than traditional DHE because ECC arithmetic on 256-bit numbers is quicker than exponentiation with 2048+ bit primes, and you still get forward secrecy via ephemeral keys.\n[...]\nLike the RSA + DHE scenario, an RSA certificate can be used with ECDHE purely for authentication (the server proves its identity with the RSA certificate, while the actual shared secret is derived via elliptic-curve DH). This makes ECC an ideal choice for modern cryptographic applications, where efficiency and security are critical (where are they not?), and ECDHE is now standard for key exchange. (For a detailed NetScaler configuration guide implementing these methods, see'Networking SSL/TLS Best Practices (Q1 2025 Edition)'.)\n\n---\n\nTitle: Cryptography Services: NCC Group\nURL: https://cryptoservices.github.io/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nNCC Group’s Cryptography Services practice is a specialized team of consultants focusing exclusively on the most challenging projects involving cryptographic primitives, protocols, implementations, systems, and applications.
