N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n## Digital Signatures Scheme\n[...]\nFollow these steps to create and verify digital signatures using RSA with SHA-1 hashing:\n[...]\n### 📝 Step 1: Enter Plaintext and Generate Hash\n[...]\nThe first step involves entering your message and creating its cryptographic hash:\n[...]\n- This hash represents a fixed-size fingerprint of your original message\n- The hash will automatically appear in the Hash output (hex) field below\n- Click the \"Generate SHA-1 Hash\" button to compute the SHA-1 hash\n- Enter your message in the Plaintext (string) field (default: \"test\")\n[...]\n### 🔑 Step 2: Input Hash to RSA\n[...]\nThe second step prepares the hash for RSA signature creation:\n[...]\n- Click \"Apply RSA Signature\" to create the digital signature using RSA encryption\n- Verify that both hash fields contain identical values\n- If needed, you can manually copy the hash value from Step 1\n- The system automatically copies the generated hash to the Input to RSA (hex) field\n[...]\n### ✅ Step 3: View Digital Signature Results\n[...]\nThe third step displays your digital signature in multiple formats:\n[...]\n- Digital Signature Summary: A comprehensive table showing all parameters and results\n- Status: Shows success confirmation with processing details or error messages\n- Digital Signature (base64): The same signature in base64 encoding for easy transmission\n- Digital Signature (hex): The signature in hexadecimal format for technical analysis\n[...]\n### ⚙️ Step 4: Select RSA Public Key\n[...]\nThe fourth step involves configuring the RSA cryptographic parameters:\n[...]\nKey Size Selection: Choose from four available options:\n[...]\n- Load 512-bit Key (e=3): 512-bit key with small exponent 3 (fastest, least secure)\n- Load 512-bit Key (e=F4): Smaller 512-bit key with exponent 65537 (educational use)\n- Load 1024-bit Key (e=3): 1024-bit key with small exponent 3 (faster)\n- Load 1024-bit Key (e=F4): Standard 1024-bit key with exponent 65537 (recommended)\n[...]\n### 🔍 Understanding the Digital Signature Process\n[...]\n#### Cryptographic Workflow:\n[...]\n1. Integrity Assurance: Any message modification will result in signature verification failure\n2. Public Key Verification: Others can verify authenticity using the corresponding public key\n3. Private Key Signing: RSA encrypts the hash with the private key to create the signature\n4. Message Hashing: SHA-1 creates a 160-bit digest of any input message\n[...]\n- Non-repudiation: Prevents denial of signature creation\n- Integrity: Detects any message tampering\n- Authentication: Confirms the signer's identity\n[...]\n### 🔧 Troubleshooting Common Issues\n[...]\n| Issue | Solution |\n| --- | --- |\n| \"Invalid RSA public key\" | Select a key size in Step 4 before applying RSA signature |\n| \"Hash and RSA input mismatch\" | Ensure both hash fields contain identical values |\n| \"No hash generated\" | Click the \"Generate SHA-1 Hash\" button in Step 1 first |\n| Empty signature results | Verify all previous steps are completed in order |\n| Mobile display issues | Use landscape orientation or access from desktop for best experience |\n\n---\n\nTitle: Digital Signatures Guide - How They Work\nURL: https://fixmycert.com/guides/digital-signatures\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: FixMyCert\nHighlights:\nDigital Signatures Guide - How They Work\n[...]\nBack to Interactive Demo\n[...]\nSignatures & VerificationCryptography\n[...]\n# Digital Signatures: Complete Guide\n[...]\nUnderstand how digital signatures prove authenticity and integrity using cryptographic hashing and asymmetric key pairs.\n[...]\nTry the Interactive Demo\n[...]\nA digital signature is a cryptographic proof that a message came from a specific sender and hasn't been modified. It's the digital equivalent of a handwritten signature, but far more secure.\n[...]\nOnly the owner of the private key can create a valid signature, but anyone with the public key can verify it.\n[...]\n## How Digital Signing Works\n[...]\nDigital signatures combine two cryptographic operations: hashing and asymmetric encryption. Here's the step-by-step process:\n[...]\nThe sender encrypts the hash using their private key. This encrypted hash IS the digital signature. Only the private key holder can create this specific signature.\n[...]\nThe original message and the signature are sent together. The message is NOT encrypted— anyone can read it. The signature only proves who sent it and that it wasn't modified.\n[...]\n#### Step 4: Verify with Public Key\n[...]\nThe receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's public key to get the original hash. They also hash the received message independently. If both hashes match, the signature is valid.\n[...]\n- ✓ Authenticity: The message came from the private key holder\n- ✓ Integrity: The message wasn't modified in transit\n[...]\n## Private Keys in Signing\n[...]\nThe private key is what makes a digital signature unique to its owner. It's the secret component that proves identity.\n[...]\n#### Public Key\n[...]\n- • Freely distributed to anyone\n- • Used to VERIFY signatures\n- • Embedded in certificates\n- • Published in directories/websites\n[...]\n#### If Your Private Key is Compromised\n[...]\nAnyone with your private key can sign documents as you. Immediately revoke any associated certificates, generate a new key pair, and notify anyone who might verify your signatures.\n[...]\n## Signature Verification\n[...]\nVerification is the reverse of signing. The receiver uses the sender's public key to check if the signature is authentic.\n[...]\n### Verification Steps\n[...]\n1. 1. Receive the message and the signature\n2. 2. Obtain the sender's public key (from certificate or key server)\n3. 3. Decrypt the signature using the public key → Original hash\n4. 4. Hash the received message independently → Calculated hash\n5. 5. Compare the two hashes\n6. 6. If hashes match: Signature is VALID\n7. 7. If hashes differ: Signature is INVALID\n[...]\n#### Wrong Public Key\n[...]\nThe signature was created with a different private key than the public key you're using. Ensure you have the correct sender's key.\n[...]\nWho can read? | Anyone (\n[...]\nnot hidden) | Only key holder |\n[...]\nPublic key (\n[...]\nVerify/Decrypt with | Public key | Private key |\n| Who can verify? | Anyone with public key | N\n[...]\nPro tip: You can combine both! Sign-then-encrypt: First sign the message (proves sender), then encrypt the message+signature (hides content). This provides both authenticity AND confidentiality.\n[...]\nOpenSSL Examples\n[...]\n### Verify a Signature\n[...]\n```\n# Verify signature using public key\nopenssl dgst -sha256 -verify public_key.pem -signature signature.bin document.pdf\n\n# Output if valid:   Verified OK\n# Output if invalid: Verification Failure\n```\n[...]\n# Verify ECDSA signature\nopenssl dgst -sha256 -verify ec_public.pem -signature ec_signature.bin document.pdf\n[...]\n### Can someone forge my signature if they have my public key?\n[...]\nNo. The public key can only VERIFY signatures, not create them. Creating a valid signature requires the private key. This is the fundamental asymmetry that makes digital signatures secure.\n[...]\n## Ready to see it in action?\n[...]\nWatch the signing and verification process step-by-step in our interactive demo.\n[...]\nTry the Digital Signatures Demo\n\n---\n\nTitle: Lab-06: PQC Code Signing | QPKI\nURL: https://qpki.io/qlab/journey/06-code-signing/readme/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nmust remain valid for years. PQC ensures they can’t be forged by future quantum computers\n[...]\n## The Solution: Code Signing\n[...]\n```\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐│                                                                  ││  WITH CODE SIGNING                                               ││                                                                  ││\n[...]\n││        │                                                         ││        │  1. Signs firmware.bin with ML-DSA                      ││        ▼                                                         ││    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                          ││    │  firmware.bin                   │                          ││    │  + firmware.bin.p7s (signature\n[...]\n│                          ││\n[...]\n+ signing.crt (certificate)    │                          │\n[...]\n└─────────────────────────────────┘                          ││        │                                                         ││        ▼                                                         ││\n[...]\n┌──────────\n[...]\n2. Verifies the signature\n[...]\n✓ Hash matches\n[...]\n││\n[...]\n✓ Signature valid                              ││    │          │  ✓ Certificate in the chain                     ││    │          │                                                  ││    │\n[...]\nauthorized                      │\n[...]\nis modified:\n[...]\n❌ Hash doesn\n[...]\nt match →\n[...]\ninvalid → RE\n[...]\n## What We\n[...]\n1. What’s Next?\n2. Create a Code Signing CA (ML-DSA-65) 1b. Issue a code signing certificate\n3. Sign a firmware binary (CMS/PKCS#7) 2b. Verify the signature (VALID)\n4. Tamper with the binary and verify again (INVALID)\n[...]\n## The Commands\n[...]\n### Step 1: Create Code Signing CA\n[...]\n```\n# Create a PQC CA for code signingqpki ca init --profile profiles/pqc-ca.yaml \\    --var cn=\"Code Signing CA\" \\    --ca-dir output/code-caqpki ca export --ca-dir output/code-ca --out output/code-ca/ca.crt\n[...]\n### Step 1b: Issue Code Signing Certificate\n[...]\n```\n# Generate key and CSRqpki csr gen --algorithm ml-dsa-65 \\    --keyout output/code-signing.key \\    --cn \"ACME Software\" \\    --out output/code-signing.csrqpki cert issue --ca-dir output/code-ca \\    --profile profiles/pqc-code-signing.yaml \\    --csr output/code-signing.csr \\    --out output/code-signing.crt\n```\n[...]\n### Step 2: Sign a Binary\n[...]\n```\n# Create a test firmwaredd if=/dev/urandom of=output/firmware.bin bs=1024 count=100qpki cms sign --data output/firmware.bin \\    --cert output/code-signing.crt \\    --key output/code-signing.key \\    --out output/firmware.p7s\n```\n[...]\n### Step 2b: Verify the Signature\n[...]\n```\n# Verify: 1) hash matches 2) signature valid 3) cert chain trustedqpki cms verify output/firmware.p7s \\    --data output/firmware.bin \\    --ca output/code-ca/ca.crt# Result: VALID\n```\n[...]\n### Step 3: Tamper and Verify Again\n[...]\n```\n# Any modification changes the hash → signature becomes invalidecho \"MALWARE\" >> output/firmware.binqpki cms verify output/firmware.p7s \\    --data output/firmware.bin \\    --ca output/code-ca/ca.crt# Result: INVALID - hash mismatch\n```\n[...]\n## Certificate Extensions\n[...]\n| Extension | Value | Purpose |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Extended Key Usage | `codeSigning` | Limits certificate use |\n| Key Usage | `digitalSignature` | Signing operations only |\n| Basic Constraints | `CA: false` | End-entity certificate |\n\n---\n\nTitle: Interactive PKI Demos - This lesson is meant for developers who wish to incorporate security functionality into their programs, including cryptography services.\n[...]\nThis lesson demonstrates the use of the JDK Security API with respect to signing documents. The lesson shows what one program, executed by the person who has the original document, would do to generate keys, generate a digital signature for the document using the private key, and export the public key and the signature to files.\n[...]\nThen it shows an example of another program, executed by the receiver of the document, signature, and public key. It shows how the program could import the public key and verify the authenticity of the signature. The lesson also discusses and demonstrates possible alternative approaches and methods of supplying and importing keys, including in certificates.\n[...]\nIn this lesson you create two basic applications, one for the digital signature generation and the other for the verification. This is followed by a discussion and demonstration of potential enhancements. The lesson contains three sections.\n[...]\nGenerating a Digital Signature shows using the API to generate keys and a digital signature for data using the private key and to export the public key and the signature to files. The application gets the data file name from the command line.\n[...]\nVerifying a Digital Signature shows using the API to import a public key and a signature that is alleged to be the signature of a specified data file and to verify the authenticity of the signature. The data, public key, and signature file names are specified on the command line.\n[...]\nWeaknesses and Alternatives discusses potential weaknesses of the approach used by the basic programs. It then presents and demonstrates possible alternative approaches and methods of supplying and importing keys, including the use of files containing encoded key bytes and the use of certificates containing public keys.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Exercise 3 (Sign and Verify) - Hyper Protect Virtual Servers (HPVS) Hosted Trial\nURL: https://ibm-hyper-protect.github.io/hyper-protect-virtual-servers-hosted-trial/grep11/lab-exercise3/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nExercise 3 (Sign and Verify) - Hyper Protect Virtual Servers (HPVS) Hosted Trial\n[...]\n# Exercise 3 - Sign and Verify Data¶\n[...]\nExercise three uses public key cryptography to demonstrate signing of data and then verifying the signature.\n[...]\nThese key pairs can be used for encryption and decryption, as well as for creating and verifying digital signatures.\n[...]\nPublic key cryptography and digital signatures\n[...]\nYour private key can be used to allow you to digitally \"sign\" data, which is an attestation that you have seen the data that you have signed, analagous to your signing by pen a letter or contract on a piece of paper.\n[...]\nThere are different digital signature algorithms, but in general, they follow the following pattern. Assume you want to digitally sign some data you send to somebody:\n[...]\n4. The recipient calculates the digest, or hash, of the data, using the same algorithm that you used in step 1. If the data has remained intact in transit, they will calculate the same hash that you did in step 1.\n[...]\n5. They then decrypt your signature (from step 2) with your public key. This retrieves in plaintext the hash you signed in step 2.\n[...]\n6. If the hash they calculated in step 4 matches the hash that you encrypted with your private key in step 2 (and retrieved by them in step 5), then it is proof that you have seen the data they have received.\n[...]\nAlso, observe that the validity of this digital signature is independent of whether or not the data that you send is plaintext or ciphertext.\n[...]\nEP11 supports both RSA and elliptic curve cryptography, as you saw in the GREP11 sample. This exercise will dig a little deeper into elliptic curves.\n[...]\n## Run Exercise 3¶\n[...]\nRun the third exercise:\n[...]\n```\n./lab --ex3\n\n```\n[...]\nThe first line of the output shows the Object ID of the curve that was used. These Object IDs are assigned by standards bodies. The Object ID used here is`1.3.132.0.35`. Stick 1.3.132.0.35 in your favorite Google machine and see what you find. Or take my word for it that this identifies the secp521r1 curve.\n[...]\nThe second line of the output shows ASN.1 BER encoding of 1.3.132.0.35, which is 6 5 43 129 4 0 35. If you were to look up how ASN.1 BER encoding works, your first question would be, \"Why?\". Don't go there now. But I bring it up because you will notice that a few lines down in the output, in the key pair public key blob section, you'll see that encoded string, 6 5 43 129 4 0 35, about 14 bytes from the beginning. In fact, while the whole point of using the GREP11 server and the Crypto Express 7S cards is to keep secrets from an adversary, the information shown in the key pair public key blob section is \"in the clear\"- you could give this to your adversary without fear of having your secrets compromised.\n[...]\nSince the PUBLIC KEY is, by definition, shareable with the world, we can use the common`openssl` utility to look at it.\n[...]\nTry it like this:\n\nCopy the lines from near the bottom of your output that have your public key , including the BEGIN PUBLIC KEY and END PUBLIC KEY header and footer lines, and the dashes, into your clipboard\n\nIn a terminal window, type`echo \"`(that is a single pair of double-quotes) and then paste your clipboard contents onto the command line\n\nType`\" | openssl ec -in - -pubin -noout -text` and then press Enter\n[...]\nIf you are a Linux guru you could create a file with vi and copy the key into the file, and then provide that as input to the openssl command, e.g.,`openssl ec -in baz -pubin -noout -text` if baz was the file you created.\n[...]\nIn the exercise output I first printed the public key as an array of decimal numbers (worthless!), then I printed it in PEM-encoded format (boring!) but now you see the key in hexadecimal format (that's incredible!!). You also get the added bonus of knowing it's a legit public key since the`openssl` utility made sense of it.\n[...]\nTry using a different curve:\n[...]\n```\n./lab --ex3 --curve P224\n\n```",

The CA verifies your identity or organization before issuing the certificate.\n[...]\nCompile your application, create installers, or package your scripts. The signing happens AFTER the build is complete.\n[...]\nThe signing tool calculates a cryptographic hash (SHA-256) of your entire executable. This creates a unique fingerprint of your software.\n[...]\nThe hash is encrypted with your private key. This encrypted hash, along with your certificate, is embedded in the executable (or attached as a separate file).\n[...]\n#### Step 5: Add Timestamp\n[...]\nA timestamp from a trusted TSA (Timestamp Authority) proves when the signing occurred. This keeps signatures valid even after the certificate expires.\n[...]\nUpload your signed software to distribution channels. The signature travels with the software and can be verified by any user's operating system.\n[...]\n- • Private key on hardware token (USB)\n- • Immediate SmartScreen reputation\n- • No initial warnings\n- • Required for Windows kernel drivers\n[...]\n## Platform-Specific Requirements\n[...]\n#### Windows (Authenticode)\n[...]\n- • Uses Microsoft Authenticode technology\n- • SmartScreen filter checks reputation\n- • Kernel drivers require EV + Microsoft attestation\n- • Supports .exe, .dll, .sys, .msi, .cab, PowerShell scripts\n[...]\n## How Verification Works\n[...]\nWhen a user runs signed software, the operating system automatically verifies the signature.\n[...]\n1. 1. OS extracts the signature and certificate from the executable\n2. 2. Validates certificate chain up to a trusted Root CA\n3. 3. Checks certificate is not expired or revoked\n4. 4. Calculates fresh hash of the executable\n5. 5. Decrypts signature hash using publisher's public key\n6. 6. Compares calculated hash with decrypted hash\n7. 7. If match: Publisher verified, code untampered\n[...]\n### Verification Outcomes\n[...]\n#### Valid Signature\n[...]\nUser sees publisher name, reduced warnings, and can trust the software identity.\n[...]\nUser sees \"Unknown Publisher\" warning. On macOS, app may be blocked entirely.\n[...]\nTimestamp proves signing happened when certificate was valid. Software remains trusted indefinitely (as long as the timestamp is valid).\n[...]\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| signtool | Windows\n[...]\nAuthenticode signing\n[...]\n.dll,\n[...]\n.) |\n| codesign |\n[...]\n| Apple code signing |\n[...]\ngpg |\n[...]\nosslsigncode\n[...]\n### Verify a Signed File\n[...]\nsigntool\n[...]\n## See it in action\n[...]\nWatch how software gets signed and verified step-by-step in our interactive demo.\n[...]\nTry the Code Signing Demo\n\n---\n\nTitle: CA Hierarchy Demo - Root & Intermediate CAs\nURL: https://fixmycert.com/demos/ca-hierarchy\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: FixMyCert\nHighlights:\nCA Hierarchy Demo - Root & Intermediate CAs\n[...]\n# Certificate Authority Hierarchy\n[...]\nVisualize the CA trust chain from root to intermediate to end-entity certificates. Verify the status of any SSL certificate in real-time with precision and speed.\n[...]\nThe Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) represents the modern standard for maintaining trust on the internet. Defined in IETF RFC 6960, it is a protocol used by web browsers and other clients to determine the current status of a digital certificate without requiring the download of a massive list of revoked items.\n[...]\nUnlike its predecessor, the Certificate Revocation List (CRL), which forced clients to download a bulk list of every revoked certificate ever issued by a CA, OCSP allows for a surgical, real-time query about a single certificate. This efficiency is critical for the modern web, where speed and security must coexist.\n[...]\nSimply type the domain name (e.g.,`example.com`) you wish to check. Our tool looks up the public certificate automatically.\n[...]\nWe perform a live handshake, extract the AIA extension, and contact the Issuer's OCSP responder.\n[...]\nView the raw response and status code. Look for our Advanced CA Matcher integration if you need to verify the chain specifically.\n[...]\nOne advanced feature you should be aware of is OCSP Stapling. In a standard OCSP check, the browser contacts the CA. This leaks user browsing habits to the CA (privacy concern) and relies on the CA's server uptime.\n[...]\nWith OCSP Stapling, your web server caches the OCSP response and \"staples\" it to the TLS handshake. The browser gets the certificate and the proof of validity in one go. It's faster and more private. Learn more at Let's Encrypt Documentation.\n[...]\nWhile both mechanisms serve to invalidate compromised certificates, OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) offers a significant performance and bandwidth advantage over CRLs (Certificate Revocation Lists).\n[...]\n- Real-Time Granularity: OCSP checks the status of a specific certificate at the moment of the handshake, whereas CRLs are large, periodically updated lists that can be hours or days old.\n- Bandwidth Efficiency: A CRL download can be several megabytes (impacting network metrics), blocking the page load until complete. An OCSP request is a lightweight (KB-sized) query.\n[...]\nOCSP Stapling (defined in RFC 6066) resolves two critical flaws in standard OCSP checks: privacy leaks and dependency on CA servers.\n[...]\nIn standard OCSP, the browser contacts the CA directly, revealing who is visiting your site. Stapling eliminates this by having the server fetch the response.\n[...]\nThe server caches the signed OCSP response and \"staples\" it to the TLS handshake. This saves the client an extra DNS lookup and round-trip.\n[...]\nYes, the Must-Staple extension (OID`1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.24`) is a certificate flag that tells browsers: \"If you don't receive a valid stapled OCSP response during the handshake, hard-fail the connection.\"\n[...]\nGoogle Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers moved away from live, blocking OCSP checks due to latency and privacy concerns.\n[...]\nInstead, they rely on a proprietary mechanism called CRLSets. This is a curated list of high-value revoked certificates pushed directly to the browser via updates. While faster, it is not comprehensive. This is why server-side OCSP Stapling is crucial—it brings back the comprehensive check without the browser penalty.\n[...]\nYes, system administrators can use the`openssl` command-line tool for diagnostics. A typical command looks like this:\n[...]\n`openssl ocsp -issuer chain.pem -cert cert.pem -text -url http://ocsp.example.com`\n[...]\nThis requires you to have the intermediate chain file (`chain.pem`) and the leaf certificate (`cert.pem`) saved locally. Our online tool simplifies this by extracting these automatically from the live server.\n[...]\nThis can add anywhere\n\n---\n\nTitle: Interactive PKI Demos - Hands-On SSL/TLS Learning\nURL: https://fixmycert.com/demos\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: FixMyCert\nHighlights:\nDeep dive into certificate revocation: CRL, OCSP, OCSP Stapling, and why browsers handle revocation checking differently.\n[...]\nCompare certificate revocation methods: CRL, OCSP, and OCSP Stapling. Walfield\nHighlights:\nThe web of trust is a flexible, decentralized trust model created for PGP. PGP and GnuPG include implementations of the web of trust, and OpenPGP defines a number of authentication mechanisms that form the basis of both implementations.¶\n[...]\nThis draft defines the semantics of the web of trust as implemented by Sequoia. Sequoia models the web of trust as a flow network, and authentication as a maximum flow problem. Although its semantics differ from both PGP's and GnuPG's semantics, in practice, it is largely compatible with both implementations.¶\n[...]\nevery user has their\n[...]\nroots, and certifications may not be public\n[...]\n, authentication in the web of\n[...]\na certification network\n[...]\nis available locally,\n[...]\nfinding a valid\n[...]\nuser's trust roots to the\n[...]\nThis draft specifies a path finding algorithm for a web of trust using the mechanisms specified by [RFC4880]. Insofar as authentication mechanisms are specified by [RFC4880], they are used accordingly in this draft. [RFC4880], however, leaves many details unspecified including but not limited to: how to handle different Trust Signatures by the same issuer on multiple User IDs on the same certificate; the semantics of a Trust Signature on a third-party direct-key signature; and whether regular expressions need to match certifications of trusted introducers. This draft fills in the missing details.¶\n[...]\nUsing this model, authenticating a binding is a question of finding a flow from a set of trust roots to the binding with sufficient capacity. Unfortunately, OpenPGP certifications can impose constraints on the rest of the path. This means that most path finding algorithms cannot be used as-is. This draft describes how to use a variant of Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm to do path finding in this type of network.¶\n[...]\nsemantics that this\n[...]\nassigns to these mechanisms.\n[...]\nThe`trust amount` parameter is controlled by the Trust Signature subpacket. It is the degree to which the issuer of a certification is convinced that the binding is correct. This can vary from 0 to 255. Values that are 120 or larger mean that the issuer is fully convinced. Traditionally, an issuer uses 60 to indicate that they are partially (aka marginally) convinced, however, any value between 1 and 119 can be used. A value of 0 means that the target should not be considered as certified. A certification whose trust amount is 0 should not be ignored: it overrides earlier certifications.\n[...]\nIt is used to indicate that\n[...]\ncertification's target should be considered a trusted introducer.\n[...]\nThe regular expression parameter controls the scope of a delegation. A certification can include zero or more regular expressions. If it includes at least one regular expression, then at least one of them MUST match the User ID of the binding that is being authenticated for the path to be valid. A regular expression does not need to match intermediate trusted introducers.¶\n[...]\nAuthenticating a binding is a two-phase process. First, a network is built. Then, one or more paths starting at the trust roots and ending at the binding are located in the network.¶\n[...]\nTo authenticate a binding, it is necessary to find one or more valid paths from the roots to the binding in the network.¶\n[...]\nA path is valid if it starts from a trust root, ends at the target certificate, the last edge is a certification over the target User ID, all certificates, certifications, and the target User ID are in scope (that is, any trust depth parameters are respected, and for each edge that has regular expressions, at last one regular expression matches the target User ID), and the target User ID is not revoked.¶\n[...]\nA binding is fully authenticated if the trust amount of the valid paths is at least 120. It is partially authenticated if the trust amount is between 1 and 119.¶\n\n---\n\nTitle: yarmo/opsv: OPSV has been integrated in Keyoxide and is no longer updated - Codeberg.org\nURL: https://codeberg.org/yarmo/opsv\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: yarmo\nHighlights:\n# Open PGP Signature Verification (OPSV)\n[...]\nOPSV is a FOSS solution for easy PGP signature verification.\n[...]\nOPSV is integrated in the larger keyoxide project and will therefore no longer be updated.\n[...]\nThis project uses openpgp.js loaded in the browser, meaning all processing is done on the device itself and no data is ever sent to the server. It supports loading public keys directly through:\n[...]\n1. plaintext input\n2. web key directory (WKD)\n3. HTTP Keyserver Protocol (HKP).\n[...]\nOPSV will always use the first input method it detects in the order described above.\n[...]\nIt's also possible to not provide a public key. Read more about this in\n[...]\n`Using no public key at all` section below.\n[...]\nVisit https://opsv.foss.guru/. On this website, you can enter a signed message (see example below) and any of the three supported public key inputs to verify that the owner of that public key was indeed the person to have signed that message.\n[...]\nUse this as \"Signature\" on OPSV.\n[...]\n### Using plaintext public key\n[...]\nNow, let's check the signature. Go to my personal website and copy-paste the \"plaintext\" key in the \"Public Key (1: plaintext)\" field.\n[...]\n### Using web key directory (WKD)\n[...]\nRemove the contents from the \"Public Key (1: plaintext)\" field. Now, in the \"Public Key (2: web key directory)\", write`yarmo@yarmo.eu` and verify the signature again. It is still verified. Try using`jane@doe.org` or any other input, it won't verify.\n[...]\n### Using HTTP Keyserver Protocol (HKP)\n[...]\nRemove the contents from the \"Public Key (2: web key directory)\" field. I uploaded my keys to the https://keys.openpgp.org/ HKP server, which is the default server used by OPSV. All you need to do is once again go to my personal website and copy-paste the \"Fingerprint\" in the \"Public Key (3: HKP)\" field (the second field!). Still verified!\n[...]\n### Using no public key at all\n[...]\nverifying the signature against?\n[...]\nPGP signatures can contain the`userId` of the signer. If OPSV finds a`userId`, it will use it to perform a HKP lookup.\n[...]\nRemove the contents from the \"Public Key (3: HKP)\" field. It again verifies BUT against the information contained within the signature itself. You should carefully check the information OPSV returns. In this case, the authenticity is confirmed because the`userId`(yarmo@yarmo.eu) matches the one I use.\n[...]\nOnce again, the signature verifies. And again, it only verifies against the information contained within itself so that doesn't prove anything about its authenticity. Anyone can write this and the signature will return verified.\n[...]\nExcept now, there is no`userId` for easy manual verification. So, you need to either take the`keyId` or the`fingerprint` and find some other way of verifying it, for example by contacting the person who supposedly wrote the message.\n[...]\nIn my case, you can simply visit my personal website and compare the`fingerprint`.\n[...]\nGiven the wording of the statement, you naturally doubt the origin of it being me. You run it through OPSV and indeed, this is not what I wrote!\n\n---\n\nTitle: keyoxide/keyoxide-web: Verifying online identity with cryptography - Codeberg.org\nURL: https://codeberg.org/keyoxide/web\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: keyoxide\nHighlights:\nkeyoxide/keyoxide-web: Verifying online identity with cryptography - Codeberg.org\n[...]\nVerifying online identity with cryptography https://keyoxide.org\n[...]\ndecentralized identity keyoxide nodejs\n[...]\n# keyoxide-web\n[...]\n`keyoxide-web` is the web client that powers keyoxide.org and which you can freely host on your own infrastructure.\n[...]\nSelf-hosting Keyoxide is an important aspect of the project. You see at a glance if they are who they claim to be.\n[...]\nSigil ties your website to an identity through a single cryptographic file. This file is pulled down by others to verify that you are who you really claim to be.\n[...]\nThere's no Sigil Inc. deciding who's trustworthy. The system builds on GPG's existing web of trust and WebAuthn, both open standards. Verification is done entirely client-side. We don't see your browsing data.\n[...]\nSigil uses GPG and webauthn as its backbone. These have been battle tested over centuries and provide a perfect basis for establishing trust.\n[...]\nwebsite is run by a human. Sigil allows agents to maintain a single identity across the various sites they may produce. Rich metadata allows for agents to define all their most important characteristics, including tying themselves to a human owner.\n[...]\npress \"Register a Bot\" in Sigil.\n[...]\nWhen verified users vouch for each other through GPG signatures, trust propagates through the network. A vouch from a high-trust user carries weight. Over time, transitive trust makes the network grow stronger.\n[...]\n## Trust is earned, not declared.\n[...]\nAutomated verification is robust, but never enough. Your score is accumulated through the amount of checks you complete as well as community vouches.\n[...]\nUp to 25 points if a proof of key ownership is available.\n[...]\nHuman proof with attestation\n[...]\nUp to 50 points for a valid webauthn attestation. Quick ceremonies score higher, indicating no tampering.\n[...]\nUp to 25 or more points from the GPG web of trust. High-trust users can vouch for the identities of others.\n[...]\nNowadays, telling apart what's human and what's agentic is critical. Sigil doesn't make you sign up for anything, it doesn't require any custom infrastructure. Just place a file on your website and that's it.\n[...]\n### Sign a challenge with your PGP key\n[...]\nSigil uses PGP keys as digital certificates of your identity. You create a signature using this key and the result gets placed on your site.\n[...]\n### Prove you're physically there\n[...]\nA WebAuthn ceremony confirms that someone with real hardware is present. The signed hardware attestation gets bundled with the challenge, linking your physical presence to the domain.\n[...]\n### Verification is Automatic\n[...]\nWhen someone with Sigil visits your site, the signature is pulled down, checked and validated. A trust score is displayed using local computation only. No middlemen, no accounts.\n[...]\nIf you operate a crawler, a monitoring service, or any automated system with a web presence, Sigil lets you declare that openly. Skip the WebAuthn step and produce a signed bot manifest instead.\n[...]\nA valid bot manifest with a matching GPG signature earns a full 100/100 bot trust score. It's displayed differently in the extension so visitors can tell it's an automated service and not a human.\n\n---\n\nTitle: PGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys (Client‑Side)  – Free Tool | 8gwifi.org\nURL: https://8gwifi.org/pgp-suite.jsp\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: Anish Nath\nHighlights:\nPGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys (Client‑Side) – Free Tool | 8gwifi.org \n\n \n\n---\n\n \n\n \n\n# PGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys\n\nCreate and verify signatures, and generate keys — all client‑side\n\n---\n\nPrivate: All signing happens locally using OpenPGP.js v5. Verify the fingerprint over a trusted channel.\n- Signing: Proves author and protects from tampering; does not hide content.\n- Encryption: Hides content; does not prove author unless also signed.\n- Types: Cleartext signatures (inline), Detached signatures (.asc/.sig), Attached/opaque signatures.\n- Formats: .asc (armored text), .pgp/.gpg (binary).\n- Trust: Direct trust, Web of Trust, WKD/HKP discovery.\n- Key Hygiene: Use strong passphrases, keep revocation cert offline, rotate/expire keys, prefer Ed25519 for signing.\n[...]\nCommon issues: invalid signature (wrong key/tampered), wrong\n[...]\n, mixed line-endings.\n[...]\n- Author: Anish Nath(Security Engineer)\n- Library: OpenPGP.js v5.11.2 (client-side only)\n- Data handling: Keys and files never leave your device\n- Updated: 2025-11-25\n[...]\n- Detached signature does not modify the original file.\n- Share both the file and the signature with the recipient.\n- Recipients can verify with File Signature Verify.\n\n \n\n \n\n##### Verify Cleartext Signed Message\n[...]\nVerify Message Signature\n\n \n\n##### Verify File with Detached Signature\n[...]\n##### Signer Public Key\n\n—\n[...]\nVerify Detached Signature\n[...]\n##### Verification Tips\n[...]\n- Always confirm the public key fingerprint via trusted channels.\n- Cleartext verification extracts the original message if valid.\n- Detached signatures require both original file and signature.\n\n \n\n#####\n[...]\nPrivacy Guarantee: Private keys you enter or generate are never stored on our servers. A: Cleartext signatures keep the message human‑readable with an inline signature block; detached signatures are separate .asc/.sig files used to verify any file without changing it.\n[...]\nQ: How do I verify a signed message? Scanner](InspectJSFunctions.jsp) [Subdomain Finder](subdomain.jsp)\n    \n\n# PGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys\n\nCreate and verify signatures, and generate keys — all client‑side\n\n---\n\n**Private:** All signing happens locally using OpenPGP.js v5. Your private key and passphrase never leave your device.\n\nNeed verification? Use [PGP File Signature Verification](pgpfileverify.jsp) or the main [Signature Verifier](PGPFunctionality?invalidate=yes).\n\n-   [Sign Message (Cleartext)](#tab-message)\n-   [Sign File (Detached)](#tab-file)\n-   [Verify Signatures](#tab-verify)\n-   [Generate Keys](#tab-generate)\n\n##### Message\n\n##### PGP Private Key\n\n—\n\nKey is processed locally and never uploaded.\n\n##### Passphrase\n\nSign Message Demo: Sign & Verify\n\n##### Signed Message\n\nCopy Download .asc Use to Verify\n\n##### Tips\n\n-   Use a long, unique passphrase.\n-   Share only the signed output; keep your private key secret.\n-   Verify here: [Verify Signatures](#tab-verify).\n\n##### PGP Overview\n\n**PGP** (RFC 4880) provides confidentiality (encryption), authenticity (signatures), and integrity.\n\n-   **Keys:** Public (share) + Private (keep secret). Verify the *fingerprint* over a trusted channel.\n-   **Signing:** Proves author and protects from tampering; does not hide content.\n-   **Encryption:** Hides content; does not prove author unless also signed.\n-   **Types:** Cleartext signatures (inline), Detached signatures (.asc/.sig), Attached/opaque signatures.\n-   **Formats:** .asc (armored text), .pgp/.gpg (binary).\n-   **Trust:** Direct trust, Web of Trust, WKD/HKP discovery.\n-   **Key Hygiene:** Use strong passphrases, keep revocation cert offline, rotate/expire keys, prefer Ed25519 for signing.\n\n**Common issues:** invalid signature (wrong key/tampered), wrong passphrase, mixed line-endings.\n\nReferences: [RFC 4880](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4880), [OpenPGP.js](https://openpgpjs.org/), [GnuPG](https://www.gnupg.org/documentation/)\n\n##### About & Trust\n\n-   **Author:** [Anish Nath](https://x.com/anish2good) (Security Engineer)\n-   **Library:** OpenPGP.js v5.11.2 (client-side only)\n-   **Data handling:** Keys and files never leave your device\n-   **Updated:** 2025-11-25\n\nReferences: [RFC 4880](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4880), [OpenPGP.js Docs](https://openpgpjs.org/), [GnuPG Docs](https://www.gnupg.org/documentation/)\n\n##### Select File\n\n##### Drag & Drop file here\n\nor click to browse\n\n##### PGP Private Key\n\n—\n\n##### Passphrase\n\n##### Signature Format\n\n ASCII Armored (.asc)\n\n Binary (.sig)\n\nCreate Detached Signature\n\n##### Good to Know\n\n-   Detached signature does not modify the original file.\n-   Share *both* the file and the signature with the recipient.\n-   Recipients can verify with [File Signature Verify](pgpfileverify.jsp).\n\n##### Verify Cleartext Signed Message\n\n##### Signer Public Key\n\n—\n\nVerify Message Signature\n\n##### Verify File with Detached Signature\n\nOriginal File\n\n###### Drop file here\n\nor click to browse\n\nDetached Signature (.asc or .sig)\n\n###### Drop signature here\n\nor click to browse\n\n##### Signer Public Key\n\n—\n\nVerify Detached Signature\n\n##### Verification Tips\n\n-   Always confirm the public key fingerprint via trusted channels.\n-   Cleartext verification extracts the original message if valid.\n-   Detached signatures require both original file and signature.\n\n##### Identity\n\nName \n\nEmail \n\n##### Algorithm\n\nType Ed25519 (recommended) RSA\n\nRSA Bits 2048 3072 4096\n\nEd25519 offers smaller keys and faster operations; RSA maintains broad compatibility.\n\n##### Passphrase\n\nGenerate Copy\n\nPassphrase is required.\n\nUse a long, unique passphrase (20+ chars recommended).\n\nGenerate Key Pair\n\n##### Key Safety\n\n-   Backup your private key and passphrase securely.\n-   Share only the public key; keep private key secret.\n-   Consider generating a revocation certificate.\n\n#### Support This Free Tool\n\nEvery coffee helps keep the servers running. [Transliteration Tool](/transliteration-tool.jsp)\n\n \n\n##### PGP FAQ\n\n**Q: What is the difference between cleartext and detached signatures?**  \nA: Cleartext signatures keep the message human‑readable with an inline signature block; detached signatures are separate .asc/.sig files used to verify any file without changing it.\n\n**Q: How do I verify a signed message?**  \nA: Paste the signed text and the signer’s public key in the Verify tab and click Verify. You’ll see validity, signer KeyID, fingerprint, UID and the signature time.\n\n**Q: How do I verify a file with a detached signature?**  \nA: Select the original file and the .asc/.sig signature, paste the signer’s public key, then verify. The suite lists each signature and status.\n\n**Q: Can I recover my private‑key passphrase if I forget it?**  \nA: No. Passphrases are not recoverable. Generate a new key pair and distribute the new public key; revoke the old key if possible.\n\n**Q: Is this secure? Do keys leave my device?**  \nA: All operations happen client‑side in your browser using OpenPGP.js. Keys and files never leave your device.\n\n**Q: How do I confirm I have the right public key?**  \nA: Compare the fingerprint over a trusted channel (in‑person, voice, QR, business card) before trusting signatures or encrypting to the key.\n\n**Q: Can I sign with multiple keys or add notations?**  \nA: Advanced options like multi‑sign and notations are planned. For now, you can sign with one key and verify multi‑signature messages.",

16:39:13 +02:00\n\n#### \n\n**[README.md](#readme)**\n\n# Open PGP Signature Verification (OPSV)\n\n[OPSV](https://opsv.foss.guru) is a FOSS solution for easy PGP signature verification.\n\n## WARNING\n\nOPSV is integrated in the larger keyoxide project and will therefore no longer be updated.\n\n[https://codeberg.org/yarmo/keyoxide](https://codeberg.org/yarmo/keyoxide)\n\n## About\n\nThis project uses [openpgp.js](https://openpgpjs.org/) loaded in the browser, meaning all processing is done on the device itself and no data is ever sent to the server. It supports loading public keys directly through:\n\n1.  plaintext input\n2.  web key directory (WKD)\n3.  HTTP Keyserver Protocol (HKP).\n\nOPSV will always use the first input method it detects in the order described above.\n\nIt's also possible to not provide a public key. Read more about this in the `Using no public key at all` section below.\n\n## Usage\n\nVisit [https://opsv.foss.guru/](https://opsv.foss.guru/). On this website, you can enter a signed message (see example below) and any of the three supported public key inputs to verify that the owner of that public key was indeed the person to have signed that message.\n\n## Example\n\nLet's say I, Yarmo, would really like the world to know that I like pineapple. Using my private key, I've signed that statement so you can verify I wrote that message.\n\nThe signed statement:\n\n```plaintext\n-----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE-----\nHash: SHA256\n\nI like pineapple.\n-----BEGIN PGP SIGNATURE-----\n\niQJDBAEBCAAtFiEEog/Pt4tEmnyVrrtlNzZ/SvQIetEFAl70mVUPHHlhcm1vQHlh\ncm1vLmV1AAoJEDc2f0r0CHrRQXIP/08uza9zOtmZXv5K+uPGVzDKwkgPgZJEezX7\n6iQ358f1pjSRvYfQ5aB13k2epUHoqCKArMYu1zPqxhvLvvAvp8uOHABnr9NGL3El\nu7UUgaeUNHkr0gxCKEq3p81abrrbbWveP8OBP4RyxmaFx13Xcj7mfDluiBHmjVvv\nWU09EdH9VPlJ7WfZ+2G2ZZDHuE5XiaeP7ocugTxXXLkp33zwpDX0+ZuCIXM6fQGe\nOccSffglFPdNBnfasuuxDWxTQPsEbWGOPJV+CAPmBDeApX+TBF9bovO3hw4Uozk2\nVT7EAy8Hb0SOrUb3UNGxzoKv++5676IxyB4JXX0Tr9O4ZxhO8o9pEEHwirtn/J1+\nMWven4gVlWM/6bMeUqx6ydyNc2nqF5059yfRmwGMlp09x82G4x1bcf6aDZ+5njDG\nfS5T2OpXRIkZHJx8BhmZjsxiDR0KV44zwHpt06+96ef3EDWB0BcP6M+a5Rtc33zf\nirRmQd2M6RLyXCYtdGIiiAFRuomw802U4F0P4LwVrZdbGA6ObqBv1k8BUFCMbMz8\nAb4hF7kO4z0Vh3JaKzcHey0pOzdNCPpAHZ51sAoAnFDM4PdMBgQxxVweCMu4KYMZ\nFN8sNn42oY/b7gDmwCelVhgD+rvUn/a8+B7CDmCp+wIquyrjrTt00voATcb+ZPMJ\npTXJ/NcM\n=rqTX\n```\n\nUse this as \"Signature\" on [OPSV](https://opsv.foss.guru/).\n\n### Using plaintext public key\n\nNow, let's check the signature. Go to [my personal website](https://yarmo.eu/pgp) and copy-paste the \"plaintext\" key in the \"Public Key (1: plaintext)\" field.\n\nYou will see a green message confirming that my key was used to sign this message. I really do like pineapple.\n\n### Using web key directory (WKD)\n\nRemove the contents from the \"Public Key (1: plaintext)\" field. Now, in the \"Public Key (2: web key directory)\", write `yarmo@yarmo.eu` and verify the signature again. It is still verified. Try using `jane@doe.org` or any other input, it won't verify.\n\n### Using HTTP Keyserver Protocol (HKP)\n\nRemove the contents from the \"Public Key (2: web key directory)\" field. I uploaded my keys to the [https://keys.openpgp.org/](https://keys.openpgp.org/) HKP server,

CAs have to observe tightly controlled and audited procedures to create, manage, and utilize a root, and to minimize exposure, they will normally use a root to issue intermediate certificates. These intermediates can then be used to issue their customers’ certificates. A root CA certificate is self-signed, and the private key is protected under stringent procedures. Issuance typically occurs through intermediaries, not directly from the roots.\n[...]\nBrowsers are shipped with a built-in list of trusted roots. (These are roots from CAs who have passed the browser’s stringent criteria for inclusion.) To verify a certificate, a browser will obtain a sequence of certificates, each one having signed the next certificate in the sequence, connecting the signing CA’s root to the server’s certificate.\n[...]\nThis sequence of certificates is called a certification path. The path’s root is called a trust anchor and the server’s certificate is called the leaf or end entity certificate.\n[...]\nOften, web browsers and software clients in general may build to multiple candidate trust anchors (e.g., due to cross-signs) and frequently fetch missing intermediates via AIA to complete a chain. Even though a path may contain certificates that “chain” together properly to a known anchor, the path itself may be rejected due to restrictions on path length, domain name, certificate usage, or policy.\n[...]\nAfter a candidate certification path is constructed, browsers validate it using information contained in the certificates. A path is valid if browsers can cryptographically prove that, starting from a certificate directly signed by a trust anchor, each certificate’s corresponding private key was used to issue the next one in the path, all the way down to the leaf certificate.\n[...]\nAs previously noted, as of August 2024, RFC 9618, an Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) document, has updated the algorithm for validating policy constraints in X.509 digital certificates from RFC 5280. Basically, browsers iterate through all certificates in the path starting with the trust anchor (i.e. the root certificate), validating each certificate’s basic information and critical extensions.\n[...]\nprocedure concludes with\n[...]\nlast certificate in\n[...]\nthen the path is\n[...]\nClients verify that each certificate in the path is signed by the previous certificate’s public key and that Issuer/Subject names match exactly along the path. For added security, most PKI implementations also verify that the issuer’s key is the same as the key that signed the current certificate. (Note that this is not true for the trust anchor, since roots are self-issued – i.e., they have the same issuer and subject.)\n[...]\nA privately owned (but publicly trusted) intermediate CA with the appropriate name constraints can provide an organization with fine-grained control over certificate management and issuance. Certificates can be limited to a specific domain or domain tree (i.e., including subdomains) for a company or organization’s domain name. Name constraints are often used for intermediate CA certificates purchased from a publicly trusted CA to prevent the intermediate CA from issuing perfectly valid certificates for third-party domains (e.g.`google.com`).\n[...]\n9 v3 format\n[...]\nover how far each certificate can\n[...]\nplaced in a\n[...]\nto disregard the certification path length\n[...]\nwebsite’s leaf certificate to forge a\n[...]\ncertificate for a\n[...]\nAfter processing the extensions mentioned above, browsers proceed to validate\n[...]\nthat the current certificate designates\n[...]\ncritical, before moving on to the next. This page explains what each part does so you can read, debug, and configure certificates correctly in PKI and TLS.\n[...]\nUnderstanding certificate anatomy is critical for: validating trust chains, diagnosing SSL/TLS errors, implementing certificate generation, configuring proper extensions, and performing security assessments.\n[...]\nSubject Alternative Name (SAN): Specifies additional identities bound to this certificate. For TLS certificates, this is where domain names must appear (not in CN).\n[...]\nExtended Key Usage (EKU): Specifies application-specific purposes.\n[...]\na CA certificate\n[...]\nAuthority Key Identifier (AKI): Identifies the CA’s key that signed this certificate. Helps with chain building.\n[...]\nSubject Key Identifier (SKI): Unique identifier for this certificate’s public key. Used in chain validation.\n[...]\n1. Validity period - Most common issue: expired certificates\n2. Subject Alternative Name - Must match requested hostname for TLS\n3. Key Usage - Must match intended use (signing vs. encryption)\n4. Certificate chain - Check Authority Key Identifier matches parent’s Subject Key Identifier\n[...]\n1. Basic Constraints - Verify end-entity certs have `CA:FALSE`\n2. Extended Key Usage - Verify appropriate restrictions (serverAuth for TLS servers)\n3. Serial number - Check for sufficient entropy (modern CAs use 64+ bits)\n4. Signature algorithm - Verify SHA-256 or better (no MD5, SHA-1)\n[...]\n- End-entity certificate with `CA:TRUE` (can issue certificates inappropriately)\n- Missing Subject Alternative Name on TLS certificates (will fail in modern browsers)\n- Key Usage doesn’t match certificate purpose (e\n[...]\ng., encryption-only key used for signing)\n- Certificate validity >398 days for publicly-trusted certificates\n[...]\nates CA/B Forum\n[...]\n- Putting hostname only in Common Name, not Subject Alternative Name\n- Not marking Basic Constraints as critical for CA certificates\n- Using overly permissive Extended Key Usage (no restrictions)\n- Not including Authority Information Access (breaks OCSP validation)\n- Copy/paste existing certificates without understanding field requirements\n[...]\nCheck for required extensions:\n[...]\n- TLS server certs must have: SAN, Key Usage, Extended Key Usage\n- CA certs must have: Basic Constraints (CA:TRUE), Key Usage (Certificate Sign)\n[...]\nMissing SAN: Older certificates relied on CN for domain name. Modern browsers require SAN.\n[...]\nChain building failures: Missing or incorrect AKI/SKI, preventing proper chain construction.\n[...]\nExpired intermediate CA: Even if leaf certificate valid, expired intermediate breaks chain.\n[...]\nFor TLS, only SAN matters for hostname validation. CN is ignored by modern browsers. Attackers exploited this by getting certificates with legitimate-looking CN but malicious SAN.\n[...]\nVortex migrated to Kubernetes with automated\n[...]\nmanagement, we initially generated certificates with hostnames\n[...]\nin Common Name (\n[...]\n), not Subject Alternative Name (SAN). This worked in testing but failed in production:\n[...]\nRoot cause: Modern browsers ignore Common Name entirely for hostname validation - only SAN matters. Our certificate generation used legacy patterns that no longer met browser requirements.\n[...]\nrelied upon)\n[...]\ncertificates without SAN\n[...]\nRoot cause: Missing Subject Key Identifier (SKI) / Authority Key Identifier (AKI) in certificates. Some TLS implementations cached intermediate certificates, others didn’t. When intermediate wasn’t cached, chain building failed because client couldn’t determine which cached intermediate to use.\n[...]\n- Regenerated all certificates with proper SKI/AKI\n- Ensured AKI in child certificate matched SKI in parent\n- Implemented automated chain validation before certificate deployment\n- Added monitoring for “missing intermediate certificate” errors\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSL Certificate Chain Configuration - MOSS\nURL: https://moss.sh/ssl-security/ssl-certificate-chain-configuration/\nPublished: 2025-11-18T18:01:21.000Z\nAuthor: Jack Williams\nHighlights:\nAnatomy of a Certificate Chain ExplainedThe certificate chain (also called the chain of trust) links your server certificate to a trusted root CA through one or more intermediate certificates. At a high level, the chain contains: your end-entity certificate (the server cert), zero or more intermediate CA certs, and a root CA certificate. Each certificate is an X.509 structure signed by the certificate above it, following RFC 5280 rules.\n[...]\nA typical chain flow: Client trusts the root CA, the root signs an intermediate CA, and that intermediate signs your server certificate. Clients verify signatures, check validity periods, confirm subject names match (via SAN or CN), and validate extension constraints (like Basic Constraints or Key Usage). Missing an intermediate often results in “unable to get local issuer certificate” errors, while an expired intermediate causes a complete chain failure even if the server cert is valid. Tools like openssl and sslyze help inspect the full chain and surface mismatches.\n[...]\nKey terms to remember: X.509, subject, issuer, signature algorithm (e.g., SHA-256), SAN, and path length constraint. Keeping the correct certificate order (server first, then intermediates) and ensuring intermediates are current is critical for cross-platform compatibility.\n[...]\nCommon Chain Validation Errors and FixesCommon validation failures include: missing intermediate, incorrect order, expired intermediate, host name mismatch, unknown trust anchor, and incomplete chain. Error messages vary by client — browsers often show user-friendly warnings, while command-line tools produce terse errors.\n[...]\n- OCSP/CRL failures: enable OCSP stapling, check network connectivity to OCSP responders, and validate response freshness.\n[...]\n- Unknown trust anchor: check the client’s trust store; for private CAs consider distributing the root via configuration management, or use a publicly trusted CA.\n- Host name mismatch: obtain a certificate whose SAN includes the server’s DNS names or IPs; avoid using CN-only certs.\n- Expired intermediate: request a new intermediate from the CA or switch to a new chain that references current intermediates.\n- Incorrect order: reorder to ensure end-entity certificate is first, followed by intermediates.\n- Missing intermediate: append the correct intermediate certificates to your chain bundle and restart the server.\n[...]\nIntermediate Certificates: Why They Cause FailuresIntermediate certificates sit between the end-entity and root and are a frequent source of misconfiguration. Reasons they cause failures include: they’re missing from the chain, they’ve expired, the server presents them in the wrong order, or they contain restrictive path length constraints. Additionally, a mismatch between the CA that issued the server cert and the intermediate sent by the server creates signature verification failures.\n[...]\nA certificate chain is the ordered set of certificates linking an end-entity certificate (your server cert) through one or more intermediate certificates to a trusted root CA or trust anchor. Validation confirms signatures, validity periods, and extension constraints. Proper ordering and presence of required intermediates are essential for successful TLS handshakes.\n[...]\nBrowsers report untrusted certificates when they cannot build a path from the server’s certificate to a trusted root in the client’s trust store. Common causes are missing intermediates, incorrect chain order, an expired intermediate, or the server using a certificate from a private CA not present in the browser’s trust store.\n[...]\n. Send the end\n[...]\nnecessary intermediate cert\n[...]\nIntermediate failures stem from missing intermediates, wrong ordering, expired intermediates, or incorrect importation into platform-specific stores (e.g., Java keystores). Fix by obtaining the correct intermediate from the CA and assembling or importing the chain in the platform’s expected format.\n\n---\n\nTitle: openssl-verification-options - OpenSSL Documentation\nURL: https://docs.openssl.org/4.0/man1/openssl-verification-options/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nIn a nutshell, a valid chain of certificates needs to be constructed and checked starting from the target certificate that is to be validated and ending in a trust anchor, which usually is a certificate that due to some policy is trusted. Certificate validation can be performed in the context of a purpose, which is a high-level specification of the intended use of the target certificate, such as`sslserver` for (D)TLS servers, or (by default) for any purpose.\n[...]\nThe most crucial input to certificate validation is a trust store, which includes a collection of certificates and validation options. This is akin to what is used in the trust stores of Mozilla Firefox, or Apple's and Microsoft's certificate stores, ... By default all apparently self-signed certificates in the trust store are considered to be trust anchors, for all or for specified purposes.\n[...]\nIndependent of any extended key usage (EKU) X.509v3 extension included, from the OpenSSL perspective a trust anchor certificate should be augmented with local trust attributes, giving an explicit designation for which uses of a target certificate the certificate may serve as a trust anchor. If this information is missing, it is by default trusted for all uses. In PEM encoding, the presence of trust attributes is indicated by the`TRUSTED CERTIFICATE` string. Such a designation provides a set of positive trust attributes explicitly stating acceptance for the listed uses and/or a set of negative trust attributes explicitly rejecting the listed uses. These uses are encoded using the OID values defined for the extended key usages that may be included in X.509v3 certificate extensions. For the list of EKUs with currently predefined meaning see the \"Extended Key Usage\" section below. The special EKU anyExtendedKeyUsage enables all uses when trusted or blocks all uses when rejected.\n[...]\n- It is an element of the trust store.\n- It does not have a negative trust attribute rejecting the EKU associated with the intended purpose.\n- It has a positive trust attribute accepting the EKU associated with the intended purpose or it does not have any positive trust attribute and one of the following compatibility conditions apply: It is apparently self-signed or the -partial_chain option is given (which corresponds to the X509_V_FLAG_PARTIAL_CHAIN flag being set).\n[...]\ngoal is that\n[...]\nCertificate extensions are\n[...]\n.50\n[...]\nv3, and though\n[...]\nare optional,\n[...]\nrequired. If it does not find a match there it consults the list of untrusted (\"intermediate\" CA) certificates, if provided. If one issuer certificate was found in the trust store, the list of untrusted certificates will not be consulted anymore to find further issuer certificates. Therefore, either only the root certificate or an uninterrupted chain to the root certificate must be provided in the trust store for a successful verification, if X509_V_FLAG_PARTIAL_CHAIN is not enabled.\n[...]\nThe following options specify how to supply the certificates that can be used as trust anchors for certain uses. Specifies additional hostnames the certificate is valid for:\n[...]\n```\nX509v3 Subject Alternative Name:\n    DNS:example.com, DNS:www.example.com, DNS:*.example.com\n[...]\nWhy it matters: Modern browsers only check SAN, not the Subject CN. Expiry check: All certificates must be within their validity period\n4. Revocation check: Check OCSP or CRL for revoked certificates\n5. Name validation: Hostname matches SAN or CN\n6. Always include SAN extension — CN alone is insufficient\n2. Use 2048-bit RSA minimum — or ECDSA P-256 for modern systems\n3. Prefer 90-day validity — shorter lifespans reduce risk from compromised keys\n4. Set appropriate EKU — don't use serverAuth certificates for code signing\n5. The PKIX standards were developed by the IETF PKIX working group.\n[...]\nIf the user does not have a trusted copy of the public key of the CA that signed the subject's public key certificate, then another public key certificate vouching for the signing CA is required. This logic can be applied recursively, until a chain of certificates (or a certification path) is discovered from a trust anchor or a most-trusted CA to the target subject (commonly referred to as the end-entity). The most-trusted CA is usually specified by a certificate issued to a CA that the user directly trusts. In general, a certification path is an ordered list of certificates, usually comprised of the end-entity's public key certificate and zero or more additional certificates. A certification path typically has one or more encodings, allowing it to be safely transmitted across networks and to different operating system architectures.\n[...]\nA certification path must be validated before it can be relied on to establish trust in a subject's public key. Validation can consist of various checks on the certificates contained in the certification path, such as verifying the signatures and checking that each certificate has not been revoked. The PKIX standards define an algorithm for validating certification paths consisting of X.509 certificates.\n[...]\nBuilding and validating certification paths is an important part of many standard security protocols such as SSL/TLS/DTLS, S/MIME, and IPsec. The Java Certification Path API provides a set of classes and interfaces for developers who need to integrate this functionality into their applications. This API benefits two types of developers: those who need to write service provider implementations for a specific certification path building or validation algorithm; and those who need to access standard algorithms for creating, building, and validating certification paths in an implementation-independent manner.\n[...]\nThe Java Certification Path API includes classes and interfaces for validating\n[...]\npaths. An application uses an instance of the`CertPathValidator` class to validate a`CertPath` object. If successful, the result of the validation algorithm is returned in an object implementing the`CertPathValidatorResult` interface.\n[...]\nThe initialization parameters (`params`) are specific to the repository type. For example, the initialization parameters for a server-based repository may include the hostname and the port of the server. An`InvalidAlgorithmParameterException` is thrown if the parameters are invalid for this`CertStore` type. The`getCertStoreParameters` method returns the`CertStoreParameters` that were used to initialize a`CertStore`:\n[...]\nX.509`CertPath` object and a`PKIXParameters` object are passed as arguments to the`validate` method of a CertPathValidator instance implementing the PKIX algorithm. The`CertPathValidator` uses the parameters to initialize the PKIX certification path validation algorithm.\n[...]\nThe`maxPathLength` parameter specifies the maximum number of non-self-issued intermediate certificates that may exist in a certification path. A`CertPathBuilder` instance implementing the PKIX algorithm must not build paths longer than the length specified. If the value is 0, the path can only contain a single certificate. If the value is -1, the path length is unconstrained (i.e., there is no maximum). The default maximum path length, if not specified, is 5. This method is useful to prevent the`CertPathBuilder` from spending resources and time constructing long paths that may or may not meet the caller's requirements\n\n---\n\nTitle: RFC 4158: Internet X.509 Public Key Infrastructure: Certification Path Building\nURL: https://rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4158.html\nPublished: 2020-05-03T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nkey, in order\n[...]\nsupport public key\n[...]\nand public key\n[...]\nprior to using the public key contained in a certificate, an\n   application first has to determine the authenticity of that\n   certificate, and specifically, the validity of all the certificates\n   leading to a trusted public key, called a trust anchor. In this document we will be referring to the current standard in use for web pki: x509 v3, which is described in detail in RFC 5280. In general, x509 certificates bind a signature to a validity period, a public key, a subject, an issuer, and a set of extensions. The extensions define extra properties of the certificate such as extra attributes of the certificate or constraints on the use of the certificate. In order for a certificate to be valid these three requirements must be met:\n[...]\n1. Revocation checks are considered OK for that particular validation.\n2. The attributes of the certificates in the verification path have valid parameters for that verification (for example the validity period of all the certificates are valid for the time the verification is being done)\n3. There is a verification path from the site certificate to a trusted certificate of the user agent (ie if you follow the issuer path you will end on a self-signed certificate that is considered trusted by the browser).\n4. Generate the end entity certificate\n[...]\nOne issue that is not commonly known is that the x509 trust graph is not a forest (a bunch of trees where each root is a trusted root) but a cyclic graph, where the same key/issuer can be a root or an intermediate for another root in the browsers key store (when roots create intermediates for each other it is called cross-signing).\n[...]\nThis extension defines what other names (such as DNS names) are valid for this certificate. This allows for a certificate to be used for more than one FQDN, for example you can have a certificate that is valid for both a.example.com and b.example.com\n[...]\nThis allows certificates to be asserted as issuing certificates (it is mandatory for CA certificates). It can also be used to express the maximum depth of the trust path from the CA.\n[...]\nThis extension is primarily used to to describe the OCSP location for revocation checking. It is mandatory for certificates that chain up to a root in the Mozilla CA program.\n[...]\nWhen you visit a secure website, Firefox will validate the website’s certificate by checking that the certificate that signed it is valid, and checking that the certificate that signed the parent certificate is valid and so forth up to a root certificate that is known to be valid. This chain of certificates is called the Certificate Hierarchy.\n[...]\nIf you are going to have your own CA, we recommend building 3 certificates: a long term root cert, a medium term intermediate cert, and a short term end-entity cert. This type of hierarchy allows for a relatively simple long term root to be distributed to clients, and some flexibility on the intermediate cert so that you can change parameters based on best practices and security research.\n[...]\n## Generate your Intermediate cert\n[...]\nThe following steps create an intermediate cert that is valid for 8 years.\n[...]\n- authorityInfoAccess = OCSP;URI: http://ocsp.example.com:8888/\n- extendedKeyUsage =serverAuth\n- keyUsage = keyCertSign, cRLSign\n- subjectKeyIdentifier = hash\n- authorityKeyIdentifier = keyid, issuer\n- basicConstraints = critical, CA:TRUE\n[...]\nSign the intermediate csr with the root key and the intermediate extensions\n[...]\n- \"openssl x509 -req -sha256 -days 2922 -in int.csr -CAkey rootkey.pem -CA root.pem -set_serial $SOME_LARGE_INTEGER -out int.pem -extfile openssl.int.cnf\"\n[...]\n## Generate the end entity certificate\n[...]\nsp.example.com:80/\n[...]\nIntermediate sings the csr (using SHA256) and appends the extensions described in the file\n[...]\n- \"openssl x509 -req -sha256 -days 1096 -in example.csr -CAkey intkey.pem -CA int.pem -set_serial $SOME_LARGE_INTEGER -out www.example.com.pem -extfile openssl.int.cnf\"\n[...]\n| SEC_ERROR_UNKNOWN_ISSUER | Either a missing intermediate or root certificate is necessary to verify the certificate | Import the root certificate into Firefox or have the server send the intermediate |",
