Certificate validation can be performed in the context of a purpose, which is a high-level specification of the intended use of the target certificate, such as`sslserver` for (D)TLS servers, or (by default) for any purpose.\n[...]\nThe most crucial input to certificate validation is a trust store, which includes a collection of certificates and validation options. This is akin to what is used in the trust stores of Mozilla Firefox, or Apple's and Microsoft's certificate stores, ... By default all apparently self-signed certificates in the trust store are considered to be trust anchors, for all or for specified purposes.\n[...]\n- It is an element of the trust store.\n- It does not have a negative trust attribute rejecting the EKU associated with the intended purpose.\n[...]\n- It has a positive trust attribute accepting the EKU associated with\n[...]\nintended purpose or it does not have any positive trust attribute and one of the following compatibility conditions apply: It is apparently self-signed or the -partial_chain option is given (which corresponds to the X509_V_FLAG_PARTIAL_CHAIN flag\n[...]\nCertificate extensions are available since X.509v3, and though many are optional, some are generally recommended or required. For certificate chain building the subjectKeyIdentifier and authorityKeyIdentifier extensions are very useful. The subject alternative names (SAN) and issuer alternative names given in subjectAltName or issuerAltName extensions are not relevant for chain building.\n[...]\nThe lookup first searches for issuer certificates in the trust store. If it does not find a match there it consults the list of untrusted (\"intermediate\" CA) certificates, if provided. If one issuer certificate was found in the trust store, the list of untrusted certificates will not be consulted anymore to find further issuer certificates. Therefore, either only the root certificate or an uninterrupted chain to the root certificate must be provided in the trust store for a successful verification, if X509_V_FLAG_PARTIAL_CHAIN is not enabled.\n[...]\nThe following options specify how to supply the certificates that can be used as trust anchors for certain uses. As mentioned, a collection of such certificates is called a trust store.\n[...]\nthe specified directory as a collection of trusted certificates, i.e., a trust store. Files should be named with the hash value of the X.509 SubjectName of each certificate. This is so that the library can extract the IssuerName, hash it, and directly lookup\n[...]\nthe issuer certificate.\n[...]\nof directory.\n[...]\nA high-level specification of the intended use of the target certificate. Currently predefined purposes are`sslclient`,`sslserver`,`nssslserver`,`smimesign`,`smimeencrypt`,`crlsign`,`ocsphelper`,`timestampsign`,`codesign` and`any`. If peer certificate verification is enabled, by default the TLS implementation and thus the commands openssl-s_client(1) and openssl-s_server(\n[...]\n) check for consistency with TLS server (`sslserver`) or TLS client use (`sslclient`), respectively. By default, CMS signature validation, which can\n[...]\nsl-cms(1), checks for consistency with the S/MIME signing\n[...]\n(`smimesign\n[...]\n-verify_hostname hostname\n[...]\nVerify if hostname matches DNS name in Subject Alternative Name or Common Name in the subject certificate.\n[...]\nThe issuer checks still suffer from limitations in the underlying X509_LOOKUP API. Trusted certificates with matching subject name must appear in a file (as specified by the -CAfile option), a directory (as specified by -CApath), or a store (as specified by -CAstore). If there are multiple such matches, possibly in multiple locations, only the first one (in the mentioned order of locations) is recognised.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Security with network protocols  |  Android Developers\nURL: https://developer.android.com/privacy-and-security/security-ssl?authuser=1\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nServers usually rely on Certificate Authorities (CAs) certificates to issue certificates, which keeps the client-side configuration more stable over time. A CA signs a server certificate using its private key. The client can then check that the server has a platform-known CA certificate.\n[...]\nClient apps need a mechanism to verify the server because the CA offers certificates for numerous servers. The CA's certificate identifies the server using either a specific name, such as gmail.com , or using a wildcard, such as**.google.com*.\n[...]\nTo customize HTTP requests, cast to HttpURLConnection. The Android`HttpURLConnection` documentation includes examples for handling request and response headers, publishing content, managing cookies, using proxies, caching responses, and more. The Android framework verifies certificates and hostnames using these APIs.\n[...]\narises because the system doesn't trust the CA\n[...]\nis operating on an earlier\n[...]\nThird, SSLHandshakeException occurs due to missing intermediate CA. Public CAs rarely sign server certificates. Instead, the root CA signs intermediate CAs.\n[...]\nTo reduce compromise risk, CAs keep the root CA offline. However, operating systems like Android normally trust only root CAs directly, leaving a short trust gap between the server certificate---signed by the intermediate CA---and the certificate verifier, which recognizes the root CA.\n[...]\nTo remove this trust gap, the server sends a chain of certificates from the server CA through any intermediates to a trusted root CA during the TLS handshake.\n[...]\n```\n$ openssl s_client\n[...]\nconnect mail.google.com:443\n[...]\n---\nCertificate chain\n 0 s:/C=US/ST=California/L=Mountain View/O=Google LLC/CN=mail.google.com\n   i:/C=ZA/O=Thawte Consulting (Pty) Ltd./CN=Thawte SGC CA\n 1 s:/C=ZA/O=Thawte Consulting (Pty) Ltd./CN=Thawte SGC CA\n   i:/C=US/O=VeriSign, Inc./OU=Class 3 Public Primary Certification Authority\n[...]\nThis shows that the server sends a certificate for mail.google.com issued by the Thawte SGC CA, which is an intermediate CA, and a second certificate for the Thawte SGC CA issued by a Verisign CA, which is the primary CA that's trusted by Android.\n[...]\nUnlike an unknown CA or self-signed server certificate, most desktop browsers don't produce an error while communicating with this server. Desktop browsers cache trusted intermediate CAs. After learning about an intermediate CA from one site, a browser won't need it in the certificate chain again.\n[...]\nTo fix this issue, configure the server to include the intermediary CA in the server chain. Most CAs provide instructions on how to do this for common web servers.\n[...]\nCaution: `SSLSocket` does not perform hostname verification. It is up to your app to do its own hostname verification, preferably by calling getDefaultHostnameVerifier() with the expected hostname. Also, be aware thatHostnameVerifier.verify()doesn't throw an exception on error. Instead, it returns a boolean result that you must explicitly check.\n[...]\nTLS relies on CAs to issue certificates to only the verified owners of servers and domains. In rare cases, CAs are either tricked or, in the case of Comodo or DigiNotar, breached, resulting in the certificates for a hostname being issued to someone other than the owner of the server or domain.\n[...]\nTo mitigate this risk, Android handles certificate revocation system-wide, through a combination of a blocklist and certificate transparency, without relying on on-line certificate verification. In addition, Android will validate OCSP responses stapled to the TLS handshake.\n[...]\n- ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an alias for ChaCha20/Poly1305/NoPadding.\n- Hostnames with trailing dots aren't considered valid SNI hostnames.\n- The supported_signature_algorithms extension in CertificateRequest is respected when choosing a signing key for certificate responses.\n- Opaque signing keys, such as those from Android Keystore, can be used with RSA-PSS signatures in TLS.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Hostname Validation (RFC 6125) | TLSleuth\nURL: https://tlsleuth.com/blog/hostname-validation-rfc6125.html\nPublished: 2026-03-01T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nA common misconception about TLS is that certificate chain validation alone proves the identity of a server.\n[...]\nCertificate chain validation only proves:\n[...]\n- the certificate was issued by a trusted CA\n- the certificate has not expired\n- the certificate chain satisfies PKI policy constraints\n[...]\nIt does not prove that the certificate belongs to the hostname the client intended to connect to.\n[...]\nThis is why TLS clients perform a second validation step called hostname validation, defined in RFC 6125 — Service Identity Verification.\n[...]\nHostname validation ensures that the certificate presented by the server actually corresponds to the domain name the client requested.\n[...]\nWithout hostname validation, a valid certificate for any domain could impersonate any other domain.\n[...]\nTLS validation therefore consists of two independent checks.\n[...]\n```\nflowchart TD\n    Cert[Server Certificate]\n\n    Cert --> ChainValidation\n    Cert --> HostnameValidation\n\n    ChainValidation --> TrustDecision\n    HostnameValidation --> TrustDecision\n\n    TrustDecision --> Result[Connection Trusted]\n[...]\nBoth checks must succeed.\n[...]\n| Validation Step | Purpose |\n| --- | --- |\n| Chain validation | Verify certificate trust chain |\n| Hostname validation | Verify server identity |\n[...]\nIf either check fails, the TLS connection must be rejected.\n[...]\nIf the client only verified the certificate chain, the connection would appear valid even though\n[...]\nHostname validation prevents this class of attack.\n[...]\n# Subject Alternative Name (SAN)\n[...]\nModern TLS implementations perform hostname validation using the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) extension.\n[...]\nRFC 6125 specifies that clients must use SAN if it exists.\n[...]\nThe older Common Name (CN) field should only be used as a fallback for legacy certificates.\n[...]\n# Hostname Matching Rules\n[...]\nRFC 6125 defines precise rules for hostname comparison.\n[...]\nThe client must check whether the requested hostname matches one of the SAN entries.\n[...]\nMatching must be exact unless wildcard rules apply.\n[...]\nare restricted to a single label.\n[...]\n# Internationalized Domain Names (IDN)\n[...]\nBefore validation, hostnames must be converted into Punycode form.\n[...]\nBoth the certificate SAN and the requested hostname must be compared in normalized form.\n[...]\n# IP Address Validation\n[...]\nWhen clients connect to a server using an IP address, the certificate must include an IP SAN entry.\n[...]\nDNS SAN entries cannot be used to validate IP addresses.\n[...]\n# Hostname Validation Flow\n[...]\nCert[Server Certificate]\n[...]\nCert --> Extract\n[...]\nExtractSAN --> CompareHost\n[...]\n--> Match{Match Found?}\n\n    Match -->|Yes|\n[...]\nMatch -->|No| Reject\n[...]\nThe client extracts the SAN entries and checks whether any entry matches the requested hostname.\n[...]\nIf none match, the connection must be rejected.\n[...]\n# Interaction with Server Name Indication (SNI)\n[...]\nHostname validation works together with Server Name Indication (SNI).\n[...]\nSNI allows the client to specify the hostname it intends to connect to during the TLS handshake.\n[...]\n| --- | --- |\n[...]\n|\n|\n[...]\n|\n| IP connection failure | certificate lacks IP SAN |\n[...]\n| Library | Behavior |\n| --- | --- |\n| OpenSSL | hostname validation must be enabled explicitly |\n| NSS (Firefox) | strict RFC 6125 compliance |\n| Windows CryptoAPI | integrated hostname checks |\n| BoringSSL | simplified validation logic |\n[...]\nCertificate chain validation confirms that a certificate is cryptographically trusted.\n[...]\nHostname validation confirms that the certificate actually represents the intended service identity.\n[...]\nBoth checks are essential.\n[...]\nWithout hostname validation, any valid certificate issued by a trusted CA could impersonate any domain.\n[...]\nThis separation of trust validation and identity validation is a critical design principle of TLS and modern PKI.\n[...]\n- Part 2:\n[...]\n3: Certificate\n[...]\n: Trust Stores and\n\n---\n\nTitle: Checking the certificate trust chain for an HTTPS endpoint | Microsoft Community Hub\nURL: https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/iis-support-blog/checking-the-certificate-trust-chain-for-an-https-endpoint/1616682\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSometimes our client apps, including browsers, are unable or unwilling to connect to an HTTPS site. A common cause: the certificate presented by the server endpoint fails the validation; the client does not trust the certificate presented by the server. This article is a continuation of http://linqto.me/https.\n[...]\nDuring the TLS handshake,\n[...]\nthe secure channel is established for HTTPS, before any HTTP traffic can take place, the\n[...]\nis presenting its certificate. The server has to authenticate itself. And the\n[...]\nis checking the certificate:\n[...]\n1. Is the certificate still valid? Isn’t it expired?\n2. Is the certificate issued for the domain that the server claims to be? Does the Subject name in the certificate match the site name (host-name) of the endpoint URL?\n3. Does the client trust the certificate chain? Does it trust the issuing authority or the entity endorsing the certificate authority?\n4. Was the certificate revoked by its issuing authority?\n5. Do the cryptographic details match, key and algorithms?\n[...]\nBelow, we treat a bit on the third question: trusting the certificate chain. We check certificate identifiers against the Windows certificate store.\n[...]\nSo what’s the certificate’s trust chain? Well, the certificate of a server is issued by an authority that checks somehow the authenticity of that server or service. We call it the Certificate Authority or Issuing Authority. That authority should be trusted. Or we should trust, at least, the authority that is endorsing the Issuing Authority, which we call Root Authority. Sometimes, this chain of certification may be even longer.\n[...]\n2. The Issuing Authority, the one that signed and generated the service certificate\n[...]\n3. The Root Authority,\n[...]\nMost of the browsers allow to see the certificate of an HTTPS site, along with the trust chain. Identifiers can be picked from there too. Other browsers or technologies may use other APIs or crypto libraries for validating certificates. But Windows relies on its certificate store. So, we need to check if an issuing authority or its endorsing authority is trusted: does its certificate appear in the certificate store, in the needed location?\n[...]\nOpening the certificates console, we check the Trusted/Third-Party Root Certification Authorities or the Intermediate Certification Authorities.\n[...]\nIf we can’t find a valid entity’s certificate there, then perhaps we should install it. Luckily, this is done simply opening and importing the CER file of an authority. In some cases, a PFX container file has inside certificates and keys; it is common that entire certificate chains are included in the PFX container – importing the PFX may install all the contained certificates, including those of issuing or endorsing authorities.\n[...]\nThe Windows certificate repository is using the certificate computed SHA-1 Fingerprint/Hash, or Thumbprint, as certificate identifier. The binding is asserted by having a trusted certificate authority (CA) such as SSL.com verify the identity of prospective certificate owners, via automated and manual checks against qualified databases.\n[...]\nCertificates are digital files in every respect, which means that\n[...]\nneed to follow a file format to\n[...]\n., signatures, keys, issuers, etc.). While\n[...]\ntheir certificates, publicly trusted\n[...]\nthose trusted by the browsers\n[...]\nto create,\n[...]\nBrowsers are shipped with a built-in list of trusted roots. (These are roots from CAs who have passed the browser’s stringent criteria for inclusion.) To verify a certificate, a browser will obtain a sequence of certificates, each one having signed the next certificate in the sequence, connecting the signing CA’s root to the server’s certificate.\n[...]\nThis sequence of certificates is called a certification path. The path’s root is called a trust anchor and the server’s certificate is called the leaf or end entity certificate.\n[...]\nOften, web browsers and software clients in general may build to multiple candidate trust anchors (e.g., due to cross-signs) and frequently fetch missing intermediates via AIA to complete a chain. Even though a path may contain certificates that “chain” together properly to a known anchor, the path itself may be rejected due to restrictions on path length, domain name, certificate usage, or policy.\n[...]\nAfter a candidate certification path is constructed, browsers validate it using information contained in the certificates. A path is valid if browsers can cryptographically prove that, starting from a certificate directly signed by a trust anchor, each certificate’s corresponding private key was used to issue the next one in the path, all the way down to the leaf certificate.\n[...]\nAs previously noted, as of August 2024, RFC 9618, an Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) document, has updated the algorithm for validating policy constraints in X.509 digital certificates from RFC 5280. Basically, browsers iterate through all certificates in the path starting with the trust anchor (i.e. the root certificate), validating each certificate’s basic information and critical extensions.\n[...]\nIf the procedure concludes with the last certificate in the path without errors, then the path is accepted as valid. If errors are produced, the path is marked as invalid.\n[...]\n, browsers must always verify\n[...]\nthe signature or the issuer\n[...]\nClients verify that each certificate in the path is signed by the previous certificate’s public key and that Issuer/Subject names match exactly along the path. For added security, most PKI implementations also verify that the issuer’s key is the same as the key that signed the current certificate. (Note that this is not true for the trust anchor, since roots are self-issued – i.e., they have the same issuer and subject.)\n[...]\nA privately owned (but publicly trusted\n[...]\nintermediate CA with the appropriate name constraints can provide an organization with fine-grained control over certificate management and issuance. Certificates can be limited to a specific domain or domain tree (i.e., including subdomains) for a company or organization’s domain\n[...]\n. Name constraints are often used for intermediate CA certificates purchased from a publicly trusted CA to prevent\n[...]\nintermediate CA from issuing perfectly valid certificates for third\n[...]\nAfter processing the extensions mentioned above, browsers proceed to validate all remaining extensions that the current certificate designates as critical, before moving on to the next. If a browser reaches a path’s leaf certificate without error, then the path is accepted as valid. If any errors are produced the path is marked as invalid and a secure connection is not established.",

  [Trust Anchors](#trust-anchors)\n    -   [Certification Path Building](#certification-path-building)\n    -   [Certification Path Validation](#certification-path-validation)\n    \n-   [OPTIONS](#options)\n    \n    -   [Trusted Certificate Options](#trusted-certificate-options)\n    -   [Verification Options](#verification-options)\n    -   [Extended Verification Options](#extended-verification-options)\n    -   [Certificate Extensions](#certificate-extensions)\n        \n        -   [Basic Constraints](#basic-constraints)\n        -   [Key Usage](#key-usage)\n        -   [Extended Key Usage](#extended-key-usage)\n        -   [Duplicate Extensions](#duplicate-extensions)\n        -   [Checks Implied by Specific Predefined Policies](#checks-implied-by-specific-predefined-policies)\n        \n    \n-   [BUGS](#bugs)\n-   [SEE ALSO](#see-also)\n-   [HISTORY](#history)\n-   [COPYRIGHT](#copyright)\n\n# openssl-verification-options[¶](#openssl-verification-options \"Permanent link\")\n\n## NAME[¶](#name \"Permanent link\")\n\nopenssl-verification-options - generic X.509 certificate verification options\n\n## SYNOPSIS[¶](#synopsis \"Permanent link\")\n\n**openssl***command* \\[ *options* ... \\] \\[ *parameters* ... \\]\n\n## DESCRIPTION[¶](#description \"Permanent link\")\n\nThere are many situations where X.509 public-key certificates are verified within the OpenSSL libraries and in various OpenSSL commands. For checking certificates, the term *validation* would actually be more appropriate, but for historical reasons the term *verification* is often used instead.\n\nIn the sequel, the certificate to be validated is called *target certificate*. Since it usually is an end-entity (EE) certificate, it is also known as \"leaf\" certificate (of a chain or tree), but it may also be a CA certificate. Certificate verification is implemented by [X509\\_verify\\_cert(3)](../../man3/X509_verify_cert/). The validation process involves several steps and depends on multiple options. The most important of them are detailed in the following sections.\n\nIn a nutshell, a valid chain of certificates needs to be constructed and checked starting from the *target certificate* that is to be validated and ending in a trust anchor, which usually is a certificate that due to some policy is trusted. Certificate validation can be performed in the context of a *purpose*, which is a high-level specification of the intended use of the target certificate, such as `sslserver` for (D)TLS servers, or (by default) for any purpose.\n\nAll certificates in the chain except the target ceritificate must be CA certificates. An X.509v3 certificate is considered a CA certificate if it has a basicConstraints extension with the \\`CA\\` flag set. If a pathLenConstraint is included then the chain is checked to satisfy it. If a CA certificate has a keyUsage extension then the latter must include the keyCertSign bit, and for proxy certificates used for issuing a certificate it must include also the digitalSignature bit.\n\nThe details of how each OpenSSL command handles errors are documented on the specific command page.\n\nDANE support is documented in [openssl-s\\_client(1)](../openssl-s_client/), [SSL\\_CTX\\_dane\\_enable(3)](../../man3/SSL_CTX_dane_enable/), [SSL\\_set1\\_host(3)](../../man3/SSL_set1_host/), [X509\\_VERIFY\\_PARAM\\_set\\_flags(3)](../../man3/X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set_flags/), and [X509\\_check\\_host(3)](../../man3/X509_check_host/).\n\n### Trust Anchors[¶](#trust-anchors \"Permanent link\")\n\nIn general, according to RFC 4158 and RFC 5280, a *trust anchor* is just a public key (with algorithm and any parameters) and an associated subject name that in a suitable context is considered trusted for some purpose(s) and thus is acceptable as the root of a chain of certificates.\n\nIn practice, trust anchors are given in the form of certificates. Commonly they are self-signed root CA certificates. Their most essential fields are the subjectPublicKeyInfo and the subject distinguished name (DN), while the subject key identifier extension is generally optional but highly recommended to be included in CA certificates because they help chain building. In addition to the requirements given in RFC 5280 for trust anchor handling, OpenSSL performs on trust anchor certificates the same checks as on regular CA certificates, in particular those regarding the validity period and X.509v3 extensions like basic constraints and key usage. Depending on the given validation purpose, in part even the extended key usage is checked - for details see the [\"Extended Key Usage\"](#extended-key-usage) section below.\n\nThe most crucial input to certificate validation is a *trust store*, which includes a collection of certificates and validation options. This is akin to what is used in the trust stores of Mozilla Firefox, or Apple's and Microsoft's certificate stores, ... By default all apparently self-signed certificates in the trust store are considered to be trust anchors, for all or for specified purposes.\n\nFor efficiency reasons, apparently self-signed certificates (based on certificate metadata) are not subjected to cryptographic self-signature check. A certificate is apparently self-signed if and only if all of the following conditions are met:\n\n-   it is self-issued, i.e., its `subject` and `issuer` fields are equal,\n-   the algorithm identifiers of its public key and signature match,\n-   if it has an authorityKeyIdentifier (AKID) extension containing the `keyIdentifier` field, its value matches the subjectKeyIdentifier (SKID) extension if present,\n-   if it has an AKID containing the `authorityCertSerialNumber` field, its value equals the serial number of the certificate, and\n-   if it has an AKID containing the `authorityCertIssuer` field and its `GeneralNames` include at least one `directoryName`, the first such name equals the `issuer` field of the certificate.\n\nIndependent of any extended key usage (EKU) X.509v3 extension included, from the OpenSSL perspective a trust anchor certificate should be augmented with local trust attributes, giving an explicit designation for which uses of a target certificate the certificate may serve as a trust anchor. If this information is missing, it is by default trusted for all uses. In PEM encoding, the presence of trust attributes is indicated by the `TRUSTED CERTIFICATE` string. Such a designation provides a set of positive trust attributes explicitly stating acceptance for the listed uses and/or a set of negative trust attributes explicitly rejecting the listed uses. These uses are encoded using the OID values defined for the extended key usages that may be included in X.509v3 certificate extensions. For the list of EKUs with currently predefined meaning see the [\"Extended Key Usage\"](#extended-key-usage) section below. The special EKU **anyExtendedKeyUsage** enables all uses when trusted or blocks all uses when rejected.\n\nA certificate, which may be CA certificate or an end-entity certificate, is considered a trust anchor for the intended use if and only if all the following conditions hold:\n\n-   It is an element of the trust store.\n-   It does not have a negative trust attribute rejecting the EKU associated with the intended purpose.\n-   It has a positive trust attribute accepting the EKU associated with the intended purpose or it does not have any positive trust attribute and one of the following compatibility conditions apply: It is apparently self-signed or the **\\-partial\\_chain** option is given (which corresponds to the **X509\\_V\\_FLAG\\_PARTIAL\\_CHAIN** flag being set).\n\n### Certification Path Building[¶](#certification-path-building \"Permanent link\")\n\nFirst, a certificate chain is built up starting from the target certificate and ending in a trust anchor. The goal is that for each certificate in the chain, except for the final one, the subject of the successor is the issuer of the former certificate. Since it often happens that the subject and issuer Distinguished Names are not unique, the subject and issuer key identifier extensions, if present in the certificates, are used to disambiguate conflicts.\n\nThe chain is built up iteratively, looking up each certificate in turn that matches as an issuer of the current \"subject\" certificate as described below. If there is more than one matching certificate, the algorithm uses the first certificate found that has a suitable validity period, otherwise the one with the latest expiration date of all matching certificates. For efficiency, the algorithm does not allow for backtracking, thus any further candidate issuer certificates that would match equally are ignored.\n\nCertificate extensions are available since X.509v3, and though many are optional, some are generally recommended or required. For certificate chain building the subjectKeyIdentifier and authorityKeyIdentifier extensions are very useful. The subject alternative names (SAN) and issuer alternative names given in subjectAltName or issuerAltName extensions are not relevant for chain building.\n\nOnce a apparently self-signed certificate has been added, chain construction stops. In this case it must fully match a trust anchor, otherwise chain building fails.\n\nA candidate issuer certificate matches a subject certificate if all of the following conditions hold:\n\n-   Its subject Distinguished Name (DN) matches the issuer DN of the subject certificate.\n-   If the subject certificate has an authority key identifier extension, each of its sub-fields equals the corresponding subject key identifier, serial number, and issuer field of the candidate issuer certificate, as far as the respective fields are present in both certificates.\n-   The certificate signature algorithm used to sign the subject certificate is supported and equals the public key algorithm of the candidate issuer certificate.\n    \n    item \\*\n    \n    If the issuer certificate includes a key usage restriction, it must allow for certificate signing (keyCertSign).\n    \n\nThe lookup first searches for issuer certificates in the trust store. If it does not find a match there it consults the list of untrusted (\"intermediate\" CA) certificates, if provided. If one issuer certificate was found in the trust store, the list of untrusted certificates will not be consulted anymore to find further issuer certificates. Therefore, either only the root certificate or an uninterrupted chain to the root certificate must be provided in the trust store for a successful verification, if **X509\\_V\\_FLAG\\_PARTIAL\\_CHAIN** is not enabled.\n\n### Certification Path Validation[¶](#certification-path-validation \"Permanent link\")\n\nWhen the certificate chain building process was successful the chain components and their links are checked thoroughly.\n\nThe first step is to check that each certificate is well-formed. Part of these checks are enabled only if the **\\-x509\\_strict** option is given.\n\nThe second step is to check the X.509v3 extensions of every certificate for consistency with the intended specific purpose, if any. If no purpose is given explicitly then no such checks are done except for CMS signature checking, where by default `smimesign` is checked, and SSL/(D)TLS connection setup, where by default `sslserver` or `sslclient` are checked. The X.509v3 extensions of the target or \"leaf\" certificate must be compatible with the specified purpose. All other certificates down the chain are checked to be valid CA certificates, and possibly also further non-standard checks are performed. The precise extensions required are described in detail in the [\"Certificate Extensions\"](#certificate-extensions) section below.\n\nThe third step is to check the trust settings on the last certificate or trust anchor, which typically is given as a self-signed root CA certificate. If specified, it must be trusted for the given use. For compatibility, an apparently self-signed certificate with no trust attributes is considered to be valid for all uses.\n\nThe fourth, and final, step is to check the validity of the certificate chain. For each element in the chain, including the root CA certificate, the validity period as specified by the `notBefore` and `notAfter` fields is checked against the current system time. The **\\-attime** flag may be used to use a reference time other than \"now.\" The certificate signature is checked as well (except for the signature of the typically self-signed root CA certificate, which is verified only if the **\\-check\\_ss\\_sig** option is given). When verifying a certificate signature the keyUsage extension (if present) of the candidate issuer certificate is checked to permit digitalSignature for signing proxy certificates or to permit keyCertSign for signing other certificates, respectively. If all operations complete successfully then certificate is considered valid. If any operation fails then the certificate is not valid.\n\n## OPTIONS[¶](#options \"Permanent link\")\n\n### Trusted Certificate Options[¶](#trusted-certificate-options \"Permanent link\")\n\nThe following options specify how to supply the certificates that can be used as trust anchors for certain uses. As mentioned, a collection of such certificates is called a *trust store*.\n\nNote that OpenSSL does not provide a default set of trust anchors. Many Linux distributions include a system default and configure OpenSSL to point to that. Mozilla maintains an influential trust store that can be found at [https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/about/governance/policies/security-group/certs/](https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/about/governance/policies/security-group/certs/).\n\nThe certificates to add to the trust store can be specified using following options.\n\n-   **\\-CAfile** *file*\n    \n    Load the specified file which contains a trusted certificate in DER format or potentially several of them in case the input is in PEM format. PEM-encoded certificates may also have trust attributes set.\n    \n-   **\\-no-CAfile**\n    \n    Do not load the default file of trusted certificates.\n    \n-   **\\-CApath** *dir*\n    \n    Use the specified directory as a collection of trusted certificates, i.e., a trust store. Files should be named with the hash value of the X.509 SubjectName of each certificate. This is so that the library can extract the IssuerName, hash it, and directly lookup the file to get the issuer certificate. See [openssl-rehash(1)](../openssl-rehash/) for information on creating this type of directory.\n    \n-   **\\-no-CApath**\n    \n    Do not use the default directory of trusted certificates.\n    \n-   **\\-CAstore** *uri*\n    \n    Use *uri* as a store of CA certificates. The URI may indicate a single certificate, as well as a collection of them. With URIs in the `file:` scheme, this acts as **\\-CAfile** or **\\-CApath**, depending on if the URI indicates a single file or directory. See [ossl\\_store-file(7)](../../man7/ossl_store-file/) for more information on the `file:` scheme.\n    \n    These certificates are also used when building the server certificate chain (for example with [openssl-s\\_server(1)](../openssl-s_server/)) or client certificate chain (for example with [openssl-s\\_time(1)](../openssl-s_time/)).\n    \n-   **\\-no-CAstore**\n    \n    Do not use the default store of trusted CA certificates.\n    \n\n### Verification Options[¶](#verification-options \"Permanent link\")\n\nThe certificate verification can be fine-tuned with the following flags.\n\n-   **\\-verbose**\n    \n    Print extra information about the operations being performed.\n    \n-   **\\-attime** *timestamp*\n    \n    Perform validation checks using time specified by *timestamp* and not current system time. *timestamp* is the number of seconds since January 1, 1970 (i.e., the Unix Epoch).\n    \n-   **\\-no\\_check\\_time**\n    \n    This option suppresses checking the validity period of certificates and CRLs against the current time. If option **\\-attime** is used to specify a verification time, the check is not suppressed.\n    \n-   **\\-x509\\_strict**\n    \n    This disables non-compliant workarounds for broken certificates. Thus errors are thrown on certificates not compliant with RFC 5280.\n    \n    When this option is set, among others, the following certificate well-formedness conditions are checked:\n    \n    -   CA certificates must explicitly include the keyUsage extension.\n    -   If a pathLenConstraint is given the key usage keyCertSign must be allowed.\n    -   The pathLenConstraint must not be given for non-CA certificates.\n    -   The issuer name of any certificate must not be empty.\n    -   The subject name of CA certs, certs with keyUsage crlSign, and certs without subjectAlternativeName must not be empty.\n    -   If a subjectAlternativeName extension is given it must not be empty.\n    -   The signatureAlgorithm field and the cert signature must be consistent.\n    -   The authorityKeyIdentifier must be given for X.509v3 certs unless they are apparently self-signed.\n    -   The subjectKeyIdentifier must be given for all X.509v3 CA certs.\n-   **\\-ignore\\_critical**\n    \n    Normally if an unhandled critical extension is present that is not supported by OpenSSL the certificate is rejected (as required by RFC5280). If this option is set critical extensions are ignored.\n    \n-   **\\-issuer\\_checks**\n    \n    Ignored.\n    \n-   **\\-crl\\_check**\n    \n    Checks end-entity certificate validity by attempting to look up a valid CRL. If a valid CRL cannot be found an error occurs.\n    \n-   **\\-crl\\_check\\_all**\n    \n    Checks the validity of *all* certificates in the chain by attempting to look up valid CRLs.\n    \n-   **\\-use\\_deltas**\n    \n    Enable support for delta CRLs.\n    \n-   **\\-extended\\_crl**\n    \n    Enable extended CRL features such as indirect CRLs and alternate CRL signing keys.\n    \n-   **\\-suiteB\\_128\\_only**, **\\-suiteB\\_128**, **\\-suiteB\\_192**\n    \n    Enable the Suite B mode operation at 128 bit Level of Security, 128 bit or 192 bit, or only 192 bit Level of Security respectively. See RFC6460 for details. In particular the supported signature algorithms are reduced to support only ECDSA and SHA256 or SHA384 and only the elliptic curves P-256 and P-384.\n    \n-   **\\-auth\\_level** *level*\n    \n    Set the certificate chain authentication security level to *level*. The authentication security level determines the acceptable signature and public key strength when verifying certificate chains. For a certificate chain to validate, the public keys of all the certificates must meet the specified security *level*. The signature algorithm security level is enforced for all the certificates in the chain except for the chain's *trust anchor*, which is either directly trusted or validated by means other than its signature. See [SSL\\_CTX\\_set\\_security\\_level(3)](../../man3/SSL_CTX_set_security_level/) for the definitions of the available levels. The default security level is -1, or \"not set\". At security level 0 or lower all algorithms are acceptable. Security level 1 requires at least 80-bit-equivalent security and is broadly interoperable, though it will, for example, reject MD5 signatures or RSA keys shorter than 1024 bits.\n    \n-   **\\-partial\\_chain**\n    \n    Allow verification to succeed if an incomplete chain can be built. That is, a chain ending in a certificate that normally would not be trusted (because it is not self-signed and has no matching positive trust attributes) but is an element of the trust store. This certificate may be self-issued or belong to an intermediate CA.\n    \n-   **\\-check\\_ss\\_sig**\n    \n    Verify the signature of the last certificate in a chain if the certificate is apparently self-signed. This is prohibited and will result in an error if it is a non-conforming CA certificate with key usage restrictions not including the keyCertSign bit. This verification is disabled by default because it doesn't add any security.\n    \n-   **\\-allow\\_proxy\\_certs**\n    \n    Allow the verification of proxy certificates.\n    \n-   **\\-trusted\\_first**\n    \n    This option is on by default and cannot be disabled.\n    \n    When constructing the certificate chain, the trusted certificates specified via **\\-CAfile**, **\\-CApath**, **\\-CAstore** or **\\-trusted** are always used before any certificates specified via **\\-untrusted**.\n    \n-   **\\-no\\_alt\\_chains**\n    \n    Since **\\-trusted\\_first** is always on, this option has no effect.\n    \n-   **\\-trusted** *file*\n    \n    Parse *file* as a set of one or more certificates. Each of them qualifies as trusted if has a suitable positive trust attribute or it is apparently self-signed or the **\\-partial\\_chain** option is specified. This option implies the **\\-no-CAfile**, **\\-no-CApath**, and **\\-no-CAstore** options and it cannot be used with the **\\-CAfile**, **\\-CApath** or **\\-CAstore** options, so only certificates specified using the **\\-trusted** option are trust anchors. This option may be used multiple times.\n    \n-   **\\-untrusted** *file*\n    \n    Parse *file* as a set of one or more certificates. All certificates (typically of intermediate CAs) are considered untrusted and may be used to construct a certificate chain from the target certificate to a trust anchor. This option may be used multiple times.\n    \n-   **\\-policy** *arg*\n    \n    Enable policy processing and add *arg* to the user-initial-policy-set (see RFC5280). The policy *arg* can be an object name or an OID in numeric form. This argument can appear more than once.\n    \n-   **\\-explicit\\_policy**\n    \n    Set policy variable require-explicit-policy (see RFC5280).\n    \n-   **\\-policy\\_check**\n    \n    Enables certificate policy processing.\n    \n-   **\\-policy\\_print**\n    \n    Print out diagnostics related to policy processing.\n    \n-   **\\-inhibit\\_any**\n    \n    Set policy variable inhibit-any-policy (see RFC5280).\n    \n-   **\\-inhibit\\_map**\n    \n    Set policy variable inhibit-policy-mapping (see RFC5280).\n    \n-   **\\-purpose** *purpose*\n    \n    A high-level specification of the intended use of the target certificate. Currently predefined purposes are `sslclient`, `sslserver`, `nssslserver`, `smimesign`, `smimeencrypt`, `crlsign`, `ocsphelper`, `timestampsign`, `codesign` and `any`. If peer certificate verification is enabled, by default the TLS implementation and thus the commands [openssl-s\\_client(1)](../openssl-s_client/) and [openssl-s\\_server(1)](../openssl-s_server/) check for consistency with TLS server (`sslserver`) or TLS client use (`sslclient`), respectively. By default, CMS signature validation, which can be done via [openssl-cms(1)](../openssl-cms/), checks for consistency with the S/MIME signing purpose (`smimesign`).\n    \n    While IETF RFC 5280 says that **id-kp-serverAuth** and **id-kp-clientAuth** are only for WWW use, in practice they are used for all kinds of TLS clients and servers, and this is what OpenSSL assumes as well.\n    \n    For details on the checks performed see the [\"Certificate Extensions\"](#certificate-extensions) section.\n    \n-   **\\-verify\\_depth** *num*\n    \n    Limit the certificate chain to *num* intermediate CA certificates. A maximal depth chain can have up to *num*+2 certificates, since neither the end-entity certificate nor the trust-anchor certificate count against the **\\-verify\\_depth** limit.\n    \n-   **\\-verify\\_email** *email*\n    \n    Verify if *email* matches the email address in Subject Alternative Name or the email in the subject Distinguished Name.\n    \n-   **\\-verify\\_hostname** *hostname*\n    \n    Verify if *hostname* matches DNS name in Subject Alternative Name or Common Name in the subject certificate.\n    \n-   **\\-verify\\_ip** *ip*\n    \n    Verify if *ip* matches the IP address in Subject Alternative Name of the subject certificate.\n    \n-   **\\-verify\\_name** *name*\n    \n    Use a set of verification parameters, also known as verification method, identified by *name*. The currently predefined methods are named `ssl_client`, `ssl_server`, `smime_sign` with alias `pkcs7`, `smime_encrypt`, `code_sign`, and `default`. These mimic the combinations of purpose and trust settings used in SSL/(D)TLS, CMS/PKCS7 (including S/MIME), and code signing.\n    \n    The verification parameters include the trust model, various flags that can partly be set also via other command-line options, and the verification purpose, which in turn implies certificate key usage and extended key usage requirements.\n    \n    The trust model determines which auxiliary trust or reject OIDs are applicable to verifying the given certificate chain. They can be given using the **\\-addtrust** and **\\-addreject** options for [openssl-x509(1)](../openssl-x509/).\n    \n\n### Extended Verification Options[¶](#extended-verification-options \"Permanent link\")\n\nSometimes there may be more than one certificate chain leading to an end-entity certificate. This usually happens when a root or intermediate CA signs a certificate for another a CA in other organization. Another reason is when a CA might have intermediates that use two different signature formats, such as a SHA-1 and a SHA-256 digest.\n\nThe following options can be used to provide data that will allow the OpenSSL command to generate an alternative chain.\n\n-   **\\-xkey** *infile*, **\\-xcert** *infile*, **\\-xchain**\n    \n    Specify an extra certificate, private key and certificate chain. These behave in the same manner as the **\\-cert**, **\\-key** and **\\-cert\\_chain** options. When specified, the callback returning the first valid chain will be in use by the client.\n    \n-   **\\-xchain\\_build**\n    \n    Specify whether the application should build the certificate chain to be provided to the server for the extra certificates via the **\\-xkey**, **\\-xcert**, and **\\-xchain** options.\n    \n-   **\\-xcertform** **DER**|**PEM**|**P12**\n    \n    The input format for the extra certificate. This option has no effect and is retained for backward compatibility only.\n    \n-   **\\-xkeyform** **DER**|**PEM**|**P12**\n    \n    The input format for the extra key. This option has no effect and is retained for backward compatibility only.\n    \n\n### Certificate Extensions[¶](#certificate-extensions \"Permanent link\")\n\nOptions like **\\-purpose** and **\\-verify\\_name** trigger the processing of specific certificate extensions, which determine what certificates can be used for.\n\nThe following two subsections on the **basicConstraints** and **keyUsage** extensions and on X.509v1 certificates apply to *all* CA certificates regardless of any required purpose.\n\n#### Basic Constraints[¶](#basic-constraints \"Permanent link\")\n\nThe basicConstraints extension CA flag is used to determine whether the certificate can be used as a CA. If the CA flag is true then it is a CA, if the CA flag is false then it is not a CA. *All* CAs should have the CA flag set to true.\n\nIf the basicConstraints extension is absent, which includes the case that it is an X.509v1 certificate, then the certificate is considered to be a \"possible CA\" and other extensions are checked according to the intended use of the certificate. The treatment of certificates without basicConstraints as a CA is presently supported, but this could change in the future.\n\n#### Key Usage[¶](#key-usage \"Permanent link\")\n\nIf the keyUsage extension is present then additional restraints are made on the uses of the certificate. A CA certificate *must* have the keyCertSign bit set if the keyUsage extension is present. Further restrictions are checked depending on the required purpose, if any; for details see the list given in the next subsection.\n\n#### Extended Key Usage[¶](#extended-key-usage \"Permanent link\")\n\nThe extKeyUsage (EKU) extension places additional restrictions on certificate use. If this extension is present (whether critical or not) in an end-entity certificate, the key is allowed only for the uses specified, while the special EKU **anyExtendedKeyUsage** allows for all uses.\n\nNote that according to RFC 5280 section 4.2.1.12, the Extended Key Usage extension will appear only in end-entity certificates, and consequently the standard certification path validation described in its section 6 does not include EKU checks for CA certificates. The CA/Browser Forum requires for TLS server, S/MIME, and code signing use the presence of respective EKUs in subordinate CA certificates (while excluding them for root CA certificates), while taking over from RFC 5280 the certificate validity concept and certificate path validation.\n\nFor historic reasons, OpenSSL has its own way of interpreting and checking EKU extensions on CA certificates, which may change in the future. It does not require the presence of EKU extensions in CA certificates, but in case the verification purpose is `sslclient`, `nssslserver`, `sslserver`, `smimesign`, or `smimeencrypt`, it checks that any present EKU extension (that does not contain **anyExtendedKeyUsage**) contains the respective EKU as detailed below. Moreover, it does these checks even for trust anchor certificates.\n\n#### Duplicate Extensions[¶](#duplicate-extensions \"Permanent link\")\n\nAccording to RFC 5280 section 4.2, a certificate MUST NOT include more than one instance of a particular extension. If a duplicate extension is detected, the certificate is rejected. This check applies to all certificates: end-entity certificates, intermediate CA certificates, and root CA (trust anchor) certificates.\n\n#### Checks Implied by Specific Predefined Policies[¶](#checks-implied-by-specific-predefined-policies \"Permanent link\")\n\nA specific description of each check is given below. The comments about basicConstraints and keyUsage and X.509v1 certificates above apply to **all** CA certificates.\n\n-   **(D)TLS Client** (`sslclient`)\n    \n    Any given extended key usage extension must allow for `clientAuth` (\"TLS WWW client authentication\").\n    \n    For target certificates, the key usage must allow for `digitalSignature` and/or `keyAgreement`. The Netscape certificate type must be absent or have the SSL client bit set.\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply. In addition, the Netscape certificate type must be absent or have the SSL CA bit set. This is used as a workaround if the basicConstraints extension is absent.\n    \n-   **(D)TLS Server** (`sslserver`)\n    \n    Any given extended key usage extension must allow for `serverAuth` (\"TLS WWW server authentication\") and/or include one of the SGC OIDs.\n    \n    For target certificates, the key usage must allow for `digitalSignature`, `keyEncipherment`, and/or `keyAgreement`. The Netscape certificate type must be absent or have the SSL server bit set.\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply. In addition, the Netscape certificate type must be absent or have the SSL CA bit set. This is used as a workaround if the basicConstraints extension is absent.\n    \n-   **Netscape SSL Server** (`nssslserver`)\n    \n    In addition to what has been described for **sslserver**, for a Netscape SSL client to connect to an SSL server, its EE certificate must have the **keyEncipherment** bit set if the keyUsage extension is present. This isn't always valid because some cipher suites use the key for digital signing. Otherwise it is the same as a normal SSL server.\n    \n-   **Common S/MIME Checks**\n    \n    Any given extended key usage extension must allow for `emailProtection`.\n    \n    For target certificates, the Netscape certificate type must be absent or should have the S/MIME bit set. If the S/MIME bit is not set in the Netscape certificate type then the SSL client bit is tolerated as an alternative but a warning is shown. This is because some Verisign certificates don't set the S/MIME bit.\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply. In addition, the Netscape certificate type must be absent or have the S/MIME CA bit set. This is used as a workaround if the basicConstraints extension is absent.\n    \n-   **S/MIME Signing** (`smimesign`)\n    \n    In addition to the common S/MIME checks, for target certificates the key usage must allow for `digitalSignature` and/or **nonRepudiation** (or its alias name **contentCommitment**).\n    \n-   **S/MIME Encryption** (`smimeencrypt`)\n    \n    In addition to the common S/MIME checks, for target certificates the key usage must allow for `keyEncipherment`.\n    \n-   **CRL Signing** (`crlsign`)\n    \n    For target certificates, the key usage must allow for `cRLSign`.\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply. Except in this case the basicConstraints extension must be present.\n    \n-   **OCSP Helper** (`ocsphelper`)\n    \n    For target certificates, no checks are performed at this stage, but special checks apply; see [OCSP\\_basic\\_verify(3)](../../man3/OCSP_resp_find_status/).\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply.\n    \n-   **Timestamp Signing** (`timestampsign`)\n    \n    For target certificates, if the key usage extension is present, it must include `digitalSignature` and/or `nonRepudiation` (or its alias name `contentCommitment`) and must not include other bits. The EKU extension must be present and contain `timeStamping` only. Moreover, it must be marked as critical.\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply.\n    \n-   **Code Signing** (`codesign`)\n    \n    For target certificates, the key usage extension must be present and marked critical and include <digitalSignature>, but must not include `keyCertSign` nor `cRLSign`. The EKU extension must be present and contain `codeSign`, but must not include `anyExtendedKeyUsage` nor `serverAuth`.\n    \n    For all other certificates the normal CA checks apply.\n    \n\n## BUGS[¶](#bugs \"Permanent link\")\n\nThe issuer checks still suffer from limitations in the underlying X509\\_LOOKUP API. Trusted certificates with matching subject name must appear in a file (as specified by the **\\-CAfile** option), a directory (as specified by **\\-CApath**), or a store (as specified by **\\-CAstore**). If there are multiple such matches, possibly in multiple locations, only the first one (in the mentioned order of locations) is recognised.\n\n## SEE ALSO[¶](#see-also \"Permanent link\")\n\n[X509\\_verify\\_cert(3)](../../man3/X509_verify_cert/), [OCSP\\_basic\\_verify(3)](../../man3/OCSP_resp_find_status/), [openssl-verify(1)](../openssl-verify/), [openssl-ocsp(1)](../openssl-ocsp/), [openssl-ts(1)](../openssl-ts/), [openssl-s\\_client(1)](../openssl-s_client/), [openssl-s\\_server(1)](../openssl-s_server/), [openssl-smime(1)](../openssl-smime/), [openssl-cmp(1)](../openssl-cmp/), [openssl-cms(1)](../openssl-cms/)\n\n## HISTORY[¶](#history \"Permanent link\")\n\nSince OpenSSL 1.1.0, the **\\-trusted\\_first** option is always enabled.\n\nThe checks enabled by **\\-x509\\_strict** have been extended in OpenSSL 3.0, which has been partially reverted in OpenSSL 4.0.\n\n## COPYRIGHT[¶](#copyright \"Permanent link\")\n\nCopyright 2000-2026 The OpenSSL Project Authors. You can obtain a copy in the file LICENSE in the source distribution or at [https://www.openssl.org/source/license.html](https://www.openssl.org/source/license.html).\n\nMade with [Material for MkDocs](https://squidfunk.github.io/mkdocs-material/)",

[MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US)\n2.  [Mozilla](../index.html)\n3.  [Browser security](../Security.html)\n4.  A Web PKI x509 certificate primer\n\n# A Web PKI x509 certificate primer\n\n[In This Article](x509_Certificates.html#toc)\n\n1.  [Extensions](x509_Certificates.html#Extensions_2)\n    1.  [Subject alternate name](x509_Certificates.html#Subject_alternate_name)\n    2.  [Basic constraints](x509_Certificates.html#Basic_constraints)\n    3.  [Key usage](x509_Certificates.html#Key_usage)\n    4.  [Extended key usages](x509_Certificates.html#Extended_key_usages)\n    5.  [Name constraints](x509_Certificates.html#Name_constraints)\n    6.  [Authority information access](x509_Certificates.html#Authority_information_access)\n2.  [Self-signed certificates](x509_Certificates.html#Self-signed_certificates)\n3.  [CAs included in Firefox](x509_Certificates.html#CAs_included_in_Firefox)\n4.  [Running your Own CA](x509_Certificates.html#Running_your_Own_CA_2)\n5.  [Generate your CA Root](x509_Certificates.html#Generate_your_CA_Root_2)\n6.  [Generate your Intermediate cert](x509_Certificates.html#Generate_your_Intermediate_cert_2)\n7.  [Generate the end entity certificate](x509_Certificates.html#Generate_the_end_entity_certificate_2)\n\nX.509 (in this document referred as x509) is an ITU standard to describe certificates. This article provides an overview of what these are and how they work.\n\nThree versions of the x509 standard have been defined for web-pki. In this document we will be referring to the current standard in use for web pki: x509 v3, which is described in detail in [RFC 5280](http://tools.ietf.org/html/5280). In general, x509 certificates bind a signature to a validity period, a public key, a subject, an issuer, and a set of extensions. The extensions define extra properties of the certificate such as extra attributes of the certificate or constraints on the use of the certificate. In order for a certificate to be valid these three requirements must be met:\n\n1.  There is a verification path from the site certificate to a trusted certificate of the user agent (ie if you follow the issuer path you will end on a self-signed certificate that is considered trusted by the browser).\n2.  The attributes of the certificates in the verification path have valid parameters for that verification (for example the validity period of all the certificates are valid for the time the verification is being done)\n3.  Revocation checks are considered OK for that particular validation.\n\nOne issue that is not commonly known is that the x509 trust graph is not a forest (a bunch of trees where each root is a trusted root) but a cyclic graph, where the same key/issuer can be a root or an intermediate for another root in the browsers key store (when roots create intermediates for each other it is called cross-signing).\n\n## Extensions\n\nWhile [RFC 5280](http://tools.ietf.org/html/5280) defines 16 extensions for webpki in this document we will be describing the six extensions we considered critical for understanding. Certificates can have other extensions not described on [RFC 5280](http://tools.ietf.org/html/5280), but that is out of the scope of this document. Extensions can be marked as critical or non-critical, conforming certificate verification libraries should stop processing verification when encountering a critical extension that they do not understand ( and should continue processing if the extension is marked as non-critical) mozila::pkix has this behavior.\n\n### Subject alternate name\n\nThis extension defines what other names (such as DNS names) are valid for this certificate. This allows for a certificate to be used for more than one [FQDN](http://www.tech-faq.com/fqdn.html), for example you can have a certificate that is valid for both a.example.com and b.example.com\n\n### Basic constraints\n\nThis allows certificates to be asserted as issuing certificates (it is mandatory for CA certificates). It can also be used to express the maximum depth of the trust path from the CA.\n\n### Key usage\n\nThis extension is used to constrain the purpose for the key in the certificate. More than one key usage can be asserted. Examples of key usages are: `digitalSignature`, `keyEncipherment`, `dataEncipherment`, `keyCertSign`, and `cRLSign`. For CA certificates the `keyCertSign` bit **must** be set.\n\n### Extended key usages\n\nThis is another bitfield to constrain the usages of the key of the certificate. Its is directed mostly at what type of application the certificate was issued for. Examples of extended key usages are: `serverAuth`, `clientAuth`, and `OCSPSigning`. For end-entity certificates for PKI this extension is required to exist with the `serverAuth` bit asserted.\n\n### Name constraints\n\nThis is an extension exclusive for CA and indicates limits on the name space for its children. This is one of the most powerful extensions for businesses to have to help limit risk imposed by losing the private key of the CA.\n\n### Authority information access\n\nThis extension is primarily used to to describe the OCSP location for revocation checking. It is mandatory for certificates that chain up to a root in the Mozilla CA program.\n\n## Self-signed certificates\n\nThese are the steps to generate a certificate for www.example.com. Replace this value with the actual server name in the steps below.  \n1\\. Generate the key using the following command:\n\n-   `openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out key.pem -pkeyopt rsa_keygen_bits:2048`\n-   2048 is considered secure for the next 4 years.\n\n2\\. Generate CSR using this command:\n\n-   `openssl req -new -key key.pem -days 1096 -extensions v3_ca -batch -out example.csr -utf8 -subj '/CN=www.example.com'`\n-   This creates a new Certificate Signing Request (CSR) that will be valid for 3 years.\n\n3\\. Write extensions file by creating a new file with name `openssl.ss.cnf` with the following contents:\n\n-   basicConstraints = CA:FALSE\n-   subjectAltName =DNS:www.example.com\n-   extendedKeyUsage =serverAuth\n\n4\\. Self-sign csr (using SHA256) and append the extensions described in the file\n\n-   \"openssl x509 -req -sha256 -days 3650 -in example.csr -signkey key.pem -set\\_serial $ANY\\_INTEGER -extfile openssl.ss.cnf -out example.pem\"\n\nYou can now use example.pem as your certfile\n\n## CAs included in Firefox\n\nWhen you visit a secure website, Firefox will validate the website’s certificate by checking that the certificate that signed it is valid, and checking that the certificate that signed the parent certificate is valid and so forth up to a root certificate that is known to be valid. This chain of certificates is called the Certificate Hierarchy.\n\nIf your certificates will only be used to verify one domain (e.g. \\*.yourcompany.com) but you want others outside of your organization to be able to browse to your website using https without having to manually import a root certificate, then you can get an SSL certificate from one of the CAs who already have a root certificate included in the major browsers.\n\nFirefox uses a default set of X.509v3 root certificates for various [Certification Authorities (CAs)](http://www.mozilla.org/projects/security/certs/included/). The root certificates included by default have their \"trust bits\" set to indicate if the CA's root certificates may be used to verify certificates for SSL servers, S/MIME email users, and/or digitally-signed code objects without having to ask users for further permission or information.\n\nCAs apply to have their root certificates [included by default in Mozilla products](http://www.mozilla.org/projects/security/certs/included/) by following the [Mozilla CA Certificate Policy](http://www.mozilla.org/projects/security/certs/policy/) and applying for inclusion as per [CA:How\\_to\\_apply](https://wiki.mozilla.org/CA:How_to_apply \"CA:How to apply\"). Users may override the default root certificate settings using the [Certificate Manager](https://wiki.mozilla.org/CA:UserCertDB).\n\nSome organizations make use of the set of CAs included in Mozilla's products. If you wish to do this, you should read the relevant part of the [Mozilla CA FAQ](https://wiki.mozilla.org/CA:FAQ#Can_I_use_Mozilla.27s_set_of_CA_certificates.3F) before doing so.\n\n## Running your Own CA\n\nIf you are going to have your own CA, we recommend building 3 certificates: a long term root cert, a medium term intermediate cert, and a short term end-entity cert. This type of hierarchy allows for a relatively simple long term root to be distributed to clients, and some flexibility on the intermediate cert so that you can change parameters based on best practices and security research.\n\n## Generate your CA Root\n\nUpdate \\*.example.com and \\*.example.net below to match your domains.\n\nAssumptions:\n\n-   You are the domain owner of \\*.example.com and \\*.example.net.\n-   Your computer is **not** connected to the internet.\n\nSteps to generate your CA root certificate:\n\n1.  Generate key\n    -   \"openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out rootkey.pem -pkeyopt rsa\\_keygen\\_bits:4096\"\n    -   4096 is considered secure for the next 15 years.\n2.  Generate csr\n    -   \"openssl req -new -key rootkey.pem -days 5480 -extensions v3\\_ca -batch -out root.csr -utf8 -subj '/C=US/O=Orgname/OU=SomeInternalName'\n    -   Make a new Certificate Signing Request (CSR) that will be valid for 15 years.\n3.  Write extensions File (openssl.root.cnf)\n    -   basicConstraints = critical, CA:TRUE\n    -   keyUsage = keyCertSign, cRLSign\n    -   subjectKeyIdentifier = hash\n    -   nameConstraints = permitted;DNS:example.com,permitted;DNS:example.net\n4.  Self-sign csr (using SHA256) and append the extensions described in the file\n    -   \"openssl x509 -req -sha256 -days 3650 -in root.csr -signkey rootkey.pem -set\\_serial $ANY\\_SMALL\\_INTEGER -extfile openssl.root.cnf -out root.pem\"\n\nNow you have CA pem file with its associated key.\n\n## Generate your Intermediate cert\n\nThe following steps create an intermediate cert that is valid for 8 years.\n\n1.  Generate key\n    -   \"openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out r=intkey.pem -pkeyopt rsa\\_keygen\\_bits: 3072\"\n    -   A 3072 bit key is considered secure for the next 8 years.\n2.  Generate csr\n    -   \"openssl req -new -key intkey.pem -days 2922 -extensions v3\\_ca -batch -out int.csr - utf8 -subj '/C=US/O=Orgname/OU=SomeInternalName2'\n    -   Make a new Certificate Signing Request (CSR) that will be valid for 8 years.\n3.  Write extensions File (openssl.int.cnf)\n    -   basicConstraints = critical, CA:TRUE\n    -   authorityKeyIdentifier = keyid, issuer\n    -   subjectKeyIdentifier = hash\n    -   keyUsage = keyCertSign, cRLSign\n    -   extendedKeyUsage =serverAuth\n    -   authorityInfoAccess = OCSP;URI:[http://ocsp.example.com:8888/](http://ocsp.example.com:8888/)\n4.  Sign the intermediate csr with the root key and the intermediate extensions\n    -   \"openssl x509 -req -sha256 -days 2922 -in int.csr -CAkey rootkey.pem -CA root.pem -set\\_serial $SOME\\_LARGE\\_INTEGER -out int.pem -extfile openssl.int.cnf\"\n\n## Generate the end entity certificate\n\nUpdate www.example.com below to match your domain.\n\n1.  Generate key\n    -   \"openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out eekey.pem -pkeyopt rsa\\_keygen\\_bits: 2048\"\n    -   2048 is considered secure for the next 4 years.\n2.  Generate csr\n    -   \"openssl req -new -key key.pem -days 1096 -extensions v3\\_ca -batch -out example.csr - utf8 -subj '/CN=www.example.com'\n    -   Make a new Certificate Signing Request (CSR) that will be valid for 3 years.\n3.  Write extensions file (make a new file with name openssl.ss.cnf with the following contents)\n    -   basicConstraints = CA:FALSE\n    -   subjectAltName =DNS:www.example.com\n    -   extendedKeyUsage =serverAuth\n    -   authorityInfoAccess = OCSP;URI:[http://ocsp.example.com:80/](http://ocsp.example.com:80/)\n4.  Intermediate sings the csr (using SHA256) and appends the extensions described in the file\n    -   \"openssl x509 -req -sha256 -days 1096 -in example.csr -CAkey intkey.pem -CA int.pem -set\\_serial $SOME\\_LARGE\\_INTEGER -out www.example.com.pem -extfile openssl.int.cnf\"\n\n# Security Notes\n\nThere are several organizations that provide recommendations regarding the security parameters for key/hash sizes given current computational power. For the end date of the root cert created following the instructions in this page (year 2017). These are the recomendations of bit sizes (from [http://www.keylength.com/](http://www.keylength.com/)):\n\n \n\nAsymmetric\n\nECC(Key)\n\nHash\n\nLinestra(2004)\n\n1902\n\n172\n\n172\n\nEcrypt 2012\n\n2432\n\n224\n\n224\n\nNIST 2012\n\n2048\n\n224\n\n224\n\nANSSI 2010\n\n4096\n\n200\n\n256\n\n[RFC 3766](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3766)\n\n2358\n\n200\n\n\\---\n\nBSI\n\n1976\n\n256\n\n256\n\nIn other words, SHA1 is now deprecated for new uses. We should use at least 3072 key sizes and at least a 256 ECC curve. Thus the recommendation here is for the root to be 4096 if using RSA and p384 for the root key. (p384 also chosen for compatibility as most SSL/TLS implementations support this part of suite B).\n\n# Error Codes in Firefox\n\nHere are some common errors that might be encountered when working with certificates in Firefox.\n\nError Code\n\nWhat It Means\n\nWhat Can I Do\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_BAD\\_DER\n\nA certificate is not properly encoded according to ASN.1 (DER) encoding\n\nRe-generate the improperly-encoded certificate\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_CA\\_CERT\\_INVALID\n\nAn end-entity certificate is being used to issue another certificate\n\nEnsure that any certificate intended to issue certificates has a basic constraints extension with cA: TRUE\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_BAD\\_SIGNATURE\n\nA signature on a certificate is improperly formatted or the certificate has been tampered with\n\nRe-issue the certificate with the bad signature\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_CERT\\_BAD\\_ACCESS\\_LOCATION\n\nThe OCSP URI in the authorityInformationAccess extension is improperly formed\n\nRe-generate the certificate with a well-formed OCSP URI\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_CERT\\_NOT\\_IN\\_NAME\\_SPACE\n\nA certificate has a common name or subject alternative name that is not in the namespace of an issuing certificate\n\nRe-issue the certificate with names that are within the namespace of all certificates in the chain\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_CERT\\_SIGNATURE\\_ALGORITHM\\_DISABLED\n\nA certificate has been signed with an obsolete algorithm\n\nRe-sign the certificate using a modern algorithm\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_EXPIRED\\_CERTIFICATE\n\nA certificate is too old to be used\n\nRe-generate the certificate\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_EXTENSION\\_VALUE\\_INVALID\n\nA certificate has an extension with an empty value\n\nRe-generate the certificate without the extension, or re-generate it with a non-empty value\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_INADEQUATE\\_CERT\\_TYPE\n\nA certificate has an extended key usage extension that does not assert a required usage, or an end-entity certificate asserts the id-kp-OCSPSigning usage when it shouldn't\n\nRe-generate the certificate with the appropriate extended key usage values\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_INADEQUATE\\_KEY\\_USAGE\n\nA certificate has a key usage extension that does not assert a required usage\n\nRe-generate the certificate with the appropriate key usage values\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_INVALID\\_ALGORITHM\n\nA certificate has been signed with an unknown algorithm\n\nRe-sign the certificate with a standardized certificate signing algorithm\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_INVALID\\_TIME\n\nA time field in a certificate has an invalid value\n\nRe-generate the certificate with valid encodings for time fields\n\nMOZILLA\\_PKIX\\_ERROR\\_KEY\\_PINNING\\_FAILURE\n\n \n\n \n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_PATH\\_LEN\\_CONSTRAINT\\_INVALID\n\n \n\n \n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_UNKNOWN\\_CRITICAL\\_EXTENSION\n\nA certificate contains an extension marked as critical that is not handled by mozilla::pkix\n\nRe-generate the certificate without the extension or with it not marked as critical\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_UNKNOWN\\_ISSUER\n\nEither a missing intermediate or root certificate is necessary to verify the certificate\n\nImport the root certificate into Firefox or have the server send the intermediate\n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_INVALID\\_KEY\n\n \n\n \n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_UNSUPPORTED\\_KEYALG\n\n \n\n \n\nSEC\\_ERROR\\_EXPIRED\\_ISSUER\\_CERTIFICATE\n\nAn issuer certificate is too old\n\nRe-issue the issuer certificate\n\nMOZILLA\\_PKIX\\_ERROR\\_CA\\_CERT\\_USED\\_AS\\_END\\_ENTITY\n\nA certificate with a basic constraints extension with cA:TRUE is being used as an end-entity certificate\n\nRe-generate the end-entity certificate without the basic constraints extension\n\nMOZILLA\\_PKIX\\_ERROR\\_INADEQUATE\\_KEY\\_SIZE\n\nA certificate has a key that is too small to be secure\n\nRe-generate a larger key and issue a certificate using that key\n\n## Document Tags and Contributors\n\n**Tags:** \n\n-   [Firefox](../../tag/Firefox.html)\n-   [Guide](../../tag/Guide.html)\n-   [Security](../../tag/Security.html)\n\n **Contributors to this page:** [hbloomer](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/profiles/hbloomer), No data is sent to any server.\n[...]\nPaste one or more PEM-encoded certificates above to validate the certificate chain. The tool checks chain order, expiration, self-signed roots, completeness, and duplicates. All processing happens locally in your browser.\n[...]\nYou have a bundle of SSL certificates and need to verify that the chain is complete and correctly ordered — leaf certificate first, intermediates next, root CA last. This tool parses the chain and validates each link.\n[...]\n1. Chain order: Each certificate’s Issuer must match the next certificate’s Subject\n2. Expiration: Flags expired certificates and those expiring within 30 days\n3. Self-signed root: The last certificate should be self-signed (Subject equals Issuer)\n4. Completeness: Warns if the chain appears incomplete (e.g., missing intermediate)\n5. Duplicates: Detects duplicate certificates in the chain\n[...]\nA typical certificate chain has three levels:\n[...]\n```\n[Leaf Certificate]     ← Your domain's certificate\n  └── signed by →\n[Intermediate CA]      ← Certificate Authority's intermediate\n  └── signed by →\n[Root CA]              ← Trust anchor (self-signed)\n```\n[...]\nWeb servers should send the leaf and intermediate certificates. The root CA is usually pre-installed in browsers and operating systems.\n[...]\nDoes this verify cryptographic signatures? This tool validates the chain structure (issuer/subject matching, order, expiration). Full cryptographic signature verification requires the complete trust store, which is beyond browser-based processing.\n[...]\nWhat format is expected? PEM format — one or more certificate blocks starting with`-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----`.\n[...]\nHow is this different from the SSL Certificate Decoder? The decoder shows detailed fields of individual certificates. The chain validator checks the relationships between certificates in a chain.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Interactive PKI Demos - Hands-On SSL/TLS Learning\nURL: https://fixmycert.com/demos\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: FixMyCert\nHighlights:\nfree interactive demos\n[...]\nVisualize the CA trust chain from root to intermediate to end-entity certificates. No upload to a backend.\n[...]\nThis analyzer helps you examine X.509 certificates in detail, understand their properties, verify their validity, and identify potential security concerns. Whether you're a DevOps engineer deploying certificates, a security professional auditing systems, or a developer debugging TLS issues, this tool provides comprehensive insights.\n[...]\n1. Paste Certificate: Copy your PEM-encoded certificate and paste it in the input area\n2. Upload File: Or upload a .pem, .crt, or .cer file directly\n3. Analyze: Click the 'Analyze Certificate' button to parse and examine the certificate\n4. Review Results: Explore the certificate chain, details, and security analysis\n[...]\n- Paste multiple certificates to analyze the complete chain\n- The tool automatically identifies Root CAs, Intermediate CAs, and Leaf certificates\n- Visual hierarchy shows the trust relationship between certificates\n[...]\n- Subject: The entity the certificate identifies (e.g., domain name, organization)\n- Issuer: The Certificate Authority (CA) that issued and signed the certificate\n- Serial Number: Unique identifier assigned by the CA\n- Version: X.509 version (v3 is current standard with extension support)\n[...]\n- Not Before: Date when the certificate becomes valid\n- Not After: Expiration date - certificate must be renewed before this date\n- Public TLS certificates are limited to 398 days maximum validity\n[...]\n- Subject Alternative Names (SAN): Additional domain names the certificate covers\n- Key Usage: Defines what cryptographic operations the key can perform\n- Extended Key Usage: Specific purposes like server auth, client auth, code signing\n- Basic Constraints: Indicates if certificate can sign other certificates (CA flag)\n[...]\nA certificate chain (or chain of trust) establishes the trustworthiness of a certificate through a hierarchy:\n[...]\n```\nRoot CA (Self-signed, trusted by browsers/OS)\n└── Intermediate CA (Signed by Root)\n    └── Leaf Certificate (Your domain certificate)\n```\n[...]\nValidation Process:\n[...]\n1. Browser receives the leaf certificate for your domain\n2. Checks if the issuer's certificate is available (intermediate CA)\n3. Continues up the chain until reaching a trusted root CA\n4. Verifies each signature and validity period along the way\n[...]\n- Incomplete Chain: Missing intermediate certificates cause validation failures\n- Wrong Order: Certificates must be served in correct order (leaf first)\n- Expired Intermediate: Even if leaf is valid, expired intermediate breaks the chain\n[...]\n- Expired certificates\n- Certificates not yet valid\n- Certificates expiring within 7 days\n[...]\n- Self-signed non-CA certificates\n- Missing Subject Alternative Names\n- Certificates expiring within 90 days\n[...]\n- Use RSA 2048-bit or higher (4096-bit for long-term security)\n- Use ECDSA P-256 or P-384 for modern deployments\n- Always use SHA-256 or stronger signature algorithms\n- Include all domain names in SAN extension\n- Automate renewal before expiration (Let's Encrypt, Certbot)\n[...]\n- Domain names in SAN match your configuration\n- Validity period is appropriate\n- Chain is complete and properly ordered\n- No security issues are flagged\n[...]\n- Check if certificate has expired\n- Verify domain names match\n- Ensure intermediate certificates are included\n- Confirm certificate is for correct environment (staging vs production)\n[...]\n### Q: Why do I need intermediate certificates?\n[...]\nA: Root CA certificates are kept offline for security. Intermediate CAs sign end-entity certificates on behalf of the root. Your server must send the complete chain (except the root) for browsers to validate the certificate.\n[...]\nA: Yes! All parsing happens locally in your browser using WebAssembly. No certificate data is transmitted to any server. This tool is completely client-side.\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSL Checker – SSL Certificate Checker, Chain Validator & TLS Test | VINAR TECH TOOLS\nURL: https://tools.vinar.tech/ssl/ssl-checker\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: VINAR TECH\nHighlights:\nThis free SSL Checker verifies your SSL certificate in real-time and analyzes expiry, issuer, SANs, chain hierarchy, TLS versions, cipher suites and server configuration.
