The 'public key blob' may contain\n certificates.\n\n Any public key algorithm may be offered for use in authentication.\n In particular, the list is not constrained by what was negotiated\n during key exchange. If the server does not support some algorithm,\n it MUST simply reject the request.\n\n The server MUST respond to this message with either\n SSH_MSG_USERAUTH_FAILURE or with the following:\n\n byte SSH_MSG_USERAUTH_PK_OK\n string public key algorithm name from the request\n string public key blob from the request\n[...]\nSSH Authentication Protocol\n[...]\n9. Host-Based Authentication: \"hostbased\"\n\n Some sites wish to allow authentication based on the host that the\n user is coming from and the user name on the remote host. While this\n form of authentication is not suitable for high-security sites, it\n can be very convenient in many environments. This form of\n authentication is OPTIONAL. When used, special care SHOULD be taken\n to prevent a regular user from obtaining the private host key.\n\n The client requests this form of authentication by sending the\n following message. It is similar to the UNIX \"rhosts\" and\n \"hosts.equiv\" styles of authentication, except that the identity of\n the client host is checked more rigorously.\n\n This method works by having the client send a signature created with\n the private key of the client host, which the server checks with that\n host's public key. Once the client host's identity is established,\n authorization (but no further authentication) is performed based on\n the user names on the server and the client, and the client host\n name.\n[...]\nRFC 4252 SSH Authentication Protocol January 2006\n\n byte SSH_MSG_USERAUTH_REQUEST\n string user name\n string service name\n string \"hostbased\"\n string public key algorithm for host key\n string public host key and certificates for client host\n string client host name expressed as the FQDN in US-ASCII\n string user name on the client host in ISO-10646 UTF-8 encoding\n [RFC3629]\n string signature\n\n Public key algorithm names for use in 'public key algorithm for host\n key' are defined in the transport layer specification [SSH-TRANS].\n The 'public host key and certificates for client host' may include\n certificates.\n\n The value of 'signature' is a signature with the private host key of\n the following data, in this order:\n\n string session identifier\n byte SSH_MSG_USERAUTH_REQUEST\n string user name\n string service name\n string \"hostbased\"\n string public key algorithm for host key\n string public host key and certificates for client host\n string client host name expressed as the FQDN in US-ASCII\n string user name on the client host in ISO-10646 UTF-8 encoding\n [RFC3629]\n\n The server MUST verify that the host key actually belongs to the\n client host named in the message, that the given user on that host is\n allowed to log in, and that the 'signature' value is a valid\n signature on the appropriate value by the given host key. The server\n MAY ignore the client 'user name', if it wants to authenticate only\n the client host.\n\n Whenever possible, it is RECOMMENDED that the server perform\n additional checks to verify that the network address obtained from\n the (untrusted) network matches the given client host name. This\n makes exploiting compromised host keys more difficult. Note that\n this may require special handling for connections coming through a\n firewall.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Release 9.3\nURL: https://www.openssh.com/txt/release-9.3\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nssh-add(1): when adding smartcard keys to ssh-agent(1) with the\n per-hop destination constraints (ssh-add -h ...) added in OpenSSH\n 8.9, a logic error prevented the constraints from being\n communicated to the agent. Your SSH client also attempts to authenticate the host. To do so your client needs to know the host's public key. Host public keys are stored in a simple database in`~/.ssh/known_hosts`. If your client can't find the host's public key in this database you get this warning. It's telling you that the host can't be authenticated!\n[...]\nWhat you're supposed to do is verify the key fingerprint out-of-band by asking an administrator or consulting a database or something. But no one does that. When you type \"yes\" the connection proceeds without authentication and the public key is permanently added to`~/.ssh/known_hosts`. This is the trust on first use (TOFU) anti-pattern.\n[...]\nSSH public key authentication introduces some weird operational constraints around host names that certificate authentication eliminates. As we've seen, when an SSH client connects to a host for the first time it displays a TOFU warning to the user. When the user types \"yes\" the host's public key is added locally to`~/.ssh/known_hosts`. This binding between the host name and a specific public key is permanent. If the host presents a different public key later, the user gets an even scarier host key verification failure error message that looks like this:\n[...]\nThe fingerprint for the ECDSA key sent by the remote host is\n[...]\nSHA256:2ae53QcOB0W6HO+XtPmMXk7To/MvMuhFxTj8ZD7eSsE.\n[...]\nPlease contact your system administrator.\n[...]\nAdd correct host key in ~/.ssh/known_hosts to get rid of this message.\nOffending ECDSA key in ~/.ssh/known_hosts:11\nECDSA host key for ec2-54-161-77-102.compute-1.amazonaws.com has changed and you have requested strict checking.\nHost key verification failed.\n[...]\nThis makes it operationally challenging to reuse host names. If`prod01.example.com` has a hardware failure, and it's replaced with a new host using the same name, host key verification failures will ensue. This usually results in a bunch engineers contacting secops to tell them they're being hacked.\n[...]\nIn any case, certificates fix all of this since a current name-to-public-key binding is communicated when a connection is established. Changing the host's public key is fine, as long as the host also gets a new certificate. You can safely reuse host names and even run multiple hosts with the same name. You'll never see a host key verification failure again. Beyond name reuse, we'll soon see that eliminating host key verification failures is one of the many ways certificate authentication facilitates good security hygiene.\n[...]\nignore serious security warnings\n[...]\n) and triggers spurious security errors. This is more\n[...]\nnuisance. Confusion caused by\n[...]\n(i.e\n[...]\nkey pair).\n[...]\nso periodic rekeying\n[...]\nRekeying\n[...]\na breach or after offboarding\n[...]\nto avoid disruption\n[...]\nSSH certificate authentication does a lot to improve SSH. It eliminates spurious TOFU warnings and host key verification failures. It lets you drop complex key approval & distribution processes and extend SSO to SSH. It makes rekeying possible for hosts and easier than key reuse for users. It makes SSH keys ephemeral, making key management oversights fail-secure.\n\n---\n\nTitle: DIY Single Sign-On for SSH\nURL: https://smallstep.com/blog/diy-single-sign-on-for-ssh/index.html\nPublished: 2020-03-23T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: But I don't want to run a Certificate Authority!\nHighlights:\nSSH. In this tutorial, you'll learn how to use SSH certificates (for hosts & users) generated by open source`step-ca` using the step ssh sub-command.\n[...]\n- Setup VM\n- Configure ssh client to accept host certs\n- Configure sshd to accept user certs\n- Login to VM via SSH user cert\n- Generate ssh host certificates\n- Generate your own PKI for step-ca\n[...]\nYou're going to run a CA in your local environment and we'll use SSH to connect to a Vagrant VM (representing a remote host) that has`sshd` pre-configured to accept SSH user certificates signed by our CA.\n[...]\n==> testhost: Running provisioner: shell...\n    testhost: Running: inline script\n    testhost: Add following line to your local hosts ~/.ssh/known_hosts file to accept host certs\n    testhost: @cert-authority * ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 AAAAE2VjZHNhLXNoYTItbmlzdHAyNTYAAAAIbmlzdHAyNTYAAABBBJJM+jkIdieQvdPb8DwnfnJudEc9PgVBqLDWHKgvqoIiMXhuIyGstQ9ULOBMdJkqxMjkRTFZp1iFvIk+iU6hwTA=\n    testhost: Add a /etc/hosts file entry testhost to resolve to 192.168.0.101\n    testhost: Check out README.md to learn how to grab user ssh certs to log into testhost\n\n```\n[...]\n## Configure SSH client to accept host certs\n[...]\nGo ahead and follow the instructions printed by Vagrant. This will enable your local SSH client to accept SSH host certificates (signed by the root SSH host private key). The following command will append the SSH host CA key (root SSH host public key corresponding to the root SSH host private key) to your local known_hosts file:\n[...]\n```\n$ echo \"@cert-authority * ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 AAAAE2VjZHNhLXNoYTItbmlzdHAyNTYAAAAIbmlzdHAyNTYAAABBBJJM+jkIdieQvdPb8DwnfnJudEc9PgVBqLDWHKgvqoIiMXhuIyGstQ9ULOBMdJkqxMjkRTFZp1iFvIk+iU6hwTA=\" >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts\n\n```\n[...]\nYou can also find the root SSH host CA key stored at step/certs/ssh_host_key.pub in this repo.\n[...]\nSSH certificates bind names to public keys. This SSH host certificate has the identity testhost which is why the following entry must be added to the local /etc/hosts file on the VM:\n[...]\n```\n$ tail -n 1 /etc/hosts\n192.168.0.101   testhost\n\n```\n[...]\n## Configure SSHD to accept user certs\n[...]\nVagrant has already configured`sshd` on testhost, the VM generated by Vagrant. Please note that for demo purposes the PKI is shared with the VM using a shared directory mount. Below you can see the relevant lines from the testhost VM's`sshd_config`:\n[...]\n```\n$ tail -n 5 /etc/ssh/sshd_config\n#       PermitTTY no\n#       ForceCommand cvs server\nTrustedUserCAKeys /keys/ssh_user_key.pub\nHostKey /keys/ssh_host_ecdsa_key\nHostCertificate /keys/ssh_host_ecdsa_key-cert.pub\n\n```\n[...]\n- TrustUserCAKeys: The root SSH user public key used to verify SSH user certificates.\n- HostKey: The SSH private key specific to this host.\n- HostCertificate: The SSH public certificate that uniquely identifies this host (signed by the root SSH host private key).\n[...]\n## Generate SSH host certificates\n[...]\nThis example repo includes a pre-generated SSH host certificate and key. To replace it, or generate SSH certificates for other hosts, run the following command:\n[...]\n```\nstep ssh certificate --host --principal testhost --principal testhost.internal testhost ssh_host_ecdsa_key\n\n```\n[...]\nWhere`--principal` identifies the hostname(s), ideally FQDNs, for the machine. For a single principal you can short cut the command to:\n[...]\n```\nstep ssh certificate --host testhost ssh_host_ecdsa_key\n\n```\n[...]\nuser_key\n[...]\n✔ SSH host root certificate: /tmp/mystep/certs/ssh_host_key.pub\n✔ SSH host root private key: /tmp/mystep/secrets/ssh_host_key\n[...]\nPlease note that after you regenerate ssh_host_key.pub and ssh_user_key.pub, you will have to reconfigure`ssh` and`sshd` for clients and hosts to accept the new CA keys. Check out this host bootstrapping script for configuration examples.\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSH vs. X.509 Certificates\nURL: https://smallstep.com/blog/ssh-vs-x509-certificates/index.html\nPublished: 2022-11-01T09:18:49.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nkey authentication provides\n[...]\nsecure alternative to passwords\n[...]\nbut it is still\n[...]\nmust have their own public\n[...]\nprivate key pairs.\n[...]\nFirst, the client will try to confirm that it’s connecting to a trusted server by checking if the server’s public key is in its`known\\_hosts`file. If the client does not recognize the server’s public key, it throws an error (as shown) saying that the server’s authenticity can’t be proven but asking if it should connect anyway:\n[...]\nIn this situation, the proper thing would be for the user to send the key fingerprint to the server administrator for verification. Because what if an attacker somehow managed to assign a well-known, trusted IP address to a rogue machine?\n[...]\nHowever, the reality is that most people choose the easy way out and type yes. When you type “yes”, a connection to the server is established without authentication and the server's public key is permanently added to the client's known-hosts file. This presents the first flaw of public-key-based SSH authentication: it encourages the trust on first use (TOFU) anti-pattern.\n[...]\nSSH certificate-based authentication works similarly to X.509 certificates but with a considerably simpler implementation. With SSH certificate-based authentication, clients and hosts mutually authenticate each other, thereby eliminating TOFU.\n[...]\nFirst, set up an SSH CA server (aka an “online CA”) that will house two SSH CA key pairs: a host CA key pair for signing and authenticating certificates for host machines and a user CA key pair for signing and authenticating certificates for clients. For SSH, people use the term CA (certificate authority) to interchangeably \\*\\*\\*\\*refer both to the signing key pairs and the SSH CA server.\n[...]\nAfter you generate the host CA and user CA key pairs, configure all host machines to trust certificates signed by the public key of the user CA. You will also need to configure all clients to trust certificates signed by the public key of the host CA.\n[...]\nTo issue certificates to host machines, you'll have to generate public-private key pairs for all host servers and sign them with the host CA keys. To issue client (user) certificates, You'll also need to generate key pairs for all clients and sign them with the user CA keys. Ideally, the process of issuing user certificates is completely automated. For example, you could build a flow where users can automatically get signed certificates when they authenticate with your company's identity provider.\n[...]\nWhen a client wants to access a host, it'll present its certificate to the host, and the host will do the same. Host servers will only allow access to clients with certificates trusted by keys, and clients can rest assured that they're connecting to a trusted host.\n[...]\nIn X.509 implementation, certificate authorities always have their own certificates and can have intermediate certificate authorities (certificate chaining). SSH does not allow all that. Following a more simplistic approach allows SSH certificate configuration to be easier and minimizes the attack surface.\n[...]\nAlso, there are two SSH certificate formats: host certificate and user certificate. In X.509 you specify whether a certificate allows for “server authentication” or “client authentication” (a single X.509 certificate can allow for both)\n[...]\nNo. To support SSH certificate-based authentication, you will need to maintain another CA that can handle SSH certificate signing and issuance because OpenSSH uses a custom (simpler) certificate format.\n[...]\nWe know that one of the biggest headaches about using certificate-based authentication is setting up a certificate authority. Enter Smallstep! With our open source toolstep-ca, you can easily set up an SSH CA to issue SSH certificates to users and hosts. You can also leverage your existing OIDC flow to authenticate users and make the issuance of personal certificates simple for your whole team.\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSH Host Certificate Quick Setup Tutorial - Smallstep\nURL: https://smallstep.com/docs/ssh/hosts/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n- `step-ssh-renew` rotates the SSH host certificate every eight hours.\n- `step-ssh-metadata` syncs user ACLs with your CA every 3 seconds.\n[...]\n- Install the`step` CLI and`step-ssh` utilities\n- Comment out the broken`pam_systemd.so` in`common-session`, which causes a delay at login (this is a known Ubuntu/Debian bug)\n- Bootstrap your host to your CA\n- Enroll your host with an SSH host certificate\n- Configure SSHD to use certificate authentication\n- Activate`step-ssh` PAM/NSS modules\n- Add the host to the CA's inventory\n[...]\nYou'll be prompted for your team ID, enrollment token, hostname, and a list of space-separated host tags (eg. \"`db=dev region=us-west-\n[...]\n- `--team \"[your team ID]\"`\n- `--token \"[your enrollment token]\"`\n- `--hostname \"[the hostname]\"`\n- One or more`--tag \"key=value\"` to assign host tags\n[...]\n- Any number of`--principal \"[hostname or IP]\"`, to add additional hostnames or IPs to the host certificate. Add all hostnames or IPs that people will use to access the host.\n- `--is-bastion` to indicate a bastion host\n- `--bastion \"[bastion hostname]\"` to specify the bastion that sits in front of this host\n[...]\nSuspect host or user certificates are not working? In other words, your ssh client fails to log in or shows \"trust on first use\" warning? Late last year, I wrote a blog post sharing this discovery. That post blew up. It made front pages everywhere relevant, and many readers reached out to discuss how certificate-based SSH access might work in their world.\n[...]\n- Single sign-on SSH as a service for $3/host/month + Team account level or higher. We run a certificate authority for you with KMS-backed private keys. No contracts, no minimums, no fuss.\n[...]\n- One-line configuration, then use`ssh` like you’re used to. Built on our open source step-ca with standards-based single sign-on (OAuth OIDC) from your existing identity provider (e.g., Okta, Azure AD, G Suite) for easy integration and minimal lock-in.\n[...]\n- OpenSSH, but better. Smallstep SSH works by configuring standard OpenSSH clients and servers. It works across clouds, on-prem, and anywhere else OpenSSH does.\n[...]\n### From certificates to single sign-on for SSH\n[...]\nAfter our blog post, we quickly put together a crude demonstration of what life with SSH certificates might look like, using our open-source toolchain, and started showing it to anyone interested. Through these conversations, a product took shape.\n[...]\nWe quickly realized that certificates, while important, weren’t the right lede. Certificates are like browser cookies: an ephemeral byproduct of the login process used to establish a session. More important is what certificates allow: they let you establish an SSH session using single sign-on and say goodbye to SSH key management forever. When users are added to the right group, they get SSH access. When they leave the company, SSH access is revoked immediately and automatically. In between, they use SSH like they’re used to, but with less hassle and less liability.\n[...]\nSSH key management is a waste of time. We talked to countless front-line operators and SREs who lamented time spent pushing keys around. Time that could have been invested in strategic initiatives. But, they told us, simply issuing certificates was not enough. Even with a certificate, you can’t SSH to a server unless you have a user account and a home directory. To really save time, we learned, we needed to handle these tasks as well.\n[...]\nSo we built a lightweight toolchain for hosts to manage user accounts and access control. It plugs in, non-intrusively, via standard OS mechanisms (NSS and PAM). If you choose to use these features, they won’t interfere with your existing user management at all. As a bonus, we added SSH session audit logging to support basic compliance requirements.\n[...]\nUnsurprisingly, security was also top of mind. Certificates have some immediate security benefits: we’re able to leverage existing, hardened, authentication services to issue ephemeral credentials, hold keys in memory, and rotate them frequently. That makes it a lot more difficult for an attacker to jump from a popped endpoint into your production environment via SSH.\n[...]\nBut running a secure, highly available certificate authority is non-trivial (although`step-ca` makes it easier than it’s ever been before). So we decided to start with a hosted offering. Click a couple buttons and we’ll run the certificate authority for you, with private signing keys held securely in a KMS.\n[...]\nSome of these devices are resource-constrained. Others have intermittent internet connectivity. Some operate in regulated environments, or get deployed into the world where they’re susceptible to physical attacks. Beyond operational efficiencies, customers showed us how certificate-based authentication brings unique operational\n[...]\n. It works where\n[...]\nExperience SSH certificates for yourself in <5min⚡!",

When  run  without\n       arguments,  it  adds  the files ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa, ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa_sk, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 and\n       ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_sk.  After loading a private key, ssh-add will try to  load  corresponding  certificate\n       information  from  the  filename  obtained  by  appending  -cert.pub to the name of the private key file.\n       Alternative file names can be given on the command line.\n\n       If any file requires a passphrase, ssh-add asks for the passphrase from the user.  The passphrase is read\n       from the user's tty.  ssh-add retries the last passphrase if multiple identity files are given.\n\n       The authentication agent must be running and the SSH_AUTH_SOCK environment variable must contain the name\n       of its socket for ssh-add to work.\n\n       The options are as follows:\n\n       -C      When loading keys into or deleting keys from the agent, process certificates only and skip  plain\n               keys.\n\n       -c      Indicates\n[...]\nadded  identities  should  be  subject  to  confirmation\n[...]\nbefore  being used for\n               authentication.   Confirmation\n[...]\naskpass(1).   Successful  confirmation\n[...]\nsign\n[...]\nby a zero exit status from ssh-\n[...]\npass(1), rather than text entered into the requester.\n\n       -D      Deletes all identities from the agent.\n\n       -\n[...]\nLoad resident keys\n[...]\nWhen loading keys into\n[...]\npublic key parameters\n[...]\n-l      Lists fingerprints\n[...]\nall identities currently represented by\n[...]\n-add will request\n[...]\nthe agent automatically delete the\n               certificate shortly after the certificate's expiry date\n[...]\nThis flag suppresses this behaviour and\n[...]\nspecify a lifetime for certificates added to an agent\n[...]\n-q      Be quiet after a successful operation.\n[...]\n-S provider\n               Specifies  a  path  to  a  library  that will be used when adding FIDO authenticator-hosted keys,\n               overriding the default of using\n[...]\ninternal USB HID support\n[...]\n-s pkcs1\n[...]\nPKCS#11 shared library\n[...]\nperforming sign\n[...]\nin\n               seconds\n[...]\nhelpful in\n[...]\n```\n\n       DISPLAY, SSH_ASKPASS and SSH_ASKPASS_REQUIRE\n               If ssh-add needs a passphrase, it will read the passphrase from the current terminal  if  it  was\n               run  from  a  terminal.   If  ssh-add does not have a terminal associated with it but DISPLAY and\n               SSH_ASKPASS  are  set,  it  will  execute  the  program  specified  by  SSH_ASKPASS  (by  default\n               “ssh-askpass”)  and  open an X11 window to read the passphrase.  This is particularly useful when\n               calling ssh-add from a .xsession or related script.\n\n               SSH_ASKPASS_REQUIRE allows further control over the use of an askpass program.  If this  variable\n               is  set  to  “never”  then ssh-add will never attempt to use one.  If it is set to “prefer”, then\n               ssh-add will prefer to use the askpass program instead of  the  TTY  when  requesting  passwords.\n               Finally,  if  the  variable  is  set  to  “force”,  then the askpass program will be used for all\n               passphrase input regardless of whether DISPLAY is set.\n\n       SSH_AUTH_SOCK\n               Identifies the path of a Unix-domain socket used to communicate with the agent.\n\n       SSH_SK_PROVIDER\n               Specifies a path to a library that will be used when loading any FIDO authenticator-hosted  keys,\n               overriding the default of using the built-in USB HID support.\n\n---\n\nTitle: How SSH Key Pairs Work\nURL: https://docs.oracle.com/en/operating-systems/oracle-linux/openssh/openssh-HowSSHKeyPairsWork.html\nPublished: 2025-07-02T21:09:28.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nTo use key authentication, you must first have a key pair: a public key and a corresponding private key. You can either use an existing key pair or generate a new one. Typically, you only generate an SSH key pair one time and only change the key pair if it might have been compromised or when using a key to access systems with different encryption standards. Not all key pairs are compatible with OpenSSH and you might need to convert keys as required. For example, keys generated using the PuTTY ssh client software aren't directly compatible with OpenSSH and might need to be converted before use. See the client software documentation if you're unsure about key format.\n[...]\nAfter you have obtained a key pair, copy the public key to any server to which you want to connect. Then to connect to the server, provide the matching private key. You can store the private key on a single client that you use to access the servers. For security, avoid copying the private key to several locations.\n[...]\nWhen generating key pairs, you can either configure them to have a password or not. Key pairs that don't have passphrases, can help with scripted automation as they can access remote systems instantly so you won't need to enter the passphrase each time you connect. However, using a key without a passphrase can be poor security practice. Instead, you can use SSH Agent to remember a key passphrase for the entire login session.\n[...]\nConsider using SSH Agent Forwarding to connect from trusted system to trusted system, or use the ProxyJump command option where you might need to connect to another system through an untrusted or heavily shared bastion host.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Ubuntu Manpage:\n\n       ssh-agent — OpenSSH authentication agent\nURL: https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/jammy/en/man1/ssh-agent.1.html\nPublished: 2019-01-01T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: Canonical\nHighlights:\n_AGENT_PID environment variable).\n\n       -P allowed_providers\n               Specify a pattern-\n[...]\nof acceptable paths for PKCS#11 provider and FIDO authenticator middleware\n               shared libraries that may be used with the -S or -s options to ssh-add(1).  Libraries that do not\n               match  the  pattern  list  will  be  refused.  See PATTERNS in ssh_config(5) for a description of\n               pattern-list syntax.  The default list is “/usr/lib/*,/usr/local/lib/*”.\n\n       -s      Generate Bourne shell commands on stdout.  This is the default if SHELL does not look like it's a\n               csh style of shell.\n\n       -t life\n               Set a default value for the maximum lifetime of identities added to the agent.  The lifetime  may\n               be  specified  in  seconds or in a time format specified in sshd_config(5).  A lifetime specified\n               for an identity with ssh-add(1) overrides this value.  Without this option  the  default  maximum\n               lifetime is forever.\n\n       command [arg ...]\n               If  a  command  (and optional arguments) is given, this is executed as a subprocess of the agent.\n               The agent exits automatically when the command given on the command line terminates.\n\n       There are two main ways to get an agent set up.  The first is at the start of an  X  session,  where  all\n       other  windows  or programs are started as children of the ssh-agent program.  The agent starts a command\n       under which its environment variables are exported, for example ssh-agent  xterm  &.   When  the  command\n       terminates, so does the agent.\n\n       The  second  method is used for a login session.  When ssh-agent is started, it prints the shell commands\n       required to set its environment variables, which in turn can be  evaluated  in  the  calling  shell,  for\n       example eval `ssh-agent -s`.\n\n       In both cases, ssh(1) looks at these environment variables and uses them to establish a connection to the\n       agent.\n\n       The  agent  initially  does not have any private keys.  Keys are added using ssh-add(1) or by ssh(1) when\n       AddKeysToAgent is set in ssh_config(5).  Multiple identities may be stored in ssh-agent concurrently  and\n       ssh(1) will automatically use them if present.  ssh-add(1) is also used to remove keys from ssh-agent and\n       to query the keys that are held in one.\n\n       Connections  to  ssh-agent  may be forwarded from further remote hosts using the -A option to ssh(1) (but\n       see the caveats documented therein), avoiding the need for authentication data  to  be  stored  on  other\n       machines.   Authentication  passphrases and private keys never go over the network: the connection to the\n       agent is forwarded over SSH remote connections and the result is returned to the requester, allowing  the\n       user access to their identities anywhere in the network in a secure fashion.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Using the SSH Key Agent to Remember Passphrases\nURL: https://docs.oracle.com/en/operating-systems/oracle-linux/openssh/openssh-UsingtheSSHKeyAgenttoRememberPassphrases.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nUsing the SSH Key Agent to Remember Passphrases\n\nPrevious Next JavaScript must be enabled to correctly display this content\n\n## Using the SSH Key Agent to Remember Passphrases\n\nUse the SSH Key Agent to enter the passphrases for any of the SSH keys a single time throughout the login session. In this manner, you avoid the poor security practice of creating SSH keys without passphrases.\n\nAfter you log in, check that the agent is started:\n\n```\nps -ef|grep -i ssh-agent\n```\n\nIf the agent is not started, start it by using the running the following command:\n\n```\neval $(ssh-agent -s)\n```\n\nRun the`ssh-add` command to add any of the ssh keys to the agent. For example:\n\n```\nssh-add $HOME/.ssh/id_sshkey\n```\n\nThe command prompts you for the key passphrase. The passphrase applies through the entire login session. If you use the key to connect to another system, the prompt for a passphrase no longer appears.\n\nNote:\n\nThe error message`Could not open a connection to your authentication\n agent` indicates that the agent might not be running.\n\nRepeat the command for each key that you want to add.\n\nAfter adding the keys to the agent, you can open SSH connections to any systems that have the paired public key configured in the`authorized_hosts` file, without being prompted for a passphrase. This behavior applies also to any scripts that are run as the user.\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSH agent restriction\nURL: https://www.openssh.org/agent-restrict.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nOpenSSH 8.9 will include the ability to control how and where keys in ssh-agent may be used, both locally and when forwarded (subject to some limitations).\n[...]\nThe OpenSSH SSH protocol implementation includes the ssh-agent authentication agent. Specifically, one or more of the following per-hop constraints will be encoded:\n[...]\nssh-agent records each hop constraint against the key for later permission checking. The hostnames in the constraint are used primarily for hostname checking in certificate hostkeys (i.e. when key_is_ca is true). The from_hostname and from_keyspec fields may be empty to signify the origin host, but they are always mandatory for the to/through host.\n[...]\nThe path visible to ssh-agent, from the origin through forwarding hosts to a destination authentication request is established by ssh sending a session binding message as the first message on an agent connection after it is established. This message cryptographically links the SSH connection's session identifier (as described in RFC4253 section 7.2) with the server's hostkey for the life of the agent connection. This allows the agent to establish a trustworthy linkage between an agent connection and a SSH connection.\n[...]\nTo make an authentication request using the agent, the client will pass the data to be signed to the agent via a SSH2_AGENTC_SIGN_REQUEST message. ssh-agent can now attempt to parse the to-be-signed data and extract the session identifier, server username and, thanks to the above extension, server hostkey.\n[...]\nAt this point,\n[...]\n. To add an extra layer of security, you can add a passphrase to your SSH key. To avoid entering the passphrase every time you connect, you can securely cache the key in the SSH agent.\n\n## Adding or changing a passphrase\n\nYou can change the passphrase for an existing private key without regenerating the keypair by typing the following command:\n\n```shell\n$ ssh-keygen -p -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519\n> Enter old passphrase: [Type old passphrase]\n> Key has comment 'your_email@example.com'\n> Enter new passphrase (empty for no passphrase): [Type new passphrase]\n> Enter same passphrase again: [Repeat the new passphrase]\n> Your identification has been saved with the new passphrase.\n\n```\n\nIf your key already has a passphrase, you will be prompted to enter it before you can change to a new passphrase.\n\n## Auto-launching `ssh-agent` on Git for Windows\n\nYou can run `ssh-agent` automatically when you open bash or Git shell. Copy the following lines and paste them into your `~/.profile` or `~/.bashrc` file in Git shell:\n\n```bash\nenv=~/.ssh/agent.env\n\nagent_load_env () { test -f \"$env\" && . \"$env\" >| /dev/null ; }\n\nagent_start () {\n    (umask 077; ssh-agent >| \"$env\")\n    . \"$env\" >| /dev/null ; }\n\nagent_load_env\n\n# agent_run_state: 0=agent running w/ key; 1=agent w/o key; 2=agent not running\nagent_run_state=$(ssh-add -l >| /dev/null 2>&1; echo $?)\n\nif [ ! \"$SSH_AUTH_SOCK\" ] || [ $agent_run_state = 2 ]; then\n    agent_start\n    ssh-add\nelif [ \"$SSH_AUTH_SOCK\" ] && [ $agent_run_state = 1 ]; then\n    ssh-add\nfi\n\nunset env\n\n```\n\nIf your private key is not stored in one of the default locations (like `~/.ssh/id_rsa`), you'll need to tell your SSH authentication agent where to find it. To add your key to ssh-agent, type `ssh-add ~/path/to/my_key`. For more information, see Generating a new SSH key and adding it to the ssh-agent.\n\n> [!TIP]\n> If you want `ssh-agent` to forget your key after some time, you can configure it to do so by running `ssh-add -t `.\n\nNow, when you first run Git Bash, you are prompted for your passphrase:\n\n```shell\n> Initializing new SSH agent...\n> succeeded\n> Enter passphrase for /c/Users/YOU/.ssh/id_rsa:\n> Identity added: /c/Users/YOU/.ssh/id_rsa (/c/Users/YOU/.ssh/id_rsa)\n> Welcome to Git (version 1.6.0.2-preview20080923)\n>\n> Run 'git help git' to display the help index.\n> Run 'git help <command>' to display help for specific commands.\n\n```\n\nThe `ssh-agent` process will continue to run until you log out, shut down your computer, or kill the process.\n\n## Saving your passphrase in the keychain\n\nOn Mac OS X Leopard through OS X El Capitan, these default private key files are handled automatically:\n\n- .ssh/id_rsa\n- .ssh/identity\n\nThe first time you use your key, you will be prompted to enter your passphrase. If you choose to save the passphrase with your keychain, you won't have to enter it again.\n\nOtherwise, you can store your passphrase in the keychain when you add your key to the ssh-agent. For more information, see Generating a new SSH key and adding it to the ssh-agent.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Generating a new SSH key and adding it to the ssh-agent\nURL: https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nAfter you've checked for existing SSH keys, you can generate a new SSH key to use for authentication, then add it to the ssh-agent.\n[...]\nWhen you generate an SSH key, you can add a passphrase to further secure the key. Whenever you use the key, you must enter the passphrase. If your key has a passphrase and you don't want to enter the passphrase every time you use the key, you can add your key to the SSH agent. The SSH agent manages your SSH keys and remembers your passphrase.\n[...]\nIf you don't already have an SSH key, you must generate a new SSH key to use for authentication.             "output": "Title: Setup Servers and User Machines to Use SSH Certificates — trfore.smallstep  documentation\nURL: https://trfore.github.io/ansible-smallstep/branch/main/docsite/guide_ssh.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSetup Servers and User Machines to Use SSH Certificates — trfore.smallstep documentation\n[...]\n# Setup Servers and User Machines to Use SSH Certificates\n[...]\nThis guide will demonstrate how to setup a server and client machine to use SSH certificates to authenticate remote connections. It assumes the following:\n[...]\nA CA is already configured to issue SSH certificates, and has a JWK, OIDC and SSHPOP provider configured. For instructions see: CA Server (x509 and SSH Certificates).\n[...]\nThe root CA certificate fingerprint is known, i.e. on the CA server run`step certificate fingerprint /etc/step-ca/certs/root_ca.crt`.\n[...]\nBoth the server and user machine will need to trust the central CA server then request:\n[...]\nA host certificate for server.\n[...]\nA user certificate for the user machine.\n[...]\nCurrently, the`step_ssh` role supports request authentication using the JWK provisioner for host certificates. And its recommended to use an OIDC provisioner for user certs (see below).\n[...]\n## Configure Servers to Use SSH Certificates\n[...]\nTo configure a server to use SSH certificate the following playbook will:\n[...]\nUse the default JWK provider to request a SSH host certificate.\n[...]\nUse the SSHPOP provider for SSH host certificate renewal.\n[...]\nThis is a minimal playbook that demonstrates the server using SSH certificates without requesting x509 certs. To also request x509 certs see: non-production.yml (link).\n[...]\n```\n- name: Setup Step CA Clients (Servers)\n  hosts: ca_clients\n  become: true\n  gather_facts: true\n  roles:\n    - name: Add Step CA Root Certificate to Trust Store\n      role: trfore.smallstep.step_ca_cert\n      vars:\n        step_ca_fingerprint: \"CA_FINGERPRINT\"\n        step_ca_url: \"CA_URL\" # https://ca.example.com\n\n    - name: Configure Host for SSH Certificates\n      role: trfore.smallstep.step_ssh\n      vars:\n        step_ssh_provisioner: \"Example.com\" # JWK provisioner\n        step_ssh_provisioner_password: \"password02\" # JWK provisioner password\n\n```\n[...]\n## Configure User Machine for SSH User Certificate\n[...]\nUser is a authorized on the server and their`USERNAME` matches one of the principals in the SSH certificate, see below: OIDC Provisioner: Email Addresses With Special Characters.\n[...]\nTo configure a user machine to use SSH certificate do the following:\n[...]\n### 1. Download and Add CA Certificate to Trust Store\n[...]\nBootstrap the machine to download root CA certificate and create a step configuration file at`/home/USER/.step`.\n[...]\n```\n# download root CA cert and create step configuration\n$ step ca bootstrap --ca-url [CA_URL] --fingerprint [CA_FINGERPRINT]\n[...]\n# add root CA cert to trust store, e.g. /etc/ssl/certs\n$ step certificate install $(step path)/certs/root_ca.crt\n[...]\n### 2. Request SSH User Certificate\n[...]\nRequest a user certificate using a OIDC (recommended) or JWK provisioner:\n[...]\nFor OIDC, the user will be redirected to the OAuth2 identity provider.\n[...]\nFor a JWK provisioner, the user will need a token. Note: The default JWK token should only be used in testing, as it provides full access to the CA. Additionally, providing short-lived encrypted single-use tokens is beyond the scope of the role and collection.\n[...]\n```\n# OIDC\n$ step ssh login USERNAME@example.com --provisioner \"google\"\n[...]\n# JWK\n$ step ssh login USERNAME --provisioner \"JWK_NAME\"\n[...]\n### 3. Configure User’s SSH client\n[...]\n```\n$ step ssh config\n\n```\n[...]\n### 4. Test the Connection\n[...]\n```\n$ ssh USERNAME@server01.example.com\n\n```\n[...]\nOptional, add SSH options and host server`USERNAME` to`~/.ssh/config`.\n[...]\n```\nHost server01.example.com\n    User USERNAME\n    ControlMaster auto\n    ControlPath ~/.ssh/control-%r@%h:%p\n    ControlPersist 15s\n[...]\n```\n/home/USER/.step/\n├── certs\n│   └── root_ca.crt\n├── config\n│   └── defaults.json\n└── ssh\n    ├── config\n    ├── includes\n    └── known_hosts\n\n```\n\n---\n\nTitle: Check and Verify SSH fingerprints - Development Setup Handbook\nURL: https://learn-with-me.github.io/Development-Setup-Handbook/Local/SSH/ssh_verify_fingerprints/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nCheck and Verify SSH fingerprints - Development Setup Handbook\n[...]\n# Check and Verify SSH fingerprints\n[...]\nOnce you SSH into a remote server, server fingerprint and key are added to`~/.ssh/known_hosts` file. This file contains a list of remote servers you've connected to in the past. File ensures that you are connected to the correct server and not a fake impersonator.\n[...]\nIf you try connecting to a remote host with an IP previously assigned to another machine, you'll get a host mismatch error. This is because the remote host fingerprint does not match to your`known_hosts` file.\n[...]\n```\n# Find the remote host fingerprint on server\n$ ssh root@104.105.103.102\nroot@instance:~# ssh-keygen -l -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key.pub\n\n-l - tells the keygen that we want the fingerprint\n-f - tell the keygen where to find the public key\n[...]\n# Remove all the keys related to a remote host machine\n$ ssh-keygen -R 104.105.103.102\n[...]\n# Find the remote host fingerprint on local machine\n$ ssh root@104.105.103.102\n\n# With first time connections, the server shows its fingerprint. Match this to the one you found on the server\n\n```\n\n---\n\nTitle: Password-less SSH with a Certificate Authority and OpenSSH | Techn0mad\nURL: https://techn0mad.github.io/2022/02/12/ssh-ca-authentication-for-the-win.html\nPublished: 2022-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: Larry Gadallah\nHighlights:\nA little-known capability of the OpenSSH server in common use is that it can be configured to allow SSH server and clients to trust each other via the use of server and client SSH host keys that are signed by a mutually trusted certificate authority (CA). Once this trust is established, no keys or passwords need to be exchanged between clients and servers, and password-less logins are enabled.\n[...]\nA sshd server may be configured to allow authentication via certified keys, by extending the existing`~/.ssh/authorized_keys` mechanism to allow specification of certification authority keys in addition to raw user keys. The SSH client will support automatic verification of acceptance of certified host keys, by adding a similar ability to specify CA keys in`~/.ssh/known_hosts`.\n[...]\nauthority (CA\n[...]\n- Provide CA certificates to associate SSH servers and clients with the CA\n- Authenticate known SSH servers\n- Authenticate known SSH public keys\n[...]\nOne significant advantage of this approach is the transitive trust it provides: This allows a SSH server to be trusted by a SSH client, without having to directly provide any information about the SSH server to the SSH client. Since an SSH server has its SSH host key signed by the CA (Vault), a SSH client that can access the CA can use the signature to validate the authenticity of the SSH server.\n[...]\nSince a SSH client also has its SSH public key similarly signed by the CA, a SSH server can similarly validate the authenticity of a SSH client at connection time without having to receive any additional information about the SSH client, because it can determine that it’s own SSH host key and the client’s SSH public key are both valid and signed by the same CA.\n[...]\nserver, the SSH client will:\n[...]\n- a.) Receive the SSH server host key\n- b.) Check to see if the server host key matches the entry in its`~/.ssh/known_hosts` file\n- c.) Present its signed SSH client key to the server\n[...]\n- d.) Check to see if the presented SSH client key has been signed by the client CA\n[...]\nPer Hashicorp’s recommendations, we implement two SSH secrets engine endpoints in Vault, one at the path`HOST_CA_PATH` for configuring SSH servers, and one at the path`CLIENT_CA_PATH` for configuring SSH clients. These endpoints are configured by a Vault administrator as follows, after authentication as described in “Authentication and Authorization” above has occurred:\n[...]\nRead`${CLIENT_CA_PATH}/config/ca:public_key` to obtain SSH client certificate public key\n[...]\nWrite to`${HOST_CA_PATH}/sign/hostrole:signed_key` to sign SSH server host key with host certificate\n[...]\nEach SSH server needs a copy of the client CA certificate from Vault, so that they can validate client public SSH keys that are signed with that certificate:\n[...]\n### Sign the SSH Server Host Key (4)\n[...]\nThe SSH server host key for each SSH server also needs to be signed with the Vault host CA certificate:\n[...]\n## SSH Client Configuration\n[...]\nRead from`${HOST_CA_PATH}/config/ca:public_key` to obtain SSH server certificate public key\n[...]\nWrite to`${CLIENT_CA_PATH}/sign/clientrole:signed_key` to sign SSH client key with client certificate\n[...]\nEach SSH client needs a copy of the server CA certificate from Vault, so that they can validate server public SSH keys that are signed with that certificate:\n[...]\n```\n@client:$ export VAULT_TOKEN=$(cat ~/.vault-token)`\n@client:$ VALID_DOMAINS=\"*,mydomain.org,myotherdomain.io\"\n@client:$ SSH_CA_KEY=$(vault read -field=public_key ${HOST_CA_PATH}/config/ca)\n@client:$ echo \"@cert-authority ${VALID_DOMAINS} ${SSH_CA_KEY}\" >> \\\n> ~/.ssh/known_hosts\n[...]\nThe SSH client key for each SSH client also needs to be signed with the Vault client CA certificate:\n[...]\n```\n@client:$ VALID_PRINCIPALS=\"admin,myusername\"\n[...]\n@client:$ cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | vault write -field=signed_key \\\n> ${CLIENT_CA_PATH}/sign/clientrole  valid_principals=${VALID_PRINCIPALS} \\\n> public_key=- > ~/.ssh/id_rsa-cert.pub\n\n---\n\nTitle: Logging in with SSH certificates\nURL: https://realrolfje.github.io/2019/06/18/logging-in-with-ssh-certificates\nPublished: 2019-06-18T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: Creating the Certificate authority\nHighlights:\nLogging in with SSH certificates | www.rolfje.com\n[...]\n# Logging in with SSH certificates\n[...]\nSuppose you want to provide ssh access to a server or machine without having to change the server configuration for each new user that requires acces, there is a very nice feature in ssh called \"SSH Certificates\". This blogpost explains a simple setup to get you started.\n[...]\n#### Creating the Certificate authority\n[...]\nFirst, create an SSH Certificate keypair. This is a regular public/private key like always, but not used as your default identity (preferably). You can do this on the server, but it is safer to keep this keypair on a separate machine. Keep the private key safe, and give it a strong password:\n[...]\n-b 40\n[...]\n6 -t\n[...]\nCA key for example.com\n[...]\n#### Adding the Certificate authority to the server\n[...]\nFrom the certificate authority keypair you just created, take the public key and add the contents of the ca\\_rsa.pub file to the*\\~/.ssh/authorized\\_keys*file of the user account you want to provide access to. Prefix the line with \"cert-authority\", like so:\n[...]\n`cert-authority ssh-rsa AAAAB[...]Z04e8c CA key for example.com`\n[...]\nYou can have multiple lines in the authorized keys if you want, so you can have several SSH Certificates. This enables you to give multiple people the power to sign client keys for scalability.\n[...]\n#### Signing a client key\n[...]\nTo give a client/user access to the account, ask for his public ssh key and sign it. If the user does not yet have a personal keypair, he can create that with\n[...]\n`$ ssh-keygen -b 4096 -t rsa -C \"John Doe\"`\n[...]\nThis will produce an*\\~/.ssh/id\\_rsa*and*\\~/.ssh/id\\_rsa.pub*file on the user's machine. Ask for the public key, take that the Certificate authority machine and sign the public key:\n[...]\n`$ ssh-keygen -s ca\\_rsa -I \"John Doe\" id\\_rsa.pub`\n[...]\nThis will produce an*id\\_rsa-cert.pub*file. This file needs to be sent back to the user, and needs to be stored in the \\~/.ssh folder. If there is already a file with this name, the contents can be appended.\n[...]\n#### Signing in\n[...]\nAfter all the setup above, the user should be able to connect to the server by just issuing\n[...]\n`$ ssh <<servername\\>`\n[...]\nThe ssh client will negotiate public/private keypair authentication, and if there is a signature file it will be provided to the server. The server checks the validity of the signature and if it looks ok, it will provice access to the user.\n[...]\n#### Who signed in and when?\n[...]\nA standard sshd configuration will log all authentication attempts to*/var/log/auth*. When a user logs in with a signed public key, the authentication log will list the certificate and the name of the user as provided when signing the key. This way you will be able to tell who logged in, and when.\n[...]\nThis whole mechanism will enable Raspberry Pi hobbyists with a pretty powerful way to provice access to remote machines without having to add all users to all machines. The SSH Certificate mechanism has more powers than this, please check revocation lists and other cool stuff atef.gy\n\n---\n\nTitle: WARNING: Remote Host Identification has Changed – Mitesh Shah\nURL: https://miteshshah.github.io/linux/tutorials/warning-remote-host-identification-has-changed/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: 1 minute to read   (149 words)  ( )\nHighlights:\nWARNING: Remote Host Identification has Changed – Mitesh Shah\n[...]\n# WARNING: Remote Host Identification has Changed\n[...]\n- Mostly after change the server or change ssh keys we get following message\n[...]\n```\n$ ssh mitesh@example.com\n[...]\n@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@\n@    WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!     @\n@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@\n[...]\nIT IS POSSIBLE THAT SOMEONE IS DOING SOMETHING NASTY!\nSomeone could be eavesdropping on you right now (man-in-the-middle attack)!\nIt is also possible that a host key has just been changed.\nThe fingerprint for the RSA key sent by the remote host is\n4e:24:e3:a6:83:da:48:ae:11:f3:5d:95:69:03:d3:cb.\nPlease contact your system administrator.\nAdd correct host key in /Users/Mitesh/.ssh/known_hosts to get rid of this message.\nOffending RSA key in /Users/Mitesh/.ssh/known_hosts:37\nRSA host key for example.com has changed and you have requested strict checking.\nHost key verification failed.\n```\n[...]\nHow to Fix:\n[...]\n```\n$ ssh-keygen -R example.com\n# Host example.com found: line 37 type RSA\n/Users/Mitesh/.ssh/known_hosts updated.\nOriginal contents retained as /Users/Mitesh/.ssh/known_hosts.old\n```",

In this tutorial, you'll learn how to use SSH certificates (for hosts & users) generated by open source`step-ca` using the step ssh sub-command.\n[...]\n- Learn how to configure OpenSSH and SSHD for certificate authentication using`step-ca`\n- Examples include copy/paste code blocks and pre-generated PKI.\n- When complete, you will be able to use OpenSSH and SSHD for SSH certificate login with a private SSH certificate authority.\n- Estimated effort: Reading time ~10 mins, Lab time ~60 to 180 mins.\n[...]\n- Setup VM\n- Configure ssh client to accept host certs\n- Configure sshd to accept user certs\n- Login to VM via SSH user cert\n- Generate ssh host certificates\n- Generate your own PKI for step-ca\n[...]\nYou're going to run a CA in your local environment and we'll use SSH to connect to a Vagrant VM (representing a remote host) that has`sshd` pre-configured to accept SSH user certificates signed by our CA.\n[...]\n==> testhost: Running provisioner: shell...\n    testhost: Running: inline script\n    testhost: Add following line to your local hosts ~/.ssh/known_hosts file to accept host certs\n    testhost: @cert-authority * ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 AAAAE2VjZHNhLXNoYTItbmlzdHAyNTYAAAAIbmlzdHAyNTYAAABBBJJM+jkIdieQvdPb8DwnfnJudEc9PgVBqLDWHKgvqoIiMXhuIyGstQ9ULOBMdJkqxMjkRTFZp1iFvIk+iU6hwTA=\n    testhost: Add a /etc/hosts file entry testhost to resolve to 192.168.0.101\n    testhost: Check out README.md to learn how to grab user ssh certs to log into testhost\n[...]\n## Configure SSH client to accept host certs\n[...]\nGo ahead and follow the instructions printed by Vagrant. This will enable your local SSH client to accept SSH host certificates (signed by the root SSH host private key). The following command will append the SSH host CA key (root SSH host public key corresponding to the root SSH host private key) to your local known_hosts file:\n[...]\n```\n$ echo \"@cert-authority * ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 AAAAE2VjZHNhLXNoYTItbmlzdHAyNTYAAAAIbmlzdHAyNTYAAABBBJJM+jkIdieQvdPb8DwnfnJudEc9PgVBqLDWHKgvqoIiMXhuIyGstQ9ULOBMdJkqxMjkRTFZp1iFvIk+iU6hwTA=\" >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts\n\n```\n[...]\nSSH certificates bind names to public keys. This SSH host certificate has the identity testhost which is why the following entry must be added to the local /etc/hosts file on the VM:\n[...]\n## Configure SSHD to accept user certs\n[...]\nVagrant has already configured`sshd` on testhost, the VM generated by Vagrant. Please note that for demo purposes the PKI is shared with the VM using a shared directory mount. Below you can see the relevant lines from the testhost VM's`sshd_config`:\n[...]\n/ssh_host_ecdsa_key-cert.pub\n[...]\n- TrustUserCAKeys: The root SSH user public key used to verify SSH user certificates.\n- HostKey: The SSH private key specific to this host.\n- HostCertificate: The SSH public certificate that uniquely identifies this host (signed by the root SSH host private key).\n[...]\nA valid user certificate is required to log into the testhost VM. Using`step`, you can authenticate with your SSH-enabled CA and fetch a new SSH certificate.\n[...]\n## Generate SSH host certificates\n[...]\nThis example repo includes a pre-generated SSH host certificate and key. To replace it, or generate SSH certificates for other hosts, run the following command:\n[...]\n```\nstep ssh certificate --host --principal testhost --principal testhost.internal testhost ssh_host_ecdsa_key\n\n```\n[...]\nWhere`--principal` identifies the hostname(s), ideally FQDNs, for the machine. For a single principal you can short cut the command to:\n[...]\n```\nstep ssh certificate --host testhost ssh_host_ecdsa_key\n[...]\nPlease note that after you regenerate ssh_host_key.pub and ssh_user_key.pub, you will have to reconfigure`ssh` and`sshd` for clients and hosts to accept the new CA keys. Check out this host bootstrapping script for configuration examples.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Working With known_hosts\nURL: https://docs.oracle.com/en/operating-systems/oracle-linux/openssh/openssh-WorkingWithknownhosts.html\nPublished: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nWorking With known_hosts\n[...]\n## Working With known_hosts\n[...]\nWhenever you connect to a remote host, the SSH server on the remote host provides a public key. You can use this key to validate that you're connecting to the same host in the future to prevent Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attacks. On the server side, this public key is stored as part of the`HostKey` pair. On the client system, the`known_hosts` database stores the public key for the host in the file`$HOME/.ssh/known_hosts`.\n[...]\nSSH Host key fingerprints from remote systems\n[...]\nWhen you connect to a remote system and the`known_hosts` database doesn't contain a key, the client prompts you to accept the fingerprint for the key. For example:\n[...]\nIf you accept the fingerprint, the`$HOME/.ssh/known_hosts` file stores the public key on the client system and you're no longer prompted every time you connect.\n[...]\nListing key fingerprints in local known_hosts\n[...]\nYou can list the fingerprints for keys stored in the known_hosts database by running:\n[...]\n```\nssh-keygen -l -f $HOME/.ssh/known_hosts\n```\n[...]\nStrictHostKeyChecking\n[...]\nIf the OpenSSH client has the`StrictHostKeyChecking` option set by default and the public key returned by the server changes, you're unable to connect to the remote server and a warning is displayed:\n[...]\nThe ED25519 host key for server1.example.com has changed,\nand the key for the corresponding IP address 198.51.100.172\nis unchanged. This could either mean that\nDNS SPOOFING is happening or the IP address for the host\nand its host key have changed at the same time.\n[...]\nOffending key for IP in /home/user/.ssh/known_hosts:20\n[...]\nThe fingerprint for the ED25519 key sent by the remote host is\nSHA256:qMBpuwP/LXLV8F5awaYtbXHO8v7LrqyY2BwVZk7tDxY.\n[...]\nPlease contact your system administrator.\nAdd correct host key in /home/user/.ssh/known_hosts to get rid of this message.\nOffending ED25519 key in /home/user/.ssh/known_hosts:125\n[...]\nrequested strict checking\n[...]\nHost keys don't change, so when you see this warning, you might not be connecting to the same system that you have connected to before. However, the key can be different for a legitimate reason, such as if the remote system is reinstalled, the OpenSSH Server keys are regenerated, or the domain name entry or IP address is reassigned to a new system. In such cases, you might want to remove any existing record of the system in the`known_hosts` database.\n[...]\nRemoving an existing key\n[...]\nIf you're certain that you can trust a new key provided by a remote server, you can remove an existing key from the`known_hosts` database by running:\n[...]\n```\nssh-keygen -R server1.example.com\n[...]\nDisabling StrictHostKeyChecking\n[...]\nIn test environments, where servers are constantly reinstalled or replaced, you might want to disable`StrictHostKeyChecking` for particular hosts. You can disable host key checking when you connect to a remote system as follows:\n[...]\n```\nssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no user@server1.example.com\n[...]\nIf you need to constantly disable strict host checking, consider adding this option to a host entry in the client configuration. See Setting SSH Client Configuration Options For a Host for more information.\n[...]\nStrict host key checking is enabled by default to prevent Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attacks, so disabling that functionality isn't considered good security practice and isn't recommended on production systems.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Server Recommendations - SSH at KIT\nURL: https://docs.cert.kit.edu/ssh/en/OpenSSH/server/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: KIT-CERT <cert@kit.edu>\nHighlights:\n## Using SSH certificates\n[...]\nThe trust-on-first-use security model does not provide a good user experience and can encourage bad habits, when users delete their`~/.ssh/known_hosts` file to get rid of warnings about changed keys.\n[...]\nOpenSSH also supports a trust model similar to X.509 certificates. A SSH key pair can be used to sign host keys as a certificate authority (CA). Only this key needs to be trusted by clients to establish trust to all host keys signed.\n[...]\nTake extra precautions to keep the CA’s private key secure. The usual approaches are to use a hardware security module (HSM) or keep the keys on an offline computer.\n[...]\nThe following example creates a CA and signs a host key. See the`ssh-keygen` man page for details on the command line options.\n[...]\nCreate a CA key pair:\n[...]\n```\nmkdir -m 0700 ~/ssh-ca\nssh-keygen -q -t ed25519 -f ~/ssh-ca/server_ca -C server_ca\n[...]\nCreate a keypair for a host and sign it:\n[...]\n```\nmkdir -m 0700 ~/ssh-host-keys\nssh-keygen -q -N '' -t ed25519 -f ~/ssh-host-keys/host1_ed25519 -C host1\nssh-keygen -s ~/ssh-ca/server_ca -I host1 -h -n host1.cluster.kit.edu -V +52w ~/ssh-host-keys/host1_ed25519.pub\n\n```\n[...]\nUpload the private key and the certificate to the server’s`/etc/ssh/` directory and add the following to`sshd_config`:\n[...]\n```\nHostCertificate /etc/ssh/host1_ed25519-cert.pub\n\n```\n[...]\nNow instruct you users to add the CA certificate to their known hosts file (either`~/.ssh/known_hosts` or`/etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts`):\n[...]\n```\n@cert-authority *.cluster.kit.edu ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAID9icauGFYzZCWe7P6tVH9/70pGON39H+CVB0DEY2PSg server_ca\n\n```\n[...]\nDespite the absence of hosts1’s public key, the client will connect to`host1.cluster.kit.edu` without a warning.\n[...]\nThe certificate authority concept can also be applied to sign and revoke user’s public keys.\n[...]\nThe signature in this example is valid for a limited time (52 weeks,`-V +52w`) to ensure that host keys are renewed regularly.\n[...]\nPlease consult the documentation before rolling out an SSH certificate authority! To keep this document short we skipped details like HSMs, key revocation lists, serial numbers, and restriction mechanisms like principals and options.\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSH Host Certificate Quick Setup Tutorial | Smallstep\nURL: https://smallstep.com/docs/ssh/hosts/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSSH Host Certificate Quick Setup Tutorial | Smallstep\n[...]\n# Smallstep SSH Host Quickstart\n[...]\n## Quick installation instructions\n[...]\nOur installation script will guide you through host setup. Based on one-way cryptography, an ssh key has two parts: a public key and a private key. You can distribute the public key to a variety of server systems that you like to log in to. You keep the private key on client machines that you log in from. Often these client machines are ones you have a certain amount of physical control over — a home computer or a laptop that you own. That physical security is coupled with a lengthy passphrase needed to activate the private key. Once activated, the private key can be kept active through a work session, allowing you to repeatedly log in to clients that have your public key.\n[...]\nYou will be prompted for a passphrase. This is used to protect your private key in case someone gains access to the machine/account where you have it stored. Do choose one. Even though the command prompt says it’s optional, you don’t want to have an unprotected private key around. Most people use much longer passphrases than a typical password, but generally place less emphasis on odd character substitutions that make the phrase harder to type.\n[...]\n```\nssh\n[...]\ni path/to/your/\n[...]\nyourCSLogin@linux.cs.odu\n[...]\nshould be prompted for the passphrase for your new\n[...]\n## 2.3 Key Agents\n[...]\nBut usually, we don’t activate the private key for a one-shot login. Instead, we run a key agent on our client machine. We tell it to activate our private key (giving it the passphrase to prove that we are its owner). It then watches for subsequent`ssh` connection attempts and offers up the activated private key.\n[...]\nStart an agent on your client machine:\n[...]\n```\neval `ssh-agent -t 120m`\n[...]\nThis launches a new key agent and tells it to watch for incoming requests to validate that private key. By understanding host key fundamentals, readers will learn how to protect their network connections from potential security threats and implement best practices for SSH key verification.\n[...]\nSSH host keys are cryptographic keys used in the Secure Shell (SSH) protocol to authenticate and verify the identity of remote servers during network connections. These unique keys prevent man-in-the-middle attacks by ensuring the server you're connecting to is genuine.\n[...]\n### SSH Host Key Verification Process\n[...]\n```\n## View current SSH host keys\nsudo ls /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*_key.pub\n[...]\n## Example of host key generation during SSH installation\nssh-keygen -ARUN \n```\n[...]\nWhen connecting to a new SSH server, the client receives the server's public host key. The client checks this key against known host keys stored in`~/.ssh/known_hosts` to validate the server's identity.\n[...]\n### Practical Host Key Verification Example\n[...]\n```\n## Connect to a remote server and verify host key\nssh user@example.com\n\n## First-time connection will prompt host key verification\n## The fingerprint will be added to known_hosts fileRUN \n```\n[...]\nDuring the initial connection, SSH will display the server's host key fingerprint, allowing manual verification of the server's identity before establishing a secure connection.\n[...]\n### Managing Known Hosts File\n[...]\n| Action | Command | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Remove Specific Host | `ssh-keygen -R hostname` | Removes a specific host's key |\n| View Known Hosts | `cat ~/.ssh/known_hosts` | Displays stored host keys |\n| Manually Add Host Key | `ssh-keygen -F hostname` | Finds and verifies a host key |\n[...]\n### Host Key Rotation Procedure\n[...]\n## Verify the new host key fingerprint\nssh-keygen -l -F example.comRUN \n```\n[...]\nThe process involves removing the old host key and re-establishing trust by manually verifying the new server key during the next connection attempt. This approach ensures secure server authentication while allowing legitimate host key changes.\n[...]\n| Security Parameter | Recommended Setting | Configuration Location |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Port | Non-standard port | `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` |\n| Root Login | Disabled | `PermitRootLogin no` |\n| Password Authentication | Disabled | `PasswordAuthentication no` |\n| Protocol Version | SSH2 | Default in modern systems |\n[...]\n### Implementing Key-Based Authentication\n[...]\n```\n## Generate SSH key pair\nssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/secure_key\n\n## Copy public key to remote server\nssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/secure_key.pub user@example.com\n[...]\n## Configure SSH config for key-based authentication\nnano ~/.ssh/config\nHost example\nIdentityFile ~/.ssh/secure_key\nPasswordAuthentication noRUN \n```\n[...]\nfocuses on minimizing attack surfaces by implementing strict\n[...]\nSSH host keys are fundamental to establishing secure network connections, offering robust protection against man-in-the-middle attacks. By mastering host key verification processes, understanding different key types, and implementing proper management strategies, professionals can significantly enhance their network security infrastructure and maintain the integrity of remote server connections.\n\n---\n\nTitle: SSH host keys | Linux-Cli-Tutorial(by Saman Khalife)\nURL: https://linux-tutorial-cli.gitbook.io/linux-cli-tutorial/txt-files/file-systems-cocepts/lpic1-101/ssh-host-keys\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSSH host keys are cryptographic keys used by the SSH (Secure Shell) protocol to verify the identity of a server to a client. These keys play a crucial role in securing SSH connections by preventing man-in-the-middle attacks and ensuring that the client is connecting to the correct server.\n[...]\n1. Server Authentication: SSH host keys allow clients to verify that they are connecting to the correct server. When a client connects to a server for the first time, the server presents its host key, which the client can then store for future reference.\n2. Security: By ensuring that the server’s identity can be verified, SSH host keys help protect against man-in-the-middle attacks.\n3. Encryption: Host keys are part of the initial key exchange process that sets up encrypted communication between the client and server.\n[...]\nSSH host keys are typically stored in the `/etc/ssh/` directory on the server:\n[...]\n- RSA: `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key` and `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key.pub`\n- ECDSA: `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key` and `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key.pub`\n- ED25519: `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key` and `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub`\n- DSA: `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key` and `/etc/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key.pub`\n[...]\nTo generate new SSH host keys, you can use the `ssh-keygen` command. This might be necessary if the keys have been compromised or if you want to upgrade to a more secure key type.\n[...]\nReplace `rsa` with `ecdsa`, `ed25519`, or `dsa` to generate keys of other types. The `-N ''` part ensures that the key has no passphrase.\n[...]\nEnsure that your `sshd_config` file (typically located at `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`) includes the correct paths to the host keys:\n[...]\n```sh\nHostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_\n[...]\n_key\nHostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_\n[...]\n#### Verifying SSH Host Keys on the Client Side\n[...]\nWhen a client connects to an SSH server for the first time, it stores the server’s host key in the `~/.ssh/known_hosts` file. The next time the client connects to the server, it verifies the server’s identity using this stored key.\n[...]\nYou can manually add a server’s host key to the `known_hosts` file to avoid the prompt during the first connection:\n[...]\n```sh\nssh-keyscan -H <server-ip-or-hostname> >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts\n\n```\n[...]\n#### Ensuring SSHD Uses the\n[...]\n`/etc/\n[...]\nAdd or verify the following lines:\n[...]\n```sh\nHostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key\nHostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecd\n[...]\n_key\nHostKey /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key\n[...]\n# Agent Instructions: Querying This Documentation\n[...]\nIf you need additional information that is not directly available in this page, you can query the documentation dynamically by asking a question.\n[...]\nPerform an HTTP GET request on the current page URL with the `ask` query parameter:\n[...]\n```\nGET https://linux-tutorial-cli.gitbook.io/linux-cli-tutorial/txt-files/file-systems-cocepts/lpic1-101/ssh-host-keys.md?ask=<question>\n\n```\n[...]\nThe question should be specific, self-contained, and written in natural language.\n[...]\nThe response will contain a direct answer to the question and relevant excerpts and sources from the documentation.",
