This chapter is a quick-start guide that covers the core functionality of GnuPG. This includes keypair creation, exchanging and verifying keys, encrypting and decrypting documents, and authenticating documents with digital signatures. It does not explain in detail the concepts behind public-key cryptography, encryption, and digital signatures. This is covered in Chapter 2. It also does not explain how to use GnuPG wisely. This is covered in Chapters 3 and 4.\n[...]\nOnce a key is imported it should be validated. GnuPG uses a powerful and flexible trust model that does not require you to personally validate each key you import. Some keys may need to be personally validated, however. A key is validated by verifying the key's fingerprint and then signing the key to certify it as a valid key. A key's fingerprint can be quickly viewed with the --fingerprint command-line option, but in order to certify the key you must edit it.\n[...]\nA key's fingerprint is verified with the key's owner. This may be done in person or over the phone or through any other means as long as you can guarantee that you are communicating with the key's true owner. If the fingerprint you get is the same as the fingerprint the key's owner gets, then you can be sure that you have a correct copy of the key.\n[...]\nAfter checking the fingerprint, you may sign the key to validate it. Since key verification is a weak point in public-key cryptography, you should be extremely careful and always check a key's fingerprint with the owner before signing the key.\n[...]\n1 a procedure was given\n[...]\nvalidate your correspondents' public keys\n[...]\nchecking the fingerprint\n[...]\nto him, and since\n[...]\nhave signed they key, you can be sure to detect any\n[...]\nwith it in the future.\n[...]\n, this procedure is\n[...]\nmust validate a large number of keys or communicate with people whom\n[...]\ndo not know\n[...]\nGnuPG addresses this problem with a mechanism popularly known as the web of trust. In the web of trust model, responsibility for validating public keys is delegated to people you trust. For example, suppose\n[...]\n. In general\n[...]\neverybody to properly validate keys they sign\n[...]\nany key signed\n[...]\na valid key\n[...]\nroot is Alice\n[...]\naxiomatically assumed to be valid.\n[...]\n## Trust in a key's owner\n[...]\nIn practice trust is subjective. For example, Blake's key is valid to Alice since she signed it, but she may not trust Blake to properly validate keys that he signs. In that case, she would not take Chloe's and Dharma's key as valid based on Blake's signatures alone. The web of trust model accounts for this by associating with each public key on your keyring an indication of how much you trust the key's owner. There are four trust levels.\n[...]\nThe GnuPG key editor may be used to adjust your trust in a key's owner. The command is trust. In this example Alice edits her\n[...]\nin Blake and then updates the trust database to recompute which keys are valid based on her new trust in Blake.\n[...]\n## Using trust to validate keys\n[...]\nThe web of trust allows a more elaborate algorithm to be used to validate a key. Formerly, a key was considered valid only if you signed it personally. A more flexible algorithm can now be used: a key K is considered valid if it meets two conditions:\n[...]\nThe web of trust model is a flexible approach to the problem of safe public key exchange. It permits you to tune GnuPG to reflect how you use it. At one extreme you may insist on multiple, short paths from your key to another key K in order to trust it. On the other hand, you may be satisfied with longer paths and perhaps as little as one path from your key to the other key K. Requiring multiple, short paths is a strong guarantee that K belongs to whom your think it does. The price, of course, is that it is more difficult to validate keys since you must personally sign more keys than if you accepted fewer and longer paths.\n\n---\n\nTitle: PGP and You\nURL: https://thoughtbot.com/blog/pgp-and-you\nPublished: 2014-10-31T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n- A public key, which you’ll publish, allows others to encrypt messages to you and verify messages from you.\n[...]\n- A private key, which you’ll keep secret and safe, allows you to decrypt the messages encrypted to your public key and to sign messages so that others can verify they are from you.\n[...]\nNow that my public key is in your keyring you can encrypt and sign messages to that key and verify that messages signed or encrypted with that key are valid. You can’t, however, actually trust that the person who controls that key is me.\n[...]\nTo do that, you’d need to verify the key’s fingerprint by somehow having me tell it to you in a venue where you can be sure that you are talking to me.\n[...]\nIdeally this would be in person with me reading my fingerprint to you aloud while you verify the fingerprint on your own computer. This isn’t always practical but you definitely want to be absolutely sure the person telling you that the key is mine is me. A phone or video call (if you would recognize my face or voice), verifying on social media, instant messaging, checking the signatures of others who have signed my key (and who you trust), or preferably some combination of these are all potential avenues for verification.\n[...]\n### Signing a key\n[...]\nSigning a key marks an implicit trust. This means that you have done some amount of work to verify the identity of the keyholder.\n[...]\nThe level of trust can be specified, and ranges from “I didn’t actually check anything” to “I have verified this key’s fingerprint and this person’s identity in person using legal documents”.\n[...]\nThe default level, which GnuPG will assume if you don’t specify that it should ask, is`I will not answer.` This is a reasonable default, but I prefer to be prompted and to specify how well I have verified a signature.\n[...]\nA conceptual similarity to signing a key at the highest level (`I have done very careful checking.`) would be to vouch in court that the person the key represents is the person you met and that they are the person they claim to be.\n[...]\nIdeally, this would mean that you had personally met the person and verified their fingerprint.\n[...]\nSigning a key is useful because it informs others that they can trust the signature to the extent that they trust you to verify signatures.\n[...]\nIf you do not personally know the person you are exchanging keys with, actual verification of legal identity (photo id) and of the ownership of the email address are important steps. PGP Key Signing has a good overview of how these verifications could be accomplished.\n[...]\nSince I don’t have George here to tell me his fingerprint in person, I’ll go through a few extra steps. I’ve already confirmed that my trusted introducer, Mike Burns, has signed George’s key. Next, I’ll ask George to give me his fingerprint in a couple of different locations: an email from George’s thoughtbot address, our company’s Slack room, and his Twitter account. Once it’s been confirmed in those locations, and given that I know George personally and he gave me the key id to download in the first place, I’m willing to sign his key and confirm that I believe it to be controlled by George.\n[...]\n### Trusting a key\n[...]\nMuch of PGP’s strength comes from its Web of Trust concept.\n[...]\nPGP allows you to note the extent to which you trust someone’s veracity at verifying the identity of keyholders.\n[...]\nFirst of all, this isn’t something you would do for every key. Trust is personal and will never be shared. You can feel free to be truthful about your trust that this person is properly verifying keys - they won’t ever know what level you give unless you tell them. You can also feel free not to trust a signature at all.\n[...]\nOnce I’ve verified the fingerprint as discussed above, I can assign trust.\n[...]\nwith a signature\n\n---\n\nTitle: Validating other keys on your public keyring\nURL: https://www.gnupg.org/gph/en/manual/x332.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nIn Chapter 1 a procedure was given to validate your correspondents' public keys: a correspondent's key is validated by personally checking his key's fingerprint and then signing his public key with your private key. By personally checking the fingerprint you can be sure that the key really does belong to him, and since you have signed they key, you can be sure to detect any tampering with it in the future. Unfortunately, this procedure is awkward when either you must validate a large number of keys or communicate with people whom you do not know personally.\n[...]\nGnuPG addresses this problem with a mechanism popularly known as the web of trust. In the web of trust model, responsibility for validating public keys is delegated to people you trust. For example, suppose\n[...]\nIf Alice trusts Blake to properly validate keys that he signs, then Alice can infer that Chloe's and Dharma's keys are valid without having to personally check them. She simply uses her validated copy of Blake's public key to check that Blake's signatures on Chloe's and Dharma's are good. In general, assuming that Alice fully trusts everybody to properly validate keys they sign, then any key signed by a valid key is also considered valid. The root is Alice's key, which is axiomatically assumed to be valid.\n[...]\nIn practice trust is subjective. For example, Blake's key is valid to Alice since she signed it, but she may not trust Blake to properly validate keys that he signs. In that case, she would not take Chloe's and Dharma's key as valid based on Blake's signatures alone. The web of trust model accounts for this by associating with each public key on your keyring an indication of how much you trust the key's owner. There are four trust levels.\n[...]\nThe web of trust allows a more elaborate algorithm to be used to validate a key. Formerly, a key was considered valid only if you signed it personally. A more flexible algorithm can now be used: a key K is considered valid if it meets two conditions:\n[...]\nthe path of signed keys leading from K back to your own key is five steps or shorter.\n[...]\nWhen computing valid keys in the example, Blake and Dharma's are always considered fully valid since they were signed directly by Alice. The validity of the other keys depends on trust. In the first case, Dharma is trusted fully, which implies that Chloe's and Francis's keys will be considered valid. In the second example, Blake and Dharma are trusted marginally. Since two marginally trusted keys are needed to fully validate a key, Chloe's key will be considered fully valid, but Francis's key will be considered only marginally valid. In the case where Chloe and Dharma are marginally trusted, Chloe's key will be marginally valid since Dharma's key is fully valid. Francis's key, however, will also be considered marginally valid since only a fully valid key can be used to validate other keys, and Dharma's key is the only fully valid key that has been used to sign Francis's key. When marginal trust in Blake is added, Chloe's key becomes fully valid and can then be used to fully validate Francis's key and marginally validate Elena's key. Lastly, when Blake, Chloe, and Elena are fully trusted, this is still insufficient to validate Geoff's key since the maximum certification path is three, but the path length from Geoff back to Alice is four.\n[...]\nThe web of trust model is a flexible approach to the problem of safe public key exchange. It permits you to tune GnuPG to reflect how you use it. At one extreme you may insist on multiple, short paths from your key to another key K in order to trust it. On the other hand, you may be satisfied with longer paths and perhaps as little as one path from your key to the other key K. Requiring multiple, short paths is a strong guarantee that K belongs to whom your think it does. The price, of course, is that it is more difficult to validate keys since you must personally sign more keys than if you accepted fewer and longer paths.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Using PGP to Verify Digital Signatures\nURL: https://www.sei.cmu.edu/documents/306/2001_019_001_52468.pdf\nPublished: 2001-01-01T00:00:00.000Z\nAuthor: mdd\nHighlights:\nPGP stands for Pretty Good Privacy. It is a computer program that uses mathematical \nalgorithms to encrypt files and protect them from unauthorized access. It is also used to \ndigitally sign and verify documents. Versions of the PGP program are available for most \npopular computer operating systems—Microsoft Windows, MacOS, and UNIX, to name \na few.\n[...]\nBecause most of our constituents receive documents that are signed with the CERT/CC \nPGP key, we focus on the second use. In this paper, we provide some background \ninformation about PGP and explain how to check signatures for validity.\n[...]\nKeys are bundled with information about the key, such as the name of the owner and the \ndate the key was generated. The bundles of information containing the key are called key \ncertificates. Many key certificates include signatures of individuals or groups that help \nattest to the veracity of the key (there’s more on these signatures in the Web of trust \nsection). Key certificates are stored in files called key rings. Your private key ring holds \nyour private key, and your public key ring holds the public keys of everyone whose sends \nyou documents signed or encrypted with PGP\n[...]\nA key (pun intentional) concept for PGP is that of a web of trust. Owners of PGP keys \nget others people’s signatures on their key, enabling you to infer a level of confidence \nthat the signed key belongs to the person named in the key’s userid section. For example, \nlet’s say that you receive a message signed with the key of John Doe, an individual you \ndon’t know. If John’s PGP key contains a signature of a person or group you do know \nand trust, you gain trust in the identity of the stranger by virtue of your mutual, trusted \nacquaintance.\n[...]\nLet’s say that John Doe’s key is signed by Rich Pethia of the CERT/CC and ISA or Dave \nMcCurdy of the Electronic Industries Alliance and ISA. You trust them because you \nknow them, either personally or by reputation, and are confident that they are \ntrustworthy, ethical individuals. Moreover, you have validated their keys—verified that \nthe key claimed to be theirs is indeed their own key, a critical step for building trust. (If \nyou see a signature that claims to belong to someone you trust, you should not believe it \nuntil you have checked to see if the signature is valid.) Then, when you see Rich Pethia’s \nand Dave McCurdy’s valid signatures on John Doe’s key, you can have a high level of \ntrust in John by inference.\n[...]\nthe owner of \nthe\n[...]\nPGP signature in a document\n[...]\nAlthough specific “how to” steps vary according to the particular PGP software program \nyou use, you can follow these general steps to check digital signatures.\n[...]\n3. Check that you have a public key ring. The name of the default public key ring is \noften called pubring.pkr, though this may vary depending on the particular \nsoftware program you get.\n[...]\n5. Get the “fingerprint” of the key according to the instructions for your particular \nversion of the software.\n[...]\n6. Verify the fingerprint by calling the CERT hotline 1-412-268-7090 during regular \nbusiness hours—8:00-5:00 EST (GMT –5)/EDT (GMT –4) Monday through \nFriday. You can also check our encryption web page, mentioned in step 4. \nThe important thing is to use an “out-of-band” method for verification. \nVerification can be undermined if you use the same method for all \ncommunication about the key.\n[...]\n7. When you receive a PGP-signed document from the CERT/CC, you can check the \nsignature. The method varies according to your software, so you might need to \nconsult someone in your organization for exact instructions.\n[...]\nYou can add keys of others who give you PGP signed or encrypted files. An \nimportant thing to remember as you decide to add public keys to your key ring is that \nthey might not belong to the person indicated by the key certificate. Out-of-band \nverification and trusted, verified signatures are important.\n\n---\n\nTitle: The comp.security.pgp FAQ - Part \n  6/11\nURL: https://www.pgp.net/pgp-faq/faq-06.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: Arnoud Engelfriet\nHighlights:\nOK, you just got a copy of John Smith's public encryption key. How do you know that the key really belongs to John Smith and not to some impostor? The answer to this is key signatures. They are similar to message signatures in that they can't be forged. Let's say that you don't know that you have John Smith's real key. But let's say that you DO have a trusted key from Joe Blow. Let's say that you trust Joe Blow and that he has added his signature to John Smith's key. By inference, you can now trust that you have a valid copy of John Smith's key. That is what key signing is all about. This chain of trust can be carried to several levels, such as A trusts B who trusts C who trusts D, therefore A can trust D. You have control in the PGP configuration file over exactly how many levels this chain of trust is allowed to proceed.\n[...]\nThe options (which can be set in PGP's configuration file, CONFIG.TXT) to control this are`Cert_Dept = n` This indicates the maximum depth of your \"web of trust\". A key which is n keys \"away\" from your own key will not be used to introduce other keys.`Completes_Needed = n` This indicates the number of completely trusted keys required to make a key valid. A key is completely trusted if it is valid, and you choose option \"4. Yes, always\" when PGP asked you if you trust this person to introduce others.`Marginals_Needed = n` This indicates the number of marginally trusted keys required to make a key valid. A key is marginally trusted if you answered \"3. Sometimes\" to the question above. In all other cases, the key is not trusted at all.\n[...]\nYou can display the trust parameters for a key with`pgp -kc`. See also question 4.7. Be careful about keys that are several levels removed from your immediate trust.\n[...]\nThis adds your signature (signed with the private key for yourid, if you specify it) to the key identified with keyid. If keyid is a user ID, you will sign that particular user ID; otherwise, you will sign the default user ID on that key (the first one you see when you list the key with`pgp -kv `).\n[...]\nIt is very easy for someone to generate a key with a false ID and send e-mail with fraudulent headers, or for a node which routes the e-mail to you to substitute a different key. Finger servers are harder to tamper with, but not impossible. The problem is that while public key exchange does not require a secure channel (eavesdropping is not a problem) it does require a tamper-proof channel (key-substitution is a problem).\n[...]\nIf it is a key from someone you know well and whose voice you recognize then it is sufficient to give them a phone call and have them read their key's fingerprint (obtained with`pgp -kvc `). To be sure, also ask them for the key size and its key ID. There are ways to create a forged key with an identical fingerprint (see question 4.10 for details). You can of course also check these details in another way, for example if he has printed it on his business card.\n[...]\ntime. If the user ID includes an e\n[...]\nmail address, verify\n[...]\nby exchanging an agreed\n[...]\nThough the idea is simple, actually doing it is a bit complex, because you don't want to compromise other people's private keys or spread viruses (which is a risk whenever floppies are swapped willy-nilly). Usually, these parties involve meeting everyone at the party, verifying their identity and getting key fingerprints from them, and signing their key at home.\n[...]\n2. You compile\n[...]\ninto a single keyring, run\n[...]\nkeyring, and save\n[...]\na file.\n[...]\n6. Each person securely obtains their own fingerprint, and after being vouched for, they then read out their fingerprint out loud so everyone can verify it on the printout they have.\n[...]\n7. After everyone finishes this protocol, they can go home, obtain the keyring, run`pgp -kvc` on it themselves, and re-verify the bits, and sign the keys at their own leisure.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Phil's PGP Docs\nURL: http://phildev.net/pgp/gpgsigning.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSigning someone's key is saying to the world I have verified the identify of this person to the extent which I put in the signing. I believe this key belongs to the name in it, and the email listed also belongs to that person. That's a pretty strong statement! As such there are certain precautions you should take.\n[...]\nIn order to verify the key you'll want them to bring you a hard copy of their fingerprint. This is to check that the copy of the key you have obtained either from a keyserver or email is actually the key this person uses and you haven't somehow gotten a forged key. Therefore, this should be generated by them on the machine that holds their secret key or better yet, from their private key. If you or they generate this by grabbing their key from a server and comparing it to what you're about to sign, then you're not verifying anything! Think about it: if you're trying to verify the integrity of something, you can't use that something to do the verification.\n[...]\nIn order to verify their identity, you'll need them to provide at least one form of photo ID. Even if you work with the person or know the person, it's important to do this step if you have never checked their legal identity. This is to be sure that this person has the legal right to use the name they are using. It is up to you how much ID you want to see and to what level you will set your verification of the key during signing. In order to set full verification (3), I require two forms of ID, at least one of which is photo ID, and at least one of which is government-issued.\n[...]\nThis is one many people forget, but it's important. One of PGP's most common uses is to verify email, so verifying an email address is critical. If you sign a key for Bill Doe and verify the identity, but don't verify the email which is, say, bill@whitehouse.gov, you've now vouched for email signed by that key from that email.\n[...]\nWhen signing the key (which is covered below) rather than the default of signing all UIDs on the key, you sign each UID separately. You then encrypt-email the signed UID to the email address in that UID. In order for the person to get that signature and make it available (by, for example, uploading it to keyservers), they have to have both the private key and the email address. This ensures that the person who you are verifying has access to the private key and also owns that email address.\n[...]\nThe only way\n[...]\n1 could be in this\n[...]\nis if the person who owns the key Person 2 presented also has access to the email - they had to get the email and decrypt it. This combined with num2 which ensures that the\n[...]\nthe person that Person 1 spoke to (namely Person 2) earlier that day comfirms to Person 1 that person he\n[...]\nto earlier owns that private key and that email address.\n[...]\n## Verify their identity\n[...]\nHave them come to your office/room/whatever with their fingerprint on a piece of paper (they should generate this by doing`gpg -K --fingerprint them@something.com` on the machine with their secret key!) and photo ID. Then type:\n[...]\nAnd have them read off their fingerprint. If what you see and what you hear match, then you know you have the correct key. Now make sure they are who they say they are (either with ID, or by knowing who they are). Once you are positive the key they use is the key you are about to sign (they have the same fingerprint), and they are who they say they are...\n[...]\n. If you don't\n[...]\nthey've gotten\n[...]\nThen repeat this process for each UID. Note well, it is important to re-verify the fingerprint each time if your method of re-import is from a keyserver. If it's from a file on your local machine than verifying the fingerprint once for the key is sufficient.\n[...]\nOnce people have signed your key and send signed versions to you, you'll want to make this signatures available. The easiest way to do this is to import the signatures and then send your key to a keyserver. You can import the signature with`\n\n---\n\nTitle: Web of Trust, Explained: Decentralized Public‑Key Authenticity in OpenPGP – TheLinuxCode\nURL: https://thelinuxcode.com/web-of-trust-explained-decentralized-publickey-authenticity-in-openpgp/\nPublished: 2026-02-03T20:57:16.000Z\nAuthor: Linux Code\nHighlights:\nThe Web of Trust is one of the oldest and most practical answers to that problem in OpenPGP ecosystems (PGP, GnuPG, and other compatible tooling). It replaces a central certificate authority with social proof encoded as cryptographic signatures: I verify a person’s identity, I verify their key fingerprint, and then I sign their public key to publish that relationship. From there, other people can make informed trust decisions based on who signed what.\n[...]\nA centralized public key infrastructure (PKI) answers this with certificate authorities and certificate chains. Web of Trust answers it with people (or organizations) making explicit, auditable statements: “I checked this key belongs to this identity.”\n[...]\nWeb of Trust is a decentralized trust model used to assess whether a public key is authentic for a given identity.\n[...]\nInstead of trusting a single authority (or hierarchy), you rely on a network of signatures made by individuals you already trust. Those signatures bind a public key to a user identity (name, email, sometimes an organization). The network of who signed whom becomes a web.\n[...]\nbelongs to the\n[...]\n## How the Web Gets Built: Finger\n[...]\nA Web of Trust has three moving parts: public keys, fingerprints, and signatures.\n[...]\nIn OpenPGP, a public key typically contains one or more user IDs (often formatted like a name plus email). It can also contain subkeys for encryption and signing, expiration metadata, and more.\n[...]\n### 2) Fingerprints are the anchor\n[...]\nA fingerprint is a short, human‑checkable identifier derived from the public key material. Think of it as a checksum that is stable across copies of the same key.\n[...]\nWhen I verify a key, I verify the fingerprint through a channel I consider trustworthy: in person, a video call where the person shows an ID, an internal directory plus an out‑of‑band confirmation, or a company HR process. The exact ritual matters less than the fact that you’re not relying on the same channel that delivered the key.\n[...]\n### 3) Key signing is the commitment\n[...]\nOnce you verify identity and fingerprint, you sign that public key using your private key. That signature becomes evidence other people can inspect.\n[...]\n- You already have keys for a few long‑tenured engineers you trust.\n[...]\n- Priya generates a keypair and shares the public key.\n[...]\n- During onboarding (in person or via a verified HR video call), you compare Priya’s claimed fingerprint with what`gpg --fingerprint` shows.\n[...]\n- You sign Priya’s key.\n[...]\n- Priya’s key is now “introduced” into your team’s trust network.\n[...]\n- Import their key.\n[...]\n- Verify fingerprint out‑of‑band.\n[...]\n- Sign their key.\n[...]\n- Send back your signed copy (or publish your signature so others can see it).\n[...]\nThis doesn’t replace Web of Trust by itself;\n[...]\n### Operational tip: write down fingerprints\n[...]\n- Put the expected fingerprint for your release key in your repo’s`SECURITY.md` or an internal equivalent.\n[...]\n- Put it in your password manager as a secure note.\n- Put it in your on‑call runbook.\n[...]\nFirst, pick one workflow where key authenticity matters and where people already accept some ceremony—release signing, incident reports, or sharing production credentials during emergencies. Second, establish a tiny trust core (two or three senior engineers) and write a short key‑signing policy that includes out‑of‑band fingerprint checks and a minimum key expiration. Third, generate revocation certificates immediately and store them safely, because key loss is not a hypothetical.\n[...]\nOnce that’s running, add guardrails: document fingerprints in your runbooks, require multiple signatures for high‑impact roles, and set reminders for key rotation before expirations hit. You’ll quickly notice that the Web of Trust is less about cryptographic math and more about making accountability visible—who verified whom, when, and under what policy.",

Advanced material: Signatures on components — OpenPGP for application developers\nURL: https://openpgp.dev/book/adv/signing_components.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n### 22.1.4. Remove or revoke a User ID¶\n[...]\nSince OpenPGP certificates are often distributed by the means of key servers, new signatures on a certificate are often “\n[...]\n” into existing\n[...]\nof the certificate\n[...]\nTo effectively mark a User ID as invalid, the user can publish a copy of their certificate with a Certification Revocation signature(type ID`0x30`) attached to the invalidated User ID. This signature signals that the specified User ID is no longer valid or associated with the certificate holder.\n[...]\nThe structure of a Certification Revocation signature is as follows:\n[...]\nFor User ID revocations, the Reason for Revocation subpacket is crucial. A value of`0` means no specific reason, leading to a hard revocation, while`32` indicates the User ID is no longer valid, resulting in a soft revocation. Omitting the reason subpacket is also equivalent to a hard revocation.\n[...]\nIt is generally advisable to use reason code`32` for revoking User IDs.\n[...]\n### 22.1.5. Add a subkey¶\n[...]\nAs part of life-cycle management, users may need to add a new subkey to their OpenPGP certificate, often for reasons such as upgrading to a subkey with more advanced cryptographic algorithms. The process involves creating a specific signature structure:\n[...]\n### 22.1.6. Revoke a subkey¶\n[...]\nSubkeys, like User IDs, can be individually revoked in OpenPGP. This is done by issuing a Subkey Revocation signature(type ID`0x28`) using the primary key.\n[...]\nThe structure of such a signature is straightforward:\n[...]\nReason for Revocation\n[...]\nDetermines soft or\n[...]\nIn Subkey Revocation signatures, the reason for revocation subpacket can only have values in the range of`0-3`. The values`1`(key superseded) and`3`(key retired and no longer used) indicate soft revocations, whereas values`0`(no reason) and`2`(key compromised) indicate hard revocations.\n[...]\nNote that a value of`32` is not applicable in these signatures.\n[...]\n### 22.1.7. Revoke a certificate¶\n[...]\nUsers may find themselves needing to revoke their entire OpenPGP certificate, rendering it unusable. This could be for various reasons, such as migrating to a new certificate or in response to a compromise of the certificate’s secret key material.\n[...]\nWhile a soft-revoked certificate can be re-validated at a later time with a new certification, a hard revocation is permanent.\n[...]\nThe recommended way to revoke a certificate is by issuing a Key Revocation signature(type ID`0x20`). Its structure is similar to that of a Certification Revocation signature.\n[...]\nReason for Revocation\n[...]\nDetermines soft or hard revocation\n[...]\nFor Key Revocation signatures, the guidelines regarding the Reason for Revocation subpacket are the same as those for Subkey Revocation signatures.\n[...]\n### 22.1.8. Common subpackets in OpenPGP signatures¶\n[...]\nIn OpenPGP, certain subpackets are universally expected across all types of signatures, serving fundamental roles in the signature’s structure, verification and validation:\n[...]\nSignature Creation Time: This is a mandatory subpacket in every OpenPGP signature. It contains the timestamp of when the signature was created. For security and integrity, this subpacket must be located in the hashed area of the signature and is recommended to be marked as critical.\n[...]\nIssuer Fingerprint: Essential for signature validation, this subpacket identifies the key(or subkey) that was used to create the signature. OpenPGP v6 signatures should include the Issuer Fingerprint subpacket, containing the 32-byte fingerprint of the key.\n[...]\nare composed of lists of subpackets. Inherently\n[...]\nthis structure permits\n[...]\nsame subpacket, which could lead to conflicts. To manage these potential\n[...]\nHandling conflicts within\n[...]\nsame area: Conflicts can still arise within the same area, such as when two subpackets have different\n[...]\ntimes. In such cases, the OpenPG\n[...]\nlast occurrence of\n[...]\nconflicting subpacket in\n\n---\n\nTitle: Keys, subkeys, and certificates\nURL: https://book.sequoia-pgp.org/bckgrnd_keys_certificates.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n## Keys, subkeys, and certificates\n[...]\nA key in the context of OpenPGP is actually a dataset containing User IDs, key material (both public and secret), metadata like the expiration date, preferences, certifications, signatures, and subkeys – to name a few. This dataset is not fixed; parts of it will (likely) be modified over time as new certifications might be added, User IDs might change, and the expiration might be extended.\n[...]\npublic representation of a\n[...]\nIt contains the same objects as the\n[...]\nexcept for the\n[...]\nmaterial. It is\n[...]\neveryone should be able to do,\n[...]\nKeys (and certificates) can and usually have subkeys. These are keys that are bound to another key (called the primary key) by a subkey binding signature.\n[...]\nSubkeys were added to OpenPGP to solve a few shortcomings:\n[...]\n- By careful arrangement, the primary key of this bundle can be kept offline, as its secret key material is then only needed for updating metadata (User IDs, expiration) and subkey bindings. Day-to-day usage can then be completely delegated to subkeys.\n[...]\n- Subkeys have flags which indicate their usage (signing, encryption, etc.). OpenPGP allows having more than one subkey with the same flags. Having two subkeys flagged for encryption in a certificate will encrypt a message for both subkeys.\n[...]\n- Subkeys are grouped under a primary key, which represents the bundle. Hence, designating this bundle is done via the fingerprint of the primary key. This allows rotation or revocation of subkeys without affecting existing certifications, as the primary key doesn’t change. The subkey binding signature is used to extend the certification of the primary key to its subkeys.\n[...]\n- Subkeys introduce new key material to a key to prevent the use of the same key material for potentially conflicting operations. Both decrypting and signing are technically an encryption using the private key material. Signing an encrypted message might therefore inadvertently decrypt the message. OpenPGP prevents this as signatures are never created using the original data, but a checksum of it. Still, this is uncomfortably close and open to implementation mistakes. Using different keypairs for encryption/decryption and signing/verifying eliminates this problem.\n[...]\nSubkeys do not have User IDs, as these are associated with the primary key, but they can have an expiration date.\n[...]\nThere is no limit to the number of subkeys a key can have. When generating a new key,`sq` creates three subkeys with it. One for encryption, one for signing, and one for authentication. The primary key is used to certify the authenticity of the subkeys. As subkeys have markings (flags) for their use case, an application (like`sq`) can choose the right subkey for each operation. In some ways, a key acts like a little CA, with the primary key acting as a certificate authority, certifying the subkeys.\n[...]\n```\n$ sq key generate --own-key --userid alice --output - --rev-cert /dev/null --overwrite | sq packet dump\n\n```\n[...]\nThis example generates a key with a User ID of “alice”, it specifies`--output`, so that the generated key is not added to the key store. Using`--output -` redirects the new key to`STDOUT`, and piping the output into`sq packet dump` displays its expanded contents.`--rev-cert` and`--overwrite` are needed as a side effect of`--output`- in this example the result is discarded.`--own-key` authorizes the new key to introduce certificates - you can try`--shared-key` instead to see an unauthorized key.\n\n---\n\nTitle: 2.5. Schlüsselmanagement mit GPG und das Trust Problem | Sichere Email\n[...]\nThis video belongs to the openHPI course Sichere Email. Do you want to see more?\n[...]\n## 2.5. Schlüsselmanagement mit GPG und das Trust Problem",

            "output": "             4\\. Certificates — OpenPGP for application developers            \n\n \n\n-   ← [3\\. Cryptographic concepts and terms](cryptography.html \"Previous document\")\n-   [5\\. Managing private key material in OpenPGP](private_keys.html \"Next document\") →\n\n# 4\\. Certificates[¶](#certificates \"Link to this heading\")\n\nOpenPGP fundamentally hinges on the concept of “[OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate),” also known as “[OpenPGP public keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Public-Key).” These [certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) are complex data structures essential for [identity verification](glossary.html#term-Identity-Verification), data encryption, and [digital signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet). Understanding their structure and function is pivotal to effectively applying the OpenPGP standard.\n\nAn [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), by definition, does not contain [private key material](glossary.html#term-Private-Key-Material).\n\nFundamentally, the effective management of [certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) and a thorough grasp of their [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication) and [trust models](glossary.html#term-Trust-Model) are crucial for proficient OpenPGP usage. Although this document offers just a brief overview of these aspects, they form a fundamental part of the broader OpenPGP framework and warrant further study.\n\n-   For an in-depth exploration of OpenPGP’s [private key material](glossary.html#term-Private-Key-Material), refer to [Managing private key material in OpenPGP](private_keys.html). This chapter provides essential insights into [private key](glossary.html#term-Transferable-Secret-Key) management and security practices.\n    \n-   The bindings that link the [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) are comprehensively discussed in [Signatures on components](signing_components.html), offering a deeper understanding of [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) structure and integrity.\n    \n-   Finally, our chapter [Zooming in: Packet structure of certificates](zoom/certificates.html) discusses the internal structure of [certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) in detail.\n    \n\n## 4.1. Terminology: Understanding “keys”[¶](#terminology-understanding-keys \"Link to this heading\")\n\nThe term “[(cryptographic) keys](glossary.html#term-Cryptographic-Key)” is central to grasping the concept of [OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate). However, it can refer to different entities, making it a potentially confusing term. Let’s clarify those differences.\n\n### 4.1.1. Public vs. private keys[¶](#public-vs-private-keys \"Link to this heading\")\n\nThe term “[key](glossary.html#term-Key),” without additional context, can refer to either public or private [asymmetric](glossary.html#term-Asymmetric-Cryptography) key material. Additionally, [symmetric](glossary.html#term-Symmetric-Cryptography) keys may be used in OpenPGP to encrypt [private key material](glossary.html#term-Private-Key-Material), adding a layer of security and complexity.\n\n### 4.1.2. Layers of keys in OpenPGP[¶](#layers-of-keys-in-openpgp \"Link to this heading\")\n\nIn OpenPGP, the term “[key](glossary.html#term-Key)” may refer to three distinct layers, each serving a unique purpose:\n\n1.  A (bare) [“cryptographic key”](cryptography.html#asymmetric-key-pair) comprises the private and/or public parameters forming a key. For instance, in the case of an RSA [private key](glossary.html#term-Transferable-Secret-Key), the key consists of the exponent `d` along with the prime numbers `p` and `q`.\n    \n2.  An OpenPGP *[component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key)* includes either an “[OpenPGP primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key)” or an “[OpenPGP subkey](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey).” It is a building block of an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), consisting of a cryptographic keypair coupled with some invariant [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata), such as key [creation time](glossary.html#term-Creation-Time).\n    \n3.  An “[OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)” (or “OpenPGP key”) consists of several [component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key), [identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component), and other elements. These [certificates](glossary.html#term-Certificate) are dynamic, evolving over time as [components](glossary.html#term-Component) are added, [expire](glossary.html#term-Expiration), or are marked as [invalid](glossary.html#term-Validation).\n    \n\nThe following section will delve into the OpenPGP-specific layers (2 and 3) to provide a clearer understanding of their roles within [OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\n## 4.2. Structure of OpenPGP certificates[¶](#structure-of-openpgp-certificates \"Link to this heading\")\n\nAn [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) (or “[OpenPGP key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Key)”) is a collection of an arbitrary number of elements[\\[1\\]](#packets):\n\n-   [Component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key)\n    \n-   [Identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component)\n    \n-   Additional [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata), including connections between the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s [components](glossary.html#term-Component)\n    \n\nThis documentation collectively refers to [component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) and [identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component) as “the [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).”\n\n![Depicts a box with white background and the title \"OpenPGP certificate\". In the box several other boxes and accompanying texts, representing component keys and User IDs, are shown. There are three component keys boxes with a green frame, each with a dotted lower-left section, that shows the text \"key creation time\" and the green public key symbol in the lower right area. All three have a title, a unique fingerprint below the box and a unique capability keyword, perpendicular to the box on the right side. The top-most component key box has a light-green background, with the title \"Component Key (primary)\" and capability keyword \"certification\". The second-to-top component key box has a white background, with the title \"Component Key\" and capability keyword \"encryption\". The lowest component key box has a white background, with the title \"Component Key\" and capability keyword \"signing\". There are two User ID boxes, each with a black frame, open to top left and lower right corner. Both boxes have a user icon on the top left side, the title \"User ID\" on the top right side and a User ID string at the bottom. The top box has \"Alice Adams <alice@example.org>\" and the lower box has \"Alice\" as User ID string.](_images/Components_of_an_OpenPGP_Certificate.svg)\n\nFig. 4 Typical [components](glossary.html#term-Component) in an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)[¶](#fig-openpgp-certificate-components \"Link to this image\")\n\nEvery element in an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) revolves around a central [component](glossary.html#term-Component): the *[OpenPGP primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key)*. The primary key acts as a personal *[certification authority](glossary.html#term-Certification-Authority)* ([CA](glossary.html#term-Certification-Authority)) for the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s owner, enabling cryptographic statements regarding [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey), [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity), [expiration](glossary.html#term-Expiration), [revocation](glossary.html#term-Revocation), and more.\n\nNote\n\n[OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) tend to have a long lifespan, with the potential for modifications (typically by their owner) over time. [Components](glossary.html#term-Component) may be added or [invalidated](glossary.html#term-Validation) throughout a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s lifetime. However, once published, [components](glossary.html#term-Component) [cannot be removed](adv/certificates.html#append-only) from [certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\n## 4.3. Component keys[¶](#component-keys \"Link to this heading\")\n\nAn [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) usually contains multiple [component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key). [Component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) serve in one of two roles: either as an “[OpenPGP primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key)” or as an “[OpenPGP subkey](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey).”\n\n[OpenPGP component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) logically consist of an [asymmetric cryptographic keypair](cryptography.html#asymmetric-key-pair) and a creation timestamp. Once created, these attributes of a [component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) remain fixed (for ECDH keys, two additional parameters are part of a [component key](glossary.html#term-Component-Key)’s constitutive data[\\[2\\]](#ecdh-parameters)).\n\n![Depicts a box with white background and no title. In the box one other box is shown. The inner box has a green frame, with a dotted lower-left section, that shows the text \"key creation time\" and the green public key symbol, as well as the red-dotted private key symbol in the lower right area. In the top left of the inner box the text reads \"Component Key.\"](_images/Component_Key.svg)\n\nFig. 5 An [OpenPGP component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key)[¶](#fig-component-key \"Link to this image\")\n\n[Component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) containing [private key material](glossary.html#term-Private-Key-Material) also include [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) specifying the password protection scheme. This is another facet of [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata), akin to the aforementioned creation timestamp and additional parameters for certain algorithms. However, this discussion focuses on [OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), in which the [component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) contain only the public part of its cryptographic key data. For information on [private keys](glossary.html#term-Transferable-Secret-Key) in OpenPGP, see [Managing private key material in OpenPGP](private_keys.html).\n\n### 4.3.1. Fingerprint[¶](#fingerprint \"Link to this heading\")\n\nEach [OpenPGP component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) possesses an *[OpenPGP fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint)*. This [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) is derived from the [public key material](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), the [creation timestamp](glossary.html#term-Creation-Time), and, when relevant, the ECDH parameters.\n\n![Depicts a box with white background and the title \"Fingerprint of an OpenPGP component key.\" Inside, another box with a green frame, the title \"Component Key\", the text \"key creation time\" on the lower left and a the green public key symbol on the lower right is shown. Below the component key box a fingerprint in a box with a light-yellow background and a yellow dotted line is depicted. The word \"Fingerprint\" is shown left of the box with the fingerprint and both are connected with a yellow dotted line.](_images/Fingerprint.svg)\n\nFig. 6 Every [OpenPGP component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) is identifiable by a [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint).[¶](#fig-fingerprint \"Link to this image\")\n\nThe [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) of our example [OpenPGP component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) is `C0A5 8384 A438 E5A1 4F73 7124 26A4 D45D  BAEE F4A3 9E6B 30B0 9D55 13F9 78AC CA94`[\\[3\\]](#keyid).\n\nNote\n\nIn practice, the [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) of a [component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key), while not theoretically unique, functions effectively as a unique identifier. The use of a [cryptographic hash algorithm](cryptography.html#cryptographic-hash) in generating [fingerprints](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) makes the occurrence of two different [component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) with the same [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) extremely unlikely[\\[4\\]](#finger-unique).\n\n### 4.3.2. Primary key[¶](#primary-key \"Link to this heading\")\n\nThe [OpenPGP primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) is a [component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) that serves a distinct, central role in an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate):\n\n-   Its [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) acts as an identifier for the entire [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n    \n-   It facilitates lifecycle operations, such as adding or [invalidating](glossary.html#term-Validation) [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) or [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity) within a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n    \n\nTerminology\n\nIn the [RFC](glossary.html#term-RFC), the [OpenPGP primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) is occasionally referred to as “top-level key.” Informally, it has also been termed the “[master key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key).”\n\n### 4.3.3. Subkeys[¶](#subkeys \"Link to this heading\")\n\nModern [OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) typically include several [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) in addition to the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key), although these [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) are optional.\n\nWhile [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) have the same structural attributes as the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key), they fulfill different roles. [Subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) are cryptographically linked with the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key), a relationship further discussed in [Section 8.2.1](signing_components.html#bind-subkey).\n\n![Diagram depicting three component keys. The primary key is positioned at the top, designated for certification. Below it, connected by arrows, are two subkeys labeled as \"for encryption\" and \"for signing,\" respectively.](_images/Binding_Subkeys.svg)\n\nFig. 7 [OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) can contain multiple [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey).[¶](#fig-subkeys \"Link to this image\")\n\n## 4.4. Identity components[¶](#identity-components \"Link to this heading\")\n\n[Identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component) in an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) are used by the [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder) to state that they are known by a certain identifier (like a name, or an email address).\n\n### 4.4.1. User IDs in OpenPGP certificates[¶](#user-ids-in-openpgp-certificates \"Link to this heading\")\n\n[OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) can contain multiple [User IDs](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#uid). Each [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) associates the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) with an [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity).\n\n![Depicts a diagram with white background and the title \"User IDs\". Inside, a public primary component key for certification and a User ID is shown. A green arrow points from component key to User ID and is annotated with a signature.](_images/Binding_a_UserID.svg)\n\nFig. 8 Relationship of [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) to primary [component key](glossary.html#term-Component-Key) in an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)[¶](#fig-user-ids \"Link to this image\")\n\nA typical [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity) is a UTF-8-encoded string composed of a name and an email address. By convention, [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID) align with the format described in [RFC2822](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2822) as a *name-addr*.\n\nFor further conventions on [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID), refer to the document [draft-dkg-openpgp-userid-conventions-00](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-dkg-openpgp-userid-conventions/), dated 25 August 2023.\n\n**Split User IDs**\n\nOne proposed variant for encoding [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity) in [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID) is to use [“split User IDs”](https://dkg.fifthhorseman.net/blog/2021-dkg-openpgp-transition.html#split-user-ids). Although uncommon, there are currently no significant technical barriers to implementing this format[\\[5\\]](#dkg-split).\n\nThe rationale for split [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID) lies in the distinction between a name and an email address, which represent two separate facets of an individual’s [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity). Separating these elements simplifies the process for third parties tasked with certifying that an [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity) is legitimately connected to a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\nConsider this scenario: A third party is confident about the email-based [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity) of an individual (e.g.,`<alice@example.org>`) and is willing to certify it. However, they might not have sufficient knowledge about the person’s name-based [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity) (e.g., `Alice Adams`), so are unwilling to extend the same level of [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification). Split [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID) address this dichotomy by allowing distinct [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification) processes for each type of [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity).\n\n### 4.4.2. Implications of the Primary User ID[¶](#implications-of-the-primary-user-id \"Link to this heading\")\n\nWithin a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), a specific [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) is designated as the [Primary User ID](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#name-primary-user-id).\n\nEach [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) carries associated preference settings, such as preferred encryption algorithms, which is detailed in [Section 26.4](zoom/certificates.html#zoom-user-id)). When a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) is used in the context of a specific [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity), then the preferences associated with that [identity component](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component) are used. When a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) is used without reference to a specific [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity), the preferences associated with the [direct key signature](glossary.html#term-Direct-Key-Signature), or the [primary User ID](glossary.html#term-Primary-User-ID) take precedence by default.\n\nThe [primary User ID](glossary.html#term-Primary-User-ID) was historically the main store for preferences that apply to the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) as a whole. For more on this, see [Adding global metadata to a certificate](signing_components.html#primary-metadata).\n\n### 4.4.3. User attributes in OpenPGP[¶](#user-attributes-in-openpgp \"Link to this heading\")\n\nWhile [user attributes](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#user-attribute-packet) are similar to [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID), they are less commonly used.\n\nCurrently, the OpenPGP standard prescribes only one format to be stored in user attributes: an [image](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#name-image-attribute-subpacket) in JPEG format. Typically, this image represents the key owner, although it is not required.\n\n## 4.5. Linking the components[¶](#linking-the-components \"Link to this heading\")\n\nTo form an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), individual [components](glossary.html#term-Component) are interconnected by the [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder) using their OpenPGP software. Within OpenPGP, this process is termed “binding”, as in “a [subkey](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) is bound to the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key).” These bindings are realized using cryptographic [signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet). An in-depth discussion of this topic can be found in [Signatures on components](signing_components.html).\n\nIn very abstract terms, the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) acts as a root of trust or “[certification authority](glossary.html#term-Certification-Authority).” It is responsible for:\n\n-   issuing [signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) that express the [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder)’s intent to use specific [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) or [identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component);\n    \n-   conducting other lifecycle operations, including setting [expiration](glossary.html#term-Expiration) dates and marking [components](glossary.html#term-Component) as [invalidated](glossary.html#term-Validation) or “[revoked](glossary.html#term-Revocation).”\n    \n\nBy binding [components](glossary.html#term-Component) using digital [signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet), recipients of an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) need only [validate](glossary.html#term-Validation) the [authenticity](glossary.html#term-Authentication) of the [primary key](glossary.html#term-Primary-Key) to use for their communication partner. Traditionally, this is done by manually verifying the *[fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint)* of the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key). Once the [validity](glossary.html#term-Validation) of the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) is confirmed, the [validity](glossary.html#term-Validation) of the remaining [components](glossary.html#term-Component) can be automatically assessed by the user’s OpenPGP software. Generally, [components](glossary.html#term-Component) are [valid](glossary.html#term-Validation) parts of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) if there is a statement signed by the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) endorsing this [validity](glossary.html#term-Validation).\n\n## 4.6. Metadata in certificates[¶](#metadata-in-certificates \"Link to this heading\")\n\n[OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), their [component keys](glossary.html#term-Component-Key), and [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity) possess [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) that is not stored within the [components](glossary.html#term-Component) it pertains to. Instead, this [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) is stored within signature packets, which are integral to the structure of an OpenPGP certificate.\n\nKey attributes, such as [capabilities](glossary.html#term-Capability) (like *signing* or *encryption*) and [expiration times](glossary.html#term-Expiration-Time), are examples of [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) not stored in the [component key](glossary.html#term-Component-Key) data. How this [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) is stored depends on the [component](glossary.html#term-Component):\n\n-   **[Primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata)** is defined either through a [direct key signature](glossary.html#term-Direct-Key-Signature) on the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) (preferred in OpenPGP version 6), or by associating the [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) with the [Primary User ID](#primary-user-id).\n    \n-   **[Subkey](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata)** is defined within the [subkey binding signature](adv/signing_components.html#recipe-binding-subkeys) that links the [subkey](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) to the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n    \n-   **[Identity component](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component) [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata)** is associated via the [certifying self-signature](signing_components.html#bind-identity) that links the [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity) (usually in the form of a [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID)) to the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n    \n\nIt is crucial to note that the [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) remain static after their creation. The use of [signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) to store [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) allows for subsequent modifications without altering the original [component](glossary.html#term-Component). For instance, a [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder) can update the [expiration time](glossary.html#term-Expiration-Time) of a [component](glossary.html#term-Component) by issuing a new, superseding [signature](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet).\n\n![Depicts a direct key signature, associated with a primary component key.](_images/Primary_key_metadata.svg)\n\nFig. 9 [Metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) can be associated with the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) using a *[direct key signature](glossary.html#term-Direct-Key-Signature)*.[¶](#fig-primary-metadata \"Link to this image\")\n\n### 4.6.1. Defining operational capabilities of component keys with key flags[¶](#defining-operational-capabilities-of-component-keys-with-key-flags \"Link to this heading\")\n\nEach [component key](glossary.html#term-Component-Key) has a set of [“key flags”](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#key-flags) that delineate the operations a key can perform.\n\nCommonly used [key flags](glossary.html#term-Key-Flag) include:\n\n-   **[Certification](glossary.html#term-Certification-Key-Flag)**: enables issuing third-party [certifications](glossary.html#term-Certification)\n    \n-   **[Signing](glossary.html#term-Signing-Key-Flag)**: allows the key to sign data\n    \n-   **[Encryption](glossary.html#term-Encryption-Key-Flag)**: allows the key to encrypt data\n    \n-   **[Authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication-Key-Flag)**: primarily used for SSH authentication[\\[6\\]](#auth-flag)\n    \n\nNote\n\nDistinct [component keys](glossary.html#term-Component-Key) handle specific operations. Only the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) can be used for [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification), although it can have additional [capabilities](glossary.html#term-Capability). [Subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey) can be used for signing, encryption, and authentication but cannot have the [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification) [capability](glossary.html#term-Capability). A [component key](glossary.html#term-Component-Key) can technically have multiple [capabilities](glossary.html#term-Capability). It is considered good practice, however, to [use separate keys for each capability](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#section-10.1.5-7).\n\nNotably, in many algorithms, encryption and signing-related functionalities (i.e., [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification-Key-Flag), [signing](glossary.html#term-Signing-Key-Flag), [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication-Key-Flag)) are mutually exclusive, because the algorithms only support one of those two families of operations[\\[7\\]](#key-flag-sharing).\n\n### 4.6.2. Algorithm preferences and feature signaling[¶](#algorithm-preferences-and-feature-signaling \"Link to this heading\")\n\nOpenPGP incorporates significant [“cryptographic agility”](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptographic_agility). It doesn’t rely on a single fixed set of algorithms. Instead, it defines a suite of cryptographic primitives from which users (or their applications) can choose.\n\nThis agility facilitates the easy adoption of new cryptographic primitives into the standard, allowing for a seamless transition. Users can gradually migrate to new cryptographic mechanisms without disruption.\n\nHowever, this approach requires that OpenPGP software determine the cryptographic mechanisms that a set of communication partners can handle and prefer. OpenPGP employs several mechanisms for this purpose, which allow negotiation between sender and recipient. It’s important to note that OpenPGP is not an online scheme; thus, this negotiation is effectively one-way. The active party interprets the preferences expressed in the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) of the passive party.\n\nNegotiation mechanisms in OpenPGP include:\n\n-   [Preferred hash algorithms](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#preferred-hashes-subpacket)\n    \n-   [Preferred symmetric ciphers for v1 SEIPD](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#preferred-v1-seipd)\n    \n-   [Preferred AEAD ciphersuites](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#preferred-v2-seipd)\n    \n-   [Features subpacket](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#features-subpacket)\n    \n-   [Preferred compression algorithms](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#preferred-compression-subpacket)\n    \n\nBeyond these explicitly expressed preferences, implementations also deduce [capabilities](glossary.html#term-Capability) of communication partners based on the version of the [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) they possess.\n\n#### 4.6.2.1. User ID-specific preferences[¶](#user-id-specific-preferences \"Link to this heading\")\n\nAs a starting point, a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) has a set of preferences that apply generally. These are defined either in a [direct key signature](glossary.html#term-Direct-Key-Signature), or via the [primary User ID](glossary.html#term-Primary-User-ID) of the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\nAdditionally, OpenPGP allows modeling [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID)\\-specific preferences. The idea is that a user may prefer a different suite of algorithms on their private email account compared to their work email account. Such [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity)\\-specific preferences can be expressed on the certifying [signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) that bind [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID) to a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\n## 4.7. A typical OpenPGP certificate, revisited[¶](#a-typical-openpgp-certificate-revisited \"Link to this heading\")\n\nFollowing our review of how [keys](glossary.html#term-Component-Key) and [identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component) are linked, let’s reexamine the [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) from [Fig. 4](#fig-openpgp-certificate-components). Our focus now extends to all of its binding signatures and the [direct key signature](glossary.html#term-Direct-Key-Signature) that contains [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) for the full [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate):\n\n![Depicts an OpenPGP certificate, including a set of components, binding signatures, and a direct key signature on the primary key.](_images/OpenPGP_Certificate.svg)\n\nFig. 10 This shows a typical [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), including binding [signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) for all of its [components](glossary.html#term-Component), and a [signature](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) that associates [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) with the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key).[¶](#fig-openpgp-certificate \"Link to this image\")\n\n## 4.8. Revocations[¶](#revocations \"Link to this heading\")\n\nWhen a [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder) needs to [invalidate](glossary.html#term-Validation) certain [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of their [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), or even the entire [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), they accomplish this through “[revocation](glossary.html#term-Revocation).” [Revoking](glossary.html#term-Revocation) the [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) renders the entire [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) [invalid](glossary.html#term-Validation).\n\nNotably, [revocations](glossary.html#term-Revocation) are not the only means by which [components](glossary.html#term-Component) can become [invalid](glossary.html#term-Validation). Other factors, such as the passing of a [component](glossary.html#term-Component)’s [expiration time](glossary.html#term-Expiration-Time), can also render [components](glossary.html#term-Component) [invalid](glossary.html#term-Validation).\n\nFor more detailed information on [revoking](glossary.html#term-Revocation) specific [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), see the section on [Revocation self-signatures: Invalidating certificate components](signing_components.html#self-revocations).\n\n## 4.9. Third-party (identity) certifications[¶](#third-party-identity-certifications \"Link to this heading\")\n\nSince its inception, [third-party identity certifications](glossary.html#term-Third-party-Identity-Certification) have been a cornerstone of the OpenPGP ecosystem. The original PGP designers, starting with Phil Zimmermann, advocated for decentralized [trust models](glossary.html#term-Trust-Model) over reliance on centralized authorities. This decentralized approach in OpenPGP is known as the [“Web of Trust.”](signing_components.html#wot)\n\n[Third-party certifications](glossary.html#term-Third-party-Identity-Certification) are statements by OpenPGP users confirming that a user with a specific [identity](glossary.html#term-Identity) is the owner of a particular [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\nFor example, Bob’s OpenPGP software may issue a [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification) that Bob has checked that the [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) `Alice Adams <alice@example.org>` and the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) with the [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) `AAA1 8CBB 2546 85C5 8358 3205 63FD 37B6  7F33 00F9 FB0E C457 378C D29F 1026 98B3` are legitimately linked.\n\nTake, for instance, a scenario where Bob’s OpenPGP software issues a [certification](glossary.html#term-Certification) confirming as legitimate the link between the [User ID](glossary.html#term-User-ID) `Alice Adams <alice@example.org>` and the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) bearing the [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) `AAA1 8CBB 2546 85C5 8358 3205 63FD 37B6  7F33 00F9 FB0E C457 378C D29F 1026 98B3`.\n\nThis process assumes that Bob knows the person known as `Alice Adams` and is confident that `alice@example.org` is indeed Alice’s email address. Bob also verifies that the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) his OpenPGP software associates with Alice matches the one Alice uses. In essence, both users must have a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) for Alice with an identical [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint). In OpenPGP version 6, manual [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) comparison by end users is discouraged, with a replacement [verification](glossary.html#term-Verification) mechanism still under development. The [verification](glossary.html#term-Verification) process must occur over a sufficiently secure channel, such as an end-to-end encrypted video call or a face-to-face meeting.\n\nFor more on third-party [certifications](glossary.html#term-Certification), see [Authentication and delegation in third-party signatures](signing_components.html#third-party-certifications).\n\n\\[[1](#id2)\\]\n\nIn technical terms, the elements of an [OpenPGP certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) are a collection of “[packets](glossary.html#term-Packet).” Each [component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key) and [identity component](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component) is internally represented as a [packet](glossary.html#term-Packet). Another common type of [packet](glossary.html#term-Packet) is the “[signature](glossary.html#term-Signature)” [packet](glossary.html#term-Packet), which connect the [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate).\n\n\\[[2](#id4)\\]\n\nFor [ECDH](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#name-algorithm-specific-part-for-ecd) [component keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key), two additional algorithm parameters are integral to the [component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key)’s constitutive and immutable properties. Those parameters specify a hash function and a [symmetric](glossary.html#term-Symmetric-Cryptography) encryption algorithm.\n\n\\[[3](#id6)\\]\n\nIn OpenPGP version 4, the rightmost 64 bits were sometimes used as a shorter identifier, called “[Key ID](glossary.html#term-Key-ID).” For example, an OpenPGP version 4 [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) with the [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) `B3D2 7B09 FBA4 1235 2B41 8972 C8B8 6AC4 2455 4239` might be referenced by the 64-bit [Key ID](glossary.html#term-Key-ID) `C8B8 6AC4 2455 4239` or formatted as `0xC8B86AC424554239`.  \nHistorically, even shorter 32-bit identifiers were used, like this: `2455 4239`, or `0x24554239`. Such identifiers still appear in very old documents about PGP. However, [32-bit identifiers have been long deemed unfit for purpose](https://evil32.com/). At one point, 32-bit identifiers were called “short [Key ID](glossary.html#term-Key-ID),” while 64-bit identifiers were referred to as “long Key ID.”\n\n\\[[4](#id7)\\]\n\nFor both [OpenPGP version 6](glossary.html#term-RFC) and version 4, the likelihood of accidental occurrence of duplicate [fingerprints](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) is negligible when [key material](glossary.html#term-Key-Material) is generated based on an acceptable source of entropy. A separate question is if an attacker can purposely craft a second key with the same [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) as a given pre-existing [component key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Component-Key). With the current state of the art, this is not possible for OpenPGP version 6 and version 4 keys. However, at the time of this writing, the SHA-1-based [fingerprints](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint) of OpenPGP version 4 are considered insufficiently strong at protecting against the generation of pairs of [key material](glossary.html#term-Key-Material) with the same [fingerprint](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Fingerprint).\n\n\\[[5](#id11)\\]\n\nHistorically, the OpenPGP ecosystem faced challenges in this context. For further details, refer to Daniel Kahn Gillmor’s January 2019 article, [“What were Separated User IDs”](https://dkg.fifthhorseman.net/blog/2019-dkg-openpgp-transition.html#what-were-separated-user-ids).\n\n\\[[6](#id13)\\]\n\nIt’s important to note that the function of the [authentication](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#name-authentication-via-digital-) [key flag](glossary.html#term-Key-Flag) is unrelated to the [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication) process used in certifying OpenPGP [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity) and linking them to [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate). Rather, this flag indicates a mechanism that uses [cryptographic signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) to confirm control of [private key material](glossary.html#term-Private-Key-Material) with a remote system.\n\n\\[[7](#id14)\\]\n\nWith ECC algorithms, it’s impossible to combine [encryption](glossary.html#term-Encryption-Key-Flag) functions with those intended for [signing](glossary.html#term-Signing-Key-Flag). For example, ed25519 is specifically used for [signing](glossary.html#term-Signing-Key-Flag); cv25519 is designated for [encryption](glossary.html#term-Encryption-Key-Flag).\n\n \n\n-   ← [3\\. Cryptographic concepts and terms](cryptography.html \"Previous document\")\n-   [5\\. Managing private key material in OpenPGP](private_keys.html \"Next document\") →\n\n[![Logo of OpenPGP for application developers](_static/logo.svg)](index.html)\n\n# [OpenPGP for application developers](index.html)\n\n \n\n### Navigation\n\n-   [1\\.
