Signatures over data](signing_data.html \"Previous document\")\n-   [9\\. Signature verification](verification.html \"Next document\") →\n\n# 8\\. Signatures on components[¶](#signatures-on-components \"Link to this heading\")\n\nThis chapter examines [OpenPGP signatures](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) associated with [certificate components](glossary.html#term-Component), applying to:\n\n-   [component keys](glossary.html#term-Component-Key), encompassing [primary keys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) and [subkeys](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Subkey)\n    \n-   [identity components](glossary.html#term-Identity-Component), namely [User IDs](glossary.html#term-User-ID) and [User attributes](glossary.html#term-User-Attribute)\n    \n\n[Signatures on components](glossary.html#term-Signature-On-Component) are used to construct and maintain [certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate), and to model the [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication) of [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity).\n\nThis chapter expands on topics introduced in the [Certificates](certificates.html) chapter.\n\n## 8.1. Self-signatures vs third-party signatures[¶](#self-signatures-vs-third-party-signatures \"Link to this heading\")\n\n[Component signatures](glossary.html#term-Signature-On-Component) in OpenPGP are categorized into two distinct types:\n\n-   **[self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature)**, which are issued by the [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder) using the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key)\n    \n-   **[third-party signatures](glossary.html#term-Third-party-Signature)**, which are issued by an external entity, not the [certificate holder](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder)\n    \n\n### 8.1.1. Self-signatures[¶](#self-signatures \"Link to this heading\")\n\n[Self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature) are fundamental in creating and managing [OpenPGP certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate). They bind the various [components](glossary.html#term-Component) of a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) into one combined data structure and facilitate the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s [life-cycle management](glossary.html#term-Life-cycle-Management).\n\n[Life-cycle management](glossary.html#term-Life-cycle-Management) operations include:\n\n-   [binding](glossary.html#term-Binding-Signature) additional [components](glossary.html#term-Component) to a [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)\n    \n-   modifying [expiration time](glossary.html#term-Expiration-Time) or other [metadata](glossary.html#term-Metadata) of [components](glossary.html#term-Component)\n    \n-   revoking, and thus invalidating, [components](glossary.html#term-Component) or existing [self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature)\n    \n\n[Self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature) are issued by the [certificate’s owner](glossary.html#term-Certificate-Holder) using the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key).\n\nNote\n\nNo [key flag](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#name-key-flags) is required to issue [self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature). An [OpenPGP primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) can issue [self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature) by default.\n\n### 8.1.2. Third-party signatures[¶](#third-party-signatures \"Link to this heading\")\n\n[Third-party signatures](glossary.html#term-Third-party-Signature) are pivotal in OpenPGP for decentralized [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication), forming the basis of the [Web of Trust](glossary.html#term-Web-Of-Trust). They encode [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication)\\-related statements about [certificates](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate) and linked [identities](glossary.html#term-Identity), establishing trustworthiness of [identity claims](glossary.html#term-Identity-Claim).\n\nThird-party signatures are used to make specific statements:\n\n-   [certifying](glossary.html#term-Certification) [identity claims](glossary.html#term-Identity-Claim)\n    \n-   [delegating](glossary.html#term-Delegation) [authentication](glossary.html#term-Authentication) decisions\n    \n-   [revoking](glossary.html#term-Revocation), and thus [invalidating](glossary.html#term-Validation), prior [third-party signature](glossary.html#term-Third-party-Signature) statements\n    \n\nNote\n\nThe **[certify others](glossary.html#term-Certification-Key-Flag)** [key flag](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9580.html#name-key-flags) (`0x01`) is required to issue [third-party signatures](glossary.html#term-Third-party-Signature). By convention[\\[1\\]](#primary-certification), only the [certificate](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Certificate)’s [primary key](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Primary-Key) can hold this [key flag](glossary.html#term-Key-Flag).\n\n### 8.1.3. Distinct functions of self-signatures and third-party signatures[¶](#distinct-functions-of-self-signatures-and-third-party-signatures \"Link to this heading\")\n\nThe meaning of an [OpenPGP signature](glossary.html#term-OpenPGP-Signature-Packet) depends significantly on its [issuer](glossary.html#term-Issuer). [Self-signatures](glossary.html#term-Self-signature) and [third-p

Other people use it to encrypt messages to you or verify your signatures.\n- Private key: keep secret. You use it to decrypt messages and create signatures.\n[...]\n- PGP means Pretty Good Privacy, the original software and idea.\n- OpenPGP is the open standard for compatible PGP-style encryption.\n- GPG / GnuPG is a popular free command-line program implementing OpenPGP.\n- OpenPGP.js is the JavaScript OpenPGP library used by these browser tools.\n[...]\nUse Generate Key Pair to create your private key and public key. PGP encryption ensures that only the intended recipient can read your message, while digital signatures prove that a message genuinely came from you. Tools like KeychainPGP make this entire process accessible through a clipboard-first workflow — no command line required.\n[...]\n- KeychainPGP web app — Open the online PGP tool in any modern browser. Everything runs locally via WebAssembly compiled from Rust. No installation, no account, no data sent to a server.\n- KeychainPGP desktop app — Download the native app for Windows, macOS, Linux, or Android from the GitHub releases page. The desktop app adds global hotkeys, system tray integration, clipboard auto-clear, OS credential store support, and QR code key sync with mobile.\n[...]\n- KeychainPGP CLI — The`keychainpgp` CLI provides commands for key generation, encryption, decryption, signing, verification, and keyring management. Ideal for scripting and automation.\n[...]\n1. Open KeychainPGP (either the web app or desktop app).\n2. Navigate to the Keys tab.\n3. Click Generate New Key.\n4. Enter your name and email address. These become part of your key’s identity (called a User ID).\n5. Click Generate. KeychainPGP creates an Ed25519 + X25519 key pair — the most modern and secure algorithm available in OpenPGP.\n[...]\nOnce imported, you can encrypt messages to that contact. You should verify the key’s fingerprint through a separate, trusted channel (such as a phone call or in-person meeting) to confirm it genuinely belongs to the person you think it does.\n[...]\nWith your key pair generated and your contact’s public key imported, you are ready to encrypt. PGP encryption ensures that only the holder of the corresponding private key can read the message.\n[...]\nmessage wrapped in a\n[...]\n- The message was signed by the key with a specific\n[...]\n- The message has not been modified\n[...]\nit was signed.\n[...]\n- The signing key\n[...]\nWhat sets KeychainPGP apart from traditional PGP tools is its clipboard-\n[...]\nencryption workflow. This eliminates the need to copy text back and forth between your PGP tool and your communication app.\n[...]\nRuns entirely in the browser via WebAssembly\n- No installation, no account required\n- Perfect for quick encryption tasks or when you are on a shared or unfamiliar computer\n[...]\nKeys are stored in browser local storage (cleared when you clear site\n[...]\n- Global hotkeys for encrypt (`Ctrl+Shift+E`) and decrypt (`Ctrl+Shift+D`)\n- System tray integration — always running in the background\n- Clipboard auto-clear for decrypted text\n- OPSEC mode with RAM-only keys and panic wipe\n- Secure key storage via OS credential manager\n- Better suited for daily use and long-term key management\n[...]\nBoth apps are free, open source (MIT / Apache-2.0), and have zero telemetry or tracking. Download the desktop app from the GitHub releases page.\n[...]\nFingerprints are used to verify that a public key genuinely belongs to the person you think it does. When you receive someone’s public key, compare its fingerprint with the fingerprint the owner provides through a separate trusted channel (phone call, in-person meeting, or verified social media profile). This prevents man-in-the-middle attacks where an attacker substitutes their own public key for the real one. Note that both the`curve25519` and`ed25519` options generate a primary key for signing using Ed25519 and a subkey for encryption using Curve25519.\n[...]\n```\n(async () => {\n    const { privateKey, publicKey, revocationCertificate } = await openpgp.generateKey({\n        type: 'ecc', // Type of the key, defaults to ECC\n        curve: 'curve25519', // ECC curve name, defaults to curve25519\n        userIDs: [{ name: 'Jon Smith', email: 'jon@example.com' }], // you can pass multiple user IDs\n        passphrase: 'super long and hard to guess secret', // protects the private key\n        format: 'armored' // output key format, defaults to 'armored' (other options: 'binary' or 'object')\n    });\n\n    console.log(privateKey);     // '-----BEGIN PGP PRIVATE KEY BLOCK ... END PGP SIGNATURE-----'\n\n    const signedMessage = await openpgp.readCleartextMessage({\n        cleartextMessage // parse armored message\n    });\n    const verificationResult = await openpgp.verify({\n        message: signedMessage,\n        verificationKeys: publicKey\n    });\n    const { verified, keyID } = verificationResult.signatures[0];\n    try {\n        await verified; // throws on invalid signature\n        console.log('Signed by key id ' + keyID.toHex());\n    } catch (e) {\n        throw new Error('Signature could not be verified: ' + e.message);\n    }\n})();\n[...]\n#### Create and verify detached signatures\n[...]\n#### Streaming sign and verify Uint8Array data\n[...]\nIt should be noted that js crypto apps deployed via regular web hosting (a.k.a. host-based security) provide users with less security than installable apps with auditable static versions. Installable apps can be deployed as a Firefox or Chrome packaged app. These apps are basically signed zip files and their runtimes typically enforce a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) to protect users against XSS. Download them if you want to continue conversations later, or just generate new keys each time for practice.\n[...]\n##### Technical Details\n[...]\n###### Public Key Import:\n[...]\n- ✓ Safe to store - public keys aren't secret\n- ✓ Use their key to encrypt messages TO them\n- ✓ You import and store them locally\n- ✓ Recipients share their public keys\n[...]\n###### Encryption Workflow:\n[...]\n- 4. Only THEY can decrypt with their private key\n- 3. Encrypt with THEIR public key\n- 2. Write your message\n- 1. Choose recipient from list\n[...]\n###### Security Notes:\n[...]\n- ✓ Demo keys are for learning only\n- ✓ Keys should come from trusted sources\n- ✓ Use key fingerprints for verification\n- ✓ Verify key authenticity in real use\n\n---\n\nTitle: PGPractice - Interactive PGP Tutorial\nURL: https://pgpractice.com/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nPGPractice - Interactive PGP Tutorial\n[...]\nGnuPG (GNU Privacy Guard) is a free implementation of the OpenPGP standard that allows you to encrypt and sign your data and communications. It's a powerful command-line tool used for:\n[...]\n- Encryption: Protect sensitive information so only intended recipients can read it\n- Digital signatures: Verify the authenticity and integrity of messages and files\n- Key management: Create, import, export, and manage cryptographic keys\n- Secure communication: Enable private communication over insecure channels\n[...]\nThis interactive tutorial will teach you essential GnuPG commands through hands-on practice:\n[...]\n- Generate secure PGP key pairs\n- Export and share public keys\n- Encrypt messages for specific recipients\n- Decrypt received messages\n- Create and verify digital signatures\n[...]\nEach step includes detailed instructions, example outputs, and real-time validation of your work.\n[...]\n## Interactive PGP/GnuPG Command Line Tutorial\n[...]\n### Generate Your PGP Key Pair\n[...]\nFirst, we need to create a PGP key pair using GnuPG. We'll use the full key generation command for more control over options.\n[...]\n`gpg --full-gen-key`\n[...]\nstep guide:\n[...]\n1. Key type selection: Choose`1` for \"RSA and RSA\" (most compatible)\n\n2. Key size: Enter`4096` for maximum security (when prompted)\n\n3. Expiration: Enter`0` for no expiration (for practice purposes) Confirm with`y`\n\n4. User ID information: • Real name: Your preferred name • Email: Optionally, your email address • Comment: Optionally, a note explaining what the key is for Confirm with`o` for \"Okay\"\n\n5. Passphrase: Choose a secure passphrase when prompted (you'll need this later!)\n[...]\n#### After key generation, verify it was created:\n[...]\n`gpg --list-keys`\n[...]\n### Export Your Public Key\n[...]\nNow let's export your public key so others can use it to encrypt messages to you and verify your signatures.\n[...]\n`gpg --armor --export your-email@example.com`\n[...]\nReplace \"your-email@example.com\" with the email address you used when creating your key. The --armor flag outputs the key in ASCII format instead of binary.\n[...]\n### Encrypt a Message\n[...]\nNow let's encrypt a message using your public key. First, create a simple text file with a message.\n[...]\n#### Commands to run:\n[...]\necho \"This is a secret message\" > message.txt\n\ngpg --armor --encrypt --recipient your-email@example.com message.txt\n[...]\ncreates an encrypted\n[...]\ncalled message.txt\n[...]\n### Decrypt a Message\n[...]\nNow let's decrypt the message you encrypted in the previous step using your private key.\n[...]\n#### Command to run:\n[...]\n`gpg --decrypt message.txt.asc`\n[...]\nYou'll be prompted to enter your passphrase. GPG will then decrypt and display the original message.\n[...]\n### Encrypt with Tutorial Key\n[...]\nNow let's test encryption with a key provided by PGPractice. I'll give you a public key, and you encrypt a message with it.\n[...]\n#### PGPractice Public Key:\n[...]\n#### Commands to run:\n[...]\n1. Save the PGPractice key above to a file: pgpractice-key.asc\n\n2. Import it: gpg --import pgpractice-key.asc\n\n3. Create your message: echo \"Your secret message here\" > my-message.txt\n\n4. Encrypt: gpg --armor --encrypt --recipient tutorial@pgpractice.local my-message.txt\n[...]\nWrite any message you want! PGPractice will decrypt it and show you what you wrote to confirm encryption worked.\n[...]\n### Sign and Verify Messages\n[...]\nNow let's learn to create digital signatures and verify them. This proves authenticity and integrity.\n[...]\n#### Step 1: Sign a Message\n[...]\n##### Commands to run:\n[...]\necho \"This message is from me\" > signed-message.txt\n\ngpg --clearsign signed-message.txt\n[...]\n#### Step 2: Verify the Signature\n[...]\n##### Command to run:\n[...]\n`gpg --verify signed-message.txt.asc`\n\n---\n\nTitle: PGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys (Client‑Side)  – Free Tool | 8gwifi.org\nURL: https://8gwifi.org/pgp-suite.jsp\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: Anish Nath\nHighlights:\nPGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys (Client‑Side) – Free Tool | 8gwifi.org \n\n \n\n---\n\n \n\n \n\n# PGP Suite: Sign, Verify & Generate Keys\n[...]\nCreate and verify signatures, and generate keys — all client‑side\n\n---\n\nPrivate: All signing happens locally using OpenPGP.js v5. Your private key and passphrase never leave your device.\n[...]\nNeed verification? Use PGP File Signature Verification or the main Signature Verifier.\n\n \n\n- Sign Message (Cleartext)\n- Sign File (Detached)\n- Verify Signatures\n- Generate Keys\n[...]\n- Use a long, unique passphrase.\n[...]\n- Share only the signed output; keep your private key secret.\n- Verify\n[...]\nPGP (RFC 4880) provides confidentiality (encryption), authenticity (signatures), and integrity.\n[...]\n- Keys: Public (share) + Private (keep secret). Verify the fingerprint over a trusted channel.\n- Signing: Proves author and protects from tampering; does not hide content.\n- Encryption: Hides content; does not prove author unless also signed.\n- Types: Cleartext signatures (inline), Detached signatures (.asc/.sig), Attached/opaque signatures.\n- Formats: .asc (armored text), .pgp/.gpg (binary).\n- Trust: Direct trust, Web of Trust, WKD/HKP discovery.\n- Key Hygiene: Use strong passphrases, keep revocation cert offline, rotate/expire keys, prefer Ed25519 for signing.\n[...]\n), wrong passphrase,\n[...]\n- Author: Anish Nath(Security Engineer)\n- Library: OpenPGP.js v5.11.2 (client-side only)\n- Data handling: Keys and files never leave your device\n- Updated: 2025-11-25\n[...]\n- Detached signature does not modify the original file.\n- Share both the file and the signature with the recipient.\n- Recipients can verify with File Signature Verify.\n\n \n\n \n\n##### Verify Cleartext Signed Message\n[...]\n##### Signer Public Key\n[...]\nVerify Message Signature\n[...]\nwith Detached Signature\n[...]\n##### Signer Public Key\n\n—\n[...]\n- Always confirm the public key fingerprint via trusted channels.\n- Cleartext verification extracts the original message if valid.\n- Detached signatures require both original file and signature.\n\n \n\n#####\n[...]\nType Ed25519 (recommended) RSA\n[...]\nBits 2048 3072 4096\n[...]\nGenerate Copy\n\nPassphrase is required.\n\nUse a long, unique passphrase (20+ chars recommended).\n[...]\n- Backup your private key and passphrase securely.\n- Share only the public key; keep private key secret.\n- Consider generating a revocation certificate.\n\n \n\n \n\n#### Support This Free Tool\n[...]\nPrivacy Guarantee: Private keys you enter or generate are never stored on our servers. All tools are served over HTTPS.\n[...]\n- PGP Encryption/Decryption\n- PGP Key Generation\n- PGP Signature Verifier\n- PGP Suite: Sign/Verify/Keys\n- PGP KeyDumper\n- PGP Encrypted File Transfer\n- PGP Decrypt files\n\n \n\n###### Sharing\n[...]\nQ: What is the difference between cleartext and detached signatures? A: Cleartext signatures keep the message human‑readable with an inline signature block; detached signatures are separate .asc/.sig files used to verify any file without changing it.\n[...]\nQ: How do I verify a signed message? A: Paste the signed text and the signer’s public key in the Verify tab and click Verify. You’ll see validity, signer KeyID, fingerprint, UID and the signature time.\n[...]\nQ: How do I verify a file with a detached signature? A: Select the original file and the .asc/.sig signature, paste the signer’s public key, then verify. The suite lists each signature and status.\n[...]\nQ: Can I recover my private‑key passphrase if I forget it? A: No. Passphrases are not recoverable. Generate a new key pair and distribute the new public key; revoke the old key if possible.\n[...]\nQ: Is this secure? Do keys leave my device? A: All operations happen client‑side in your browser using OpenPGP.js. Keys and files never leave your device.\n[...]\nQ: How do I confirm I have the right public key? A: Compare the fingerprint over a trusted channel (in‑person, voice, QR, business card) before trusting signatures or encrypting to the key.\n\n---\n\nTitle: PGP Key Generator Online — Generate PGP-style RSA key pairs in the browser | BrowserUtils\nURL: https://www.browserutils.dev/tools/pgp-key-generator\nPublished: 2026-03-16T11:08:25.000Z\nAuthor: BrowserUtils\nHighlights:\nPGP Key Generator is a free online tool from BrowserUtils that generate pgp-style rsa key pairs in the browser. You receive the encrypted text via DM\n-   3\\. Paste it in your Decrypt section\n-   4\\. Only YOU can read it with your private key\n\n**Why this works:** Even if someone intercepts your Reddit DMs, they can't read the encrypted messages without the private key!\n\n**Session Keys:** Your keys only last this browser session. Download them if you want to continue conversations later, or just generate new keys each time for practice.\n\n##### Technical Details\n\n###### Public Key Import:\n\n-   ✓ Recipients share their public keys\n-   ✓ You import and store them locally\n-   ✓ Use their key to encrypt messages TO them\n-   ✓ Safe to store - public keys aren't secret\n\n###### Encryption Workflow:\n\n-   1\\. Choose recipient from list\n-   2\\. Write your message\n-   3\\. Encrypt with THEIR public key\n-   4\\. Only THEY can decrypt with their private key\n\n###### Security Notes:\n\n-   ✓ Verify key authenticity in real use\n-   ✓ Use key fingerprints for verification\n-   ✓ Keys should come from trusted sources\n-   ✓ Demo keys are for learning only\n\nEducational PGP cryptography tool - Everything runs offline for security\n\nSecure & Private",

It's a powerful command-line tool used for:\n\n-   **Encryption:** Protect sensitive information so only intended recipients can read it\n-   **Digital signatures:** Verify the authenticity and integrity of messages and files\n-   **Key management:** Create, import, export, and manage cryptographic keys\n-   **Secure communication:** Enable private communication over insecure channels\n\n### Installing GnuPG\n\n#### macOS\n\n`brew install gnupg`\n\n#### Ubuntu/Debian\n\n`sudo apt install gnupg`\n\n#### Windows\n\n`choco install gnupg`\n\nVerify installation by running `gpg --version` in your terminal.\n\n### What You'll Learn\n\nThis interactive tutorial will teach you essential GnuPG commands through hands-on practice:\n\n-   Generate secure PGP key pairs\n-   Export and share public keys\n-   Encrypt messages for specific recipients\n-   Decrypt received messages\n-   Create and verify digital signatures\n\nEach step includes detailed instructions, example outputs, and real-time validation of your work.\n\n## Interactive PGP/GnuPG Command Line Tutorial\n\n### Generate Your PGP Key Pair\n\nFirst, we need to create a PGP key pair using GnuPG. We'll use the full key generation command for more control over options.\n\n#### Command to run:\n\n`gpg --full-gen-key`\n\n#### Step-by-step guide:\n\n**1\\. Key type selection:**  \nChoose `1` for \"RSA and RSA\" (most compatible)\n\n**2\\. Key size:**  \nEnter `4096` for maximum security (when prompted)\n\n**3\\. GPG will then decrypt and display the original message.\n\n#### Paste the decryption output here:\n\n### Encrypt with Tutorial Key\n\nNow let's test encryption with a key provided by PGPractice. I'll give you a public key, and you encrypt a message with it.\n\n#### PGPractice Public Key:\n\n#### Commands to run:\n\n1\\. Save the PGPractice key above to a file: pgpractice-key.asc\n\n2\\. Import it: gpg --import pgpractice-key.asc\n\n3\\. Create your message: echo \"Your secret message here\" > my-message.txt\n\n4\\. PGP encryption ensures that only the intended recipient can read your message, while digital signatures prove that a message genuinely came from you. Tools like [KeychainPGP](/pgp-online-encrypt/) make this entire process accessible through a clipboard-first workflow — no command line required.\n\nThis PGP tutorial walks you through every step, from generating your first key pair to sending and receiving encrypted messages. Whether you are a journalist protecting a source, a developer signing commits, or someone who simply wants private communication, this guide covers everything you need to know. If you are brand new to the concept of public-key cryptography, consider reading [What is PGP?](/docs/what-is-pgp/) or [PGP for Beginners](/docs/pgp-for-beginners/) first.\n\n---\n\n## What You Need Before You Start\n\nBefore you encrypt your first message with PGP, you need a PGP application. There are several options:\n\n-   **KeychainPGP web app** — Open the [online PGP tool](/pgp-online-encrypt/) in any modern browser. Everything runs locally via WebAssembly compiled from Rust. No installation, no account, no data sent to a server.\n-   **KeychainPGP desktop app** — Download the native app for Windows, macOS, Linux, or Android from the [GitHub releases page](https://github.com/keychainpgp/keychainpgp/releases/latest). The desktop app adds global hotkeys, system tray integration, clipboard auto-clear, OS credential store support, and QR code key sync with mobile.\n-   **KeychainPGP CLI** — The `keychainpgp` CLI provides commands for key generation, encryption, decryption, signing, verification, and keyring management. Ideal for scripting and automation.\n-   **GnuPG (GPG)** — The traditional command-line tool. Powerful but requires terminal familiarity. See [PGP vs GPG](/docs/pgp-vs-gpg/) for a detailed comparison.\n\nYou also need the public key of anyone you want to send encrypted messages to. They need yours as well. This mutual key exchange is the foundation of PGP communication.\n\n> **Tip:** If you just want to try PGP encryption right now without installing anything, open the [online PGP tool](/pgp-online-encrypt/) and follow along with this tutorial.\n\n---\n\n## Step 1: Generate a PGP Key Pair\n\nA PGP key pair consists of two mathematically linked keys:\n\n-   **Public key** — You share this freely. Anyone can use it to encrypt messages to you or verify your signatures.\n-   **Private key** — You keep this secret. It decrypts messages encrypted to your public key and creates digital signatures.\n\n### Generating a key in KeychainPGP\n\n1.  Open KeychainPGP (either the web app or desktop app).\n2.  Navigate to the **Keys** tab.\n3.  Click **Generate New Key**.\n4.  Enter your name and email address. These become part of your key’s identity (called a User ID).\n5.  Click **Generate**. KeychainPGP creates an **Ed25519 + X25519** key pair — the most modern and secure algorithm available in OpenPGP.\n\nThe entire process takes a few seconds. There is no need to choose algorithm types, key sizes, or expiration dates. KeychainPGP uses secure defaults so you do not have to make cryptographic decisions.\n\n### Generating a key with GPG (command line)\n\nIf you prefer the command line, GnuPG works as well:\n\n`gpg --full-generate-key`\n\nSelect the key type (RSA or ECC), key size, and expiration period when prompted. For modern security, choose Ed25519 if your version of GPG supports it.\n\nRegardless of the tool you use, the output is the same: you now have a public key to share and a private key to protect.\n\n---\n\n## Step 2: Share Your Public Key\n\nYour public key is a block of text that looks like this:\n\n`-----BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----  mDMEZx... (base64 encoded data) ... -----END PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----`\n\nThis is called **ASCII-armored** format. You can share it anywhere:\n\n-   **Email** — Paste it in the body of an email or attach it as a `.asc` file.\n-   **Messaging apps** — Send it through Signal, WhatsApp, Matrix, or any chat platform.\n-   **Your website or social media** — Publish it on your personal site, GitHub profile, or Twitter bio.\n-   **Key servers** — Upload it to a public key server like `keys.openpgp.org` so anyone can look it up by your email address.\n\n### Exporting your public key in KeychainPGP\n\n1.  Go to the **Keys** tab.\n2.  Select your key.\n3.  Click **Export Public Key** (or use the copy button).\n4.  The ASCII-armored public key block is copied to your clipboard, ready to paste anywhere.\n\n**Important:** Only share your **public** key. Never share your private key with anyone, under any circumstances. Your private key is what keeps your messages secure.\n\n---\n\n## Step 3: Import Your Contacts’ Public Keys\n\nBefore you can send someone an encrypted message, you need their public key. Ask your contact to send you their ASCII-armored public key, or look it up on a key server.\n\n### Importing a key in KeychainPGP\n\n1.  Copy the contact’s public key block to your clipboard (the entire text from `-----BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----` to `-----END PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----`).\n2.  Open KeychainPGP and go to the **Keys** tab.\n3.  Click **Import Key** and paste the key, or KeychainPGP may detect it from your clipboard automatically.\n4.  The contact appears in your key list with their name and email.\n\n### Importing a key with GPG\n\n`gpg --import contact-public-key.asc`\n\nOnce imported, you can encrypt messages to that contact. You should verify the key’s fingerprint through a separate, trusted channel (such as a phone call or in-person meeting) to confirm it genuinely belongs to the person you think it does.\n\n---\n\n## Step 4: Encrypt a Message\n\nWith your key pair generated and your contact’s public key imported, you are ready to encrypt. PGP encryption ensures that only the holder of the corresponding private key can read the message.\n\n### Encrypting in KeychainPGP (web or desktop)\n\n1.  Go to the **Encrypt** tab.\n2.  Type or paste the message you want to encrypt.\n3.  Select the recipient from your key list. You can select multiple recipients if the message is for more than one person.\n4.  Click **Encrypt to Clipboard**. The encrypted message (an ASCII-armored PGP message block) is copied to your clipboard.\n5.  Paste the encrypted message into your email, chat, or any other communication channel.\n\nThe encrypted output looks like this:\n\n`-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----  hF4D... (encrypted data) ... -----END PGP MESSAGE-----`\n\nOnly the selected recipient(s) can decrypt this text. Even if someone intercepts it, the message is unreadable without the recipient’s private key.\n\n### Encrypting with the desktop hotkey\n\nIf you are using the KeychainPGP desktop app, the process is even faster thanks to the [clipboard-first workflow](/docs/clipboard-encryption/):\n\n1.  Type your message in any application (email client, chat window, text editor).\n2.  Select and copy the text (`Ctrl+C`).\n3.  Press **`Ctrl+Shift+E`** — KeychainPGP encrypts the clipboard contents to your default recipient.\n4.  Paste the encrypted message (`Ctrl+V`).\n\nThis works from any application on your system, with no need to switch windows. The entire encrypt-and-replace cycle takes about one second.\n\n---\n\n## Step 5: Decrypt a Message\n\nWhen someone sends you a PGP-encrypted message, you need your private key to decrypt it.\n\n### Decrypting in KeychainPGP (web or desktop)\n\n1.  Copy the entire encrypted message block (from `-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----` to `-----END PGP MESSAGE-----`).\n2.  Go to the **Decrypt** tab in KeychainPGP.\n3.  Paste the encrypted text.\n4.  Click **Decrypt**. If the message was encrypted to your public key, the decrypted plaintext appears immediately.\n\n### Decrypting with the desktop hotkey\n\n1.  Select and copy the encrypted message block from any application (`Ctrl+C`).\n2.  Press **`Ctrl+Shift+D`** — KeychainPGP decrypts the clipboard contents using your private key.\n3.  The decrypted plaintext replaces the encrypted text on your clipboard. Paste it wherever you need.\n\nThe desktop app also features **clipboard auto-clear**: after 30 seconds, the decrypted text is automatically wiped from your clipboard to prevent accidental exposure.\n\n### Decrypting with GPG\n\n`gpg --decrypt message.asc`\n\nGPG prompts for your passphrase (if your key is passphrase-protected) and outputs the decrypted text.\n\n---\n\n## Step 6: Sign a Message\n\nA PGP digital signature proves two things:\n\n1.  **Authenticity** — The message was written by the holder of the signing key.\n2.  **Integrity** — The message has not been altered since it was signed.\n\nSigning does not encrypt the message. Anyone can read a signed message, but they can verify it genuinely came from you and was not tampered with.\n\n### Signing in KeychainPGP\n\n1.  Go to the **Sign** tab (or use the sign option in the Encrypt tab).\n2.  Enter or paste the message you want to sign.\n3.  Select your key.\n4.  Click **Sign**. The signed output includes your original message wrapped in a PGP signature block.\n\nA cleartext signature looks like this:\n\n`-----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE----- Hash: SHA512  Your original message here. -----BEGIN PGP SIGNATURE-----  iHUE... (signature data) ... -----END PGP SIGNATURE-----`\n\n### When to sign messages\n\n-   Announcing software releases or security advisories\n-   Verifying your identity on public forums\n-   Signing emails to prove they came from you\n-   Git commit signing for code integrity\n\n---\n\n## Step 7: Verify a Signature\n\nWhen you receive a signed message, you can verify that it is authentic and unmodified.\n\n### Verifying in KeychainPGP\n\n1.  Copy the entire signed message (including the signature block).\n2.  Go to the **Verify** tab.\n3.  Paste the signed message.\n4.  Click **Verify**. If you have the sender’s public key in your keyring, KeychainPGP confirms whether the signature is valid.\n\nA successful verification tells you:\n\n-   The message was signed by the key with a specific fingerprint.\n-   The message has not been modified since it was signed.\n-   The signing key belongs to the name and email associated with it.\n\n### Verifying with GPG\n\n`gpg --verify signed-message.asc`\n\nGPG outputs the signing key’s fingerprint and whether the signature is valid.\n\n---\n\n## KeychainPGP Clipboard Workflow\n\nWhat sets KeychainPGP apart from traditional PGP tools is its [clipboard-based encryption workflow](/docs/clipboard-encryption/). Instead of operating on files or requiring a dedicated email client, KeychainPGP works with any application through the system clipboard.\n\nHere is the full workflow summary:\n\nAction\n\nWeb App\n\nDesktop App\n\nEncrypt\n\nPaste text, click Encrypt to Clipboard\n\nCopy text, press `Ctrl+Shift+E`\n\nDecrypt\n\nPaste ciphertext, click Decrypt\n\nCopy ciphertext, press `Ctrl+Shift+D`\n\nSign\n\nPaste text, click Sign\n\nAvailable in app window\n\nVerify\n\nPaste signed text, click Verify\n\nAvailable in app window\n\nThe desktop app’s global hotkeys (`Ctrl+Shift+E` for encrypt, `Ctrl+Shift+D` for decrypt) work from any application — email clients, web browsers, chat apps, text editors, or anything else that handles text. This eliminates the need to copy text back and forth between your PGP tool and your communication app.\n\nAdditional security features in the desktop app include:\n\n-   **Clipboard auto-clear** — Decrypted text is wiped from the clipboard after 30 seconds.\n-   **OPSEC mode** — Disguised window title, RAM-only key storage, and a panic wipe button.\n-   **OS credential store** — Private keys are stored securely using Windows Credential Manager, macOS Keychain, or Linux Secret Service.\n\n---\n\n## Web App vs Desktop App\n\nKeychainPGP is available in two forms. Both implement the same OpenPGP standard using the Sequoia-PGP library (written in Rust), so encrypted messages are fully interoperable.\n\n**Web app** ([online PGP tool](/pgp-online-encrypt/)):\n\n-   Runs entirely in the browser via WebAssembly\n-   No installation, no account required\n-   Perfect for quick encryption tasks or when you are on a shared or unfamiliar computer\n-   Keys are stored in browser local storage (cleared when you clear site data)\n\n**Desktop app** (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android):\n\n-   Global hotkeys for encrypt (`Ctrl+Shift+E`) and decrypt (`Ctrl+Shift+D`)\n-   System tray integration — always running in the background\n-   Clipboard auto-clear for decrypted text\n-   OPSEC mode with RAM-only keys and panic wipe\n-   Secure key storage via OS credential manager\n-   Better suited for daily use and long-term key management\n\nBoth apps are free, open source (MIT / Apache-2.0), and have zero telemetry or tracking. Download the desktop app from the [GitHub releases page](https://github.com/keychainpgp/keychainpgp/releases/latest).\n\n---\n\n## Frequently Asked Questions\n\n### Do I need to share my private key?\n\nNo. You must **never** share your private key with anyone. Your private key is what allows you to decrypt messages sent to you and create digital signatures. If someone else obtains your private key, they can read all messages encrypted to you and impersonate you. Only share your **public** key. If you suspect your private key has been compromised, generate a new key pair immediately and notify your contacts.\n\n### What happens if I lose my private key?\n\nIf you lose your private key, you permanently lose the ability to decrypt any messages that were encrypted to the corresponding public key. There is no recovery mechanism — this is by design, as it ensures that no backdoor exists. Always keep a secure backup of your private key, such as an encrypted USB drive stored in a safe location. KeychainPGP’s desktop app stores keys in your OS credential manager, but you should still maintain an independent backup.\n\n### Can I encrypt files with PGP?\n\nYes. PGP can encrypt both text messages and binary files. While KeychainPGP focuses on text-based clipboard encryption, tools like GnuPG support file encryption directly:\n\n`gpg --encrypt --recipient [[email protected]](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection) document.pdf`\n\nThis produces an encrypted file (`document.pdf.gpg`) that only the recipient can decrypt. File encryption is useful for protecting sensitive documents, archives, and backups. For a detailed comparison of PGP tools and their file encryption capabilities, see the [PGP vs GPG](/docs/pgp-vs-gpg/) guide.\n\n### How secure is PGP encryption?\n\nPGP encryption with modern algorithms is extremely secure. KeychainPGP uses Ed25519 for signatures and X25519 for key agreement — both are elliptic curve algorithms that provide security equivalent to RSA-3072 at a fraction of the key size. The underlying mathematics have not been broken, and no practical attack against properly implemented PGP is known. The main risks are not cryptographic but operational: weak passphrases, compromised devices, or careless key management. Using a dedicated tool like KeychainPGP with clipboard auto-clear and OPSEC mode mitigates many of these risks.\n\n### What is a PGP fingerprint?\n\nA PGP fingerprint is a 40-character hexadecimal string that uniquely identifies a PGP key. It looks like this:\n\n`E4B7 C614 3A5F 7D28 9B06  6A2F 8E1D 4C95 A703 B8F2`\n\nFingerprints are used to verify that a public key genuinely belongs to the person you think it does. When you receive someone’s public key, compare its fingerprint with the fingerprint the owner provides through a separate trusted channel (phone call, in-person meeting, or verified social media profile). This prevents **man-in-the-middle attacks** where an attacker substitutes their own public key for the real one. In KeychainPGP, you can view a key’s fingerprint in the Keys tab by selecting the key and checking its details.\n\n### Try PGP Encryption Now\n\nEncrypt and decrypt messages directly in your browser. No installation required.\n\n[Open Online PGP Tool](https://keychainpgp.github.io) [Download KeychainPGP](/pgp-online-encrypt/)\n\n#### On this page\n\n-   [How to Use PGP](#how-to-use-pgp-overview)\n-   [What You Need Before You Start](#what-you-need-before-you-start)\n-   [Step 1: Generate a Key Pair](#step-1-generate-a-pgp-key-pair)\n-   [Step 2: Share Your Public Key](#step-2-share-your-public-key)\n-   [Step 3: Import Contacts' Keys](#step-3-import-your-contacts-public-keys)\n-   [Step 4: Encrypt a Message](#step-4-encrypt-a-message)\n-   [Step 5: Decrypt a Message](#step-5-decrypt-a-message)\n-   [Step 6: Sign a Message](#step-6-sign-a-message)\n-   [Step 7: Verify a Signature](#step-7-verify-a-signature)\n-   [KeychainPGP Clipboard Workflow](#keychainpgp-clipboard-workflow)\n-   [Web App vs Desktop App](#web-app-vs-desktop-app)\n-   [FAQ](#frequently-asked-questions)\n\n![KeychainPGP](/icons/icon-64.png) KeychainPGP\n\n[Online PGP Tool](/pgp-online-encrypt/) [What is PGP?](/docs/what-is-pgp/) [How to Use PGP](/docs/how-to-use-pgp/) [PGP vs GPG](/docs/pgp-vs-gpg/) [PGP for Beginners](/docs/pgp-for-beginners/) [Best PGP Tools](/blog/best-pgp-tools-2026/)\n\n[Source Code](https://github.com/keychainpgp/keychainpgp) [Online Tool](https://keychainpgp.github.io) · MIT / Apache-2.0\n\nNo telemetry.             "output": "  Online/Offline PGP Tools \n\n# Online/Offline PGP Tools\n\nA simple offline-capable toolkit for generating OpenPGP keys, encrypting and decrypting messages, signing text and files, and verifying signatures.\n\nThese tools run locally in your browser using `openpgp.min.js`. Nothing is uploaded to a server. For sensitive use, open this site in a private/incognito browser window and disconnect from the internet. Or download the [full folder](pgptools.com.zip) and run it locally offline on your device.\n\n### Start Here: Generate Key Pair\n\nCreate your own private key and public key.\n\n[Open Tool](generate-key.html) [How to use](#tool-generate)\n\n### Encrypt Message\n\nUse the recipient's public key to encrypt a message.\n\n[Open Tool](encrypt.html) [How to use](#tool-encrypt)\n\n### Decrypt Message\n\nUse your private key and password to read encrypted messages.\n\n[Open Tool](decrypt.html) [How to use](#tool-decrypt)\n\n### Sign Text\n\nUse your private key to prove a message came from you.\n\n[Open Tool](sign.html) [How to use](#tool-sign)\n\n### Verify Signed Text\n\nUse the sender's public key to verify authenticity.\n\n[Open Tool](verify.html) [How to use](#tool-verify)\n\n### Sign File\n\nCreate a detached `.asc` signature for any file.\n\n[Open Tool](sign-file.html) [How to use](#tool-sign-file)\n\n### Verify File Signature\n\nCheck that a downloaded file was signed and not modified.\n\n[Open Tool](verify-file.html) [How to use](#tool-verify-file)\n\n[How to use OpenPGP](#howto) [PGP basics](#basics) [Why file signatures matter](#file-signatures) [Tool guide](#tool-details) [Security notes](#security)\n\n## What is PGP?\n\nPGP is a system for protecting messages and files. It can be used for two main purposes:\n\n-   **Encryption:** keeping data private so only the intended recipient can read it.\n-   **Signing:** proving that a message or file came from a particular key and was not changed.\n\n### Public key and private key\n\nOpenPGP uses a key pair:\n\n-   **Public key:** safe to share. Other people use it to encrypt messages to you or verify your signatures.\n-   **Private key:** keep secret. You use it to decrypt messages and create signatures.\n\nYour private key should be protected with a strong password.\n\n### PGP, OpenPGP, GPG, and OpenPGP.js\n\n-   **PGP** means Pretty Good Privacy, the original software and idea.\n-   **OpenPGP** is the open standard for compatible PGP-style encryption.\n-   **GPG / GnuPG** is a popular free command-line program implementing OpenPGP.\n-   **OpenPGP.js** is the JavaScript OpenPGP library used by these browser tools.\n\nSimple summary: **PGP is the idea, OpenPGP is the standard, GPG is a program.**\n\n## How to use OpenPGP step by step\n\n### 1\\. Create a key pair\n\nUse **Generate Key Pair** to create your private key and public key. Keep the private key secret. Share the public key with people who need to encrypt messages to you or verify your signatures.\n\n### 2\\. Encrypt a message\n\nTo send a private message to someone, use their public key. Only their private key can decrypt it.\n\n### 3\\. Decrypt a message\n\nWhen someone sends you an encrypted message, use your private key and password to decrypt it.\n\n### 4\\. Sign text\n\nSigning proves that a message was created by the holder of the private key. It does not hide the message.\n\n### 5\\. Verify signed text\n\nUse the sender's public key to check whether the signed message is valid and unchanged.\n\n### 6\\. Sign a file\n\nA file signature creates a separate `.asc` file. The original file is not changed.\n\n### 7\\. Verify downloaded files\n\nFor software downloads, especially security-sensitive software, verify the file using the developer's public key and the matching signature file before running it.\n\n## Why file signatures matter\n\nFile signatures are important for security-sensitive downloads such as crypto wallets, password managers, encryption tools, operating system images, and software installers.\n\nA valid OpenPGP signature can help confirm that:\n\n-   the file was signed by the expected developer or project key;\n-   the file was not modified after signing;\n-   the downloaded executable matches the file approved by the signer.\n\nA valid signature proves that the file matches the signing key. It does not automatically prove that the key itself is trustworthy. Always obtain public keys from official project sources and compare fingerprints when available.\n\n## Tool guide\n\n### Generate Key Pair\n\n**Purpose:** create a new password-protected private key and matching public key.\n\n-   **Inputs:** name, email, optional comment, password, key type, expiration.\n-   **Outputs:** private key and public key.\n-   **Use when:** you do not yet have an OpenPGP key pair.\n\n[Open Generate Key Pair](generate-key.html)\n\n### Encrypt Message\n\n**Purpose:** encrypt text so only the recipient can read it.\n\n-   **Inputs:** recipient's public key and plaintext message.\n-   **Output:** armored encrypted PGP message.\n-   **Important:** encrypt with the recipient's public key, not your own, unless you are encrypting to yourself.\n\n[Open Encrypt Message](encrypt.html)\n\n### Decrypt Message\n\n**Purpose:** decrypt a message that was encrypted to your public key.\n\n-   **Inputs:** your private key, private key password, encrypted message.\n-   **Output:** decrypted plaintext.\n-   **Important:** your private key is sensitive. Use offline when possible.\n\n[Open Decrypt Message](decrypt.html)\n\n### Sign Text\n\n**Purpose:** create a clear-signed text message that others can verify.\n\n-   **Inputs:** your private key, password, plaintext message.\n-   **Output:** signed PGP message.\n-   **Use when:** you want to prove that a text message came from your key.\n\n[Open Sign Text](sign.html)\n\n### Verify Signed Text\n\n**Purpose:** verify that a signed text message is authentic and unchanged.\n\n-   **Inputs:** sender's public key and signed message.\n-   **Output:** valid or invalid result, plus signed text.\n-   **Use when:** someone sends you a PGP signed message.\n\n[Open Verify Signed Text](verify.html)\n\n### Sign File\n\n**Purpose:** create a detached armored signature for a local file.\n\n-   **Inputs:** your private key, password, file to sign.\n-   **Output:** `.asc` signature file.\n-   **Use when:** you want others to verify that a file came from you and was not modified.\n\n[Open Sign File](sign-file.html)\n\n### Verify File Signature\n\n**Purpose:** verify a detached signature for a downloaded file.\n\n-   **Inputs:** developer's public key, downloaded file, matching `.asc` or `.sig` signature.\n-   **Output:** success or failure, signer key ID, and key user IDs when available.\n-   **Use when:** verifying executables, wallets, installers, archives, or documents.\n\n[Open Verify File Signature](verify-file.html)\n\n## Security notes\n\n-   These pages do not upload data, but private keys still exist in browser memory while in use.\n-   For sensitive operations, use the tools offline and close the browser afterward.\n-   Do not load `openpgp.min.js` from a CDN for sensitive use. Keep it local in the `JS` folder.\n-   Use strong private-key passwords.\n-   Keep private keys secret. Share only public keys.\n-   For file verification, obtain public keys from official sources and compare fingerprints if available.\n-   Browser tools are convenient. Dedicated GPG software may be preferable for long-term key management.\n\n[Back to tools](#tools)",

It adds an extra layer of security if your key is ever stolen. Choose something memorable — if you forget it, there is no way to recover it or decrypt messages sent to you.\n\n [↺ Click here to regenerate another pair of keys](/)\n\n \n\nPublic Key\n\n[💾 Download public key (.ASC)](#download_pub_key \"This button will be enabled after you generate your key pair.\") [Learn More](#) **Public Key**Share this key publicly — put it on your website or in your email signature. You need it to decrypt messages sent to you using your public key. Never share it with anyone — save it securely on your device.\n\nYour Private Key\n\n  \n\n🔐 \n\nYour Message in Plain Text\n\n✏️ Sign the message  \n  \n\nSigned Message\n\n  \n[💾 Download signed message](javascript:void\\(0\\); \"Download signed text.\")\n\nSigner's Public Key\n\nPGP-Signed Message\n\n✔️ Verify signature  \n  \n\nRaw Message and Status\n\n  \n[Download as binary](javascript:void\\(0\\);) [💾 Download message](javascript:void\\(0\\);)\n\nReceiver's Public Key\n\nSigner's Private Key (for signing — optional)\n\n  \n\n🔐 \n\nYour Message in Plain Text\n\n🔒 Encrypt the message  \n  \n\nEncrypted PGP Message\n\n  \n[💾 Download encrypted message](javascript:void\\(0\\);)\n\nReceiver's Private Key (for decryption)\n\n  \n\n🔐 \n\nSigner's Public Key (optional — for verification)\n\nEncrypted PGP Message\n\n🔓 Decrypt the message  \n  \n\nDecrypted Message in Plain Text\n\n  \n[Download as binary](javascript:void\\(0\\);) [💾 Download decrypted text](javascript:void\\(0\\);)\n\nIs it safe for me to generate my PGP keys through your website?\n\nYes, it is as safe as generating your keys using a local application. The key generation on this website is done **client-side only**. This means the key pairs are generated entirely in your web browser and they never leave your computer. This website never sees any key-related data or the key itself.\n\nCan you tell me more about the keys that the program generates?\n\nSure. For starters, it enforces using a passphrase with each key generated. This ensures some level of protection if your key is ever stolen. It also automatically generates two subkeys — one for signing and one for encryption. The bit length of the generated subkeys will be identical to the length you specified for the primary key. The primary key never expires. You can, however, set the expiration date on the generated subkeys using the 'Expire' option in the key generation form.\n\nWhat is Elliptic Curve Cryptography?\n\nElliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) is an approach to public-key cryptography based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. One of the main benefits compared to non-ECC cryptography is the same level of security provided by keys of smaller size. For example, a 256-bit ECC public key provides comparable security to a 3072-bit RSA public key. ECC is still not widely supported in all PGP client applications, so use ECC keys only if you know your recipients' software supports it. Read more at [RFC 6637](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6637).\n\nWhy does my web browser or computer slow down when generating keys?\n\nPGP key generation is a resource-intensive process. As a result, you may experience increased CPU and memory usage on your device, which can result in performance issues. The performance impact depends on the hardware capabilities of your device and the selected key size.\n\n> Pretty Good Privacy (PGP) is a data encryption and decryption computer program that provides cryptographic privacy and authentication for data communication. PGP is often used for signing, encrypting, and decrypting texts, e-mails, files, directories, and whole disk partitions and to increase the security of e-mail communications. It was created by Phil Zimmermann in 1991. PGP and similar software follow the OpenPGP standard (RFC 4880) for encrypting and decrypting data. *Source: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pretty_Good_Privacy)*\n\nThis site only provides a simple and easy-to-use tool for people to generate PGP keys with. None of this would be possible without the awesome Open Source software utilized here. This tool uses [Keybase's](https://keybase.io/) JavaScript implementation of PGP — [kbpgp](https://github.com/keybase/kbpgp). For file saving capabilities it uses [Eli Grey's](https://github.com/eligrey) [FileSaver.js](https://github.com/eligrey/FileSaver.js/).\n\nThis site is Open Source and the source code is available on [GitHub](https://github.com/heiswayi/pgp) under the MIT license. Signatures on components — OpenPGP for application developers\nURL: https://openpgp.dev/book/signing_components.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nThird-party signatures are pivotal in OpenPGP for decentralized authentication, forming the basis of the Web of Trust. They encode authentication-related statements about certificates and linked identities, establishing trustworthiness of identity claims.\n[...]\nrevoking, and thus invalidating, prior third-party signature statements\n[...]\nTo safeguard against unauthorized additions or alterations of components, OpenPGP uses cryptographic signatures. These validate that all components, such as subkeys or identity components, were linked to the OpenPGP certificate by its owner, using the primary key. While anyone can still store unrelated elements to a certificate dataset, OpenPGP implementations will reject them if they lack a valid cryptographic connection with\n[...]\nSubkeys are linked to OpenPGP certificates via a subkey binding signature(type ID`0x18`). This signature type indicates the association of the primary key with the subkey.\n[...]\nA subkey binding signature binds a subkey to a primary key, and it embeds metadata into the signature packet. Once generated, the subkey binding signature packet is stored in the certificate directly after the subkey it binds.\n[...]\n### 8.2.5. Revocation self-signatures: Invalidating certificate components¶\n[...]\nRevocation self-signatures represent an important class of self-signatures, used primarily to invalidate components or retract prior signature statements.\n[...]\nA key revocation signature(type ID`0x20`) marks a primary key as revoked.\n[...]\nA subkey revocation signature(type ID`0x28`) invalidates the binding of a subkey.\n[...]\nrevocation(type ID`0x30`) invalidates the binding of a User ID or User Attribute.\n[...]\nRevocation signatures often include a Reason for Revocation subpacket, with a code specifying why the revocation was issued. This code determines\n[...]\nthe revocation is considered soft or hard.\n[...]\nThird-party signatures in OpenPGP primarily encode authentication statements for identities and delegate trust decisions. These signatures can be manually inspected or processed as machine-readable artifacts by OpenPGP software, which evaluates the authenticity of certificates based on user-specified trust anchors.\n[...]\nOpenPGP uses trust signature subpackets to delegate authentication decisions, designating the recipient certificate as a “ trusted introducer” (or a trust anchor) for the user. This includes specifying trust depth(or level) for transitive delegations and quantifying trust with numerical values, indicating the extent of reliance on the introducer’s certifications.\n[...]\nTrust signature subpackets are\n[...]\nin third-party signatures, more specifically\n[...]\nThe “ trust depth” (or level) in OpenPGP signifies the extent of transitive delegation within the authentication process. It determines how far a delegation can be extended from the original trusted introducer to subsequent intermediaries. Essentially, a certificate with a trust depth of more than one acts as a “ meta introducer,” facilitating authentication decisions across multiple levels in\n[...]\nThe Web of Trust in OpenPGP is a trust model that facilitates authentication decisions through a network of certifications and delegations. It is characterized by a so-called strong set, which refers to a group of certificates that are robustly interconnected via third-party certifications.\n[...]\nIn this model, users independently delegate authentication decisions, choosing whose certification to rely on. This delegation is based on the certificates and third-party signatures available to them, with their OpenPGP software applying the Web of Trust mechanism to discern the reliability of each certificate for an identity.\n[...]\nTo reverse a previously issued third-party signature, the issuer can generate a certification revocation signature(type ID`0x30`). The revocation must be issued by the same key that created the original signature or, in deprecated practice, by a designated Revocation Key.\n\n---\n\nTitle: Keys, subkeys, and certificates\nURL: https://book.sequoia-pgp.org/bckgrnd_keys_certificates.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\n## Keys, subkeys, and certificates\n[...]\nA key in the context of OpenPGP is actually a dataset containing User IDs, key material (both public and secret), metadata like the expiration date, preferences, certifications, signatures, and subkeys – to name a few. This dataset is not fixed; parts of it will (likely) be modified over time as new certifications might be added, User IDs might change, and the expiration might be extended.\n[...]\nA certificate is the public representation of a key. It contains the same objects as the key except for the secret key material. It is used for operations everyone should be able to do, like encryption or signature verification.\n[...]\nKeys (and certificates) can and usually have subkeys. These are keys that are bound to another key (called the primary key) by a subkey binding signature.\n[...]\nSubkeys were added to OpenPGP to solve a few shortcomings:\n[...]\n- By careful arrangement, the primary key of this bundle can be kept offline, as its secret key material is then only needed for updating metadata (User IDs, expiration) and subkey bindings. Day-to-day usage can then be completely delegated to subkeys.\n[...]\n- Subkeys have flags which indicate their usage (signing, encryption, etc.). OpenPGP allows having more than one subkey with the same flags. Having two subkeys flagged for encryption in a certificate will encrypt a message for both subkeys.\n[...]\n- Subkeys are grouped under a primary key, which represents the bundle. Hence, designating this bundle is done via the fingerprint of the primary key. This allows rotation or revocation of subkeys without affecting existing certifications, as the primary key doesn’t change. The subkey binding signature is used to extend the certification of the primary key to its subkeys.\n[...]\n- Subkeys introduce new key material to a key to prevent the use of the same key material for potentially conflicting operations. Both decrypting and signing are technically an encryption using the private key material. Signing an encrypted message might therefore inadvertently decrypt the message. OpenPGP prevents this as signatures are never created using the original data, but a checksum of it. Still, this is uncomfortably close and open to implementation mistakes. Using different keypairs for encryption/decryption and signing/verifying eliminates this problem.\n[...]\nSubkeys do not have User IDs, as these are associated with the primary key, but they can have an expiration date.\n[...]\nThere is no limit to the number of subkeys a key can have. When generating a new key,`sq` creates three subkeys with it. One for encryption, one for signing, and one for authentication. The primary key is used to certify the authenticity of the subkeys. As subkeys have markings (flags) for their use case, an application (like`sq`) can choose the right subkey for each operation. In some ways, a key acts like a little CA, with the primary key acting as a certificate authority, certifying the subkeys.\n[...]\nYou can peek into the internals of a key using:\n[...]\n```\n$ sq key generate --own-key --userid alice --output - --rev-cert /dev/null --overwrite | sq packet dump\n\n```\n[...]\nThis example generates a key with a User ID of “alice”, it specifies`--output`, so that the generated key is not added to the key store. Using`--output -` redirects the new key to`STDOUT`, and piping the output into`sq packet dump` displays its expanded contents.`--rev-cert` and`--overwrite` are needed as a side effect of`--output`- in this example the result is discarded.`--own-key` authorizes the new key to introduce certificates - you can try`--shared-key` instead to see an unauthorized key.\n\n---\n\nTitle: OpenPGP.js | OpenPGP JavaScript Implementation\nURL: https://openpgpjs.org/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nOpenPGP.js | OpenPGP JavaScript Implementation\n\n## About\n\nThis project aims to provide an Open Source OpenPGP library in JavaScript so it can be used on virtually every device. Instead of other implementations that are aimed at using native code, OpenPGP.js is meant to bypass this requirement (i.e. people will not have to install gpg on their machines in order to use the library). The idea is to implement all the needed OpenPGP functionality in a JavaScript library that can be reused in other projects that provide browser extensions or server applications. It should allow you to sign, encrypt, decrypt, and verify any kind of text - in particular e-mails - as well as managing keys.\n\nThis project is maintained by Proton Mail.\n\nContacts:\n\n- To report security issues: [email protected](Please download our PGP key, either manually or using WKD)\n- For general inquiries: [email protected](PGP key)\n\n## Download\n\n## Resources\n\n- Source code\n- Getting started\n- API documentation\n- GitHub issues\n- Wiki\n- Twitter\n\n## Gold Members / Supporters\n\nProton Mail GlobaLeaks.org Mailvelope\n\n## Who else is using OpenPGP.js\n\nFlowCrypt Mymail-Crypt UDC Encrypt.to PGP Anywhere passbolt\n\n## Architecture\n\n---\n\nTitle: 18. Advanced material: Certificates — OpenPGP for application developers\nURL: https://openpgp.dev/book/adv/certificates.html\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nSince OpenPGP certificates act as “append only” data structures, existing components or signatures cannot simply be “removed.” Instead, they can be marked as invalid by issuing revocation signatures. These additional revocation signatures are added to the certificate.\n[...]\nEach component, such as User ID and a subkey, can be revoked without affecting the rest of the certificate.\n[...]\nRevoking the primary key with a Key revocation signature(type ID`0x20`) also marks the entire certificate, including all of its components, as invalid and unusable.\n[...]\nA revocation indicates that the component should not be used. Revocation signatures over components use a Reason for Revocation subpacket to specify further details about the reason why the component or certification was revoked. The OpenPGP format specifies a set of distinct values for Reasons for Revocation, and additionally provides space for a human-readable free text field for comments about the revocation.\n[...]\n), shadowed self-signatures, and\n[...]\n## 18.5. Fingerprints and beyond: “Naming” certificates in user-facing contexts¶\n[...]\nCertificates in OpenPGP have traditionally often been “named” using hexadecimal strings of varying length.\n[...]\nWith OpenPGP version 4 certificates, it was customary that user-facing software used 20 byte (160 bit) fingerprints as an identifier for the certificate. Or alternatively, the 8 byte (64 bit) Key ID variant of the fingerprint. Both were represented in hexadecimal format, sometimes with whitespace to group the identifier into blocks for easier readability.\n[...]\nThe OpenPGP version 6 standard uses 32 byte (256 bit) fingerprints, but explicitly defines no format for displaying those fingerprints in a human-readable form. The standard recommends strongly against using version 6 fingerprints as identifiers in user-facing workflows.\n[...]\nInstead, “mechanical fingerprint transfer and comparison” should be preferred, wherever possible. The reasoning is that humans tend to be bad at comparing high-entropy data[7] (in addition, many users are probably put off by being asked to compare long hexadecimal strings).\n[...]\nHowever, both Fingerprints and Key IDs may (and usually must) be used, programmatically, by software that handles OpenPGP data, to address specific certificates. This is equally true for OpenPGP version 6.\n[...]\nA certificate holder usually\n[...]\nthat everyone else\n[...]\nfor their certificate. This way, a third\n[...]\n. Setting an\n[...]\nof time, limits\n[...]\nwill at most use a revoked certificate until its expiration time, even if they never learn of the revocation\n[...]\nThese certifications form the back-bone of the OpenPGP trust-model called the Web of Trust. The name stems from the fact that the collection of certifications forms a unidirectional graph resembling a web. Each edge of the graph connects the signing certificate to the identity component associated with another certificate.\n[...]\nOpenPGP software can inspect that graph. Based on the certification data in the graph and a set of trust anchors, it can infer whether a target certificate is legitimate.\n[...]\nThe keys.openpgp.org service is a “verifying” keyserver: the keyserver software only publishes identity components (which include email addresses) after sending a verification email to that address, and receiving opt-in consent by the user of the email address. This service makes a different tradeoff: it is centralized, and relying on it to correctly perform the verification step requires trust in the operator. The tradeoff allows the service to only list identity information with the consent of the owner of that identity, and to prevent “enumeration” of the certificates and identities it stores (that is: third parties cannot obtain a list of email addresses in the service’s database). By design, this service allows easy publication of revocations without requiring publication of any identity components.\n\n---\n\nTitle: JSDoc: Home\nURL: https://docs.openpgpjs.org/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nof the OpenPG\n[...]\nimplements RFC 9580(superseding RFC 4880 and RFC 4\n[...]\n80bis).\n[...]\n- Node.js\n- Deno (experimental)\n- Browser (webpack)\n- Browser (plain files)\n[...]\n- Encrypt and decrypt Uint8Array data with a password\n- Encrypt and decrypt String data with PGP keys\n- Encrypt symmetrically with compression\n- Streaming encrypt Uint8Array data with a password\n- Streaming encrypt and decrypt String data with PGP keys\n- Generate new key pair\n- Revoke a key\n- Sign and verify cleartext messages\n- Create and verify detached signatures\n- Streaming sign and verify Uint8Array data\n[...]\n`(or`.mjs`)\n[...]\n- In browsers: the latest versions of Chrome, Firefox, Edge and Safari support Streams, including`TransformStream` s. OpenPGP's revocation mechanisms are imperfect, not fully understood, and not as widely implemented as they could be. Additionally, some historical OpenPGP revocation mechanisms simply do not work in certain\n[...]\n. This document provides clarifying guidance on how OpenPGP revocation works, documents outstanding problems, and introduces a new mechanism for delegated revocations that improves on previous mechanism.¶\n[...]\nThere are three kinds of signatures that do revocation: Key Revocation (0x20), Subkey Revocation (0x28), and Certification Revocation (0x30).¶\n[...]\nThe Revocation Key Subpacket is deprecated because it suffers from several significant challenges in use. The Delegated Revoker mechanism (described in Section 8) is intended to avoid all of these problems.¶\n[...]\nThe \"Revocation Key\" subpacket contains only a fingerprint. If a verifier observes such a packet, and a Key Revocation Signature that claims to be issued by the identified key, how does the verifier obtain the revoking key itself if they do not already have a copy of it?¶\n[...]\nIf it's possible to find a certificate containing the matching fingerprint in a deprecated \"Revocation Key\" subpacket, then an observer can learn who the keyholder trusts even when no revocation is needed.¶\n[...]\nAn attacker that wants to target Alice, for example, can observe that Alice has indicated Bob seems trustworthy if Alice has pointed to Bob's key's fingerprint with a deprecated \"Revocation Key\" subpacket. The attacker might then go after Bob as a way\n[...]\nget to Alice.¶\n[...]\nThis document introduces a new mechanism for permitting a distinct key to revoke an OpenPGP certificate. It should effectively replace the deprecated \"Revocation Key subpacket\", and should resolve the concerns described in {#revocation-key-subpacket-challenges}.¶\n[...]\nIt uses a novel signature type and a novel subpacket type, is self-contained, irrevocable, and can only be used to revoke the entire OpenPGP certificate rooted in the primary key it corresponds to.¶\n[...]\nA Delegated Revoker Signature MUST be made over the primary key of a given certificate, and its hashed subpackets area MUST contain exactly one each of the following four subpackets:¶\n[...]\n- Delegated Revoker (defined in Section 8.2 of this document)¶\n- Revocable (see Section 5.2.3.20 of [I-D.ietf-openpgp-crypto-refresh])¶\n- Issuer Fingerprint (see Section 5.2.3.35 of [I-D.ietf-openpgp-crypto-refresh])¶\n- Signature Creation Time (see Section 5.2.3.11 of [I-D.ietf-openpgp-crypto-refresh])¶\n[...]\nThe Issuer Fingerprint subpacket contains the fingerprint of the primary key that is asserting this delegation. The Revocable subpacket flag MUST be set to 0. All subpackets MUST be marked as Critical.¶\n[...]\nThe Delegated Revoker Signature packet can be placed in an OpenPGP certificate immediately after the Primary Key packet, before any Key Revocation Signature or Direct Key Signature packet.¶\n[...]\nIt is only valid in the hashed subpackets section of a Delegated Revoker Signature (see Section 8.1) over a primary key. It MUST be marked as Critical.¶\n[...]\nUnlike other OpenPGP Signature packets, a Delegated Revoker Signature cannot be revoked, and is not superseded by any other Signature packet, including other Delegated Revoker Signature packets. If multiple valid Delegated Revoker Signatures are issued by a primary key X, they are all capable of issuing Key Revocation signatures over X on behalf of X.¶\n[...]\nIf an OpenPGP certificate with primary key X has an associated Delegated Revoker containing Public Key Y, that only indicates that the Y MAY make a valid Key Revocation signature on behalf of X, revoking X itself.¶\n[...]\nThe Delegated Revoker mechanism is only useful for a potential scenario where the keyholder has lost control of the primary secret key. Otherwise, the keyholder could simply issue the desired Key Revocation signature (type 0x20) directly.¶\n\n---\n\nTitle: PGP Key Analyser - View PGP key information in your browser\nURL: https://kriztalz.sh/pgp-key-analyser/\nPublished: N/A\nAuthor: N/A\nHighlights:\nPGP Key Analyser - View PGP key information in your browser\n[...]\n## Analyze PGP/OpenPGP Public Keys\n[...]\nUser ID- Name- Email- Comment Created- Expires- Fingerprint- Key ID- Algorithm-\n[...]\nThis free online tool helps you analyze PGP/OpenPGP public keys directly in your browser.
